She was so occupied with admiring those gentle blue eyes, engaging in conversation, and constructing these various plans in the intervals, that the night passed with unusual speed; the supper-table, which invariably concluded such gatherings and for which she was accustomed to monitor the proper hour, was fully arranged and positioned near the hearth before she realized it. With a promptness exceeding the usual motivation of a spirit that was nonetheless never unconcerned with the reputation of performing tasks skillfully and considerately, and with the genuine enthusiasm of a mind pleased by its own concepts, she proceeded to preside over the meal, offering and suggesting the minced chicken and scalloped oysters with a persistence she understood would be welcomed by the early habits and polite hesitations of their visitors.

During such events, poor Mr. Woodhouse’s sentiments were in a state of painful conflict. He enjoyed having the table set, as it had been the custom of his younger days, yet his belief that suppers were highly unhealthy made him somewhat regretful to see anything placed upon it; while his sense of hospitality would have invited his guests to partake of everything, his concern for their well-being caused him to lament that they would consume it.

Only another small bowl of light gruel like his own was something he could, with complete personal approval, suggest; although he might force himself, while the ladies were contentedly finishing the more appetizing dishes, to remark:

“Mrs. Bates, allow me to suggest you try one of these eggs. An egg boiled quite soft is not unhealthy. Serle understands how to boil an egg better than anyone. I would not suggest an egg boiled by anyone else; but you need not worry, they are quite small, you see—one of our small eggs will not harm you. Miss Bates, let Emma serve you a little bit of tart—a very little bit. Ours are all apple-tarts. You need not fear unhealthy preserves here. I do not suggest the custard. Mrs. Goddard, what do you think of half a glass of wine? A small half-glass, mixed into a tumbler of water? I do not believe it could disagree with you.”

Emma permitted her father to speak—but provided for her guests in a far more pleasing manner, and on this particular evening took special delight in ensuring they departed content. The joy of Miss Smith was entirely consistent with her expectations. Miss Woodhouse was such a significant figure in Highbury that the anticipation of the meeting had caused as much anxiety as excitement; yet the modest, appreciative young woman left with deeply satisfied feelings, thrilled by the kindness with which Miss Woodhouse had interacted with her throughout the evening, and having actually shaken hands with her at the conclusion!

Let her marry Robert Martin, and she is safe, respectable, and happy forever; but if you encourage her to expect a grand marriage, and teach her to be satisfied with nothing less than a man of status and large fortune, she may remain a student at Mrs. Goddard’s for the rest of her life—or, at least, (since Harriet Smith is a girl who will marry someone eventually,) until she grows desperate and is glad to settle for the old writing-master’s son.”

“We think so differently on this point, Mr. Knightley, that there is no use in arguing about it. We will only make each other angrier. She must live with the consequences of having refused him, whatever they may be; and as for the refusal itself, I will not pretend to say that I might not have influenced her a little; but I assure you there was very little for me or for anybody to do. “Nonsense, absolute nonsense, as ever was spoken!” cried Mr. Knightley. “Robert Martin’s manners are supported by sense, sincerity, and good humor; and his mind has more true refinement than Harriet Smith could understand.”

Emma made no answer, and tried to look cheerfully unconcerned, but she was actually feeling uncomfortable and very much wanted him to leave.

Mr. Knightley was occupied with the bottom buttons of his heavy leather gaiters, and whether from the physical effort or some other reason, his face flushed as he replied,

Emma squeezed her friend’s hand back firmly; she was sufficiently satisfied to remark,

“You lack vanity, Mr. Knightley. I will grant you that.”

He appeared barely to notice her; he was pensive—and in a manner that indicated his displeasure, he added soon after,

“So you have decided that I ought to wed Jane Fairfax?”

“Certainly not. You have reprimanded me too often for matchmaking for me to dare take such a liberty with you. My previous comment meant nothing. One makes such remarks, naturally, without any serious intent. Oh! No, I truly have no desire for you to marry Jane Fairfax or anyone else. You would not visit and sit with us in this relaxed manner if you were a married man.”

Mr. Knightley grew pensive once more. His reflection concluded with, “No, Emma, I do not believe my admiration for her will ever catch me off guard.—I have never considered her in that light, I assure you.” And shortly after, “Jane Fairfax is a very lovely young lady—but even Jane Fairfax is not flawless. She possesses a defect. She lacks the candid disposition a man would desire in a spouse.”

Emma could not help but be glad to learn she had a flaw. “Well,” she said, “and I assume you quickly quieted Mr. Cole?”

“Indeed, very quickly. He offered a subtle suggestion; I informed him he was in error; he apologized and dropped the subject. Cole has no desire to appear more intelligent or clever than his peers.”

The entire group had only just gathered again in the parlor when Mr. Weston arrived. Having returned for a belated dinner, he walked to Hartfield immediately afterward. He was anticipated by the most discerning observers, so his arrival caused no shock, yet it brought great delight. Mr. Woodhouse was nearly as pleased to welcome him now as he would have been distressed to see him earlier. John Knightley alone remained in silent disbelief. That a gentleman who could have passed his evening peacefully at home following a day of professional duties in London would depart once more, walking half a mile to another person’s residence just to endure social interaction until bedtime, and to conclude his day amidst the exertion of politeness and the clamor of a crowd, was a fact that profoundly affected him. A man who had been active since eight in the morning and could have been resting, who had been speaking at length and could have been quiet, who had been in multiple throngs and could have been solitary! For such a man to abandon the peace and autonomy of his own hearth, and on the evening of a chilly, sleety April day, to venture out into the world again! Had he been able to retrieve his wife with a mere gesture, there would have been a reason; yet his arrival would likely extend rather than conclude the gathering. John Knightley observed him with wonder, then lifted his shoulders and remarked, “I could not have credited it, even of him.”

Mr. Weston, meanwhile, entirely unaware of the resentment he was provoking, remained as joyful and buoyant as ever. Possessing the prerogative of being the primary speaker, which a day spent away from home provides, he was making himself pleasant to the others. Having answered his wife’s questions regarding his meal, assuring her that none of her meticulous instructions to the staff had been overlooked, and sharing whatever public news he had gathered, he moved on to a private matter. Although intended primarily for Mrs. Weston, he had no doubt it would be of great interest to everyone present. He handed her a letter; it was from Frank, addressed to her. He had encountered it on his journey and had taken the liberty of breaking the seal.

Mr. Woodhouse, I wish you had heard his charming compliments to me at dinner. Oh, I assure you, I began to think my dear husband would be truly jealous. I imagine I am quite a favorite; he even noticed my dress. How do you like it? It was Selina’s choice—handsome, I think, though I am not sure if it is a bit over-decorated. I have a real dislike for the idea of being over-decorated—I have a genuine horror of excessive finery. I have to wear a few ornaments now because it is expected of me. A bride, you know, must look like a bride, but my natural taste is for simplicity; a simple style of dress is so much better than being overly ornate. But I am in the minority, I suppose; few people seem to value simple dressing—show and finery are everything. I am thinking of adding a trim like this to my white and silver poplin. Do you think it will look good?”

Before this second viewing began, however, Emma walked into the hall to get a few moments to observe the entrance and the layout of the house. She had barely arrived when Jane Fairfax appeared, coming quickly in from the garden with a look of escape. Not expecting to meet Miss Woodhouse so soon, she started at first, but Miss Woodhouse was the very person she was looking for.

“Now, ma’am,” Jane remarked to her aunt, “shall we go find Mrs. Elton?”

“If you like, my dear. With all my heart. I am perfectly prepared. I was prepared to have departed with her, but this will serve just as well. We shall quickly catch up to her. There she is—no, that is someone else. That is one of the women from the Irish carriage group, not at all resembling her.—Well, I declare—”

They departed, trailed shortly thereafter by Mr. Knightley. Mr. Weston, his son, Emma, and Harriet remained, and the young man’s mood now climbed to a level nearly disagreeable. Even Emma eventually grew weary of the praise and gaiety, wishing instead to be strolling calmly with any of the others, or sitting nearly solitary, and entirely ignored, in peaceful contemplation of the lovely scenery below her. The sight of the attendants searching for them to announce the carriages was a welcome relief; and even the commotion of gathering and getting ready to leave, and the eagerness of Mrs. Elton to have her carriage arrive first, were patiently tolerated, in anticipation of the peaceful ride home that would conclude the highly dubious pleasures of this day of enjoyment. She hoped never to be lured into another such plan, made up of so many mismatched individuals, again.

While expecting the carriage, she discovered Mr. Knightley at her elbow. He glanced about, as if to ensure no one was nearby, and then remarked,

“Emma, I must speak to you once more as I have been accustomed to do: a right perhaps merely tolerated rather than permitted, yet I must still exercise it. I cannot watch you behaving incorrectly without a protest. How could you be so heartless toward Miss Bates? How could you be so rude in your humor toward a woman of her standing, years, and circumstances?—Emma, I had not imagined it possible.”

Emma remembered, flushed, felt regret, but attempted to dismiss it with a laugh.

“Why, how could I avoid saying what I did?—No one could have avoided it. It was not that terrible. I suspect she did not grasp my meaning.”

“I can confirm she did. She understood your entire intent. She has spoken of it since. I wish you could have heard how she spoke of it—with such fairness and kindness. I wish you could have heard her praising your patience, in being capable of showing her such kindness, as she was constantly receiving from you and your father, when her company must be so tiresome.”

“Oh!” Emma exclaimed, “I am aware there is not a finer person in existence: but you must concede, that what is virtuous and what is absurd are most unhappily mixed in her.”

“They are mixed,” he replied, “I concede; and, were she wealthy, I could excuse much for the occasional dominance of the absurd over the virtuous. Were she a woman of means, I would permit every harmless folly to pass, I would not argue with you for any freedoms of behavior. Were she your peer in status—but, Emma, reflect on how far this is from being the truth. She is indigent; she has fallen from the luxuries she was accustomed to; and, if she survives to old age, must likely fall further.”

Leaving the education to her seemed to imply it.

For the moment, there was nothing Emma could do for Harriet beyond offering her best wishes for the future.

“Well, Miss Woodhouse!” Harriet exclaimed, rushing into the chamber—“is this not the most peculiar news imaginable?”

“To what news do you refer?” Emma asked, unable to discern from her expression or tone if Harriet had actually been tipped off.

“Regarding Jane Fairfax. Have you ever heard anything so bizarre? Oh!—you need not hesitate to admit it to me, for Mr. Weston informed me himself. I encountered him just now. He mentioned it was meant to be a major secret; therefore, I would not dream of discussing it with anyone but you, yet he claimed you were aware of it.”

“What did Mr. Weston disclose to you?” Emma inquired, still bewildered.

“Oh! he explained everything; that Jane Fairfax and Mr. Frank Churchill are to wed, and that they have been secretly betrothed for quite some time. How truly strange!”

It was indeed so strange; Harriet’s conduct was so remarkably odd that Emma struggled to interpret it. Her personality seemed entirely transformed. She appeared to exhibit no distress, disappointment, or particular anxiety regarding the news. Emma stared at her, utterly speechless.

“Did you have any inkling,” Harriet asked, “that he was in love with her?—You, perhaps, might have.—You (flushing as she spoke) who can perceive everyone’s inner thoughts; but no one else—”

“Honestly,” Emma remarked, “I am beginning to question if I possess such a gift. Can you truly ask me, Harriet, if I suspected him of being attached to another woman while I was—implicitly, if not explicitly—urging you to indulge your own emotions?—I never had the faintest suspicion, until this past hour, that Mr. Frank Churchill held any affection for Jane Fairfax. You may be certain that had I known, I would have warned you.”

“Me!” Harriet exclaimed, blushing and surprised. “Why would you warn me?—You cannot think I have feelings for Mr. Frank Churchill.”

“I am thrilled to hear you speak so firmly on the matter,” Emma replied, smiling; “but you do not intend to deny that there was a period—and not long ago—when you led me to believe that you did care for him?”

“Him!—never, never. Dear Miss Woodhouse, how could you so misinterpret me?” she said, turning away in distress.

“Harriet!” Emma cried, after a brief silence—“What do you mean?—Good Heavens! what do you mean?—Misinterpret you!—Am I to conclude then?—”

She could not utter another syllable. Her voice failed her, and she sat down, waiting in great alarm for Harriet to respond.

Harriet, who stood some distance away with her back turned, did not reply at once; and when she finally spoke, her tone was nearly as shaken as Emma’s.

“I would not have believed it possible,” she began, “that you could have misunderstood me! I know we agreed never to mention his name—but considering how vastly superior he is to everyone else, I would not have thought it possible that I could be taken to mean anyone else. Mr. Frank Churchill, indeed! I cannot imagine who would ever notice him when in the presence of the other.”

If only the reveal could have gone just as smoothly! But unfortunately, there was no chance of that.

“If I were not certain she is happy now,” Emma stated gravely, “which, despite any minor reservations from her meticulous conscience, she must be, I could not endure these expressions of gratitude; for, oh! Mrs. Weston, if one were to tally the harm and the good I have caused Miss Fairfax!—Well (stopping herself, and attempting a lighter tone), all of this must be put aside. You are very generous to share these compelling details. They present her in the best possible light. I am certain she is very virtuous—I hope she will be very happy. It is appropriate that the wealth should be on his side, for I believe the true merit belongs entirely to her.”

Such a final remark could not go unchallenged by Mrs. Weston. She held a high opinion of Frank in nearly every regard; and, furthermore, she cared for him deeply, so her defense was passionate. She spoke with considerable logic, and at least as much affection—but she had too many points to make for Emma to follow; her thoughts soon drifted to Brunswick Square or Donwell; she stopped trying to pay attention; and when Mrs. Weston concluded with, “We have not yet received the letter we are so eager for, you know, but I trust it will arrive shortly,” she had to hesitate before replying, and eventually had to respond vaguely, before she could even recall which letter they were so anxious to receive.

“Oh! perfectly. I am always well, as you know. Make sure to inform me about the letter as soon as you can.”

Mrs. Weston’s news provided Emma with further material for uncomfortable contemplation, by deepening her respect and sympathy, as well as her awareness of her previous unfairness toward Miss Fairfax. She felt sharp regret for not having pursued a deeper friendship with her, and felt ashamed of the jealous impulses that had surely played some part. Had she acted upon Mr. Knightley’s known desires, by showing Miss Fairfax the consideration she fully deserved; had she attempted to understand her better; had she done her share to foster a bond; had she sought a friend in her rather than in Harriet Smith; she would, in all likelihood, have been saved from every distress that burdened her now.

“One natural consequence of the trouble she had brought upon herself,” she said, “was that it made her unreasonable. The awareness of having acted wrongly exposed her to endless anxieties, making her irritable and touchy to a degree that must have been—that certainly was—difficult for him to endure. ‘I did not make the allowances,’ she said, ‘that I should have made for his temperament and spirits—his delightful spirits, and that gaiety, that playful disposition, which, under any other circumstances, I am certain would have been as constantly charming to me as they were at first.’ She then began to speak of you, and of the great kindness you showed her during her illness; and with a blush that revealed how it was all connected, she asked me, whenever I had the opportunity, to thank you—I could not thank you enough—for every wish and every effort to help her. You are very kind to bring me these interesting details. They show her in the best possible light. I am sure she is very good—I hope she will be very happy. She bitterly regretted not having sought a closer acquaintance with her, and blushed for the envious feelings which had certainly been, in some measure, the cause. Had she followed Mr. Knightley’s known wishes, in paying that attention to Miss Fairfax which was in every way her due; had she tried to know her better; had she done her part toward intimacy; had she endeavored to find a friend there instead of in Harriet Smith; she would, in all probability, have been spared every pain that pressed on her now.

“One natural result of the trouble she had caused herself,” she said, “was that she became unreasonable. Knowing she had acted wrongly made her anxious, irritable, and difficult to deal with—a burden that must have been hard for him to bear. ‘I did not make the allowances I should have,’ she said, ‘for his temper and his wonderful, playful spirit. Under any other circumstances, I am sure those traits would have remained as charming to me as they were at the beginning.’ She then spoke of you and the great kindness you showed her during her illness. With a blush that revealed how much it meant to her, she asked me to thank you—I could not thank you enough—for every wish and effort you made to help her. She realized she had never properly thanked you herself.”

“If I did not know she was happy now,” Emma said seriously, “which she must be, despite her strict conscience, I could not bear these thanks. Oh, Mrs. Weston, if there were a record of all the good and bad I have done Miss Fairfax! Well,” she added, checking herself and trying to sound more cheerful, “that is all in the past. You are very kind to share these details. They show her in the best light. I am sure she is very good, and I hope she will be very happy. It is only right that the fortune should be on his side, for I believe the merit belongs entirely to her.”

Mrs. Weston could not let that comment go unanswered. She thought highly of Frank in almost every way, and because she loved him dearly, she defended him earnestly. She spoke with great logic and equal affection, but she had too much to say for Emma to pay attention. Emma’s mind wandered to Brunswick Square or Donwell; she stopped trying to listen. When Mrs. Weston finished by saying, “We are still waiting for the letter we are so anxious to receive, you know,” Emma had to pause before answering, and she eventually replied at random, struggling to remember which letter they were waiting for.

“Are you well, my Emma?” was Mrs. Weston’s parting question.

“Oh, perfectly. I am always well, you know. Please be sure to tell me about the letter as soon as possible.”

Mrs. Weston’s news gave Emma more cause for unpleasant reflection, as it increased her respect and sympathy for Miss Fairfax, along with her sense of past injustice toward her. She bitterly regretted not seeking a closer acquaintance with her, and she felt ashamed of the envious feelings that had certainly played a part in her behavior. Had she followed Mr. Knightley’s advice and shown Miss Fairfax the attention she deserved; had she tried to know her better; had she worked toward a friendship instead of focusing on Harriet Smith; she would, in all likelihood, have been spared the pain she felt now.

Having already achieved such favorable results in two requests for forgiveness, I might risk becoming overly confident regarding yours, and that of any friends who felt aggrieved. You must all try to grasp the precise circumstances I faced upon my initial arrival at Randalls; you must view me as someone burdened with a secret that had to be protected at any cost. That was the reality. Whether I was justified in placing myself in a position that necessitated such secrecy is a separate matter, which I will not debate here. Regarding my inclination to believe it was justified, I point any critic toward a brick house in Highbury, featuring sash windows on the ground floor and casements above. I could not approach her publicly; the obstacles I faced given the situation at Enscombe are surely too well understood to need detailing. Fortunately, I succeeded before we separated at Weymouth in persuading the most virtuous woman alive to stoop, out of kindness, to a clandestine engagement. Had she declined, I would have lost my senses. You might ask, what was my hope in taking this step? What did I anticipate? Anything and everything—time, luck, changing circumstances, gradual progress, sudden breakthroughs, persistence, exhaustion, health, and illness. Every potential for happiness lay before me, with the greatest blessing already attained by securing her pledge of loyalty and communication. If you require further clarification, my dear madam, I have the honor of being your husband’s son, and I possess the benefit of inheriting an optimistic nature, which is more valuable than any legacy of property or estates. Consider me, then, in these circumstances, arriving for my first visit to Randalls; here I admit to a fault, for that visit could have occurred earlier. You will recall that I did not arrive until Miss Fairfax was in Highbury, and since you were the one neglected, you will surely pardon me at once. However, I must appeal to my father’s sympathy by reminding him that for as long as I stayed away from his home, I was deprived of the joy of knowing you. My conduct during the delightful fortnight I spent with you did not, I trust, leave me open to criticism, save for one detail. I now reach the primary, indeed the only significant aspect of my behavior while in your company that causes me concern or demands careful explanation. With the utmost respect and sincere friendship, I speak of Miss Woodhouse; my father might perhaps feel I should add, with the deepest regret. A few remarks he made yesterday revealed his perspective, and I concede that I am deserving of some reproach. My conduct toward Miss Woodhouse suggested, I fear, more than was appropriate. To maintain a deception so vital to my plans, I was drawn into making excessive use of the familiarity into which we were quickly thrust. I cannot deny that Miss Woodhouse was my apparent focus, but I trust you will accept my assurance that, had I not been certain of her lack of affection for me, no selfish motive would have compelled me to continue.

Mrs. Bates and Mrs. Elton were sitting together. Miss Bates was away, which explained the earlier quiet. Emma would have preferred Mrs. Elton to be somewhere else, yet she felt inclined to be tolerant of everyone; since Mrs. Elton greeted her with unexpected warmth, she trusted the meeting would not be unpleasant. 

She quickly felt she could read Mrs. Elton’s mind and grasp why she, like Emma, was in such high spirits; it was the result of sharing Miss Fairfax’s confidence and imagining she knew something that remained hidden from everyone else. Emma noticed signs of this immediately in her facial expressions; while offering her own remarks to Mrs. Bates and pretending to listen to the elderly lady’s answers, she watched her fold up a letter with a kind of nervous, showy secrecy—a letter she had seemingly been reading aloud to Miss Fairfax—and tuck it back into the purple and gold handbag at her side, remarking with meaningful nods,

“For when a lady’s in the case,

Now I say, my dear, in our case, instead of lady, read—mum! a word to the wise.—I am in a wonderful mood, am I not? But I want to reassure you regarding Mrs. S.—My explanation, you see, has completely calmed her.”

“I mentioned no names, you will notice.—Oh! no; I am as cautious as a diplomat. I handled it perfectly.”

He hesitated briefly, smiling once more while keeping his gaze upon her. “Does nothing come to mind? Do you not recall? Harriet Smith.”

Her face reddened at the mention, and she experienced a vague sense of apprehension, though she could not define its source.

“Have you received word from her today?” he asked. “I suspect you have, and are fully informed.”

“No, I have not; I am entirely ignorant; please inform me.”

“I see you are braced for the worst—and it is indeed quite bad. Harriet Smith is to marry Robert Martin.”

Emma recoiled, appearing far from prepared, and while her wide eyes signaled, “No, this cannot be!” her mouth remained shut.

“It is true,” Mr. Knightley persisted; “I heard it from Robert Martin himself. He departed from me less than thirty minutes ago.”

She continued to stare at him in profound astonishment.

“I see you are as displeased as I anticipated. I wish we shared the same view. Yet, in time, we shall. Time will surely lead one of us to alter our perspective; until then, we need not discuss it further.”

“You misunderstand me, you entirely misunderstand,” she answered, forcing herself to speak. “It is not that such news would distress me now, but I find it incredible. It seems impossible! You cannot be suggesting that Harriet Smith has agreed to marry Robert Martin. You cannot mean he has even proposed to her again—not yet. You only mean he intends to.”

“I mean that he has already done so,” Mr. Knightley replied with a smile of firm conviction, “and has been accepted.”

“Good heavens!” she exclaimed. “Well!” Then, turning to her sewing basket to hide her face and conceal the intense joy and amusement she knew must be visible, she added, “Well, tell me everything; explain this to me. “It is a straightforward tale. He traveled to town on errands three days ago, and I requested that he carry some documents I needed to send to John. Elizabeth remained quiet, though she remained skeptical: their conduct at the ball had not been designed to win general favor; and possessing sharper perception and a less yielding disposition than her sister, alongside a perspective unclouded by any personal flattery, she felt little inclination to admire them. In truth, they were very grand ladies; they were not lacking in good nature when satisfied, nor in the ability to be pleasant when they desired, yet they were arrogant and vain. They were somewhat attractive; they had received their schooling at a premier private academy in London; they possessed a dowry of twenty thousand pounds; they were accustomed to living beyond their means and mingling with the aristocracy; consequently, they felt entirely justified in holding themselves in high regard while looking down upon others. They hailed from a reputable northern family—a fact they remembered far more vividly than the reality that their brother’s wealth, and their own, had been earned through commerce.

Mr. Bingley had received an inheritance of nearly one hundred thousand pounds from his father, who had planned to buy an estate but passed away before doing so. Mr. Bingley held the same intention and occasionally debated which county to choose; however, since he now possessed a comfortable residence and the rights to a manor, many who understood his relaxed nature doubted whether he might simply remain at Netherfield for life, leaving the purchase to his heirs.

His sisters were quite eager for him to own property; yet, even though he was merely a tenant, Miss Bingley was perfectly happy to act as his hostess, and Mrs. Hurst, who had wed a man of greater social standing than wealth, was equally ready to treat his home as her own whenever it was convenient. Mr. Bingley had been an adult for less than two years when a chance suggestion led him to view Netherfield Park. He inspected the grounds and the interior for thirty minutes; he liked the location and the main rooms, accepted the landlord’s favorable description, and rented it at once.

He and Darcy shared a very firm friendship, despite their contrasting personalities. Bingley was valued by Darcy for his relaxed, candid, and flexible nature, even though no character could be more opposite to his own, and despite the fact that Darcy never seemed unhappy with his own temperament. Bingley placed absolute trust in Darcy’s loyalty and held his intellect in the highest regard. In terms of intelligence, Darcy was the superior. Bingley was certainly not unintelligent, but Darcy was brilliant. At the same time, he was proud, distant, and difficult to please; his behavior, while polished, was not welcoming. In that regard, his friend held a significant advantage. Bingley was certain to be popular wherever he went; Darcy was constantly causing offense.

“Their manners don’t match his.”

“Certainly not at first, but they are very pleasant women once you talk to them. Miss Bingley is going to live with her brother to manage his household, and I would be very surprised if we don’t find her to be a charming neighbor.”

Elizabeth listened quietly but remained unconvinced. Their behavior at the dance had not been designed to please the crowd, and because she was more observant and less easily swayed than her sister—and because her own judgment wasn’t clouded by any personal attention from them—she was not inclined to like them. In reality, they were very posh ladies; they were good-natured enough when they were happy and could be charming when they wanted to be, but they were also arrogant and conceited. Elizabeth remained silent as she took her position, struck by the significance of standing across from Mr. Darcy, and observing the matching surprise on the faces of those around them. “My justifications for marriage are, firstly, that I believe it is proper for every clergyman in comfortable circumstances, such as myself, to provide a matrimonial example within his parish; secondly, that I am certain it will contribute significantly to my own contentment; and, thirdly, which I perhaps should have stated sooner, that it is the specific counsel and suggestion of the distinguished lady whom I have the privilege of naming my patroness. Several days elapsed before Jane found the strength to discuss her emotions with Elizabeth; eventually, once Mrs. Bennet departed, leaving them alone following a more prolonged period of agitation than usual regarding Netherfield and its proprietor, she could not refrain from remarking,

When coffee was finished, Colonel Fitzwilliam reminded Elizabeth that she had promised to play for him, and she sat down at the instrument immediately. He pulled a chair near her. Lady Catherine listened to half a song and then talked, as before, to her other nephew, until he walked away from her and, moving with his usual deliberation toward the piano, positioned himself to have a full view of the performer’s face. Elizabeth saw what he was doing, and at the first convenient pause, she turned to him with a playful smile and said,

“You intend to frighten me, Mr. Darcy, by coming over in such a formal manner to hear me. But I will not be alarmed, even if your sister does play so well. There is a stubbornness in me that cannot bear to be intimidated by the will of others. My courage always rises whenever someone tries to intimidate me.”

“I will not say that you are mistaken,” he replied, “because you could not really believe that I have any intention of alarming you; and I have known you long enough to understand that you take great pleasure in occasionally professing opinions which, in fact, are not your own.”

Darcy’s eyes had turned toward them repeatedly with a look of curiosity. It was clear that Lady Catherine shared this interest, as she did not hesitate to call out, “What are you saying, Fitzwilliam? What are you talking about? What are you telling Miss Bennet? Let me hear it.”

“We were talking about music, madam,” he said, when he could no longer avoid answering.

“Music! Then please speak up. It is my favorite subject. I must be included if you are discussing music. I imagine few people in England enjoy music more than I do, or have a better natural taste. If I had ever learned, I would have been very skilled. Anne would have been, too, if her health had allowed her to practice. I am certain she would have played beautifully. How is Georgiana doing, Darcy?”

Mr. Darcy spoke with warm praise of his sister’s musical skill.

“I am very glad to hear that,” said Lady Catherine. “Please tell her from me that she cannot expect to excel if she does not practice a great deal.”

“I assure you, madam,” he replied, “that she does not need that advice. She practices very consistently.”

“That is good. One cannot practice too much. When I write to her next, I will insist that she does not neglect it. I often tell young ladies that no one can play well without constant practice. I have told Miss Bennet several times that she will never play well unless she practices more. And although Mrs. Collins has no piano, I have often told her she is welcome to come to Rosings every day to play the instrument in Mrs. Jenkinson’s room. She would not be in anyone’s way in that part of the house.”

Mr. Darcy looked slightly embarrassed by his aunt’s rudeness and did not answer.

He spoke very little, and she did not exert herself to converse or attend to him; yet, during their third meeting, she noticed he posed several strange, disjointed inquiries regarding her enjoyment of Hunsford, her preference for lonely strolls, and her thoughts on the domestic contentment of Mr. and Mrs. Collins. This thought troubled her slightly, and she felt relieved to reach the gate in the fence across from the Parsonage.

One day, while walking and absorbed in reading Jane’s latest letter—reflecting on sections that indicated Jane’s low spirits—she looked up to find Colonel Fitzwilliam approaching, rather than the usual surprise of Mr. Darcy. Quickly stowing the letter and summoning a smile, she remarked,

“I was unaware that you ever strolled in this direction.”

“I have been circling the park,” he answered, “as is my annual custom, and I planned to finish by stopping at the Parsonage. Do you intend to walk much further?”

“No, I was just about to turn back.”

She turned as she said this, and they walked toward the Parsonage in company.

“Are you definitely departing Kent on Saturday?” she asked.

“Yes—provided Darcy does not postpone it once more. I am subject to his schedule. He manages our affairs exactly as he sees fit.”

“And if he cannot satisfy himself with the plan, he at least takes great satisfaction in his ability to decide. I cannot think of anyone who appears to relish the freedom to act as he pleases more than Mr. Darcy.”

“He certainly enjoys having his own way,” Colonel Fitzwilliam responded. “But we all do. The difference is that he possesses greater resources to achieve it than most, being wealthy while others are not. I speak from experience. A younger son, you realize, must become accustomed to sacrifice and reliance on others.”

“In my view, an earl’s younger son can understand very little of either. Tell me truly, what have you ever experienced of sacrifice or dependence? When have you been stopped by a lack of funds from traveling where you wished or obtaining anything you desired?”

“Those are pointed questions—and perhaps I cannot claim to have faced many such difficulties. Yet, in more significant matters, I may suffer due to a lack of wealth. Younger sons are not free to marry whom they choose.”

“Unless they choose women of wealth, which I suspect they frequently do.”

“Our expensive lifestyles render us too reliant on others, and few in my social standing can manage to marry without considering financial matters.”

“Is this,” Elizabeth wondered, “directed at me?” She flushed at the notion, but regaining her composure, she replied with a playful air, “And tell me, what is the typical market value of an earl’s younger son?”

It unsettled her slightly, and she was quite relieved to reach the gate in the fence opposite the Parsonage.

Darcy’s nature, despite his promise that regard for the father would keep him from revealing the son’s faults.

How differently she now viewed everything involving him! His interest in Miss King now seemed entirely and repulsively driven by greed; the modest size of her inheritance no longer suggested he was easily satisfied, but rather that he was desperate to secure any fortune. His conduct toward her could no longer be excused: he had either been misled about her wealth or had been feeding his own ego by fostering the interest she feared she had too openly displayed. Every remaining inclination to defend him faded away, and in further defense of Mr. Darcy, she had to admit that Mr. Bingley, when asked by Jane, had long ago insisted on his innocence in the matter. Despite his haughty and cold demeanor, she had never—throughout their entire acquaintance, which had recently increased and allowed her to observe his habits—witnessed anything suggesting he was dishonest or unfair, nor anything indicating he lacked religious or moral principles. She recalled that he was respected by his own circle, that even Wickham had acknowledged his virtues as a brother, and that she had frequently heard him speak of his sister with such warmth that he clearly possessed some kind heart. If his deeds had truly been as Wickham described, such a blatant disregard for morality could scarcely have been hidden from society, and a friendship between such a man and the kind-hearted Mr. Bingley would have been impossible.

She felt utterly embarrassed. She could not think of Darcy or Wickham without realizing she had been shortsighted, biased, narrow-minded, and foolish.

“How contemptibly I have behaved!” she exclaimed. “I, who boasted of my insight! I, who took pride in my own judgment! I, who often scorned my sister’s generous nature and fed my own vanity with pointless or unfounded suspicion. How degrading this realization is! Yet, how deserved! Had I been in love, I could not have been more miserably oblivious. But it was vanity, not love, that misled me. Flattered by the attention of one man and slighted by the indifference of the other from the start, I embraced my own biases and ignorance, casting aside reason whenever they were involved. Until this very instant, I never truly understood myself.”

Moving from her own failings to Jane, and from Jane to Bingley, her reflections led her to recall that Mr. Darcy’s account of that situation had seemed quite unconvincing, so she reread it. The impact of this second reading was entirely different. How could she refuse to believe his claims in this case when she had been forced to accept them in the other? He maintained that he had been completely unaware of her sister’s affection, and she could not help but recall Charlotte’s long-held view on the matter. Furthermore, she could not dispute the accuracy of his assessment of Jane. She realized that Jane’s emotions, though deep, were rarely shown, and that she possessed a consistent calmness of manner not typically found alongside intense sensitivity.

When she reached the section of the letter discussing her own family, written with such humiliating yet deserved criticism, her sense of shame was intense.

“Truly, you are in error. I have no such personal grievances to address. I am speaking of broad, general consequences, not specific ones. Mr. Bennet perceived that she was deeply invested in the matter; he took her hand kindly and replied,

“Do not distress yourself, my dear. Wherever you and Jane are known, you will be held in high regard; you will not appear any less impressive for having two—or perhaps three—very foolish sisters. Colonel Forster is a prudent man who will prevent her from getting into any genuine trouble; fortunately, she is too poor to be a target for anyone. Let us trust, therefore, that her time there might teach her how unimportant she truly is. Regardless, she cannot become much worse without giving us cause to confine her for the remainder of her days.”

Because of her ignorance and empty-headedness, she will be completely unable to deflect the universal contempt that her desperate need for attention will provoke. Kitty is also in danger. She will follow wherever Lydia leads. Vain, ignorant, lazy, and completely undisciplined! Oh, my dear father, can you really believe they won’t be criticized and looked down upon wherever they go, and that their sisters won’t often be dragged into the disgrace?”

Mr. Bennet saw how deeply she felt about the subject and, taking her hand affectionately, replied:

“Don’t worry yourself, my dear. Wherever you and Jane are known, you will be respected and valued; you won’t appear any less impressive just because you have a couple—or, I might say, three—very silly sisters. We will have no peace at Longbourn if Lydia doesn’t go to Brighton. Let her go, then. Colonel Forster is a sensible man who will keep her out of any real trouble, and she is fortunately too poor to be a target for anyone. At Brighton, she will be even less significant as a flirt than she has been here. The officers will find women more worthy of their attention. Let us hope, therefore, that her time there might teach her how insignificant she is. At any rate, she cannot get much worse without giving us cause to lock her away for the rest of her life.”

Elizabeth had to be satisfied with this answer, but her own opinion remained unchanged, and she left him feeling disappointed and sorry. It was not in her nature, however, to add to her frustrations by dwelling on them. She was confident that she had done her duty, and fretting over unavoidable problems or making them worse through anxiety was not part of her personality.

“If you realized,” Elizabeth said, “the great disadvantage to us all that comes from Lydia’s reckless and imprudent behavior—in fact, the disadvantage that has already occurred—I am sure you would view this matter differently.”

Mr. Gardiner, despite rarely finding the opportunity, possessed a great love for fishing. He became so preoccupied with observing the trout surfacing in the stream and discussing them with the attendant that his progress remained quite sluggish. As they strolled along in this leisurely fashion, they were startled once more—Elizabeth’s surprise matching her initial reaction—by the sight of Mr. Darcy drawing near. Because this section of the path lacked the heavy cover found on the opposite side, they spotted him well before they converged. Although stunned, Elizabeth felt better equipped for an encounter than previously and determined to maintain a composed demeanor should he truly intend to approach. For a brief interval, she hoped he might veer onto a different trail. This expectation held while a bend in the path hid him from sight, but once he rounded the corner, he stood directly before them. A single look confirmed that his recent courteous manner remained intact. Attempting to mirror his civility, she began to praise the scenery as they met, yet she faltered after uttering only “delightful” and “charming.” Unwelcome thoughts intruded, and she feared that her compliments regarding Pemberley might be interpreted in a troublesome light. Her face flushed, and she fell silent.

Mrs. Gardiner lingered slightly behind, and when Elizabeth paused, Darcy requested the honor of an introduction to her companions. This gesture of politeness caught her entirely off guard; she could barely hide a smile at the irony of him seeking the acquaintance of the very individuals whose social standing had once offended his pride when he proposed to her. “How shocked he will be,” she mused, “once he discovers their identity! He clearly mistakes them for people of high society.”

The introduction proceeded at once. As she identified their kinship, she cast a furtive glance at him to gauge his reaction, half-expecting him to flee from such embarrassing company. It was obvious that the connection startled him, yet he endured the revelation with composure. Far from departing, he turned to accompany them and began speaking with Mr. Gardiner. Elizabeth felt a sense of satisfaction and triumph. It was reassuring that he should realize she possessed relatives for whom she felt no shame. She paid close attention to their exchange, taking pride in every remark her uncle made that demonstrated his intellect, refined taste, or gentlemanly conduct.

The discussion soon shifted to fishing, and Elizabeth heard Mr. Darcy cordially invite her uncle to fish there whenever he pleased during his stay in the area. He offered to provide the necessary equipment and indicated the specific areas of the water where the best catches were found. Mrs. Gardiner, walking arm in arm with Elizabeth, exchanged a look of amazement with her. Elizabeth remained silent, yet she felt deeply pleased, knowing the gesture was intended for her sake. Nevertheless, her bewilderment remained profound, and she kept asking herself, “Why has he changed so much?”

Elizabeth quickly realized that Miss Bingley was keeping a sharp eye on her, ensuring that any remark she made, particularly to Miss Darcy, drew her notice. While this scrutiny would not have deterred her from attempting to converse with the younger woman, the awkward seating arrangement made it difficult; regardless, she felt relieved to avoid the burden of conversation, as her mind was occupied with her own reflections. She anticipated the arrival of the gentlemen at any second, feeling a mixture of hope and dread regarding the potential appearance of the master of the house, though she could not decide which emotion held more sway. After fifteen minutes of silence from Miss Bingley, Elizabeth was startled when she was asked, in a frigid tone, about the well-being of her relatives. She replied with matching brevity and detachment, and the conversation ceased.

The visit’s next development occurred when attendants brought in cold meats, cakes, and an assortment of seasonal fruits, though this only happened after Mrs. Annesley had exchanged several meaningful glances and smiles with Miss Darcy to prompt her duties. The entire group now had a task; even if they could not all converse, they could all partake in the refreshments, and the elegant displays of grapes, nectarines, and peaches soon drew them toward the table.

During this activity, Elizabeth had a clear chance to determine whether she truly hoped for or feared Mr. Darcy’s arrival based on her reaction to his entry; yet, despite having felt certain of her desire just moments earlier, she immediately wished he had not come.

He had spent some time with Mr. Gardiner and several other gentlemen from the estate by the river, departing only upon discovering that the ladies intended to call on Georgiana that morning. As soon as he arrived, Elizabeth made a sensible decision to remain calm and composed—a resolution that felt vital, though perhaps difficult to maintain, as she noticed the group’s suspicions were piqued, with nearly every eye tracking his conduct upon entering. Miss Bingley’s face displayed the most intense, curious scrutiny, despite the smiles she wore whenever she addressed him; her jealousy had not yet rendered her reckless, and she continued to pursue him. Upon her brother’s arrival, Miss Darcy made a greater effort to speak, and Elizabeth observed his eagerness for his sister and herself to become better acquainted, as he encouraged every conversational opening. Miss Bingley noticed this as well and, driven by rash irritation, seized the first chance to remark with mocking politeness:

She did not dare utter Wickham’s name while Darcy was present, but Elizabeth immediately understood that he was the true subject of her thoughts. The memories associated with him caused her a brief moment of pain, but she gathered her resolve to deflect the spiteful comment and soon replied with a reasonably composed air.

My father is departing for London with Colonel Forster at once to attempt to locate her. What he intends to do, I have no idea; but his extreme agitation will prevent him from acting in the most prudent manner, and Colonel Forster must return to Brighton by tomorrow evening. In such a crisis, my uncle’s counsel and help would mean everything; he will instantly grasp my feelings, and I trust in his kindness.”

“Oh! where, where is my uncle?” Elizabeth shouted, springing from her chair as she concluded the letter, desperate to pursue him without wasting a single precious moment; yet as she approached the door, it was opened by a servant, and Mr. Darcy stood there. Her pallid complexion and frantic demeanor caused him to recoil, and before he could gather his composure to speak, she, whose thoughts were entirely consumed by Lydia’s plight, quickly cried out, “I apologize, but I must depart. I must locate Mr. Gardiner this instant regarding a matter that cannot wait; I have not a second to spare.”

“Good heavens! what has happened?” he exclaimed, with more genuine concern than decorum; then, regaining his composure, “I will not hold you back for a moment; but let me, or let the servant, go after Mr. and Mrs. Gardiner. You are too unwell; you cannot go yourself.”

Elizabeth wavered; but her legs shook beneath her, and she realized how little would be achieved by her trying to follow them. Mr. Darcy paid a visit and remained closeted with him for several hours. Everything had concluded by the time I reached the house, so my inquisitiveness was not nearly as tormented as yours appears to have been. He arrived to inform Mr. Gardiner that he had located your sister and Mr. Wickham, having met and conversed with both—Wickham on multiple occasions, and Lydia once. From what I can gather, he departed Derbyshire merely a day after us and traveled to the city determined to track them down. He claimed his motivation was the belief that he was responsible for Wickham’s true nature remaining hidden, which had allowed a respectable young woman to trust and fall for him. He magnanimously attributed the entire situation to his own misguided arrogance, admitting he had previously considered it beneath his dignity to reveal his personal affairs to the public. He believed his reputation should suffice. Consequently, he felt obligated to intervene and attempt to fix a problem he had inadvertently caused. If he possessed an additional motive, I am certain it would not be to his discredit. He spent several days in London before finding them, yet he possessed a lead that we lacked, which further compelled him to pursue us. It appears a woman named Mrs. Younge, formerly a governess to Miss Darcy, had been dismissed for reasons he did not specify. She subsequently rented a large residence in Edward Street, supporting herself by providing lodgings. Knowing she was close to Wickham, he approached her for information immediately upon his arrival. It took two or three days to extract the necessary details, as she likely required payment to betray her confidence, for she was indeed aware of their whereabouts. Wickham had visited her upon reaching London, and they would have stayed with her had she been able to accommodate them. Eventually, however, our benefactor obtained the address. They were residing on ---- Street. He met with Wickham and subsequently demanded an audience with Lydia. He admitted his initial goal was to convince her to abandon her shameful circumstances and return to her family, promising his full support. Yet, he discovered Lydia was firmly committed to staying. I am certain it appeared in the ‘Times’ and the ‘Courier,’ though it was not announced as it should have been. It merely stated, ‘Lately, George Wickham, Esq., to Miss Lydia Bennet,’ without mentioning her father, her residence, or any other details. My brother Gardiner drafted it, and I am surprised he managed such a clumsy job. Did you happen to notice it?”

Bingley answered that he had, and offered his congratulations. Elizabeth did not venture to look up, so she could not observe Mr. Darcy’s expression.

“It is certainly a wonderful thing to have a daughter married well,” her mother persisted; “yet, Mr. Bingley, it is quite difficult to have her removed from my side. They have traveled to Newcastle, which seems to be quite far north, and they are to remain there for an indefinite period. His regiment is stationed there; for I assume you have heard of his departure from the ----shire militia and his entry into the Regulars. Thank goodness he has some friends, even if he perhaps does not have as many as he merits.”

Elizabeth, recognizing this remark was aimed at Mr. Darcy, felt such agonizing embarrassment that she could barely remain in her chair. It forced her to speak, however, which nothing else had managed to do; she inquired of Bingley if he intended to remain in the neighborhood for long. He believed he would stay for a few weeks.

“Once you have finished hunting your own birds, Mr. Bingley,” her mother added, “I hope you will come here and shoot as many as you like on Mr. Bennet’s estate. I am certain he would be delighted to accommodate you and will preserve the finest coveys for your benefit.”

Elizabeth’s distress grew at such uncalled-for, meddlesome hospitality! She was convinced that if the same promising situation were to emerge now as had encouraged them a year ago, everything would be rushing toward the same frustrating outcome. In that moment, she felt that years of joy could not compensate Jane or herself for such moments of painful humiliation.

“My greatest desire,” she thought to herself, “is to never again be in the presence of either of them. Their company offers no delight that could justify such misery! May I never encounter either one of them again!”

However, the suffering for which years of happiness could provide no remedy was soon eased by noticing how much her sister’s beauty reignited the admiration of her former suitor. Upon his arrival, he had spoken to her sparingly, but with every passing moment, he seemed to grant her more of his focus. He found her just as lovely as he had the previous year; just as kind and sincere, though perhaps slightly less talkative. Jane was eager that no change in her should be noticed, and she truly believed she was conversing as much as ever; yet, her thoughts were so occupied that she was not always aware when she had fallen silent.

When the men stood to depart, Mrs. Bennet remembered her planned courtesy, and they were invited and agreed to dine at Longbourn in a few days.

“Has my nephew proposed marriage to you?”

“Your Ladyship has already stated that such a thing is impossible.”

“It certainly should be, provided he remains in his right mind. However, your cunning and charms might have caused him, in a fit of madness, to disregard his duty to himself and his relatives. You may have ensnared him.”

“If I had, I would be the last person to admit it.”

“Miss Bennet, are you aware of my identity? I am not used to being spoken to in this manner. As one of his closest living relatives, I have a right to be informed of his most private affairs.”

“You have no right to know mine, and your current conduct will certainly not encourage me to be open with you.”

“Let me be perfectly clear. This union, which you are arrogant enough to desire, will never happen. Never. Mr. Darcy is betrothed to my daughter. What is your response to that?”

“Only this: if that is the case, you have no grounds to believe he would propose to me.”

Lady Catherine paused briefly before answering:

“Their engagement is of a unique nature. They have been destined for one another since they were infants. It was the cherished desire of both his mother and mine. We arranged this match while they were still in their cribs, and now, just as the sisters’ wishes are about to be fulfilled, is their marriage to be thwarted by a young woman of lower social standing, no significance, and no connection to the family? Do you have no respect for the desires of his family, or for his unspoken commitment to Miss de Bourgh? Have you no sense of decency or propriety? Have you not heard me state that he was intended for his cousin from his earliest days?”

“Yes, I had heard that previously. But how does that concern me? If there is no other obstacle to my marrying your nephew, I will not be deterred simply because his mother and aunt wanted him to marry Miss de Bourgh. You both did your part in planning the marriage, but its success relied on others. If Mr. Darcy is not bound to his cousin by either duty or desire, why shouldn't he choose someone else? And if I am that choice, why shouldn't I accept him?”

“Because honor, propriety, wisdom, and even self-interest forbid it. Yes, Miss Bennet, self-interest; do not expect to be acknowledged by his family or friends if you deliberately defy their wishes. You will be criticized, ignored, and looked down upon by everyone associated with him. Your marriage will be a scandal; your name will never be spoken by any of us.”

“Those are significant hardships,” Elizabeth answered. “But the wife of Mr. Darcy must possess such remarkable advantages that she would, on the whole, have no reason to complain.”

“You stubborn, willful girl! I am embarrassed for you! Is this how you show gratitude for the kindness I showed you last spring? Do you owe me nothing for that? Let us be seated. You must realize, Miss Bennet, that I arrived here with the firm intention of succeeding, and I will not be turned away from my goal.”

“Has he—has my nephew—asked you to marry him?”

“Your Ladyship has already stated that it is impossible.”

“Your astonishment could not exceed my own at being observed by you. My conscience whispered that I merited no exceptional courtesy, and I admit I did not anticipate obtaining more than I was entitled to.”

“My intention then,” Darcy answered, “was to demonstrate to you, through every kindness within my reach, that I was not so base as to harbor resentment for the past; and I hoped to secure your pardon, to diminish your poor opinion, by showing you that your criticisms had been heeded. How quickly other desires took root, I can scarcely say, but I suspect it was within half an hour of seeing you.”

He then spoke of Georgiana’s pleasure in her company, and of her dismay at its abrupt conclusion; this naturally brought them to the reason for that interruption, and she soon discovered that his decision to pursue her from Derbyshire in search of her sister had been reached before he left the inn, and that his solemnity and contemplation there had stemmed from nothing but the internal conflicts such an objective required.

She voiced her thanks once more, yet the topic was too distressing for either to linger upon.

Having strolled several miles at a relaxed pace, and being too preoccupied to notice their surroundings, they eventually discovered, upon checking their timepieces, that they needed to return home.

I recounted everything that had transpired to render my previous meddling in his matters ridiculous and intrusive. His astonishment was profound. He had never harbored the slightest inkling. Furthermore, I told him that I believed I had been in error to assume, as I once did, that your sister was cold toward him; and since I could readily see that his devotion to her remained unchanged, I felt certain of their future happiness.”

Elizabeth could not suppress a smile at his casual way of guiding his friend.

“Did you speak from your own assessment,” she asked, “when you informed him that my sister loved him, or simply from what I told you last spring?”

“From the former. I had watched her closely during the two visits I recently paid her here; and I was certain of her feelings.”

Because she was reluctant to admit how greatly her closeness with Mr. Darcy had been exaggerated, Elizabeth had not yet replied to Mrs. Gardiner’s lengthy letter; yet now, possessing news she knew would be most pleasing, she felt nearly embarrassed that her aunt and uncle had already missed three days of joy, and she promptly began to write:

“My dear aunt, I should have thanked you sooner, as was proper, for your lengthy, kind, and thorough account; but, to be honest, I was too irritable to write. You imagined more than was actually the case. But now, imagine whatever you wish; let your fancy roam, indulge your imagination in every possible direction, and unless you believe me already wed, you cannot be far from the truth. You must write back very soon, and offer him much more praise than you did previously. I thank you again and again for avoiding the Lakes.”

“But I was embarrassed.”

“And so was I.”

“You could have spoken to me more when you came to dinner.”

“A man who felt less might have.”

“How unfortunate that you have a logical answer, and that I am reasonable enough to accept it! But I wonder how long you would have continued if left to your own devices. I wonder when you would have spoken if I hadn’t asked you! My decision to thank you for your kindness to Lydia certainly had a major effect. Too much, I fear; for what happens to the moral of the story if our comfort comes from breaking a promise, since I shouldn’t have brought up the subject? This won’t do at all.”

“You needn’t worry. The moral will be perfectly sound. Lady Catherine’s unjustifiable attempts to separate us were the very things that removed all my doubts. I don’t owe my current happiness to your eager desire to express gratitude. I wasn’t in the mood to wait for you to open the conversation. My aunt’s news had given me hope, and I was determined to know everything immediately.”

“Lady Catherine has been incredibly useful, which should make her happy, as she loves to be useful. But tell me, why did you come down to Netherfield? Was it just to ride to Longbourn and feel awkward, or did you intend for something more serious to happen?”

“Will you ever have the courage to announce to Lady Catherine what is about to happen to her?”

Hurrying along, I had just traversed a trench I recognized as being close to the Battery and climbed the embankment beyond it, when I spotted a man sitting ahead. His back was turned, his arms were crossed, and he was nodding off, overcome by slumber. 

Thinking he might be more pleased if I surprised him with his morning meal, I approached quietly and tapped his shoulder. He sprang up immediately, and it was not the same individual, but a different man entirely!

Yet this fellow was also clad in rough grey, wore a heavy iron shackle on his leg, and was limping, raspy, and shivering—he possessed every trait of the first man, save for his features and the flat, wide-brimmed, low-crowned felt hat he wore. I took all this in instantly, as I had only a second to observe: he cursed at me, swung at me—a clumsy, feeble strike that missed and nearly toppled him over—and then he vanished into the fog, tripping twice as he fled, until he was out of sight.

"It's the young man!" I mused, my heart leaping as I recognized him. I suspect I would have felt a pang in my liver, too, had I known its location.

I reached the Battery shortly thereafter, and there stood the correct man—clutching himself and pacing back and forth, as if he had spent the entire night doing nothing else—awaiting my arrival. He was dreadfully cold, to be sure. I half anticipated seeing him collapse before me and perish from the bitter chill. His eyes appeared so ravenous that when I gave him the file and he set it upon the grass, it crossed my mind that he might have attempted to consume it had he not noticed my parcel. He did not invert me this time to reach my supplies, but allowed me to remain upright while I unpacked the bundle and cleared my pockets.

"What is inside the flask, boy?" he inquired.

"Brandy," I replied.

He was already shoveling mincemeat into his mouth in a peculiar fashion—more like someone stowing it away in a frantic rush than someone actually eating—but he paused to drink. He trembled so intensely the whole time that he struggled to keep the bottle's neck between his teeth without snapping it off.

"I believe you have the ague," I remarked.

"I share your assessment, lad," he answered.

"It is severe around here," I informed him. "You have been sleeping out on the marshes, and they are terribly prone to ague. And rheumatism as well."

"I shall finish my meal before they kill me," he declared. "I would do so even if I were to be hanged from that gallows over there immediately afterward. I will defeat the tremors that much, I promise you."

He was devouring mincemeat, meatbone, bread, cheese, and pork pie all at once, casting suspicious glances at the surrounding mist while he ate, and frequently pausing—even ceasing his chewing—to listen.

Moving along as quickly as I could, I had just crossed a ditch I knew was very close to the Battery, and had just scrambled up the mound on the other side, when I saw a man sitting in front of me. His back was turned, his arms were folded, and he was nodding forward, heavy with sleep.

I thought he would be more pleased if I surprised him with his breakfast, so I stepped forward quietly and touched him on the shoulder. He jumped up instantly, and it was not the same man, but someone else entirely!

Yet this man was also dressed in coarse grey, had a heavy iron shackle on his leg, was limping, hoarse, and cold, and was everything the other man was, except that his face was different and he wore a flat, broad-brimmed, low-crowned felt hat. I saw all this in an instant, for I only had a moment to look: he swore at me, took a swing at me—a weak, circular blow that missed and nearly knocked him over, causing him to stumble—and then he ran into the mist, tripping twice as he went, until I lost sight of him.

"It’s the young man!" I thought, feeling my heart race as I recognized him. I suspect I would have felt a pain in my liver, too, if I had known where it was located.

I reached the Battery soon after that, and there was the right man—hugging himself and limping back and forth, as if he had never stopped hugging and limping all night—waiting for me. He was incredibly cold, to be sure. I half expected to see him collapse in front of me and die from the extreme chill. His eyes looked so ravenous that when I handed him the file and he set it on the grass, it occurred to me he might have tried to eat it if he hadn't seen my bundle. He didn't turn me upside down this time to get at my things, but left me standing while I opened the bundle and emptied my pockets.

"What’s in the bottle, boy?" he asked.

"Brandy," I said.

He was already shoving mincemeat down his throat in a strange way—more like a man hiding it somewhere in a desperate hurry than a man eating—but he stopped to take some of the liquor. He shivered so violently the whole time that it was all he could do to keep the neck of the bottle between his teeth without biting it off.

"I think you have the chills," I said.

"I’m inclined to agree, boy," he replied.

"It’s bad around here," I told him. "You’ve been lying out on the marshes, and they’re terrible for chills. And rheumatism, too."

"I’ll eat my breakfast before they kill me," he said. "I’d do that even if I were going to be hanged on that gallows over there immediately afterward. I’ll beat the shivers that much, I bet you."

The sergeant finished his drink once more and appeared entirely prepared for a refill. I observed that Mr. Pumblechook, in his role as host, seemed to overlook the fact that he had gifted the wine, instead taking the bottle from Mrs. Joe and claiming the praise for serving it with a burst of cheer. Even I received a portion. He was so generous with the wine that he requested the second bottle, distributing it with equal open-handedness once the first was empty.

As I observed them gathered around the forge, clearly relishing the moment, I reflected on what an excellent seasoning for a meal my runaway acquaintance on the marshes had become. They had not enjoyed themselves nearly as much before the event was enlivened by the thrill he provided. Now, as they waited eagerly for the capture of “the two villains,” and as the bellows seemed to howl for the fugitives, the flames to blaze for them, the smoke to race in pursuit, Joe to strike and ring for them, and all the dark shadows on the wall to tremble in threat as the fire flickered and the glowing embers fell and faded, the dim afternoon outside appeared to my sympathetic young imagination to have turned pale on their behalf, poor souls.

Eventually, Joe finished his task, and the clanging and roaring ceased. While putting on his coat, Joe gathered the nerve to suggest that some of us accompany the soldiers to see the outcome of the search. Mr. Pumblechook and Mr. Hubble refused, citing a desire for a pipe and the company of the ladies; however, Mr. Wopsle agreed to go if Joe would. Joe expressed his willingness and offered to bring me, provided Mrs. Joe consented. I am certain we never would have received permission to leave, had it not been for Mrs. Joe’s desire to learn every detail of how it concluded. As it happened, she only insisted, “If you return with the boy’s head shattered by a musket, do not expect me to reassemble it.”

The sergeant bid the ladies a courteous farewell and parted from Mr. Pumblechook as if they were brothers-in-arms, though I suspect he was not as keenly aware of that man’s virtues when things were dry as he was when something wet was available. His soldiers shouldered their muskets and formed ranks. Mr. Wopsle, Joe, and I were strictly ordered to remain behind them and to stay silent once we arrived at the marshes. When we were out in the biting air and marching steadily toward our objective, I whispered treasonously to Joe, “I hope, Joe, that we do not find them.” Joe whispered back, “I would pay a shilling if they had escaped, Pip.”

No stragglers from the village joined us, as the weather was frigid and ominous, the path bleak, the ground difficult, night was approaching, and the residents were enjoying their fires indoors while observing the holiday. A few faces peered from bright windows as we passed, but no one emerged. We went by the signpost and continued directly toward the churchyard. There, we were halted for a few moments by a gesture from the sergeant, while several of his men spread out among the tombstones and inspected the porch.

The sergeant downed his drink again and looked eager for another. I noticed that Mr. Pumblechook, in his hospitality, seemed to forget he had gifted the wine; instead, he took the bottle from Mrs. Joe and claimed all the credit for serving it with a burst of cheerfulness. Even I received some. He was so generous with the wine that he even called for the second bottle, serving it with the same open-handedness once the first was empty.

As I watched them gathered around the forge, enjoying themselves so thoroughly, I thought about what an effective spice for a dinner my fugitive friend on the marshes had become. They hadn't enjoyed themselves a quarter as much before the event was enlivened by the excitement he provided. Now, as they all anticipated the capture of “the two villains,” the bellows seemed to roar for the fugitives, the fire to flare for them, the smoke to rush away in pursuit, Joe to hammer and clink for them, and all the dark shadows on the wall to shake in menace as the flames rose and fell, and the red-hot sparks dropped and died. In my sympathetic young mind, the pale afternoon outside almost seemed to have turned pale on their account, poor wretches.

His men picked up their muskets and lined up. Mr. Wopsle, Joe, and I were strictly ordered to stay in the back and to remain silent once we reached the marshes. No one from the village joined us, as the weather was cold and threatening, the path was dreary, the ground was difficult, darkness was falling, and the people were staying inside by their fires to celebrate the holiday. A few faces hurried to glowing windows to watch us pass, but no one came out. We passed the signpost and headed straight for the churchyard. There, we were stopped for a few minutes by a signal from the sergeant’s hand while two or three of his men spread out among the graves and searched the porch.

I bent toward Joe and, using my index finger to guide him, read the entire letter aloud. 

“Incredible!” Joe remarked once I concluded. “You truly are a learned fellow.” 

“How do you spell Gargery, Joe?” I inquired, adopting a tone of humble condescension. 

“I don’t spell it at all,” Joe replied. 

“But what if you were to try?” 

“It is not possible to suppose such a thing,” Joe insisted. “Even though I am quite fond of reading myself.” 

“Are you, Joe?” 

“Extremely. Give me,” Joe said, “a fine book or a quality newspaper, place me before a warm fire, and I desire nothing more. Goodness!” he added, after massaging his knees briefly, “when you reach a J and an O, and you declare, ‘Here, at last, is a J-O, Joe,’ how fascinating reading becomes!” 

I gathered from this that Joe’s schooling, much like steam power, was still in its early stages. Continuing the conversation, I asked, “Did you never attend school, Joe, back when you were my size?” 

“No, Pip.” 

“Why did you never go to school, Joe, when you were as little as I am?” 

“Well, Pip,” Joe said, grabbing the poker and preparing for his usual habit when deep in thought, which was to slowly stir the embers between the bottom grate bars; “I will explain. My father, Pip, had a weakness for drink, and when he was overcome by it, he would beat my mother quite mercilessly. It was nearly the only hammering he performed, aside from striking me. And he struck me with a vigor only matched by the intensity with which he avoided striking his anvil. You are listening and following along, Pip?” 

“Yes, Joe.” 

“As a result, my mother and I fled from my father on several occasions; then my mother would find work and say, ‘Joe,’ she would say, ‘now, God willing, you shall receive some education, my child,’ and she would enroll me in school. But my father was so kind-hearted that he could not bear to be apart from us. Consequently, he would arrive with a massive crowd and create such a disturbance at the doors of our lodgings that the people were forced to stop sheltering us and hand us back to him. Then he would bring us home and beat us. Which, you see, Pip,” Joe said, pausing his thoughtful raking of the fire to look at me, “was a hindrance to my education.” 

“Indeed, poor Joe!” 

“Though keep in mind, Pip,” Joe added, tapping the top bar with the poker in a measured way, “to give everyone their due and maintain fairness between man and man, my father was truly good-hearted, do you see?” 

I did not see, but I kept that to myself. 

“Anyway!” Joe continued, “someone has to keep the pot boiling, Pip, or the pot will not boil, don’t you understand?” 

I understood that, and told him so. 

“As a result, my father did not object to my working; so I entered my current trade, which would have been his as well, had he chosen to pursue it, and I labored quite hard, I promise you, Pip. Eventually, I was able to support him, and I cared for him until he passed away from a seizure. It was my intention to have inscribed on his headstone that, whatever his shortcomings, Remember reader he was truly good-hearted.”
