“You remain silent regarding her,” Miss Havisham observed while watching me. “She speaks quite harshly of you, yet you say nothing of her. What is your opinion of her?”

“I would rather not say,” I faltered.

“Whisper it in my ear,” Miss Havisham commanded, leaning toward me.

“I believe she is quite arrogant,” I murmured.

“Is there more?”

“I think she is very beautiful.”

“Anything else?”

“I find her very rude.” (At that moment, she regarded me with a look of intense dislike.)

“Anything else?”

“I think I would like to return home.”

“And never see her again, despite her beauty?”

“I am not certain I would not want to see her again, but I would prefer to go home now.”

“You will leave shortly,” Miss Havisham stated aloud. “Finish the game.”

Except for that one strange smile at the beginning, I felt certain Miss Havisham’s face was incapable of smiling. It had settled into a brooding, vigilant mask—likely from the time her surroundings became frozen in place—and it seemed as though nothing could ever alter it. Her posture was stooped, and her voice was low, possessing a lifeless quality; she appeared to have collapsed, both physically and spiritually, under the burden of some devastating misfortune.

I finished the game with Estella, and she left me with nothing. Once she had won every card, she tossed them onto the table as if she held them in contempt for having been taken from me.

“When shall you return?” Miss Havisham asked. “Let me consider.”

I started to mention that it was Wednesday, but she stopped me with that familiar, impatient flick of her right hand.

“Enough! I do not keep track of days or weeks. Return in six days. Do you understand?”

“Yes, ma’am.”

“Estella, lead him downstairs. Provide him with food, and let him wander the grounds while he eats. Go, Pip.”

I followed the candle down just as I had followed it up, and she placed it back where we had first seen it. Until she unlatched the side door, I had assumed, without really considering it, that it must be night. The sudden brightness of the day startled me, making it feel as though I had spent many hours in the dim light of that peculiar room.

“Wait here, boy,” Estella commanded before vanishing and shutting the door.

Left alone in the courtyard, I seized the chance to examine my rough hands and ordinary boots. I did not think highly of them. They had never bothered me previously, but now they seemed like vulgar traits. I resolved to ask Joe why he had taught me to refer to those playing cards as Jacks, when they should be called knaves. I wished Joe had been raised with more refinement, as I would have been as well.

She returned carrying bread, meat, and a small mug of beer. She set the mug upon the courtyard stones and handed me the food without glancing at me, with the same disdain one might show a disgraced dog.

“You say nothing about her,” Miss Havisham remarked to me, while watching. “She says many harsh things about you, but you say nothing about her. What is your opinion of her?”

“I would rather not say,” I stammered.

“Whisper it in my ear,” Miss Havisham said, leaning down.

“I think she is very proud,” I replied, in a whisper.

“Anything else?”

“I think she is very pretty.”

“Anything else?”

“I think she is very insulting.” (She was looking at me then with a look of intense dislike.)

“Anything else?”

“I think I would like to go home.”

“I am not sure that I wouldn’t like to see her again, but I would like to go home now.”

“You will go soon,” Miss Havisham said, aloud. “Finish the game.”

Her chest had slumped, causing her to stoop, and her voice had dropped, so that she spoke quietly and with a lifeless tone; altogether, she had the appearance of having collapsed, body and soul, inside and out, under the weight of a devastating blow.

I played the game to the end with Estella, and she cleaned me out. She threw the cards onto the table once she had won them all, as if she despised them for having been won from me.

I was starting to remind her that today was Wednesday, when she stopped me with her previous impatient flick of her right hand’s fingers.

“Yes, ma’am.”

“Well, miss?” I replied, nearly stumbling into her before catching my balance. She remained there observing me, and naturally, I remained there observing her. “Am I pretty?” “Yes; I believe you are quite pretty.” “Am I insulting?” “Not quite as much as you were previously,” I remarked. “Not quite as much?” “No.” She flared up at that final inquiry, and she struck my face with all the strength she possessed when I gave my response. “Now?” she demanded. “You small, vulgar beast, what is your opinion of me now?” “I refuse to say.” “Because you intend to report it upstairs. Is that the reason?” “No,” I replied, “that is not the reason.” “Why do you not weep again, you small wretch?” “Because I shall never weep for you again,” I stated. This was, I imagine, as untruthful a statement as was ever uttered; for I was weeping internally for her at that very moment, and I am aware of the suffering she caused me in later years. We proceeded toward the stairs following this encounter; and, as we ascended, we encountered a man feeling his way downward. “Who have we here?” the man inquired, pausing to examine me. “A boy,” Estella replied. He was a stout individual with an exceptionally swarthy complexion, possessing an unusually massive head and a hand of matching proportions. He gripped my chin with his heavy hand and tilted my face upward to inspect me by the candlelight. He was balding early at the crown of his head and featured thick, dark eyebrows that refused to lie flat, instead standing up in bristles. His eyes were deeply sunken, appearing unpleasantly keen and distrustful. He wore a heavy watch-chain and displayed dark, coarse pores where his beard and sideburns would have appeared had he allowed them to grow. He meant nothing to me, and I could have had no premonition then that he would ever hold significance for me, yet it occurred that I had this chance to study him closely. “A local boy? Eh?” he asked. “Yes, sir,” I replied. “How did you arrive here?” “Miss Havisham summoned me, sir,” I clarified. “Very well! Conduct yourself properly. I possess a rather extensive knowledge of boys, and you are a troublesome group. Now take heed!” he said, gnawing the side of his thick forefinger while scowling at me, “see that you behave!” With those remarks, he let me go—which I welcomed, as his hand carried the scent of perfumed soap—and continued his descent. I pondered if he might be a physician; but no, I reasoned; he could not be a doctor, or he would possess a more gentle and convincing demeanor. There was little time to dwell on the matter, for we soon reached Miss Havisham’s chamber, where she and all the surroundings remained exactly as I had left them. Estella abandoned me near the entrance, and I remained there until Miss Havisham turned her gaze toward me from the vanity. “So!” she remarked, showing no sign of alarm or astonishment: “the days have passed, have they?” “Yes, ma’am. Today is—” “There, there, there!” she interrupted with an impatient flick of her fingers. “I have no desire to know. Are you prepared to play?” I was forced to reply in some disarray, “I do not believe I am, ma’am.”

“Well, miss?” I replied, nearly tripping over her and catching myself.

She stood watching me, and naturally, I stood watching her.

“Am I pretty?”

“Yes; I think you are very pretty.”

“Am I insulting?”

“Not as much as you were last time,” I said.

“Not as much?”

“No.”

She flared up at that last question and slapped my face with all the strength she had when I answered it.

“Well?” she said. “You little coarse monster, what do you think of me now?”

“I won’t tell you.”

“Because you’re going to tell on me upstairs. Is that it?”

“No,” I said, “that’s not it.”

“Why don’t you cry again, you little wretch?”

“Because I’ll never cry for you again,” I said. That was, I suppose, as dishonest a statement as was ever made; for I was crying for her on the inside even then, and I know full well the pain she caused me later on.

He and my sister would engage in such absurd conjectures regarding Miss Havisham and her potential intentions for my future that I frequently felt a sharp, painful urge to break into angry sobs, attack Pumblechook, and strike him repeatedly. During these conversations, my sister addressed me as though she were painfully extracting a tooth with every remark, while Pumblechook, acting as my self-appointed benefactor, watched me with a critical gaze, much like a builder who viewed my prospects as a poor investment. Joe remained silent during these talks, yet he was often the target of my sister’s remarks because she sensed his disapproval of my removal from the forge. I was certainly old enough to begin my apprenticeship with Joe, and whenever he sat thoughtfully stirring the ashes with the poker, my sister would interpret this harmless act as defiance, snatching the tool from his grasp, shaking him, and hiding it away. Every one of these arguments concluded in a maddening fashion. Suddenly, without any warning, my sister would interrupt her own yawning, notice me as if by accident, and snap, “Enough of you! Off to bed; you have caused quite enough annoyance for one evening!” as if I had begged them to torment me.

We continued in this manner for quite a while, and it appeared we would do so indefinitely, until one day Miss Havisham paused while we walked, her weight resting on my shoulder, and remarked with a hint of annoyance, “You are getting tall, Pip!” I felt it wise to suggest, through a contemplative expression, that this was perhaps due to factors beyond my influence. She said nothing more then, but she stopped to observe me again, and then again, eventually appearing sullen and irritable. The following day, once our customary walk concluded and I had helped her to her vanity, she halted me with a sharp gesture of her fingers: “Remind me of that blacksmith’s name.” “Joe Gargery, ma’am.” “The master you were intended to serve as an apprentice?” “Yes, Miss Havisham.” “You should be apprenticed immediately. Do you think Gargery would come here with you and bring the legal papers?” I indicated that I was certain he would consider the invitation a privilege. “Then have him come.” “At any specific hour, Miss Havisham?” “Enough! I know nothing of schedules. Have him come soon, and bring him with you.” When I returned home that evening and relayed the message to Joe, my sister flew into a rage more terrifying than any I had witnessed before. She demanded to know if Joe and I considered her a mere doormat, how we dared treat her in such a manner, and what sort of company we deemed her worthy of keeping.

Then, he and my sister would pair off in such ridiculous speculations about Miss Havisham, and about what she would do with me and for me, that I used to want—quite painfully—to burst into spiteful tears, attack Pumblechook, and pummel him all over. In these dialogues, my sister spoke to me as if she were morally wrenching one of my teeth out at every reference; while Pumblechook himself, having appointed himself my patron, would sit supervising me with a critical eye, like the architect of my fortunes who thought himself engaged on a very unprofitable job.

In these discussions, Joe took no part. But he was often talked at while they were in progress, because Mrs. Joe noticed that he was not in favor of my being taken from the forge. I was fully old enough now to be apprenticed to Joe; and when Joe sat with the poker on his knees thoughtfully raking out the ashes between the lower bars, my sister would so clearly interpret that innocent action as opposition on his part that she would dive at him, take the poker out of his hands, shake him, and put it away. There was a most irritating end to every one of these debates. All in a moment, with nothing to lead up to it, my sister would stop herself in a yawn, and catching sight of me as if by accident, would swoop upon me with, “Come! That’s enough of you! You get along to bed; you’ve given enough trouble for one night, I hope!” As if I had begged them as a favor to bother my life out.

We went on in this way for a long time, and it seemed likely that we would continue to go on in this way for a long time, when one day Miss Havisham stopped short as she and I were walking, she leaning on my shoulder, and said with some displeasure, “You are growing tall, Pip!”

I thought it best to hint, through the medium of a thoughtful look, that this might be caused by circumstances over which I had no control.

She said no more at the time; but she presently stopped and looked at me again; and presently again; and after that, looked frowning and moody. On the next day of my attendance, when our usual exercise was over, and I had landed her at her dressing-table, she stopped me with a movement of her impatient fingers: “Tell me the name again of that blacksmith of yours.”

“Joe Gargery, ma’am.”

“Meaning the master you were to be apprenticed to?”

“Yes, Miss Havisham.”

“You had better be apprenticed at once. Would Gargery come here with you, and bring your indentures, do you think?”

I indicated that I had no doubt he would take it as an honor to be asked.

“Then let him come.”

“At any particular time, Miss Havisham?”

“There, there! I know nothing about times. Let him come soon, and come along with you.”

When I got home at night, and delivered this message for Joe, my sister “went on the rampage” to a more alarming degree than at any previous time. She asked me and Joe whether we supposed she was a doormat under our feet, and how we dared to treat her so, and what company we graciously thought she was fit for?

Then, my sister and Pumblechook would pair off, making such ridiculous guesses about Miss Havisham and what she might do for me, that I often felt a painful urge to burst into angry tears, attack Pumblechook, and beat him. During these talks, my sister spoke to me as if she were painfully pulling out one of my teeth with every word. Meanwhile, Pumblechook, who acted as my self-appointed patron, would watch me with a critical eye, like an architect who felt he was working on a very unprofitable project.

Joe took no part in these discussions. However, they often directed their comments at him because Mrs. Joe noticed he did not support the idea of me leaving the forge. I was now old enough to be apprenticed to Joe. When Joe sat with the poker on his knees, thoughtfully raking the ashes between the lower bars, my sister would interpret that innocent action as opposition. She would lunge at him, take the poker from his hands, shake him, and put it away. Every one of these debates had an irritating conclusion. Suddenly, without any warning, my sister would stop yawning, notice me as if by accident, and swoop down, saying, “Come! That’s enough of you! Get along to bed; you’ve caused enough trouble for one night, I hope!” She acted as if I had begged them to make my life miserable.

We continued this way for a long time, and it seemed we would go on like this indefinitely, until one day Miss Havisham stopped while we were walking, with her leaning on my shoulder, and said with some annoyance, “You are growing tall, Pip!” I thought it best to suggest, through a thoughtful look, that this might be due to circumstances beyond my control. She said nothing more then, but she soon stopped and looked at me again, then again, and after that, she looked frowning and moody. Do you think Gargery would come here with you and bring your legal papers?” I indicated that I had no doubt he would consider it an honor to be asked. “Then let him come.” “At any particular time, Miss Havisham?” “There, there! I know nothing about times. Let him come soon, and come along with you.” When I got home that night and delivered this message for Joe, my sister went on a rampage more alarming than ever before. She asked Joe and me if we thought she was a doormat under our feet, how we dared to treat her that way, and what kind of company we thought she was actually fit for.

He resided at a sluice-keeper’s dwelling out upon the marshes, and on weekdays he would shuffle from his solitary home, hands tucked into his pockets, with his midday meal knotted into a parcel about his neck, swinging against his back. On Sundays, he typically spent the entire day reclining upon the sluice-gates or leaning against haystacks and barns. He moved with a perpetual, slouching gait, his gaze fixed downward; whenever he was spoken to or otherwise compelled to lift his eyes, he would look up with a mixture of resentment and confusion, as if his only recurring thought was that it was a strange and unfair circumstance that he should never be thinking at all.

This sullen laborer held no affection for me. During my early, timid years, he led me to believe that the Devil resided in a dark recess of the forge and that he was well-acquainted with the fiend; he further claimed that the fire required a living boy as fuel every seven years, and that I ought to view myself as that very material. Once I became Joe’s apprentice, Orlick likely grew more convinced that I would eventually replace him; regardless, his dislike for me only deepened. He never explicitly stated or performed any act of overt hostility; I simply observed that he consistently directed his sparks toward me, and whenever I sang Old Clem, he would intentionally sing out of rhythm.

Dolge Orlick was present and working the following day when I mentioned my half-holiday to Joe. He remained silent at first, as he and Joe were occupied with a piece of heated iron while I operated the bellows; however, after a short time, he leaned upon his hammer and remarked, “Now, master! Surely you aren’t going to show favoritism to just one of us. If young Pip is granted a half-holiday, grant the same to old Orlick.” I estimate he was roughly twenty-five, yet he frequently referred to himself as an elderly man.

“And what would you do with a half-holiday, should you receive one?” Joe inquired.

“What would I do with it! What will he do with it? I shall make as much use of it as he does,” Orlick replied.

“As for Pip, he is heading into town,” Joe explained.

“Well then, as for old Orlick, he is heading into town as well,” the man retorted. “Two people can go to town. It isn’t only one person who is permitted to go.”

“Do not lose your temper,” Joe cautioned.

“I shall if I choose,” Orlick grumbled. “Some people and their trips to town! Now, master! Come. No partiality in this workshop. Act like a man!”

Because the master refused to discuss the matter until the journeyman’s mood improved, Orlick lunged toward the furnace, extracted a glowing bar, and lunged at me as if to pierce my body with it. He swung it around my head, placed it upon the anvil, and hammered it—I imagined it was me, and the sparks were my spraying blood—and finally, once he had hammered himself into a sweat and the iron had cooled, he leaned on his hammer once more and said, “Now, master!”

“Are you feeling better now?” Joe asked.

“Yes! I am fine,” the surly Orlick answered.

“Then, since you generally apply yourself to your labor as well as any man,” Joe stated, “let it be a half-holiday for everyone.”

My sister, who had been standing quietly in the yard within earshot—she was a relentless spy and eavesdropper—immediately peered in through one of the windows.

My sister had been standing silently in the yard, within earshot—she was a constant, dishonest spy—and she immediately looked in through one of the windows.

“Biddy,” I stated, after swearing her to silence, “my ambition is to become a gentleman.”

“Oh, I wouldn’t, if I were in your place,” she replied. “I don’t believe it would turn out well.”

“Biddy,” I said, with a touch of sternness, “I have specific motives for desiring to be a gentleman.”

“You are the best judge, Pip; but don’t you feel that you are more content as you are?”

“Biddy,” I cried, with impatience, “I am not content at all as I am. I loathe my trade and my existence. I have never grown accustomed to either since my apprenticeship began. Don’t be ridiculous.”

“Was I being ridiculous?” Biddy asked, calmly lifting her brows; “I regret that; it was not my intention. I only wish for your success and your happiness.”

“Well, then, realize once and for all that I never will or can be happy—or anything but wretched—there, Biddy!—unless I can live a life entirely unlike the one I lead at present.”

“That is a shame!” Biddy remarked, moving her head with a mournful expression.

Now, I had also frequently considered it a shame, so that, in the strange sort of internal conflict I was constantly waging, I felt nearly ready to weep from frustration and misery when Biddy voiced her opinion, which mirrored my own. I informed her she was correct, and I acknowledged it was deeply regrettable, yet it remained unavoidable.

“If I could have been content,” I told Biddy, tearing at the short grass nearby, much as I had once plucked my emotions from my hair and kicked them toward the brewery wall,—“if I could have been content and felt even half the affection for the forge that I felt when I was a child, I know it would have been far better for me. You and I and Joe would have lacked for nothing then, and Joe and I might have become partners once my time was served, and I might even have matured to court you, and we might have rested on this very bank on a pleasant Sunday, as entirely different people. I would have been suitable for you; wouldn’t I, Biddy?”

Biddy sighed as she watched the vessels passing by, and replied, “Yes; I am not overly fastidious.” It hardly felt like a compliment, yet I understood her intentions were kind.

“Instead of that,” I said, pulling up more grass and gnawing on a blade or two, “look at how I am living. Discontented, and uneasy, and—what would it matter to me, being rough and ordinary, if no one had ever told me so!”

Biddy shifted her gaze suddenly toward me, and studied me with far more focus than she had given the passing ships.

“That was neither a very honest nor a very courteous remark,” she observed, turning her eyes back to the ships. “Who told you that?”

I was rattled, for I had spoken impulsively without fully considering my path. It could not be avoided now, however, and I replied, “The lovely young lady at Miss Havisham’s, and she is more beautiful than anyone has ever been, and I admire her intensely, and I wish to be a gentleman for her sake.”

“Biddy,” I said, after making her promise to keep it a secret, “I want to be a gentleman.”

“Oh, I wouldn’t, if I were you,” she replied. “I don’t think it would work out.”

“Biddy,” I said, with some sternness, “I have specific reasons for wanting to be a gentleman.”

“You know best, Pip; but don’t you think you are happier just as you are?”

“Biddy,” I exclaimed, impatiently, “I am not at all happy as I am. I am disgusted with my trade and with my life. I have never enjoyed either, ever since I started my apprenticeship. Don’t be ridiculous.”

“Was I being ridiculous?” Biddy asked, quietly raising her eyebrows. “I am sorry for that; I didn’t mean to be. I only want you to do well and to be content.”

“Well, then, understand once and for all that I never will or can be content—or anything but miserable—there, Biddy!—unless I can lead a very different sort of life from the one I lead now.”

“That’s a shame,” Biddy said, shaking her head with a sorrowful expression.

Now, I too had so often thought it a shame that, in the strange kind of argument with myself that I was always having, I was half inclined to cry from frustration and distress when Biddy voiced her sentiment, which was also my own. I told her she was right, and I knew it was much to be regretted, but still, it could not be helped.

“If I could have settled down,” I said to Biddy, pulling up the short grass within reach, much as I had once upon a time pulled my feelings out of my hair and kicked them into the brewery wall—“if I could have settled down and been even half as fond of the forge as I was when I was little, I know it would have been much better for me. You and I and Joe would have wanted for nothing then, and Joe and I would perhaps have become partners when I finished my apprenticeship, and I might even have grown up to date you, and we might have sat on this very bank on a fine Sunday, as completely different people. I would have been good enough for you, wouldn’t I, Biddy?”

“Excellent. It would be best to test him at his own residence. The path will be cleared for you, and you may meet his son first, who resides in London. When do you intend to arrive in London?”

I replied (casting a look at Joe, who remained standing there, perfectly still), that I imagined I could depart immediately.

“To begin with,” Mr. Jaggers stated, “you ought to acquire fresh attire for your arrival, and it should not be laboring garments. Let us say this day week. You will require funds. Shall I provide you with twenty guineas?”

He drew out a lengthy purse with absolute composure, tallied the coins upon the table, and slid them toward me. This was the initial moment he had removed his leg from the chair. He sat straddling the seat once he had moved the money, swinging his purse while observing Joe.

“Well, Joseph Gargery? You appear stunned?”

“I am!” Joe declared, in a very firm tone.

“It was agreed that you desired nothing for your own benefit, keep in mind?”

“It were agreed,” Joe replied. “And it are agreed. And it ever will be the same.”

“But what,” Mr. Jaggers inquired, swinging his purse,—“what if my instructions included offering you a gift, by way of compensation?”

“Compensation for what?” Joe asked.

“For the forfeiture of his labor.”

Joe placed his hand upon my shoulder with a woman’s gentleness. I have frequently thought of him since as resembling a steam-hammer capable of crushing a man or tapping an eggshell, in his blend of power and tenderness. “Pip is most welcome,” Joe said, “to depart freely with his services, toward honor and fortune, in ways no words can express. But if you imagine that money can compensate me for the loss of the little child—who arrived at the forge—and was always the best of friends!—”

Oh dear, kind Joe, whom I was so prepared to abandon and so ungrateful toward, I behold you once more, with your powerful blacksmith’s arm covering your eyes, your broad chest rising and falling, and your voice fading away. Oh dear, kind, loyal, tender Joe, I sense the affectionate tremor of your hand upon my arm, as solemnly today as if it were the rustle of an angel’s wing!

Yet I urged Joe to be heartened at the time. I was consumed by the complexities of my future prospects and could not revisit the paths we had walked together. I implored Joe to find comfort, for (as he noted) we had always been the best of friends, and (as I noted) we always would be. Joe wiped his eyes with his free wrist, as if he intended to gouge himself, but uttered nothing further.

Mr. Jaggers had observed this, like a man who identified Joe as the village simpleton and me as his guardian. When it concluded, he remarked, balancing the purse he had stopped swinging in his hand:—

Mr. Pumblechook served me the liver wing and the finest portion of tongue (avoiding those obscure pork remnants now), while neglecting his own plate almost entirely. “Oh! poultry, poultry! You little imagined,” Mr. Pumblechook declared, addressing the bird on the platter, “when you were a fledgling, what awaited you. You little imagined you would serve as sustenance under this modest roof for one who—call it a frailty, if you choose,” Mr. Pumblechook said, rising again, “but may I? may I—?”

It became redundant to formally grant him permission, so he proceeded immediately. How he managed this repeatedly without stabbing himself with my knife, I cannot fathom.

“And your sister,” he continued, after eating steadily for a moment, “who had the distinction of raising you by hand! It is a melancholy image to consider that she is no longer capable of fully grasping that distinction. May—”

Seeing he was about to approach me again, I interrupted him.

“We shall drink to her health,” I stated.

“Ah!” Mr. Pumblechook exclaimed, reclining in his chair, utterly limp with admiration, “that is how you recognize them, sir!” (I have no idea who 'Sir' was, but it certainly was not me, and no one else was present); “that is how you recognize the noble-hearted, sir! Always forgiving and always gracious. It might,” the obsequious Pumblechook said, setting down his full glass hastily and rising again, “to an ordinary person, seem repetitive—but may I—?”

Once finished, he returned to his seat and toasted my sister. “Let us never ignore her temperamental flaws,” Mr. Pumblechook noted, “but we must hope her intentions were good.”

Around this time, I noticed his face growing ruddy; as for me, I felt entirely like a face, saturated with wine and stinging.

I informed Mr. Pumblechook that I wanted my new attire delivered to his residence, and he was thrilled that I had singled him out. I explained my desire to evade notice in the village, and he praised the idea effusively. He suggested that no one but himself was deserving of my trust, and—in short, might he? Then he asked me affectionately if I recalled our childhood arithmetic lessons, and how we had traveled together to have me apprenticed, and, essentially, how he had always been my preferred companion and chosen friend? Had I consumed ten times the amount of wine, I would have known he had never occupied that position in my life, and I would have inwardly rejected the notion. Nevertheless, I recall feeling certain that I had misjudged him, and that he was a sensible, pragmatic, kind-hearted, excellent man.

“And yet I cannot,” said Mr. Pumblechook, standing up once more, “see before me one—and likewise drink to one—without again expressing—May I—may I—?”

I told him he could, and he shook my hand again, drained his glass, and turned it upside down. I did the same; and if I had turned myself upside down before drinking, the wine could not have gone more directly to my head.

When he had finished, he sat back down and drank to my sister. “Let us never be blind,” said Mr. Pumblechook, “to her temperamental faults, but one hopes she meant well.”

Around this time, I began to notice his face was getting flushed; as for myself, I felt entirely lightheaded, soaked in wine and stinging.

I mentioned to Mr. Pumblechook that I wanted my new clothes sent to his house, and he was ecstatic that I had chosen him. I discovered then that Wemmick served as the clerk in the adjacent office. A fresh crowd of individuals waited outside, but Wemmick cleared a path by stating calmly yet firmly, “I tell you it is pointless; he will not speak to any of you;” we quickly moved past them and proceeded together. Chapter XXI. Observing Mr. Wemmick as we walked to gauge his appearance in the daylight, I saw a dry man of modest height, possessing a square, wooden face that looked as though it had been roughly carved by a blunt tool. There were indentations that might have been dimples had the surface been more yielding and the carving implement sharper, but they remained mere dints. The chisel had attempted a few such decorations near his nose before abandoning the task. I surmised he was unmarried due to his worn shirt cuffs, and he seemed to have endured many losses, as he sported at least four mourning rings and a brooch depicting a woman weeping by a tomb beneath a willow tree. I also observed various seals and rings dangling from his watch-chain, suggesting he was heavily burdened with tokens of deceased acquaintances. His eyes were bright, small, sharp, and dark, and his lips were thin, wide, and spotted. He had possessed them, I estimated, for forty to fifty years. “So you have never visited London previously?” Mr. Wemmick inquired. “No,” I replied. “I was a newcomer here once,” Mr. Wemmick remarked. “Strange to consider now!” “You are quite familiar with it at present?” “Well, yes,” Mr. Wemmick answered. “I understand how it functions.” “Is it a very sinful city?” I asked, mostly to keep the conversation going. “You might be swindled, plundered, or killed in London. But there are plenty of people everywhere willing to do that for you.” “If there is animosity between you and them,” I suggested, attempting to temper the statement. “Oh! I am not sure about animosity,” Mr. Wemmick replied; “there is not much of that involved. They will do it if there is profit to be made.” “That makes it more severe.” “You believe so?” Mr. Wemmick countered. “Much the same, I would argue.” He kept his hat pushed back and stared ahead, walking with a detached air as if the streets held no interest for him. His mouth was shaped like a post-office slot, giving him the mechanical look of a smile. We reached the summit of Holborn Hill before I realized it was merely a physical quirk and he was not actually smiling. “Do you know where Mr. Matthew Pocket resides?” I asked Mr. Wemmick. “Yes,” he said, gesturing toward the area. “In Hammersmith, to the west of London.” “Is that a great distance?”

I then discovered that Wemmick was the clerk in the adjacent office. We encountered a new group of people waiting outside, but Wemmick cleared a path through them by stating calmly yet firmly, “I’m telling you, it’s pointless; he won’t speak to any of you.” We soon moved past them and walked along side by side.

Chapter XXI.

As we walked, I looked at Mr. Wemmick to see what he looked like in the daylight. I found him to be a dry man, somewhat short, with a square, wooden face that looked as if it had been crudely carved with a dull chisel. The chisel had made three or four attempts at decoration around his nose but had given up without trying to smooth them out. “No,” I replied.

“Well, yes,” Mr. Wemmick said. “I know how it works.”

“That makes it worse.”

“You think so?” Mr. Wemmick returned. “I’d say it’s about the same.”

His mouth was shaped like a post-office slot, giving him the mechanical appearance of smiling. We had reached the top of Holborn Hill before I realized it was merely a mechanical expression and that he wasn’t smiling at all.

“Yes,” he said, nodding in that direction. “In Hammersmith, west of London.”

“Is that far?”

“You must require a great number of vessels,” I remarked.

Feeling quite overwhelmed by the scale of these dealings, I inquired where the ships he currently insured were primarily sailing.

“I haven’t started insuring quite yet,” he responded. “I am currently surveying the landscape.”

In some way, that activity felt more appropriate for Barnard’s Inn. I replied (with a tone of certainty), “Ah!”

“Indeed. I am employed in a counting-house, and I am surveying the landscape.”

“Does a counting-house provide a good income?” I questioned.

“To—are you asking if it is profitable for the young man working there?” he countered.

“Yes; for you.”

“Well, n-no; not for me.” He stated this with the demeanor of someone meticulously calculating and balancing figures. “Not directly lucrative. That is to say, it provides me no salary, and I must—support myself.”

This certainly did not appear to be a profitable arrangement, and I gestured with my head to suggest that it would be challenging to accumulate significant savings from such an income.

“But the point is,” Herbert Pocket explained, “that you survey the landscape. That is the essential thing. You are situated in a counting-house, you see, and you survey the landscape.”

It seemed a strange notion to me that one could not be elsewhere and still survey the landscape, but I quietly yielded to his expertise.

“Then the moment arrives,” Herbert continued, “when you identify your opportunity. You seize it, you pounce upon it, you generate your capital, and then you have arrived! Once you have established your capital, you simply need to put it to work.”

This was very similar to his approach during our fight in the garden; very similar indeed. His way of enduring his poverty also matched his way of enduring that loss. It appeared to me that he accepted all of life’s setbacks with the same attitude he had shown toward my blows back then. It was clear he possessed only the most basic necessities, as everything I commented upon turned out to have been provided on my behalf from the coffee-house or another source.

Yet, having already secured his fortune in his imagination, he remained so modest about it that I felt genuinely appreciative of his lack of arrogance. It was a charming quality added to his naturally agreeable personality, and we got along wonderfully. That evening we strolled through the streets and attended the theater at a reduced rate; the following day we visited Westminster Abbey for services, and later walked through the Parks. I found myself wondering who provided the horseshoes for all the animals there, and wished that Joe were the one doing it.

By a reasonable estimate, it had been many months that Sunday since I had departed from Joe and Biddy. The distance separating me from them seemed to have expanded, and our marshes felt incredibly far away. That I could have been at our local church in my old Sunday attire just the previous week seemed a collection of impossibilities—geographical, social, solar, and lunar. Nevertheless, in the London streets, so packed with people and brightly illuminated in the evening twilight, there were somber reminders of guilt for having pushed the humble kitchen at home so far into the past; and in the middle of the night, the footsteps of some incompetent porter wandering around Barnard’s Inn, pretending to keep watch, echoed hollowly within my heart.

“And to Ceylon, especially for elephant tusks.”

In a rear office, a man with hunched shoulders and a jaw wrapped in soiled flannel, wearing aged black garments that looked waxed, was bent over his task of transcribing the notes of the other two for Mr. Jaggers’s review. I accepted the offer. After Mr. Wemmick had dropped all the biscuits into the post and paid me my money from a cash-box in a safe—the key to which he kept somewhere down his back, pulling it from his coat collar like an iron pigtail—we went upstairs. “We don’t have many clerks, because there is only one Jaggers, and people won’t accept anyone else. There are only four of us. Would you like to see them? You are one of us, in a way.”

I accepted the offer. After Mr. Wemmick had mailed the biscuit and paid me my money from a cash-box in a safe—the key to which he kept somewhere down his back, pulling it from his coat-collar like an iron pigtail—we went upstairs. The house was dark and shabby, and the greasy marks on the walls suggested that people had been shuffling up and down the staircase for years. In the front room on the first floor, a clerk who looked like a mix between a tavern keeper and a rat-catcher—a large, pale, and swollen man—was busy with three or four shabby-looking people. He treated them as rudely as everyone else who contributed to Mr. Jaggers’s wealth. “Gathering evidence,” Mr. Wemmick said as we left, “for the Old Bailey.” In the room above that, a small, flabby clerk with dangling hair (it seemed he had never been given a proper haircut) was working with a man who had weak eyes. Mr. Wemmick introduced him to me as a smelter who kept his pot boiling and could melt anything I liked; the man was sweating profusely, as if he had been practicing his trade on himself. In a back room, a man with hunched shoulders and a face wrapped in dirty flannel, dressed in old black clothes that looked as if they had been waxed, was busy making clean copies of the other two men’s notes for Mr. Jaggers’s use.

That was the entire staff. When we went back downstairs, Wemmick led me into my guardian’s room and said, “You have already seen this.”

“Tell me,” I said, as the two hateful casts with their twitching leers caught my eye again, “who are those?”

“These?” said Wemmick, standing on a chair and blowing the dust off the horrible heads before bringing them down. “These are two famous ones. They were our clients and brought us a great deal of credit. This fellow (why, you must have been peeping into the inkstand at night to get that blot on your eyebrow, you old rascal!) murdered his master, and considering he wasn’t trained in legal matters, he didn’t plan it badly.”

“Is it a good likeness?” I asked, pulling back from the brute as Wemmick spat on the cast’s eyebrow and rubbed it with his sleeve.

“Like him? It is him. The cast was made in Newgate prison right after he was taken down. You were quite fond of me, weren’t you, Old Artful?” Wemmick said. He then explained this affectionate comment by touching his brooch, which showed a lady and a weeping willow at a tomb with an urn on it, saying, “I had it made for me, specifically!”

“Is the lady someone in particular?” I asked.

“No,” Wemmick replied. “Just his little joke. (You liked your little joke, didn’t you?)

"Who is the Spider?"

"The spider?" I repeated.

"That blotchy, awkward, sullen individual."

"That is Bentley Drummle," I answered; "the one with the refined features is Startop."

Ignoring the mention of the one with the refined features, he replied, "Bentley Drummle is his name, is it? I find that fellow’s appearance interesting."

He promptly started a conversation with Drummle, entirely unbothered by his blunt, reserved responses, and seemingly encouraged by them to extract more talk from him. As I watched the pair, the housekeeper stepped between us, carrying the initial course for our meal.

She appeared to be roughly forty, though I might have underestimated her age. She was somewhat tall, possessed a supple, agile frame, was remarkably pale, and had large, washed-out eyes along with a mass of flowing hair. I cannot determine if a heart condition caused her lips to remain parted as if she were breathless, or gave her countenance such a strange, startled, and agitated look; however, I recall having attended a performance of Macbeth a few nights prior, and her face reminded me of those disturbed by scorching vapors, much like the visages I had witnessed emerging from the Witches’ caldron.

She placed the dish down, tapped my guardian lightly on the arm to signal that dinner was served, and disappeared. We sat at the circular table, with my guardian keeping Drummle on one side and Startop on the other. The housekeeper had provided a magnificent fish dish, followed by a joint of equally excellent mutton, and then a bird of similar quality. Sauces, wines, and every necessary accompaniment were provided by our host from his dumb-waiter; once they had been passed around the table, he consistently returned them to their place. In the same manner, he provided fresh plates, knives, and forks for every course, dropping the used ones into two baskets positioned on the floor beside his chair. No servant other than the housekeeper was present. She placed every dish; and I consistently perceived in her features a face emerging from the caldron. Many years later, I created a haunting portrait of that woman by having a face, which shared no natural similarity to hers other than the streaming hair, pass behind a bowl of burning spirits in a darkened room.

Prompted to pay close attention to the housekeeper, both by her remarkable appearance and by Wemmick’s earlier remarks, I noticed that whenever she was present, she kept her gaze fixed on my guardian, and she would withdraw her hands from any dish she placed before him with hesitation, as if she feared he might summon her back and desired him to speak while she was near, should he have anything to communicate. I imagined I could perceive in his behavior an awareness of this, and an intention to keep her in a state of constant uncertainty.

The dinner proceeded cheerfully, and although my guardian seemed to follow the topics rather than initiate them, I understood that he was skillfully drawing the most vulnerable aspects of our characters out of us.

"Who is the Spider?"

"The spider?" I asked.

"That blotchy, sprawling, sulky guy."

"That’s Bentley Drummle," I replied; "the one with the delicate features is Startop."

Ignoring the one with the delicate features entirely, he replied, "Bentley Drummle is his name, is it? I like the look of that fellow."

He immediately started talking to Drummle, not at all discouraged by his heavy, reserved responses, but seemingly encouraged by them to force conversation out of him. I was watching the two of them when the housekeeper stepped between us, carrying the first dish for the table.

She was a woman of about forty, I guessed—though I might have thought her younger than she actually was. She was rather tall, with a lithe, agile figure, extremely pale, with large, faded eyes and a mass of flowing hair. I cannot say if some heart condition caused her lips to remain parted as if she were panting, or gave her face such a strange expression of sudden agitation; but I know that I had seen Macbeth at the theater a night or two before, and her face looked to me as if it were disturbed by a fiery atmosphere, much like the faces I had seen rising from the witches’ cauldron.

She placed the dish down, tapped my guardian lightly on the arm to signal that dinner was ready, and vanished. Dinner went well, and although my guardian seemed to follow our topics rather than start them, I knew he was skillfully drawing out the weakest parts of our personalities.

One man was taller and heavier than his companion, and by the strange logic of the world—whether regarding convicts or free men—he had been assigned the smaller set of garments. His limbs resembled oversized pincushions, and his clothing made him look ridiculous; yet, I recognized his partially shut eye immediately. There stood the individual I had encountered on the bench at the Three Jolly Bargemen on a Saturday evening, the one who had leveled me with his unseen firearm.

It was evident that he did not recognize me at all. He glanced my way, assessed my watch-chain, spat casually, and remarked something to the other prisoner. They chuckled, turned with the metallic rattle of their connecting shackle, and shifted their attention elsewhere. The large numerals on their backs, resembling house numbers; their rough, mangy, and awkward appearance, like beasts; their shackled legs, wrapped apologetically in handkerchiefs; and the way the crowd observed them while maintaining a distance, rendered them—as Herbert had noted—a truly repulsive and debased sight.

However, that was not the extent of the trouble. It turned out that a family moving from London had reserved the entire rear of the coach, leaving no room for the two captives except on the front seat behind the driver. Consequently, a hot-tempered gentleman occupying the fourth spot on that bench erupted in fury, declaring it a violation of his agreement to be seated with such criminal types, calling it toxic, ruinous, disgraceful, and scandalous, among other things. By then, the coach was prepared, the driver was eager to depart, and we were all getting ready to board, while the prisoners approached with their guard—carrying that distinct scent of bread-poultice, baize, hemp, and hearthstone that follows those in custody.

“Do not be so upset, sir,” the guard urged the irate traveler; “I will sit beside you myself. I will place them on the edge of the bench. They will not bother you, sir. You need not even acknowledge their presence.”

“And do not blame me,” the convict I knew grumbled. “I have no desire to go. I am perfectly willing to remain here. As far as I am concerned, anyone is free to take my seat.”

“Or mine,” the other added harshly. “I would not have bothered any of you, had it been my choice.” They both laughed, started cracking nuts, and spat the shells about—an action I believe I would have enjoyed myself, had I been in their position and so scorned.

Eventually, it was decided that the angry man had no alternative but to accept his unwanted companions or stay behind. He took his seat, continuing to grumble, the guard sat beside him, the convicts pulled themselves up as best they could, and the man I had recognized sat directly behind me, his breath hitting the hair on my head.

One man was taller and heavier than the other, and, following the mysterious logic of the world—both for convicts and free men—he had been assigned the smaller suit of clothes. His limbs looked like oversized pincushions, and his outfit made him look ridiculous; yet, I recognized his half-closed eye instantly. There stood the man I had seen on the bench at the Three Jolly Bargemen on a Saturday night, the one who had brought me down with his invisible gun!

It was clear he did not recognize me at all. He glanced my way, checked out my watch-chain, spat casually, and said something to the other convict. They laughed, turned around with a clink of their shackles, and looked elsewhere. The large numbers on their backs, like house addresses; their rough, mangy, awkward appearance, as if they were beasts; their shackled legs, apologetically wrapped in pocket-handkerchiefs; and the way everyone stared at them while keeping their distance—all of this made them, as Herbert had said, a truly unpleasant and degraded sight.

But that wasn't the worst of it. It turned out that a family moving from London had booked the entire back of the coach, leaving no room for the two prisoners except on the front seat behind the driver. A hot-tempered gentleman, who had taken the fourth seat there, flew into a rage, claiming it was a breach of contract to force him to sit with such villainous company. He called it poisonous, harmful, disgraceful, and shameful, among other things. By then, the coach was ready, the driver was impatient, and we were all getting on board. The prisoners arrived with their guard, bringing that strange scent of bread-poultice, felt, rope, and hearthstone that always follows convicts.

“Don’t take it so hard, sir,” the guard pleaded with the angry passenger. “I’ll sit next to you myself. I’ll put them on the outside. They won’t bother you, sir. You won’t even know they’re there.”

“And don’t blame me,” grumbled the convict I had recognized. “I don’t want to go. I’m perfectly happy to stay behind. As far as I’m concerned, anyone is welcome to my seat.”

“Or mine,” the other added gruffly. “I wouldn’t have bothered any of you if I’d had my way.” Then they both laughed, started cracking nuts, and spat the shells around—something I think I would have done myself if I were in their position and treated with such contempt.

Eventually, it was decided that the angry gentleman had no choice but to accept his company or stay behind. He took his seat, still complaining, the guard sat next to him, and the convicts hauled themselves up as best they could. The convict I had recognized sat directly behind me, his breath hitting the back of my head.

One man was taller and heavier than the other, yet, in the strange way the world works for both convicts and free men, he had been given the smaller suit of clothes. His arms and legs looked like overstuffed pincushions, and his outfit made him look ridiculous; however, I recognized his half-closed eye immediately. There stood the man I had seen sitting on the bench at the Three Jolly Bargemen on a Saturday night, the one who had brought me down with his invisible gun.

It was clear that he did not recognize me at all. He looked my way, glanced at my watch-chain, spat, and said something to the other convict. They laughed, turned around with a clink of their chains, and looked at something else. The large numbers on their backs, like house addresses; their rough, mangy, and awkward appearance, as if they were animals; their shackled legs, wrapped in handkerchiefs; and the way everyone stared at them while keeping their distance, made them a truly unpleasant and degraded sight, just as Herbert had described.

But that was not the worst of it. A hot-tempered gentleman, who had taken the fourth seat there, flew into a rage. “Don’t take it so hard, sir,” the guard pleaded with the angry passenger. “I’ll sit next to you myself. I’ll put them on the outside of the row. They won’t bother you, sir. You won’t even know they’re there.”

“And don’t blame me,” the convict I recognized growled. “I don’t want to go. I’m perfectly happy to stay behind. As far as I’m concerned, anyone is welcome to my seat.”

“Or mine,” the other added gruffly. “I wouldn’t have bothered any of you if I’d had my way.” Then they both laughed and began cracking nuts, spitting the shells around—something I think I would have liked to do myself if I were in their position and treated with such contempt.

Eventually, it was decided that the angry gentleman had no choice but to accept his company or stay behind. He took his seat while continuing to complain, the guard sat next to him, and the convicts pulled themselves up as best they could. The convict I had recognized sat directly behind me, his breath hitting the back of my head.

You arrived here with your devotion and your luggage in tow. Told me? You have been telling me constantly. When you recounted your history, you stated clearly that your worship of her began at your first meeting, when you were quite young.”

“Very well, then,” I replied, finding this perspective fresh and agreeable, “I have never ceased to worship her. She has returned, a truly stunning and refined woman. I saw her yesterday. If I loved her before, I love her twice as much now.”

“It is fortunate for you, Handel,” Herbert remarked, “that you have been chosen and destined for her. Without crossing into restricted topics, we can certainly agree that this is true. Do you have any sense of Estella’s feelings regarding this worship?”

I gave a somber shake of my head. “Oh! She is worlds apart from me,” I said.

“Be patient, my dear Handel: there is plenty of time. But is there more you wish to say?”

“I am embarrassed to admit it,” I answered, “yet it is no worse to speak it than to harbor the thought. You label me a lucky man. Naturally, I am. I was a blacksmith’s apprentice only yesterday; what am I to call myself today?”

“Call yourself a good fellow, if you need a label,” Herbert replied, grinning and patting my hand—“a good fellow, possessing a strange blend of impulsiveness and doubt, courage and shyness, action and fantasy.”

I paused briefly to reflect on whether my personality truly contained such a mixture. On balance, I did not really identify with his assessment, but decided it was not worth arguing.

“When I ask what I should call myself today, Herbert,” I continued, “I am hinting at my internal concerns. You claim I am lucky. I realize I have done nothing to elevate my station, and that only Fortune has lifted me; that is indeed great luck. And yet, when I contemplate Estella—”

(“And when do you not, really?” Herbert interjected, gazing at the fire; I found this gesture kind and understanding.)

“—Then, my dear Herbert, I cannot express how vulnerable and insecure I feel, and how susceptible I am to countless risks. While avoiding restricted topics, as you did, I can still say that my entire future rests upon the loyalty of one individual. At best, how vague and frustrating it is to have such an unclear understanding of what those prospects are!” By saying this, I unburdened myself of thoughts that had always been present, though certainly most intense since yesterday.

“Now, Handel,” Herbert answered, in his cheerful, optimistic manner, “it strikes me that in the gloom of romance, we are inspecting our gift-horse’s teeth with a magnifying lens. Furthermore, it seems we are so focused on this inspection that we ignore one of the animal’s finest qualities. Did you not mention that your guardian, Mr. Jaggers, informed you at the start that your prospects were not limited to expectations alone? And even if he had not—though that is a significant ‘if,’ I admit—could you possibly believe that of all the men in London, Mr. Jaggers would be the one to mislead you?”

You brought your devotion and your suitcase here together. Told me! Why, you have been telling me all day long. When you shared your own story, you stated clearly that you began adoring her the very first time you saw her, when you were still quite young.”

“Very well, then,” I said, finding this to be a new and not unwelcome perspective, “I have never stopped adoring her. And she has returned, a most beautiful and elegant creature. I saw her yesterday. And if I adored her before, I now adore her twice as much.”

“Lucky for you, then, Handel,” Herbert said, “that you are chosen for her and destined for her. Without crossing any forbidden lines, we can certainly agree between ourselves that this is a fact. Do you have any idea yet what Estella’s thoughts are on the subject of your adoration?”

I shook my head gloomily. “Oh! She is thousands of miles away from me,” I said.

“Patience, my dear Handel: there is time enough, plenty of time. But you have something more to say?”

“I am ashamed to say it,” I replied, “and yet it is no worse to say it than to think it. You call me a lucky guy. Of course, I am. I was a blacksmith’s apprentice just yesterday; what should I call myself today?”

“Call yourself a good guy, if you want a label,” Herbert replied, smiling and patting my hand—“a good guy, with impulsiveness and hesitation, boldness and shyness, action and dreaming, all strangely mixed together.”

I paused for a moment to consider whether this mixture really existed in my character. On the whole, I did not recognize the description, but I decided it was not worth arguing about.

“When I ask what I am to call myself today, Herbert,” I continued, “I am expressing what is on my mind. You say I am lucky. I know I have done nothing to improve my own life, and that fortune alone has elevated me; that is being very lucky. And yet, when I think of Estella—”

(“And when don’t you, really?” Herbert added, staring into the fire; which I thought was kind and sympathetic of him.)

“—Then, my dear Herbert, I cannot tell you how dependent and uncertain I feel, and how vulnerable I am to hundreds of risks. Avoiding forbidden topics, as you did just now, I can still say that all my expectations depend on the constancy of one person. And at best, how vague and unsatisfactory it is to only know so little about what those expectations actually are!” In saying this, I unburdened my mind of what had always been there, more or less, though certainly more so since yesterday.

“Now, Handel,” Herbert replied, in his cheerful, hopeful way, “it seems to me that in the despair of being in love, we are examining our gift horse’s mouth with a magnifying glass. Furthermore, it seems to me that by focusing so intently on that examination, we completely overlook one of the animal’s best features. Didn’t you tell me that your guardian, Mr. Jaggers, told you from the start that you were not just endowed with expectations? And even if he hadn’t told you so—though that is a very big ‘if,’ I admit—could you believe that of all the men in London, Mr. Jaggers would be the one to mislead you?”

“You brought your adoration and your suitcase here together. Told me! Why, you have been telling me all day long. When you told me your own story, you said clearly that you began adoring her the first time you saw her, when you were very young indeed.”

“Very well, then,” I said, finding this to be a new and not unwelcome perspective, “I have never stopped adoring her. She has come back, a most beautiful and elegant creature. I saw her yesterday. If I adored her before, I now adore her twice as much.”

I shook my head gloomily. “Oh! She is thousands of miles away from me,” I said.

I stopped for a moment to consider whether my character was really this mixture. On the whole, I did not recognize the description, but I thought it was not worth arguing about.

“When I ask what I am to call myself today, Herbert,” I continued, “I am suggesting what is on my mind. You say I am lucky. I know I have done nothing to improve my own life, and that fortune alone has raised me; that is being very lucky. And yet, when I think of Estella—”

(“And when don’t you, you know?” Herbert added, with his eyes on the fire; which I thought was kind and sympathetic of him.)

“—Then, my dear Herbert, I cannot tell you how dependent and uncertain I feel, and how exposed to hundreds of risks. Avoiding forbidden topics, as you did just now, I can still say that all my expectations depend on the loyalty of one person. And at best, how vague and unsatisfactory it is to only know so little about what they are!” In saying this, I cleared my mind of what had always been there, though certainly more so since yesterday.

“Now, Handel,” Herbert replied, in his cheerful, hopeful way, “it seems to me that in the sadness of being in love, we are examining our good fortune with a magnifying glass. It also seems to me that, by focusing so closely on the details, we completely overlook the best parts of the situation. Didn’t you tell me that your guardian, Mr. Jaggers, told you at the beginning that you were not just given expectations? And even if he had not told you so—though that is a very large 'if,' I admit—could you believe that of all men in London, Mr. Jaggers would be the one to mislead you?”

“Avoid pointless efforts. Why bother? Now, who is next?”

In this manner, we strolled through Wemmick’s yard until he pivoted toward me and remarked, “Observe the individual I am about to greet.” I would have noted him regardless of the warning, as he had not yet shaken hands with anyone.

Immediately after he spoke, a stout, erect man—whom I can still visualize as I write—wearing a faded olive frock-coat, his ruddy face marked by a strange paleness and his eyes drifting aimlessly when he attempted to focus, approached the bars. He touched his hat, which possessed a greasy, slick texture reminiscent of chilled broth, in a salute that was part military, part mocking.

“Greetings, Colonel!” Wemmick called out. “How are you, Colonel?”

“I am well, Mr. Wemmick.”

“We did everything possible, but the proof was simply too overwhelming, Colonel.”

“Yes, it was too much, sir—but I do not mind.”

“Indeed,” Wemmick replied calmly, “you do not mind.” He then turned to me and added, “This man served the King. He was a regular soldier who purchased his release.”

I replied, “Is that so?” The man’s gaze met mine, then drifted above my head, scanned the area around me, and finally, he wiped his hand across his mouth and chuckled.

“I expect to be finished here by Monday, sir,” he told Wemmick.

“Possibly,” my companion answered, “but one never knows.”

“I am pleased to have the opportunity to say farewell, Mr. Wemmick,” the man stated, reaching his hand through the iron bars.

“Thank you,” Wemmick said, grasping his hand. “The same to you, Colonel.”

“If the items I possessed when arrested had been genuine, Mr. Wemmick,” the man said, reluctant to release his grip, “I would have requested that you wear another ring as a token of my appreciation for your help.”

“I will take the intention as the act,” Wemmick replied. “By the way, I heard you were quite the pigeon enthusiast.” The man glanced toward the sky. “I am told you kept an exceptional breed of tumblers. Could you arrange for a friend to bring me a pair, provided you have no further need for them?”

“It shall be done, sir.”

“Very well,” Wemmick said, “they will be well looked after. Good afternoon, Colonel. Farewell!” They shook hands once more, and as we moved away, Wemmick remarked to me, “He is a counterfeiter, and a highly skilled one. The judge’s report is submitted today, and he will certainly be hanged on Monday. Still, you see, pigeons remain portable property regardless.” With that, he glanced back, nodded at the lifeless plant, and scanned the yard as he exited, as if contemplating which other pot would best fill the space.

Upon leaving the prison via the lodge, I realized that my guardian’s significant status was recognized by the jailers just as much as by their captives. “Well, Mr. Wemmick,” the turnkey said, keeping us between the two heavy, spiked gates and carefully locking one before opening the next, “what is Mr. Jaggers planning to do regarding that riverside murder?”

“Don’t bother with pointless gestures. Why would you? Now, who’s next?”

We walked through Wemmick’s greenhouse until he turned to me and said, “Pay attention to the man I’m about to shake hands with.” I would have done so anyway, as he hadn’t shaken hands with anyone yet.

Almost immediately, a stout, upright man (whom I can still picture as I write this) in a worn olive-colored frock coat, with a strange pallor over his ruddy complexion and eyes that darted around whenever he tried to focus, approached the bars. He touched his hat—which had a greasy, slick surface like cold broth—in a half-serious, half-joking military salute.

“Colonel, to you!” Wemmick said. “How are you, Colonel?”

“I’m alright, Mr. Wemmick.”

“Everything possible was done, but the evidence was just too strong against you, Colonel.”

“Yes, it was too strong, sir—but I don’t care.”

“No, no,” Wemmick said coolly, “you don’t care.” Then, turning to me, he added, “This man served the King. He was a regular soldier and bought his discharge.”

I said, “Really?” The man’s eyes looked at me, then over my head, then all around me, before he wiped his hand across his lips and laughed.

“I think I’ll be out of here on Monday, sir,” he told Wemmick.

“Perhaps,” my friend replied, “but there’s no way to know for sure.”

“I’m glad to have the chance to say goodbye, Mr. Wemmick,” the man said, reaching his hand through the bars.

“Thank you,” Wemmick said, shaking his hand. “Same to you, Colonel.”

“If what I had on me when I was arrested had been real, Mr. Wemmick,” the man said, reluctant to let go, “I would have asked you to wear another ring as a token of my appreciation for your help.”

“I’ll take the thought for the deed,” Wemmick said. “By the way, I heard you were quite the pigeon enthusiast.” The man looked up at the sky. “I’m told you had a remarkable breed of tumblers. Could you ask a friend to bring me a pair, if you have no further use for them?”

“It will be done, sir.”

“Alright,” Wemmick said, “they’ll be well looked after. Good afternoon, Colonel. Goodbye!” They shook hands again, and as we walked away, Wemmick said to me, “He’s a counterfeiter, a very skilled one. The Recorder’s report is being filed today, and he’s certain to be executed on Monday. Still, you see, a pair of pigeons is portable property regardless.” With that, he looked back, nodded at the dead plant, and scanned the area as we left the yard, as if considering what other pot would fit best in its place.

As we exited the prison through the gatehouse, I noticed that my guardian’s importance was recognized by the guards just as much as by the prisoners. “Well, Mr. Wemmick,” said the guard who kept us between the two heavy, spiked gates, carefully locking one before unlocking the other, “what is Mr. Jaggers going to do about that waterfront murder?”

“Don’t waste your time on useless measures. Why bother? Now, who is next?”

We walked through Wemmick’s greenhouse until he turned to me and said, “Pay attention to the man I am about to shake hands with.” I would have noticed him anyway, as he had not shaken hands with anyone else yet.

Almost immediately, a stout, upright man—whom I can still see clearly as I write this—approached the bars. He wore a worn, olive-colored coat, his face had a strange paleness over its ruddy complexion, and his eyes wandered restlessly when he tried to focus. He touched his hat, which looked greasy like cold broth, with a salute that was half-serious and half-joking.

“Colonel, hello to you!” Wemmick said. “How are you, Colonel?”

“I am doing fine, Mr. Wemmick.”

“We did everything possible, but the evidence was simply too strong against you, Colonel.”

“Yes, it was too strong, sir, but I do not care.”

“No, no,” Wemmick said calmly, “you do not care.” He turned to me and added, “This man served the King. He was a soldier and bought his discharge.”

I said, “Indeed?” The man’s eyes looked at me, then over my head, then all around me, before he wiped his hand across his lips and laughed.

“I think I will be out of here on Monday, sir,” he told Wemmick.

“Perhaps,” my friend replied, “but there is no way to be sure.”

“I am glad to have the chance to say goodbye, Mr. Wemmick,” the man said, reaching his hand through the bars.

“Thank you,” Wemmick said, shaking his hand. “Same to you, Colonel.”

“If what I had on me when I was arrested had been real, Mr. Wemmick,” the man said, reluctant to let go of his hand, “I would have asked you to wear another ring as a token of my appreciation for your help.”

“It will be done, sir.”

“All right,” Wemmick said, “they will be taken care of. Good afternoon, Colonel. Goodbye!” They shook hands again. As we walked away, Wemmick told me, “He is a counterfeiter, a very skilled workman. The judge’s report is being filed today, and he is certain to be executed on Monday. Still, you see, a pair of pigeons is portable property all the same.” He looked back, nodded at the dead plant, and glanced around as we left the yard, as if considering what other pot would look best in its place.

Whenever he sensed his situation was particularly dire and that he absolutely needed to secure an opportunity, he would visit the Exchange during peak hours, pacing back and forth in a somber, rhythmic pattern among the gathered titans of industry. “Because,” Herbert explained to me upon returning home for dinner after one of those particular outings, “I have realized, Handel, that an opportunity will not seek you out; you must seek it out yourself—which is what I have been doing.”

Had we not been so fond of each other, I suspect we would have grown to despise one another every single morning. During those periods of remorse, I loathed our rooms beyond words and could not stand the sight of the Avenger’s uniform, which seemed more costly and less profitable at that hour than at any other time of the day. As our debts mounted, breakfast turned into an increasingly empty ritual, and on one occasion, after receiving a letter threatening legal action “not entirely unrelated,” as my local newspaper might phrase it, “to jewelry,” I went so far as to grab the Avenger by his blue collar and lift him off the ground—leaving him suspended in the air like a booted Cupid—simply for daring to assume we desired a bread roll.

At certain intervals—or rather, uncertain ones, as they relied entirely on our moods—I would remark to Herbert, as if it were a brilliant revelation:

“My dear Herbert, we are faring poorly.”

“My dear Handel,” Herbert would reply with complete sincerity, “if you can believe it, those exact words were just on my tongue, by some odd coincidence.”

“In that case, Herbert,” I would answer, “let us examine our finances.”

We always gained a deep sense of gratification from scheduling a time for this task. I always felt this was true business, the proper way to face the situation, the way to seize the enemy by the throat. And I am certain Herbert felt the same.

We would order a somewhat special dinner, accompanied by a bottle of something equally out of the ordinary, so that our spirits might be bolstered for the task and we could rise to the occasion. Once dinner was finished, we brought out a bundle of pens, a generous amount of ink, and a substantial display of writing and blotting paper. There was, after all, something very reassuring about having an abundance of stationery.

I would then take a sheet of paper and write at the top, in a tidy script, the title, “Memorandum of Pip’s debts,” carefully adding Barnard’s Inn and the date. Herbert would likewise take a sheet and write with similar precision, “Memorandum of Herbert’s debts.”

Each of us would then consult a disorganized pile of documents at our sides, which had been tossed into drawers, worn thin in our pockets, partially scorched while lighting candles, wedged for weeks into the mirror frame, and otherwise ruined. The scratching of our pens provided us with immense relief, to the point where I sometimes found it hard to differentiate between this instructive business activity and the actual act of settling our accounts. In terms of their virtuous quality, the two actions seemed nearly identical.

After we had been writing for a short while, I would inquire how Herbert was progressing.

I concluded by resting my hand on his shoulder and remarking, “I cannot avoid confiding in you, even though I realize it must be a burden; yet that is your own doing, for having ever led me to this place.”

Wemmick remained quiet for a brief moment, then responded with a slight jolt, “Well, you see, Mr. Pip, I must mention one thing. This is remarkably kind of you.”

“Then promise you will assist me in being good,” I urged.

“Goodness,” Wemmick answered, moving his head, “that is not my line of work.”

“And this is not your place of business,” I countered.

“You are correct,” he replied. “You have struck the mark. Mr. Pip, I shall put on my thinking cap, and I believe everything you desire can be accomplished gradually. Skiffins—that is her brother—is an accountant and agent. I will contact him and begin the task for you.”

“I offer you ten thousand thanks.”

“Quite the opposite,” he stated, “I thank you, for although we are acting strictly in a private and personal capacity, it is worth noting that there are Newgate cobwebs present, and this helps to clear them away.”

Following a bit more discussion along these lines, we went back into the Castle, where we discovered Miss Skiffins getting tea ready. The significant task of preparing the toast was assigned to the Aged, and that fine old gentleman was so focused on the job that he appeared to be in risk of melting his own eyes. It was no mere symbolic meal we were about to consume, but a hearty reality. The Aged assembled such a mountain of buttered toast that I could barely spot him behind it as it sizzled on an iron rack attached to the top bar; meanwhile, Miss Skiffins prepared such a large pot of tea that the pig in the yard grew quite agitated, repeatedly signaling his wish to join in the feast.

The flag had been lowered and the cannon discharged at the precise hour, and I felt as securely isolated from the remainder of Walworth as if the moat were thirty feet in width and depth. Nothing interrupted the peace of the Castle, save for the occasional swinging open of the doors for John and Miss Skiffins, which were prone to a spasmodic defect that made me feel sympathetically uneasy until I grew accustomed to it. I gathered from the orderly way Miss Skiffins handled things that she prepared tea there every Sunday evening; and I suspected that a traditional brooch she wore, depicting the silhouette of an unappealing woman with a very straight nose and a crescent moon, was an item of portable property gifted to her by Wemmick.

We consumed the entire supply of toast and drank a corresponding amount of tea, and it was pleasant to observe how warm and oily we all became afterward. The Aged, in particular, might have been mistaken for a clean, elderly chief of a primitive tribe, freshly anointed. After a brief interval of rest, Miss Skiffins—since the young servant had apparently returned to her family for the Sunday afternoon—cleaned the tea service in a light, ladylike, amateur fashion that did not compromise any of us. Then, she replaced her gloves, we gathered around the hearth, and Wemmick commanded, “Now, Aged Parent, read us the newspaper.”

I finished by placing my hand on his shoulder and saying, “I can’t help confiding in you, even though I know it must be a burden; but that is your own fault for having brought me here in the first place.”

Wemmick was silent for a moment, then said with a slight start, “Well, you know, Mr. Pip, I must tell you one thing. This is incredibly kind of you.”

The wind had blown with such intensity that lead had been ripped from the roofs of tall city buildings, while in rural areas, trees were uprooted and windmill sails were swept away. Furthermore, grim reports of fatalities and shipwrecks arrived from the coast. These fierce gales were accompanied by heavy downpours, and the day that had just ended as I began my reading was the most severe of all.

Since that period, the Temple has undergone changes, making it less isolated and less vulnerable to the river than it was then. We resided in the uppermost part of the final building, and that night, the wind surging up the river rattled our home as if it were being struck by artillery fire or crashing waves. As the rain lashed against the windows, I looked up at them as they swayed and imagined I was inside a lighthouse battered by a storm. At times, smoke billowed down the chimney as if it were unable to withstand the night outside. When I opened the doors to peer down the stairwell, the lamps were extinguished. Shielding my eyes to look through the dark windows—for opening them against such a gale was impossible—I observed that the court lamps had gone out, the lights on the bridges and banks were flickering, and the coal fires on river barges were being scattered by the wind like glowing embers in the rain.

I read with my watch on the table, intending to finish at eleven. As I closed the volume, Saint Paul’s and the various city church clocks—some ahead, some in unison, some behind—chimed the hour. The wind distorted the sound, and as I listened to it being torn apart, I heard footsteps on the stairs.

It is irrelevant what anxious impulse caused me to jump and dreadfully associate the sound with my late sister’s footsteps. The feeling vanished quickly, and I listened again, hearing the person stumble as they climbed. Recalling that the stairwell lights were out, I grabbed my reading lamp and walked to the landing. Whoever was below had paused upon seeing my light, and everything fell silent.

“Is someone down there?” I asked, peering over the edge.

“Yes,” a voice replied from the darkness below.

“Which floor are you looking for?”

“The top. Mr. Pip.”

“That is me. Is something wrong?”

“Nothing is wrong,” the voice answered, and the man continued upward.

I held my lamp over the railing, and he gradually entered the glow. Because it was a shaded lamp meant for reading, its beam was very narrow; he was illuminated for only a brief moment before stepping back into the shadows. In that instant, I caught sight of a face I did not recognize, gazing up at me with an inexplicable expression of being moved and delighted to see me.
