And what follows, once that is finished? Here I am. Returning now would be just as detrimental as remaining—perhaps even more so. Furthermore, Pip, I am here because I have intended to do right by you for many years. Regarding what I am willing to risk, I am an aged creature now, having survived all sorts of snares since I first left the nest, and I am not frightened to land on a scarecrow. If Death is concealed within it, so be it; let him emerge, and I will confront him, and only then will I acknowledge him, not before. Now, let me examine my gentleman once more."

He grasped my hands again and inspected me with a sense of proud ownership, puffing on his pipe with immense satisfaction the entire time.

It seemed to me that my best course was to find him some private quarters nearby, which he could occupy once Herbert arrived, whom I anticipated in a few days. It was obvious that I must share the secret with Herbert, even setting aside the immense relief I would feel by unburdening myself to him. However, Mr. Provis—the name I decided to use for him—did not agree, withholding his approval of Herbert’s involvement until he could see him and judge his appearance for himself. "And even then, dear boy," he remarked, drawing a worn, small black Testament with a clasp from his pocket, "we shall have him swear an oath."

To claim that my frightening benefactor traveled the globe with this small volume solely to administer oaths in urgent situations would be to assert something I never fully confirmed; yet, I can say I never witnessed him utilize it for any other purpose. The book itself looked as though it had been pilfered from a courtroom, and perhaps his awareness of its history, coupled with his own background in such matters, led him to trust in its efficacy as a kind of legal incantation. When he produced it on this first occasion, I remembered how he had forced me to vow loyalty in the graveyard years before, and how he had mentioned the previous night that he habitually swore to his own intentions while in isolation.

Since he was currently attired in a sailor’s outfit, which made him appear as if he had parrots and cigars to sell, I proceeded to discuss what clothing he ought to adopt. He held a peculiar conviction regarding the effectiveness of "shorts" as a disguise, and had mentally designed an outfit for himself that would have rendered him a cross between a clergyman and a dentist. I managed with great effort to persuade him to adopt the attire of a successful farmer; we also agreed that he should crop his hair short and use a bit of powder. Finally, since the laundress and her niece had not yet spotted him, he was to remain hidden from their sight until his transformation was complete.

Deciding on these safety measures might seem straightforward, but in my bewildered, if not entirely frantic, condition, the process took so long that I did not venture out to implement them until mid-afternoon.

So, what happens once that is finished? Here I am. To turn back now would be just as bad as staying put—or worse. Besides, Pip, I am here because I have intended to do right by you for years and years. As for what I dare, I am an old bird now, having braved all kinds of traps since I first learned to fly, and I am not afraid to land on a scarecrow. If death is hidden inside it, so be it; let it come out, and I will face it, and only then will I believe in it, not before. Now, let me have another look at my gentleman.”

Once more, he took me by both hands and examined me with an air of admiring ownership, smoking with great satisfaction the entire time.

It seemed to me that I could do nothing better than secure him some quiet lodging nearby, which he could occupy when Herbert returned, whom I expected in two or three days. It was clear to me that the secret had to be shared with Herbert as a matter of absolute necessity, even if I ignored the immense relief I would feel by confiding in him. However, this was by no means clear to Mr. Provis (I decided to call him by that name), who withheld his consent to Herbert’s involvement until he could see him and form a favorable opinion of his appearance. “And even then, dear boy,” he said, pulling a greasy little black Testament with a clasp from his pocket, “we will have him swear an oath.”

To claim that my terrifying patron carried this little black book around the world solely to swear people in during emergencies would be to state something I never quite confirmed; but I can say that I never saw him use it for anything else. The book itself looked as though it had been stolen from a courtroom, and perhaps his knowledge of its history, combined with his own experiences in that regard, gave him a reliance on its power as a kind of legal spell or charm. On this first occasion of him producing it, I recalled how he had made me swear loyalty in the churchyard long ago, and how he had described himself last night as always swearing to his resolutions while in solitude.

As he was currently dressed in a seafaring outfit, in which he looked as if he had parrots and cigars to sell, I next discussed with him what he should wear. He held an extraordinary belief in the effectiveness of knee-breeches as a disguise, and had mentally designed an outfit for himself that would have made him look like a cross between a dean and a dentist. It was with considerable difficulty that I convinced him to adopt a style more like a prosperous farmer’s; we arranged that he should cut his hair short and wear a little powder. Finally, since he had not yet been seen by the laundress or her niece, he was to stay out of their sight until he had changed his clothes.

It might seem like a simple matter to decide on these precautions, but in my dazed, if not distracted, state, it took so long that I did not get out to carry them out until two or three in the afternoon.

“There was another man associated with Compeyson named Arthur—not his given name, but his surname. He suffered from a wasting disease and appeared like a mere ghost. He and Compeyson had previously swindled a wealthy woman out of a large sum of money, but Compeyson gambled his share away so recklessly he would have squandered the king’s own revenue. Consequently, Arthur was dying in poverty, plagued by terrifying delusions, while Compeyson’s wife—whom Compeyson frequently abused—tried to show him kindness, though Compeyson himself felt compassion for no one.

“I could have learned a lesson from Arthur, but I failed to do so, and I will not claim I was particularly scrupulous—for what would be the point of that, my dear boy and friend? Thus, I started working with Compeyson, becoming a mere instrument in his grasp. Arthur resided in the upper level of Compeyson’s home near Brentford, where Compeyson kept a meticulous record of his expenses for room and board, expecting him to repay the debt if he ever recovered. However, Arthur soon closed that account. During my second or third visit, he rushed into Compeyson’s parlor late at night wearing only a flannel robe, his hair damp with perspiration. He told Compeyson’s wife, ‘Sally, she is truly upstairs with me now, and I cannot make her leave. She is dressed entirely in white, with white flowers in her hair; she is terribly insane, carrying a burial shroud over her arm, and she insists she will drape it over me at five o’clock in the morning.’

“Compeyson replied, ‘You fool, don’t you realize she is a living person? How could she possibly be up there without entering through the door or window and climbing the stairs?’

“‘I do not know how she arrived,’ Arthur answered, trembling violently from his delirium, ‘but she is standing in the corner at the foot of my bed, completely mad. And where her heart is broken—which you caused!—there are droplets of blood.’

“Compeyson spoke with feigned toughness, but he was inherently a coward. ‘Go upstairs with this babbling invalid,’ he told his wife, ‘and Magwitch, assist her, will you?’ Yet, he never approached the room himself.

“Compeyson’s wife and I helped him back to bed, where he raved in a terrifying manner. ‘Look at her!’ he shouted. ‘She is shaking the shroud at me! Can’t you see her? Look at her eyes! Isn’t it horrific to see her so deranged?’ Then he cried, ‘She is going to put it on me, and then I am finished! Take it away from her, take it away!’ He grabbed onto us, continuing to speak to her and respond to her presence until I almost believed I could see her myself.

“Compeyson’s wife, accustomed to his condition, gave him some spirits to ease his terrors, and eventually he grew calm. ‘Oh, she has left! Has her attendant come to collect her?’ he asked. ‘Yes,’ Compeyson’s wife replied. ‘Did you tell him to lock and bolt her in?’ ‘Yes.’ ‘And to remove that hideous object from her?’ ‘Yes, yes, everything is fine.’ ‘You are a kind soul,’ he said, ‘do not abandon me, whatever you do, and thank you!’

My sincerity stirred a sense of wonder in her that appeared as if it might have been softened by pity, had she been able to make me at all comprehensible to her own thoughts.

“I am going,” she repeated, in a softer tone, “to marry him. The arrangements for my wedding are underway, and I shall be wed shortly. Why do you unfairly bring up the name of my adoptive mother? This is my own decision.”

“Is it your own decision, Estella, to throw yourself away on such a brute?”

“On whom should I throw myself away?” she replied, with a smile. “Should I throw myself away on the man who would most quickly realize—if people truly feel such things—that I brought him nothing? There! It is settled. I shall fare well enough, and so will my husband. Regarding your claim that I was led into this disastrous choice, Miss Havisham would have preferred that I wait and not marry yet; however, I am weary of the life I have lived, which holds very little appeal for me, and I am quite ready to alter it. Say no more. We shall never see eye to eye.”

“Such a base brute, such a dull brute!” I pleaded, in desperation.

“Do not worry about me being a blessing to him,” Estella remarked; “I shall not be that. Come! Here is my hand. Do we part on these terms, you idealistic boy—or man?”

“O Estella!” I replied, as my painful tears dropped quickly onto her hand, despite my efforts to hold them back; “even if I stayed in England and could hold my head high like the others, how could I bear to see you as Drummle’s wife?”

“Nonsense,” she answered,—“nonsense. This will be forgotten in no time.”

“Never, Estella!”

“You will banish me from your thoughts within a week.”

“Banish you from my thoughts! You are woven into my existence, a part of my very self. You have been in every sentence I have ever read since I first arrived here, the unrefined, common boy whose fragile heart you wounded even then. You have been in every view I have ever beheld since—on the river, on the sails of the vessels, on the marshes, in the clouds, in the light, in the shadows, in the wind, in the forests, in the sea, in the city streets. You have been the personification of every elegant notion my mind has ever encountered. The stones that form the most solid London structures are no more substantial, or more impossible to dislodge, than your presence and influence have been to me, there and everywhere, and will always be. Estella, until the final hour of my life, you cannot help but remain a part of my nature, part of the little good within me, and part of the evil. On my initial trip past Mill Pond Bank, Herbert and I were rowing; both on the way there and back, we observed the eastern blind being lowered. Herbert visited at least three times weekly, yet he never returned with any distressing news. Nevertheless, I remained aware of the danger and could not shake the feeling that I was under surveillance. Once such a thought takes root, it becomes persistent; it would be difficult to count the number of innocent people I wrongly suspected of spying on me.

In essence, I was perpetually anxious for the reckless man in concealment. Herbert occasionally remarked that he enjoyed standing by our windows after nightfall, watching the ebbing tide and imagining it flowing toward Clara. Conversely, I dreaded that the water was carrying toward Magwitch, and that any dark shape upon the current might be his hunters, moving rapidly, quietly, and inevitably to capture him.

Chapter XLVII.

Several weeks elapsed without any developments. We awaited word from Wemmick, but he remained silent. Had I only known him within the confines of Little Britain, rather than enjoying the intimacy of his Castle, I might have questioned his loyalty; however, knowing him as I did, I never doubted him for an instant.

My financial situation began to look bleak, and I faced pressure from several creditors. I personally started to experience a shortage of ready cash, which I addressed by selling off some jewelry I could spare. Yet, I had firmly resolved that it would be a cruel deception to accept further funds from my benefactor while my own intentions and future remained so uncertain. Consequently, I had Herbert return the unopened pocket-book to him for safekeeping, and I felt a sense of contentment—whether genuine or misguided, I cannot say—in having refrained from benefiting from his wealth since he revealed his identity.

As time progressed, a heavy conviction took hold of me that Estella had wed. Terrified of confirming what I already believed, I shunned the newspapers and requested that Herbert, who knew the details of our final meeting, never mention her name. Why did I cling to this final, tattered remnant of a hope that had long since been torn away? Why do you, the reader, engage in your own similar contradictions, as you did last year, last month, or even last week?

I led a miserable existence, and its primary worry, which loomed over every other concern like a massive peak above a mountain range, never left my thoughts. Still, no fresh threats emerged. Regardless of how often I bolted from my bed, gripped by the sudden terror that he had been found, or how I sat in dread listening for Herbert’s return at night, fearing his footsteps might be faster than usual and carry dire tidings—despite all this and more, life continued its monotonous cycle.

"To conclude, sir," Wemmick remarked, "Mr. Jaggers completely overwhelmed the jury, and they surrendered."

"Has she remained in his employ ever since?"

"Indeed; and more than that," Wemmick replied, "she entered his service right after she was acquitted, already as subdued as she is today. She has since learned various tasks related to her work, but she was broken from the start."

"Do you recall the gender of the child?"

"It was said to be a girl."

"Do you have anything else to tell me tonight?"

"Nothing. I received your note and burned it. Nothing."

However, it was half-past one before we saw the smoke, and shortly after, we saw the smoke of another steamer behind it. As they were approaching at full speed, we prepared our two bags and took the chance to say goodbye to Herbert and Startop.

With a final, weak exertion that only succeeded because I surrendered to it and helped him, he lifted my hand to his mouth. The calm gaze directed at the pale ceiling returned, then faded, and his head sank peacefully onto his chest.

Recalling what we had studied together, I remembered the two men who entered the Temple to pray, and I realized there were no finer words I could utter at his bedside than, “O Lord, be merciful to him a sinner!”

Chapter LVII.

Finding myself entirely alone, I announced my plan to vacate the Temple chambers as soon as my lease could be legally terminated, intending to sublet them in the interim. I immediately posted notices in the windows; I was burdened by debt, possessed almost no funds, and had begun to feel genuine panic regarding my financial situation. In truth, I should have been alarmed, had I possessed the vigor and focus to grasp any reality beyond the fact that I was becoming quite sick. The recent strain had allowed me to delay my illness, but not to avoid it; I understood it was overtaking me now, though I knew little else and felt indifferent even to that.

For a couple of days, I remained on the sofa or the floor—wherever I happened to collapse—suffering from a heavy head, aching limbs, and a total lack of purpose or strength. Then came a night that seemed to last an eternity, filled with dread and terror; when I attempted to rise in bed the next morning to reflect upon it, I discovered I was unable to do so.

Whether I had truly been down in Garden Court in the middle of the night, searching for a boat I imagined was there; whether I had awakened on the stairs several times in a panic, unaware of how I had left my bed; whether I had found myself igniting the lamp, convinced he was ascending the stairs and that the flames had been extinguished; whether I had been tormented by the frantic speech, laughter, and moaning of someone, and had half-suspected those noises were my own; whether there had been a sealed iron furnace in a shadowed corner of the room, and a voice had repeatedly cried out that Miss Havisham was burning inside—these were matters I attempted to resolve and organize as I lay in bed that morning. Yet, the haze of a limekiln drifted between me and these thoughts, confusing them all, and it was through that mist that I finally perceived two men observing me.

“What do you want?” I inquired, startled; “I do not recognize you.”

“Well, sir,” one of them replied, leaning forward to touch my shoulder, “this is a situation you will likely resolve quickly, I imagine, but you are under arrest.”

With one final, weak effort—which would have failed had I not yielded to it and helped him—he lifted my hand to his lips. Then, he gently let it rest back upon his chest, covering it with his own hands. The calm gaze he had fixed on the white ceiling returned, then faded, and his head sank quietly onto his chest.

Mindful of what we had read together, I thought of the two men who went to the Temple to pray, and I knew there were no better words I could say by his bedside than, “O Lord, be merciful to him, a sinner!”

The June climate was delightful. Numerous pleasant visions of the existence I would pursue there, and of the positive transformation my personality would undergo once I possessed a guiding influence by my side—someone whose uncomplicated belief and transparent domestic sagacity I had verified—charmed my journey. The lindens were present, as were the white hawthorns and the chestnut trees, and their foliage rustled in unison whenever I paused to attend; yet, the metallic ring of Joe’s mallet was absent from the midsummer breeze.

Noah Claypole ran through the streets as fast as he could, not stopping for breath until he reached the workhouse gate. After resting for a minute to prepare a loud burst of sobs and a convincing display of tears and terror, he knocked loudly at the small door. He presented such a miserable face to the elderly pauper who opened it that even the old man, who was used to seeing miserable faces, stepped back in surprise.

“Goodness, how naive!” the youth cried. “A beak is a magistrate; and when you walk by a magistrate’s command, it is never a direct path, but always an ascent, never to return. Have you never been on the treadmill?”

“What mill?” Oliver asked.

“What mill! Why, the mill—the one that occupies so little space it operates inside a prison; and it functions better when people are short on funds than when they are flush, because then they cannot find laborers. But come,” the boy said; “you need food, and you shall have it. I am low on cash myself—only one shilling and a penny; but, as far as it goes, I will pay. Get on your feet. There! Now then! Move!”

Helping Oliver stand, the youth brought him to a nearby shop, where he bought enough prepared ham and a small loaf, or, as he called it, “a fourpenny loaf!” The ham was kept tidy and protected from dirt by the clever trick of hollowing out the bread and stuffing the meat inside. Carrying the bread, the youth walked into a small tavern and guided Oliver to a back room. There, a pot of beer was ordered by the mysterious boy; and Oliver, eating at his new companion’s suggestion, enjoyed a long, satisfying meal, while the strange boy watched him periodically with intense interest.

“Yes.”

“Have you a place to stay?”

“No.”

“Any money?”

“No.”

The boy whistled and shoved his hands into his pockets as deep as his oversized sleeves allowed.

“Yes. I do, when I am home,” the boy replied. “I assume you need somewhere to sleep tonight, don’t you?”

“I certainly do,” Oliver replied. “I have not slept indoors since I departed the countryside.”

“Do not worry about that,” the youth said. “I must be in London tonight; and I know a respectable elderly gentleman there who will provide you with lodging for free, and never ask for payment—that is, if someone he knows introduces you. And does he know me? Oh, no! Not at all! By no means. Certainly not!”

The youth grinned, as if to suggest his final remarks were meant sarcastically, and drained the beer as he did so.

“Stop thief! Stop thief!” That cry possesses a strange power. The shopkeeper abandons his stall, the carter his cart; the butcher drops his tray, the baker his basket, the milkman his bucket, the messenger his packages, the student his marbles, the road-worker his tool, and the youngster his toy. They dash off in a chaotic rush, shouting and screaming, colliding with pedestrians at corners, startling dogs, and alarming chickens, while the noise reverberates through every street and square.

“Stop thief! Stop thief!” Hundreds of voices join the chorus, and the mob grows at every corner. They race forward, splashing through mire and clattering over the stones. Windows are flung open, residents rush out, and the throng surges onward. Even the spectators of a puppet show abandon the performance to join the chase, adding their voices to the clamor of “Stop thief! Stop thief!”

“Stop thief! Stop thief!” There exists a natural human instinct for the chase. One miserable, gasping boy, exhausted and terrified, with sweat pouring down his face, exerts every ounce of strength to outrun his pursuers. As they close the distance, they cheer his failing stamina. “Stop thief!” Yes, stop him for heaven’s sake, if only out of pity!

Caught at last! A well-aimed strike. He falls to the ground, and the crowd swarms around him, shoving to get a better view. “Move back!” “Give him room to breathe!” “Rubbish! He doesn’t deserve it.” “Where is the gentleman?” “He is approaching now.” “Clear a path for the gentleman!” “Is this the lad, sir?” “Yes.”

“I’m not quite sure, ma’am,” Oliver said, without taking his eyes off the canvas. “I have seen so few that I hardly know. What a beautiful, gentle face that lady has!”

“Ah!” the old lady said. “Painters always make ladies look prettier than they really are, or they wouldn’t get any business, child. The man who invented the machine for taking photographs should have known that would never catch on; it’s far too honest. Far too honest,” the old lady said, laughing heartily at her own cleverness.

“Is—is that a portrait, ma’am?” Oliver asked.

“Yes,” the old lady said, looking up for a moment from the broth. “That is a portrait.”

“Whose is it, ma’am?” Oliver asked.

“Well, really, my dear, I don’t know,” the old lady answered good-naturedly. “I expect it isn’t a likeness of anyone you or I know. It seems to catch your interest, dear.”

“It is so pretty,” Oliver replied.

“Surely you aren’t afraid of it?” the old lady asked, noticing with great surprise the look of awe with which the child regarded the painting.

“Oh no, no,” Oliver returned quickly. “But the eyes look so sorrowful, and from where I sit, they seem fixed on me. It makes my heart beat,” Oliver added in a low voice, “as if it were alive and wanted to speak to me, but couldn’t.”

Gradually, he drifted into that deep, tranquil sleep that only relief from recent suffering can bring; that calm, peaceful rest from which it is painful to wake. If this were death, who would want to be roused again to all the struggles and turmoil of life; to all its current cares, its anxieties for the future, and, most of all, its weary memories of the past?

It had been bright daylight for hours when Oliver opened his eyes; he felt cheerful and happy. The crisis of his illness had safely passed. He belonged to the world again.

The only occupant was a wretched, shoeless offender, arrested for flute-playing. Bumble, having swapped his tricorne for a round hat and wrapped himself in a blue overcoat featuring a cape, climbed onto the coach exterior alongside the paupers whose legal residence was in question, eventually reaching London with them. He faced no difficulties during the journey other than the stubborn conduct of the two paupers, who kept shivering and grumbling about the chill in a way that Mr. Bumble claimed made his own teeth chatter and left him feeling uneasy, despite his heavy coat. After securing these troublesome individuals for the evening, Mr. Bumble settled into the inn where the coach halted and enjoyed a modest meal of steaks, oyster sauce, and porter. Placing a glass of steaming gin-and-water upon the mantel, he pulled his seat toward the hearth and, while musing on the widespread vice of ingratitude and grumbling, prepared to peruse the newspaper. The initial paragraph that caught Mr. Bumble’s gaze was this notice: “FIVE GUINEAS REWARD. Whereas a young lad named Oliver Twist vanished, or was lured away, last Thursday evening from his residence in Pentonville and has not been located since. The stated reward will be granted to anyone providing details that lead to finding the said Oliver Twist or shedding light on his background, in which the advertiser is, for various reasons, deeply concerned.” There followed a detailed account of Oliver’s clothing, physical traits, appearance, and departure, along with Mr. Brownlow’s full name and residence. Mr. Bumble widened his eyes, studied the notice deliberately three times, and within five minutes was heading toward Pentonville, having in his agitation abandoned his glass of hot gin-and-water untouched. “Is Mr. Brownlow in?” Mr. Bumble asked the young woman who answered the door. She offered the typical, somewhat evasive response: “I cannot say; where are you from?” As soon as Mr. Bumble mentioned Oliver’s name to explain his business, Mrs. Bedwin, who had been eavesdropping by the parlor entrance, rushed into the hallway, gasping for air. “Enter, please enter,” the elderly woman urged. “I was certain we would receive news of him. Dear boy! I knew it! I was positive. Bless him! I maintained that all along.” With these words, the kind woman hurried back to the parlor and, sinking onto a sofa, began to weep. The servant, less emotional, had meanwhile gone upstairs and returned to ask Mr. Bumble to follow her at once, which he did. “Never mind what it involves!” Sikes retorted. “I require a boy, and he must be small. Good heavens!” Mr. Sikes remarked, lost in thought, “if only I had that lad of Ned’s, the chimney-sweep! He kept him stunted intentionally, hiring him out by the task. But the father got transported; then the Juvenile Delinquent Society stepped in, removed the boy from a trade where he was making a living, instructed him in reading and writing, and eventually apprenticed him. And so it continues,” Sikes added, his anger mounting as he recalled his grievances, “they persist; and if they possessed sufficient funds—which, fortunately, they do not—we would not have half a dozen boys remaining in the entire profession within a year or two.”

“We certainly would not,” the Jew agreed, having been deep in thought during this monologue and only hearing the final remark. “Bill!”

“What is it now?” Sikes asked.

The Jew gestured toward Nancy, who remained staring into the flames, signaling that he wanted her sent out of the room. Sikes shrugged his shoulders with annoyance, seemingly finding the measure redundant, yet he obeyed by asking Miss Nancy to bring him a pitcher of ale.

“You do not want any ale,” Nancy stated, crossing her arms and remaining seated quite calmly.

“I tell you that I do!” Sikes insisted.

“Rubbish,” the girl replied coolly. “Continue, Fagin. I am aware of what he intends to say, Bill; he need not worry about me.”

The Jew remained hesitant. Sikes looked between them, somewhat surprised.

As they drew near the City, the clamor and congestion grew steadily; by the time they navigated the thoroughfares between Shoreditch and Smithfield, it had intensified into a deafening roar of activity. The morning light was as bright as it would be until dusk, and the frantic start of the day for half of London’s inhabitants was well underway.

Veering through Sun Street and Crown Street, then traversing Finsbury Square, Mr. Sikes navigated via Chiswell Street into Barbican, continuing into Long Lane and finally reaching Smithfield; from this final location emerged a chaotic symphony of clashing noises that left Oliver Twist utterly astounded.

It was the morning of the market. The earth was blanketed, nearly to the ankles, in muck and sludge; a dense vapor, constantly emanating from the steaming hides of the livestock and blending with the mist that seemed to settle upon the chimney-pots, hung oppressively overhead. Every pen in the middle of the expansive area, along with as many makeshift enclosures as could be squeezed into the open ground, was packed with sheep; tethered to posts along the gutter were lengthy rows of cattle and oxen, standing three or four abreast. Rural folk, butchers, livestock drivers, peddlers, youths, pickpockets, loafers, and outcasts of every base sort were jumbled into a single mass; the whistling of drovers, the yapping of dogs, the lowing and thrashing of oxen, the bleating of sheep, the grunting and squealing of swine, the calls of vendors, the yelling, cursing, and brawling on every side; the tolling of bells and the thunder of voices pouring from every tavern; the shoving, jostling, herding, striking, hollering, and screaming; the ghastly and jarring din echoing from every angle of the market; and the unwashed, unshaven, wretched, and filthy individuals constantly darting back and forth, plunging into and out of the mob; created a staggering and disorienting spectacle that completely overwhelmed the senses.

Mr. Sikes, pulling Oliver along, pushed his way through the densest part of the throng, paying scant notice to the myriad sights and sounds that so bewildered the lad. He gave a nod or two to an acquaintance passing by, and, declining several offers to partake in an early drink, pushed forward with determination until they had escaped the chaos and navigated through Hosier Lane into Holborn.

“Now then, youngster!” Sikes remarked, glancing up at the St. Andrew’s Church clock, “it’s nearly seven! You need to pick up the pace. Come on, don’t start dragging your feet, Lazy-legs!”

Mr. Sikes punctuated this remark with a sharp tug on his small companion’s wrist; Oliver, accelerating his gait into a sort of trot between a brisk walk and a run, managed to keep pace with the housebreaker’s long strides as best he could.

They maintained this speed until they had moved past Hyde Park Corner and were headed toward Kensington, at which point Sikes slowed his stride until an unoccupied cart, trailing some distance behind, caught up. Observing “Hounslow” painted on the side, he inquired of the driver, with as much politeness as he could muster, if he might provide them a ride as far as Isleworth.

“Hop on,” the man replied. “Is that your lad?”

“Yes; he’s my boy,” Sikes answered, staring intently at Oliver while absentmindedly sliding his hand into the pocket containing his pistol.

“Your father moves a bit too fast for you, doesn’t he, lad?”

Bumble immediately folded his collar down once more, placed his hat and cane on a chair, and pulled another seat toward the table. As he settled himself, he gazed at the lady, who kept her eyes on the small teapot. Mr. Bumble cleared his throat again and offered a faint smile.

Mrs. Corney stood to retrieve an extra cup and saucer from the cupboard. Upon sitting back down, her gaze met that of the dashing beadle; she blushed and busied herself with preparing his tea. Mr. Bumble cleared his throat once more—this time with greater volume than before.

“Do you take it sweet, Mr. Bumble?” the matron asked, reaching for the sugar bowl.

“Extremely sweet, indeed, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble answered. He stared directly at Mrs. Corney as he spoke; if any beadle ever appeared affectionate, Mr. Bumble certainly did at that instant.

The tea was prepared and served in silence. Mr. Bumble, having laid a handkerchief across his lap to protect his fine breeches from crumbs, started to eat and drink, occasionally punctuating these activities with a heavy sigh. This did not hinder his appetite; rather, it seemed to assist his progress with the tea and toast.

“I notice you keep a cat, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble remarked, looking toward the animal resting before the hearth with her litter, “and kittens as well, I see!”

“I am so attached to them, Mr. Bumble, you have no idea,” the matron replied. “They are so joyful, so playful, and so lively that they serve as true companions to me.”

“They are delightful creatures, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble agreed, “so very domestic.”

“Indeed!” the matron added with fervor; “they are so devoted to their home that it is a genuine pleasure, I assure you.”

“Mrs. Corney, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble stated deliberately, tapping the rhythm with his teaspoon, “I mean to say this: any cat or kitten that could reside with you, ma’am, and not cherish its home, would be a fool, ma’am.”

“Oh, Mr. Bumble!” Mrs. Corney protested.

“There is no point in hiding the truth, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble declared, waving his teaspoon with a romantic gravity that made him appear doubly imposing; “I would drown such a creature myself, with pleasure.”

“Then you are a cruel man,” the matron said playfully, reaching out to take the beadle’s cup, “and a very cold-hearted one as well.”

“Cold-hearted, ma’am?” Mr. Bumble repeated. “Cold?” He surrendered his cup silently, pressed Mrs. Corney’s finger as she accepted it, struck his decorated waistcoat twice with an open hand, heaved a massive sigh, and scooted his chair a tiny distance away from the heat.

The table was circular, and since Mrs. Corney and Mr. Bumble had been seated facing one another with little room between them, both turned toward the fire, it is clear that by moving back from the hearth while remaining at the table, Mr. Bumble widened the gap between himself and Mrs. Corney. Prudent readers might admire this as an act of significant heroism on the beadle’s part, as he was tempted by the circumstances to utter romantic sentiments. While such words might suit the lighthearted, they seem far beneath the dignity of judges, members of parliament, ministers, mayors, and other public officials—and especially beneath the gravity of a beadle, who is known to be the most stern and unyielding of them all.

Judging by his manner, Toby was in no rush to begin their discussion. Initially, the Jew simply observed his face, hoping to discern some hint of the news he carried, but he found nothing. He appeared exhausted and ragged, yet his expression maintained that same calm ease it always held; beneath the grime, beard, and whiskers, the smug, self-assured grin of flash Toby Crackit remained unchanged. The Jew, suffering from intense impatience, tracked every bite the man took, pacing the room in a state of uncontrollable agitation. It was futile. Toby kept eating with total outward apathy until he was full; then, sending the Dodger away, he shut the door, prepared a drink of spirits and water, and settled in to speak.

“First of all, Faguey,” Toby began.

“Yes, yes!” the Jew interrupted, pulling his chair closer.

Mr. Crackit paused to sip his drink and remark that the gin was superb; then, propping his feet against the low mantel so his boots were nearly level with his eyes, he calmly continued.

“First of all, Faguey,” the burglar said, “how is Bill?”

“What!” the Jew shrieked, leaping from his chair.

“Wait, you don’t mean to say—” Toby started, his face losing its color.

“Mean!” the Jew shouted, stamping his feet in a rage. “Where are they? Sikes and the lad! Where are they? Where have they been? Where are they concealed? Why haven’t they arrived?”

“The burglary went wrong,” Toby said weakly.

“I am aware of that,” the Jew answered, yanking a newspaper from his coat and gesturing toward it. “What else?”

“They shot and wounded the boy. We fled across the fields behind the house, carrying him between us—in a direct line—through hedges and ditches. They pursued us. Damn it! The entire countryside was alerted, and the hounds were tracking us.”

“The boy!”

“Bill carried him on his back, running like the wind. We paused to share the burden; his head was drooping, and he felt cold. They were right behind us; it was every man for himself to escape the gallows! We split up and abandoned the youngster in a ditch. Whether he is living or dead, that is all I can tell you.”

The Jew waited to hear nothing further; instead, he let out a piercing cry, tangled his fingers in his hair, and bolted from the room and out of the house.

CHAPTER XXVI.
IN WHICH A MYSTERIOUS CHARACTER APPEARS UPON THE SCENE; AND MANY THINGS, INSEPARABLE FROM THIS HISTORY, ARE DONE AND PERFORMED

The old man reached the street corner before he began to overcome the impact of Toby Crackit’s report. He had not slowed his frantic pace; he was still hurrying forward in the same wild, chaotic fashion when the abrupt rush of a carriage and a loud warning from pedestrians, who noticed his peril, forced him back onto the sidewalk. Avoiding the main thoroughfares as much as he could and creeping only through side streets and lanes, he finally reached Snow Hill. Here he moved even more quickly; he did not slow down until he had turned into a court, at which point, as if realizing he was finally in his familiar territory, he resumed his customary shuffling gait and appeared to breathe with more ease.

Judging by appearances, Toby was in no rush to start the conversation. At first, the Jew was content to watch his face patiently, hoping to glean some clue about the news he brought, but it was useless.

He looked tired and worn, yet his features held that same complacent calm they always wore. Through the dirt, beard, and whiskers, the self-satisfied smirk of flash Toby Crackit still shone through, unimpaired. The Jew, in an agony of impatience, watched every bite he took, pacing the room in irrepressible excitement. It was all for nothing. Toby continued to eat with complete outward indifference until he could eat no more. Then, ordering the Dodger out, he closed the door, mixed a glass of spirits and water, and settled in to talk.

They haunted me, day and night; and with them arrived such a surging flood of anxieties, worries, and self-centered sorrows, lest you should perish without ever knowing how deeply I adored you, that it nearly overwhelmed my mind and judgment. You regained your strength. Gradually, and almost moment by moment, some measure of vitality returned, and blending with the exhausted and weak current of life flowing sluggishly within you, it expanded once more into a powerful, rushing tide. I have observed you transform almost from death to life, with eyes that grew dim from their intensity and profound devotion. Do not suggest that you wish I had been spared this; for it has made my heart more compassionate toward all humanity.”

“That was not my intention,” Rose replied, sobbing; “I only wish you had departed from here, so that you might have returned to lofty and honorable endeavors; to pursuits truly deserving of you.”

What course remained for Mr. Bumble? He cast a despondent glance about and crept away; as he arrived at the exit, the paupers’ snickering erupted into a high-pitched cackle of uncontrollable glee. This was the final blow. He felt diminished in their estimation; he had forfeited his rank and standing before the paupers themselves; he had plummeted from the grandeur and authority of his beadle status to the absolute nadir of a browbeaten, henpecked husband.

“All within two months!” Mr. Bumble lamented, consumed by gloomy reflections. “Two months! Barely two months have passed since I was not merely my own master, but the master of everyone else within the parochial workhouse, and now!”

It was unbearable. Mr. Bumble struck the ear of the lad who held the gate for him—having reached the entrance while lost in his musings—and wandered off into the street in a state of agitation.

He paced through various streets until the physical exertion had tempered the initial intensity of his sorrow; subsequently, a shift in his mood left him parched. He went by numerous taverns but eventually halted before one tucked away in a side street, whose parlor, as he discerned from a quick glance past the window shades, was empty except for a single patron. At that instant, a heavy rain began to fall. This settled the matter. Mr. Bumble went inside, requested a drink as he moved by the bar, and stepped into the room he had observed from the road.

The man sitting there was tall, dark-haired, and wrapped in a voluminous cloak. He appeared to be a traveler, and his worn, dusty clothing, combined with a certain gauntness in his features, suggested he had journeyed a great distance. He cast a sidelong glance at Bumble upon his arrival, yet barely bothered to incline his head in response to his greeting.

Mr. Bumble possessed sufficient self-importance for two people, even if the stranger had been more cordial; consequently, he consumed his gin-and-water quietly and perused the newspaper with an air of great ceremony and gravity.

It occurred, however—as frequently happens when individuals find themselves together in such conditions—that Mr. Bumble felt a recurring, irresistible urge to sneak a look at the stranger, only to find, upon withdrawing his gaze in embarrassment, that the stranger was simultaneously watching him. Mr. Bumble’s discomfort was deepened by the stranger’s striking gaze, which was sharp and luminous yet obscured by a scowl of wariness and doubt, unlike anything he had previously encountered and quite unpleasant to witness.

After they had met each other’s eyes repeatedly in this fashion, the stranger spoke in a grating, low tone.

“Were you searching for me,” he inquired, “when you looked through the window?”

“Not that I am conscious of, unless you happen to be Mr.—” Here Mr. Bumble paused, hoping to learn the man’s identity by letting him fill in the gap.

“I perceive you were not,” the stranger replied, a trace of subtle mockery touching his lips; “otherwise you would have known my name. You do not know it. I suggest you refrain from inquiring.”

What could Mr. Bumble do? He looked around dejectedly and slunk away; as he reached the door, the paupers’ tittering erupted into a shrill chuckle of irrepressible delight. This was the final straw. He was degraded in their eyes; he had lost his status and standing before the paupers themselves; he had fallen from the height and pomp of his position as beadle to the lowest depths of being a henpecked husband.

It was too much. Mr. Bumble boxed the ears of the boy who opened the gate for him—having reached the entrance while lost in his reverie—and walked distractedly into the street.

He walked up one street and down another until the physical exertion had calmed his initial outburst of grief; then, a shift in his mood made him thirsty. When his cavernous voice interrupted the quiet, all three jumped. They appeared to have never encountered its quality previously. 

“How did that animal arrive here?” he questioned. 

“By itself. Three hours prior.” 

“The evening news reports that Fagin has been captured. Is that factual, or a falsehood?” 

“Factual.” 

They returned to silence. 

“Curse you all!” exclaimed Sikes, wiping his brow. “Do you possess nothing to say to me?” 

There was a restless shifting among them, yet no one uttered a word. 

“You who manage this residence,” Sikes stated, directing his gaze toward Crackit, “do you intend to betray me, or permit me to remain here until this pursuit concludes?” 

“You are welcome to remain here, if you deem it secure,” replied the individual addressed, following a brief pause. 

Sikes moved his eyes gradually up the partition behind him—attempting to rotate his head rather than actually doing so—and asked, “Is—it—the corpse—has it been interred?” 

They gestured negatively.

This explanation seemed the most likely, so they accepted it as fact. The dog crawled under a chair and curled up to sleep, ignored by everyone else.

Since it was now dark, they closed the shutters, lit a candle, and set it on the table. The horrific events of the past two days had left a deep mark on all three, made worse by the danger and uncertainty of their own situation. They pulled their chairs closer together, jumping at every noise. They spoke very little, and only in whispers, feeling as silent and terrified as if the murdered woman’s body were in the next room.

They had been sitting like this for some time when suddenly there was a frantic knocking at the door downstairs.

“Young Bates,” Kags said, glancing around aggressively to hide his own fear.

The knocking came again. No, it wasn’t him. He never knocked like that.

Crackit went to the window and, shaking uncontrollably, pulled his head back inside. There was no need to tell them who it was; his pale face said it all. The dog was instantly alert, running to the door and whining.

“We have to let him in,” he said, picking up the candle.

“Is there no other way?” the other man asked in a raspy voice.

“None. He has to come in.”

“Don’t leave us in the dark,” Kags said, taking a candle from the mantelpiece and lighting it with such a trembling hand that the knocking repeated twice before he finished.

Crackit went down to the door and returned followed by a man with the lower half of his face hidden by a handkerchief and another tied over his head beneath his hat. He slowly pulled them off. A blanched face, sunken eyes, hollow cheeks, three days of beard growth, wasted flesh, and short, heavy breaths; it was the very ghost of Sikes.

He placed his hand on a chair in the middle of the room, but as he was about to collapse into it, he shuddered and, seemingly glancing over his shoulder, dragged it back against the wall—as close as it would go—and sat down.

“You can stay here, if you think it’s safe,” the man replied after a moment of hesitation.

When evaluating an author's worth and determining his place among our literary icons, we are entitled to view him from our own perspective and judge him by our own criteria. However, when assessing the strength demonstrated in his creations, we must be guided by his personal intentions, comparing his work against his own ideal to determine what might be missing. We diverge from Mr. Poe regarding the purpose of art. He considers its goal to be the generation of Beauty, and perhaps our disagreement lies solely in how that term is defined. Nevertheless, in our commentary on his literature, we shall utilize his own standard as our compass. The sanctuary of the muse is reachable from every direction, and there is ample space within for everyone who offers tributes or consults the oracle.

In his short stories, Mr. Poe has elected to demonstrate his capabilities primarily within that obscure territory extending from the furthest edges of probability into the strange borders of superstition and fantasy. He blends in a striking fashion two talents rarely seen together: an ability to sway the reader's consciousness through the intangible veils of mystery, and a precision of detail that overlooks not even the smallest object. Both are, in reality, the inevitable outcomes of the primary trait of his intellect, which we have previously mentioned: analysis. This is what sets the artist apart. His mind immediately anticipates the intended result. Having decided to evoke specific feelings in the reader, he ensures all secondary elements contribute directly to the central theme. Even his mystery is, to his own mind, mathematical. For him, X is a known variable throughout. In every scene he depicts, he comprehends the chemical nature of all his pigments. Regardless of how indistinct some of his figures might appear, or how shapeless the shadows, the outline remains as sharp and precise to him as a geometric figure. For this reason, Mr. Poe lacks any affinity for Mysticism. The Mystic resides within the mystery, wrapped in it; it tints all his reflections; it impacts his vision specifically, causing mundane objects to acquire a prismatic fringe. Mr. Poe, conversely, is an external observer. He examines, he dissects, he watches

“with an eye serene,
The very pulse of the machine,”

for that is essentially what it is to him, with gears, cogs, and pistons, all functioning to achieve a particular outcome.

This analytical inclination of his intellect counterbalances his poetic side, and by granting him the endurance to be meticulous, allows him to infuse a remarkable sense of reality into his most fantastical visions. He portrays monomania with great intensity. He enjoys dissecting these mental afflictions, tracing the intricate spread of their roots. In evoking images of terror, too, he finds peculiar success, occasionally conveying through a dark suggestion some profound doubt that serves as the core of all horror. He entrusts the imagination with the duty of completing the scene, a task only it can perform.

“For much imaginary work was there;
Conceit deceitful, so compact, so kind,
That for Achilles’ image stood his spear
Grasped in an armed hand; himself behind
Was left unseen, save to the eye of mind.”

For much imaginary work was there; Conceit deceitful, so compact, so kind, That for Achilles’ image stood his spear Grasped in an armed hand; himself behind Was left unseen, save to the eye of mind.

“My primary objective is to launch a magazine titled ‘The Stylus,’ yet it would be of no value to me, even once founded, if it remained under a publisher’s authority. Consequently, I intend to create a periodical that is entirely my own in every respect. To achieve this, I must secure a minimum of five hundred subscribers to start; I have nearly two hundred already. I plan, however, to travel South and West to visit my personal and literary associates—former college and West Point companions—to see what I can accomplish. To obtain the funds necessary for the initial phase, I intend to deliver a lecture at the Society Library on Thursday, February 3rd, and to avoid any potential for disagreement, my topic will not be literary in nature. I have selected a wide-ranging subject: ‘The Universe.’

“Having provided you with the facts of the situation, I leave the remainder to your own discretion and kindness. Gratefully, most gratefully,

“Your friend always,
“EDGAR A. POE.”

Brief and incidental as these letters may be, we believe they adequately demonstrate the presence of the very traits often denied to Mr. Poe—humility, a readiness to persist, faith in the friendship of others, and the capacity for sincere and thankful companionship! Such he certainly was when of sound mind. Such he has consistently appeared to us, throughout all our personal experiences with him during a friendship spanning five or six years. Since it is far simpler to trust what we have witnessed and experienced than what we merely hear, we recall him only with admiration and respect; these accounts of him, when he was morally unstable, seem to us like portraits of a man we knew only in health, painted during a time of illness.

Yet there exists another, more poignant, and significantly more compelling proof that there was goodness in Edgar A. Poe. To disclose it, we must risk drawing back the curtain that reverently hides the sorrow and refinement found in poverty; however, we believe it may be justified if it helps to illuminate the poet’s memory, even if there were not a more urgent and immediate purpose it might serve for the closest person affected by his passing.

Our initial awareness of Mr. Poe’s relocation to this city came through a visit from a lady who introduced herself as his wife’s mother. She was seeking work on his behalf, and she justified her request by noting that he was unwell, that her daughter was a chronic invalid, and that their situation forced her to take this task upon herself. The face of this woman, rendered beautiful and saintly by a life clearly devoted to hardship and sorrowful affection, her soft and melancholy voice pressing her request, her long-neglected but naturally and instinctively refined demeanor, and her pleading yet appreciative remarks regarding the merits and talents of her son, revealed immediately the presence of one of those earthly angels that women in adversity can become. It was a difficult destiny she was tending to. Mr. Poe composed with meticulous effort, and in a manner too elevated above the common taste to be adequately compensated.

After roughly four and a half hours, the balloon reached a sufficient state of inflation. I proceeded to fasten the car and stowed my equipment inside, making sure to include the condensing device, a generous amount of water, and a substantial store of provisions like pemmican, which offers high nutritional value in a compact form. I also brought along a cat and two pigeons. With dawn approaching, I decided it was time to leave. I dropped a lit cigar as if by mistake, and while stooping to retrieve it, I secretly lit the slow match that extended slightly from the bottom edge of one of the smaller barrels. The three creditors failed to notice this action; I leaped into the car, severed the lone rope tethering me to the ground, and was gratified to ascend rapidly, easily lifting one hundred and seventy-five pounds of lead ballast with the capacity to carry even more.

However, I had barely reached an altitude of fifty yards when a deafening, chaotic storm of fire, smoke, sulfur, limbs, gravel, burning timber, and molten metal roared upward after me. My heart failed me, and I collapsed into the base of the car, shaking with absolute terror. I realized then that I had severely miscalculated, and the primary effects of the blast were still to come. Within a second, I felt blood surge to my head, followed immediately by a blast I will never forget, which shattered the night and seemed to tear the sky apart. Upon later reflection, I correctly identified the intensity of the explosion as a result of my position directly above the epicenter. At that moment, though, my only thought was survival. The balloon first buckled, then expanded violently, spun with dizzying speed, and finally, swaying like a drunkard, threw me over the edge of the car. I was left hanging at a terrifying height, head down and facing outward, suspended by a thin three-foot cord that had slipped through a gap in the wicker and caught my left foot as I tumbled. It is truly impossible to convey the horror of that moment. I struggled for air, my body racked by a shivering fit like an ague, my eyes feeling as though they were popping from their sockets, and a wave of nausea overcame me until I finally lost consciousness.

I cannot say how long I remained in that state. It must have been quite a while, for when I regained a partial sense of awareness, dawn was breaking, the balloon was at a massive altitude above an endless expanse of ocean, and there was no sign of land anywhere across the vast horizon.

In about four and a half hours, I had the balloon sufficiently inflated. I attached the basket, then loaded all my equipment—including the condensing apparatus, plenty of water, and a large supply of provisions like pemmican, which packs a lot of nutrition into a small space. I also secured a pair of pigeons and a cat in the basket. It was nearly dawn, and I decided it was time to leave. Dropping a lit cigar on the ground as if by accident, I used the moment I stooped to pick it up to secretly light the slow match, the end of which, as I mentioned, stuck out slightly from the bottom rim of one of the smaller casks. The three debt collectors didn't notice this maneuver at all. I jumped into the basket, immediately cut the single rope holding me to the ground, and was pleased to find I shot upward with ease, carrying one hundred and seventy-five pounds of lead ballast and capable of carrying just as much again.

However, I had barely reached a height of fifty yards when a dense hurricane of fire, smoke, sulfur, limbs, gravel, burning wood, and blazing metal came roaring and rumbling up after me in a terrifying, chaotic blast. My heart sank, and I collapsed to the bottom of the basket, trembling with pure terror. I realized I had completely overdone it and that the main impact of the shock was still to come. In less than a second, I felt all the blood in my body rush to my head, followed immediately by a concussion I will never forget, which tore through the night and seemed to rip the sky apart. Later, when I had time to reflect, I attributed the extreme violence of the explosion to my position directly above it, right in the path of its greatest force. But at the time, I only thought of surviving. The balloon collapsed, then expanded violently, spun around with terrifying speed, and finally, reeling like a drunkard, threw me with great force over the edge of the basket. I was left dangling at a terrifying height, head down and facing outward, held by a thin, three-foot cord that had accidentally hung through a gap in the wickerwork and in which my left foot had fortunately become tangled as I fell. It is impossible—utterly impossible—to convey the horror of my situation. I gasped for air, a shudder like a feverish chill shook every nerve and muscle in my body, my eyes felt as if they were popping from their sockets, a sickening nausea overwhelmed me, and finally, I passed out.

I cannot say how long I remained in that state. It must have been quite a while, because when I partially regained consciousness, I found day was breaking, the balloon was at a massive altitude over a vast ocean, and there was not a trace of land to be seen anywhere within the limits of the horizon.

I was left dangling at a dizzying height, head down and facing outward, held by a thin, three-foot cord that had hung through a gap in the wicker and luckily caught my left foot as I fell.
