I cannot say how long I remained unconscious. It must have been quite a while, because when I partially regained my senses, the sun was rising. The balloon was at a massive height over a vast ocean, and there was no sign of land anywhere within the wide horizon.

Having embraced this perspective, I felt minimal hesitation. Assuming that my journey would encounter an atmosphere fundamentally identical to that at the earth's surface, I believed that M. Grimm’s highly clever device would easily allow me to compress enough of it for breathing. This would eliminate the primary barrier to a lunar voyage. I had certainly invested significant funds and effort into modifying the equipment for this purpose, and I anticipated its successful use, provided I could finish the trip in a sensible timeframe. This returns me to the question of potential travel speed.

It is a fact that balloons, during the initial phase of their departure from the ground, are known to ascend at a relatively modest speed. Now, the lifting force depends entirely on the gas inside the balloon being lighter than the surrounding air; and, at first glance, it does not seem likely that, as the balloon gains height and enters atmospheric layers of rapidly decreasing density—I mean, it does not seem logical that, during this upward movement, the initial speed would increase. Conversely, I was unaware of any documented flight where a decrease in the actual rate of ascent was noted; although that should have occurred, if only due to gas leaking from poorly made balloons coated with inferior varnish. It appeared, therefore, that the impact of such leakage merely offset the influence of some accelerating force. I concluded that, assuming I encountered the medium I had envisioned, and assuming it proved to be essentially what we call atmospheric air, it would matter little at what level of thinness I found it—that is, regarding my ability to rise—because the gas within the balloon would not only undergo a somewhat similar thinning (which would allow me to release enough to prevent bursting), but, being what it was, would in any case remain lighter than any mixture of nitrogen and oxygen. Meanwhile, the pull of gravity would steadily decrease according to the inverse square of the distance, and thus, with a vastly increasing speed, I would eventually reach those remote regions where the earth's gravitational pull is replaced by that of the moon. Based on these thoughts, I did not deem it necessary to burden myself with more supplies than would suffice for forty days.

There remained, however, another obstacle that caused me some minor anxiety. It has been noted that, during balloon flights to significant heights, in addition to the difficulty of breathing, intense discomfort is felt in the head and body, frequently accompanied by nosebleeds and other distressing symptoms, which become increasingly troublesome as the altitude increases.

Having adopted this perspective, I had little further hesitation. Assuming that during my journey I would encounter an atmosphere essentially identical to that at the earth's surface, I believed that, using M. Grimm’s highly ingenious apparatus, I could easily condense enough of it for breathing. This would eliminate the primary obstacle to a trip to the moon. I had certainly spent significant money and effort adapting the device for this purpose, and I felt confident in its successful application, provided I could finish the voyage within a reasonable timeframe. This brings me back to the potential speed of travel.

It is true that balloons, during the initial stage of their ascent from the earth, are known to rise at a relatively moderate speed. Now, the lifting power depends entirely on the gas in the balloon being lighter than the surrounding air; at first glance, it does not seem likely that as the balloon gains altitude and enters atmospheric layers of rapidly decreasing density, the initial velocity would increase. On the other hand, I was unaware of any recorded ascent where a decrease in the absolute rate of climb was apparent, even though that should have happened—if for no other reason than the leakage of gas through poorly constructed balloons sealed with nothing better than standard varnish. It seemed, therefore, that the effect of such leakage was only enough to offset some accelerating force. I now reasoned that, provided I found the medium I had imagined during my journey, and provided it proved to be essentially what we call atmospheric air, it would make little difference at what extreme level of thinness I discovered it—at least regarding my ability to ascend—because the gas in the balloon would not only be subject to similar thinning (which I could manage by releasing enough gas to prevent an explosion), but, being what it was, it would in any case remain specifically lighter than any mixture of nitrogen and oxygen. Meanwhile, the force of gravity would constantly decrease in proportion to the square of the distance, and thus, with a tremendously accelerating velocity, I would eventually reach those distant regions where the earth’s gravitational pull would be superseded by that of the moon. Based on these ideas, I did not think it necessary to burden myself with more provisions than would suffice for forty days.

There was still, however, another difficulty that caused me some anxiety. It has been noted that in balloon ascents to any significant height, in addition to the difficulty of breathing, great discomfort is felt in the head and body, often accompanied by nosebleeds and other alarming symptoms, which become increasingly troublesome as altitude increases.

Having adopted this view of the subject, I had little further hesitation. Assuming that I would encounter an atmosphere similar to that at the earth's surface during my journey, I believed that M. Grimm’s ingenious device would easily allow me to condense enough air for breathing. This would remove the main obstacle to a trip to the moon. I had spent significant money and effort adapting the equipment for this purpose, and I felt confident it would work if I could finish the voyage within a reasonable time. This brings me back to the question of how fast I could travel.

It is true that balloons usually rise at a moderate speed when they first leave the earth. The power of ascent comes entirely from the gas in the balloon being lighter than the surrounding air. At first glance, it does not seem likely that the balloon’s speed would increase as it rises into thinner layers of the atmosphere. On the other hand, I was not aware of any recorded flight where the rate of ascent actually slowed down, even though that should have happened—if only because gas leaks from poorly constructed balloons sealed with ordinary varnish. It seemed, therefore, that the effect of such leaks was only enough to cancel out some accelerating force. I concluded that if I found the medium I expected, and if it proved to be essentially atmospheric air, it would make little difference how thin it was regarding my ability to ascend. The gas in the balloon would be subject to similar thinning, and I could release enough to prevent an explosion. Regardless, it would remain lighter than any mixture of nitrogen and oxygen. Meanwhile, the force of gravity would constantly decrease in proportion to the square of the distance. Consequently, I would eventually reach those distant regions where the earth’s pull is replaced by the moon’s, traveling at a tremendously increasing speed. Based on these ideas, I did not think it necessary to carry more than forty days' worth of supplies.

There was still one other difficulty, however, that caused me some concern. It has been noted that during balloon flights to significant heights, travelers experience not only difficulty breathing but also great discomfort in the head and body. This is often accompanied by nosebleeds and other alarming symptoms, which become increasingly severe as the altitude rises.

After repeating this process several times, the room was finally filled with air suitable for breathing. However, in such a restricted area, the air would quickly grow stale and unusable due to constant inhalation. It was then expelled through a minor valve located at the base of the car, as the heavy air naturally descended into the lighter atmosphere underneath. To prevent the discomfort of creating a complete vacuum inside the chamber, this cleaning process was performed incrementally rather than all at once; the valve was opened briefly and then shut, until a few pump strokes from the condenser replaced the expelled air. For experimental purposes, I placed the cat and her kittens in a small basket, hanging it outside the car from a button near the valve, which allowed me to feed them whenever required. I performed this with some danger, reaching beneath the car with one of the previously mentioned poles equipped with a hook before sealing the chamber entrance.

“By the time I finished these preparations and filled the chamber as described, it was nearly nine o’clock. Throughout the time I spent on these tasks, I suffered extreme agony from breathing difficulties, and I deeply regretted the carelessness, or perhaps recklessness, of delaying such a vital task until the final moment. Yet, having finally succeeded, I quickly began to enjoy the rewards of my creation. I was breathing with total ease once more—and indeed, why should I not? I was also pleasantly surprised to discover that the intense pains that had plagued me were largely gone. A mild headache, along with a feeling of tightness or swelling in my wrists, ankles, and throat, was almost all that remained. It seemed clear that most of the discomfort caused by the loss of atmospheric pressure had faded, as I had anticipated, and that much of the suffering from the previous two hours should have been blamed entirely on inadequate respiration.

“At twenty minutes to nine—shortly before I sealed the chamber—the mercury reached its lowest point in the barometer, which, as I noted earlier, was of a specialized, extended design. It showed that I had reached an altitude of 132,000 feet, or twenty-five miles, allowing me to observe a portion of the earth’s surface equal to one three-hundred-and-twentieth of its total area. By nine o’clock, I had lost sight of the land to the east, but not before noticing that the balloon was moving swiftly toward the north-northwest. The curve of the ocean below was clearly visible, though my sight was frequently blocked by drifting cloud formations.

After repeating this process several times, the chamber was finally filled with air suitable for breathing. However, in such a cramped space, the air would inevitably become stale and unusable after repeated inhalation. It was then expelled through a small valve at the bottom of the car, with the dense air sinking easily into the thinner atmosphere below. To avoid the danger of creating a total vacuum inside the chamber, this purification was never done all at once, but gradually. The valve was opened for only a few seconds and then closed, until one or two strokes from the condenser pump replaced the expelled air. For experimental purposes, I had placed the cat and kittens in a small basket, hanging it outside the car from a button at the bottom, right next to the valve, where I could feed them whenever necessary. I did this at some risk before sealing the chamber, by reaching under the car with one of the previously mentioned poles, which had a hook attached to it.

“By the time I had finished these arrangements and filled the chamber as described, it was ten minutes to nine. Throughout the time I was working, I suffered terrible distress from difficulty breathing, and I bitterly regretted the negligence, or rather the recklessness, of putting off such an important task until the last moment. But having finally finished, I soon began to enjoy the benefits of my invention. Once again, I breathed with perfect freedom and ease—and why shouldn't I? I was also pleasantly surprised to find that the intense pain that had been tormenting me was largely gone. A slight headache, along with a feeling of fullness or swelling in my wrists, ankles, and throat, was nearly all I had left to complain about. It seemed clear that most of the discomfort caused by the loss of atmospheric pressure had worn off, just as I had expected, and that much of the pain I endured over the last two hours was actually due to poor respiration.

“At twenty minutes to nine—that is, shortly before I sealed the chamber—the mercury in the barometer, which I previously noted was an extended model, reached its limit or bottomed out. It indicated that I had reached an altitude of 132,000 feet, or twenty-five miles. Consequently, I was surveying an area of the earth’s surface equal to no less than one three-hundred-and-twentieth of its total area. By nine o’clock, I had lost sight of land to the east, but not before realizing that the balloon was drifting rapidly to the north-northwest. The curvature of the ocean below me was very apparent, although my view was frequently interrupted by the masses of clouds drifting back and forth.

After repeating this process several times, the chamber was finally filled with air suitable for breathing. However, in such a small space, the air would quickly become stale and unusable from being breathed repeatedly. It was then released through a small valve at the bottom of the car, as the dense air naturally sank into the thinner atmosphere below. To avoid creating a complete vacuum inside the chamber, this purification was never done all at once, but gradually. The valve was opened for only a few seconds and then closed, until the condenser pump had replaced the ejected air with a few strokes. For the sake of the experiment, I had placed the cat and her kittens in a small basket, hanging it outside the car from a button near the valve, where I could feed them whenever necessary. I did this at some risk before sealing the chamber, using one of the previously mentioned poles with a hook attached to reach under the car.

By the time I finished these arrangements and filled the chamber as described, it was ten minutes before nine o’clock. While I was working, I suffered terribly from difficulty breathing, and I bitterly regretted the negligence, or rather the recklessness, of waiting until the last moment to handle such an important task. Once I finished, however, I quickly began to enjoy the benefits of my invention. I breathed with perfect freedom and ease again—and why shouldn't I? I was also pleasantly surprised to find that the intense pains that had been tormenting me were largely gone. A slight headache, along with a feeling of fullness or swelling in my wrists, ankles, and throat, was nearly all I had left to complain about. It seemed clear that most of the discomfort caused by the loss of atmospheric pressure had faded, just as I had expected, and that much of the pain I endured over the last two hours was actually due to poor breathing.

At twenty minutes before nine—shortly before I sealed the chamber—the mercury in the barometer, which was a special extended model, reached its limit. It indicated that I had reached an altitude of 132,000 feet, or twenty-five miles. At that point, I could see an area of the earth equal to one three-hundred-and-twentieth of its entire surface. By nine o’clock, I had lost sight of land to the east, but not before noticing that the balloon was drifting rapidly to the north-northwest. The curve of the ocean below me was very clear, even though my view was often blocked by drifting clouds.

I leaned, nevertheless, toward the latter view. The icy border toward the north was becoming increasingly visible. The temperature was not nearly as severe. Nothing significant took place, and I spent the day reading, having made sure to bring a supply of books along.

“April 5th. Witnessed the strange occurrence of the sun ascending while almost the entire observable surface of the globe remained shrouded in shadow. Eventually, however, the illumination extended everywhere, and I once more observed the icy boundary to the north. It was now quite clear, appearing significantly darker in shade than the surrounding sea. I was clearly drawing nearer to it, and with significant speed. I imagined I could once again make out a stretch of land to the east, and another to the west, though I could not be sure. The weather was mild. Nothing of note transpired throughout the day. I retired to bed early.

“April 6th. I was astonished to discover the icy rim at a quite reasonable distance, with a vast expanse of the same substance extending toward the northern horizon. It was clear that if the balloon maintained its current path, it would soon reach the Frozen Ocean, and I now felt little doubt that I would eventually behold the Pole. Throughout the day, I continued to approach the ice. As night fell, the extent of my horizon expanded suddenly and significantly, surely because the earth is an oblate spheroid and I was reaching the flattened areas near the Arctic circle. When darkness finally descended, I went to bed feeling quite anxious, worried I might drift over the object of my intense curiosity while lacking the chance to study it.

“April 7th. I woke early, and, to my immense delight, finally saw what I could only conclude was the northern Pole itself. It was there, without question, directly below me; but, unfortunately, I had ascended to such a great height that nothing could be observed with precision. Indeed, judging by the sequence of figures representing my various altitudes at different times between six A.M. on April 2nd and twenty minutes before nine A.M. that same day (when the barometer ceased functioning), one might reasonably conclude that the balloon had, by four A.M. on April 7th, attained an altitude of at least 7,254 miles above sea level. This height might seem vast, but the calculation used likely produced a result far lower than the reality. Regardless, I certainly viewed the entire major diameter of the earth; the whole northern hemisphere was spread out below me like an orthographic map, and the great circle of the equator served as the edge of my horizon. Your Excellencies can, however, easily grasp that the restricted, previously uncharted regions within the Arctic circle, though located directly beneath me and thus seen without any foreshortening, were still, in themselves, relatively too small and too far from my vantage point to allow for any very detailed inspection.

I leaned, however, toward the latter opinion. The ice shelf to the north was becoming increasingly visible. The cold was by no means as severe. Nothing of importance occurred, and I spent the day reading, having made sure to bring plenty of books with me.

“April 5th. I witnessed the strange phenomenon of the sun rising while nearly the entire visible surface of the earth remained shrouded in darkness. Eventually, however, the light spread everywhere, and I once again saw the line of ice to the north. It was now very distinct and appeared much darker than the ocean waters. I was clearly approaching it, and with great speed. I imagined I could again make out a strip of land to the east, and another to the west, but I could not be certain. The weather was moderate. Nothing of any consequence happened during the day. I went to bed early.

“April 6th. I was surprised to find the ice shelf at a very moderate distance, with an immense field of the same material stretching away toward the northern horizon. It was clear that if the balloon kept its current course, it would soon arrive above the Frozen Ocean, and I now had little doubt that I would eventually see the Pole. Throughout the day, I continued to draw closer to the ice. Toward night, my horizon expanded suddenly and significantly, undoubtedly because the earth is an oblate spheroid and I was arriving above the flattened regions near the Arctic Circle. When darkness finally overtook me, I went to bed in great anxiety, fearing I might pass over the object of so much curiosity when I would have no chance to observe it.

“April 7th. I rose early and, to my great joy, finally beheld what I could not hesitate to assume was the North Pole itself. It was there, without a doubt, directly beneath my feet; but, alas! I had ascended to such a vast distance that nothing could be discerned with accuracy. Indeed, judging from the progression of the numbers indicating my various altitudes at different times between 6:00 A.M. on April 2nd and 8:40 A.M. that same day (when the barometer ran down), it could be fairly inferred that the balloon had now, at 4:00 A.M. on April 7th, reached a height of certainly no less than 7,254 miles above sea level. This elevation may seem immense, but the estimate used for the calculation likely produced a result far lower than the truth. At any rate, I undoubtedly beheld the earth’s entire major diameter; the whole northern hemisphere lay beneath me like an orthographic map, and the great circle of the equator itself formed the boundary of my horizon. Your Excellencies may, however, readily imagine that the confined regions within the Arctic Circle that remain unexplored, although situated directly beneath me and therefore seen without any appearance of foreshortening, were still, in themselves, too small and too far from my vantage point to allow for any very accurate examination.

I leaned, however, toward the latter opinion. The ice rim to the north was becoming increasingly visible. The cold was by no means as intense. Nothing of importance occurred, and I spent the day reading, having made sure to bring plenty of books with me.

“April 5th. I witnessed the strange phenomenon of the sun rising while nearly the entire visible surface of the earth remained in darkness. Eventually, however, the light spread over everything, and I again saw the line of ice to the north. It was now very clear and appeared much darker than the ocean water. I was clearly approaching it quite rapidly. I imagined I could again distinguish a strip of land to the east and another to the west, but I could not be certain. The weather was moderate. Nothing of any consequence happened during the day. I went to bed early.

“April 6th. I was surprised to find the ice rim at a very moderate distance, with an immense field of the same material stretching away toward the northern horizon. It was clear that if the balloon kept its current course, it would soon arrive above the Frozen Ocean, and I now had little doubt that I would eventually see the Pole. Throughout the day, I continued to draw closer to the ice. Toward night, my horizon suddenly and significantly expanded, undoubtedly because the earth is shaped like an oblate spheroid and I was arriving above the flattened regions near the Arctic Circle. When darkness finally overtook me, I went to bed in great anxiety, fearing I might pass over the object of my curiosity without having the chance to observe it.

This is indeed the situation. I possess a great deal—a vast amount—that I would be delighted to share. I have much to relate regarding the planet’s climate; its remarkable shifts between heat and cold, with two weeks of intense, scorching sun followed by a fortnight of arctic freezing; the continuous movement of moisture, distilled as if in a vacuum, from the area directly under the sun to the point furthest away; the shifting belt of flowing water; the inhabitants themselves; their habits, traditions, and government; their unique physical makeup; their lack of beauty; their missing ears, which are unnecessary in such a distinct atmosphere; their resulting inability to use or understand speech; their unique way of communicating without it; the mysterious link between every lunar individual and a specific person on Earth—a connection similar to, and reliant upon, the relationship between the planet and its moons, through which the fates of those on one world are tied to the lives of those on the other; and, most importantly, if it pleases your Excellencies, the dark and terrifying secrets hidden in the moon’s outer regions—areas that, due to the near-perfect synchronization of the satellite’s rotation with its orbit around the Earth, have never been, and by divine grace never will be, exposed to human telescopes. I would gladly provide all this and much more. However, to be concise, I require compensation. I am longing to return to my home and family; therefore, in exchange for further information—and considering the insight I can provide into various vital areas of physical and metaphysical study—I must request, through your honorable assembly, a pardon for the crime I committed by killing my creditors when I left Rotterdam. This is the purpose of this letter. The bearer, a lunar resident whom I have convinced and trained to act as my messenger to Earth, will wait for your Excellencies’ decision and bring the pardon back to me, should it be granted.

“I have the honor to remain, etc., your Excellencies’ most humble servant,

“HANS PFAALL.”

It is reported that upon reading this highly unusual letter, Professor Rub-a-dub was so shocked he dropped his pipe, while Mynheer Superbus Von Underduk removed his glasses, cleaned them, and put them away, momentarily forgetting his own dignity to spin around on his heels three times in sheer amazement and wonder. There was no question about it—the pardon would be secured. At least, that is what Professor Rub-a-dub vowed with a firm oath, and it was the eventual conclusion of the distinguished Von Underduk as he took his colleague’s arm and, remaining silent, hurried home to consider the necessary steps.

When I reached the cabin, I knocked as I usually did. Receiving no answer, I located the hidden key, opened the door, and stepped inside. A pleasant fire burned in the hearth, which was an unexpected and welcome sight. I removed my overcoat, settled into an armchair near the popping logs, and waited for my hosts to return.

They returned shortly after nightfall and greeted me warmly. Jupiter, wearing a wide grin, hurried to cook some marsh-hens for our meal. Legrand was in one of his typical states of intense excitement. He had discovered a previously unknown bivalve, representing a new genus, and had also captured, with Jupiter’s help, a beetle he believed to be entirely unique, though he wanted my assessment of it the following day.

“Why not tonight?” I inquired, warming my hands by the flames and wishing the entire species of beetles were elsewhere.

“If only I had known you were coming!” Legrand exclaimed. “It has been so long since we met; how could I have guessed you would visit tonight of all nights? On my way home, I ran into Lieutenant G— from the fort and foolishly lent him the insect. You won't be able to see it until morning. Stay the night, and I will have Jup fetch it at sunrise. It is the most beautiful thing in existence!”

“What? The sunrise?”

“Don't be silly! No, the beetle. It is a striking golden color, roughly the size of a large hickory nut, with two jet-black spots near one end of its back and a longer one at the other. The antennae are—”

“There ain't no tin in him, Massa Will, I keep telling you,” Jupiter interrupted. “The bug is a gold bug, solid, every bit of him, inside and out, except for his wings. I’ve never felt a bug half so heavy in my life.”

“Well, suppose it is, Jup,” Legrand answered, with more intensity than I thought necessary. “Does that mean you should let the birds burn? The color,” he said, turning to me, “is truly enough to justify Jupiter’s theory. You have never seen a more brilliant metallic shine than these scales produce, though you cannot judge for yourself until tomorrow. In the meantime, I can describe its form.” He sat at a small table holding a pen and ink, but no paper. He searched a drawer, finding nothing.

“It doesn't matter,” he finally said. “This will do.” He pulled a piece of what looked like grimy foolscap from his vest pocket and sketched a rough drawing with his pen. I remained by the fire, still feeling cold, while he worked. Once finished, he handed the sketch to me without standing. As I took it, a deep growl sounded, followed by scratching at the door. Jupiter opened it, and Legrand’s large Newfoundland dog bounded in, jumped onto my shoulders, and covered me with affection, as I had been kind to him during my earlier visits.

When I reached the hut, I knocked as I usually did. Receiving no answer, I looked for the key where I knew it was hidden, unlocked the door, and went inside. A nice fire was burning in the hearth. It was a surprise, and certainly a welcome one. I took off my overcoat, sat in an armchair by the crackling logs, and waited patiently for my hosts to arrive.

Shortly after dark, they returned and gave me a very warm welcome. Jupiter, grinning from ear to ear, bustled about to prepare some marsh hens for dinner. Legrand was in one of his moods—what else could I call them?—of intense excitement. I feared that the persistent weight of adversity had finally disturbed my companion’s sanity. Consequently, I resolved without delay to join the black man. Upon arriving at the dock, I observed a scythe and a trio of shovels, all seemingly brand new, resting inside the vessel we were to board. “What is the purpose of these, Jup?” I asked. “That’s a scythe, master, and a spade.” “Quite right; but why are they here?” “They are the tools Master Will insisted I purchase for him in town, and I had to pay a devil of a price for them.” “But what, in the name of all that is strange, does your ‘Master Will’ intend to do with scythes and shovels?” “That is more than I can say, and I’ll be damned if I don’t think it’s more than he knows, too. But it all comes down to the bug.” Realizing I would gain no clarity from Jupiter, whose mind seemed entirely consumed by “the bug,” I boarded the boat and set off. A steady, favorable wind carried us quickly to the small inlet north of Fort Moultrie, and a two-mile trek led us to the cabin. We reached our destination around three in the afternoon. Legrand had been waiting for us with intense anticipation. He seized my hand with a nervous intensity that unsettled me and confirmed my growing fears. His face was deathly pale, and his sunken eyes shone with a strange, feverish brightness. After asking about his well-being, I inquired, unsure of what else to say, if he had secured the scarabæus from Lieutenant G——. “Oh, yes,” he answered, flushing deeply, “I retrieved it from him the following morning. Nothing could induce me to give up that scarabæus. Are you aware that Jupiter is entirely correct about it?” “In what sense?” I questioned, feeling a heavy sense of dread. “In believing it to be a beetle of genuine gold.” He spoke with such intense gravity that I was profoundly disturbed. “This insect will secure my fortune,” he added with a victorious grin, “and restore my family’s estate. Is it any surprise that I value it so highly? Since Fate has seen fit to grant it to me, I need only utilize it correctly to reach the gold it points toward. Jupiter, fetch me that scarabæus!” “What! The bug, master? I would rather not bother that bug—you must get it yourself.” Legrand then stood with a solemn, dignified manner and retrieved the beetle for me from a glass display case. It was a stunning scarabæus, unknown to science at the time—naturally a significant discovery from a biological perspective. It featured two circular, dark spots near one end of its back, and a single elongated one near the other. Its shell was exceptionally hard and lustrous, appearing exactly like polished gold. The insect’s weight was quite extraordinary, and, all things considered, I could scarcely fault Jupiter for his assessment; yet, I could not for the life of me understand why Legrand agreed with him.

I feared that the relentless weight of misfortune had finally unhinged my friend’s mind. Without hesitation, I prepared to accompany the man. Upon reaching the wharf, I noticed a scythe and three spades, all seemingly new, lying in the bottom of the boat we were about to board. "What is the meaning of all this, Jup?" I asked. "It’s a scythe, master, and spades." "True, but why are they here?" "They are the scythe and spades that Master Will insisted I buy for him in town, and it cost a devil of a lot of money to get them." "But what, in the name of all that is mysterious, is your 'Master Will' going to do with scythes and spades?" "That’s more than I know, and the devil take me if I don’t believe it’s more than he knows, too. But it all comes from that bug." Finding no satisfaction from Jupiter, whose entire focus seemed consumed by "the bug," I stepped into the boat and set sail. This thought implied a certain significance—a relevance—to the skull. I also noticed the shape of the paper. Even though one corner had been damaged by some mishap, it was evident that its original shape was rectangular. It was precisely the kind of scrap that might have been selected for a note—for a record of something intended to be kept in mind and guarded with care.

“Ah, that is where the entire enigma hinges; though the secret, at this juncture, was not very hard for me to unravel. My logic was sound and could lead to only one conclusion. I reasoned, for instance, in this way: When I sketched the scarab, no skull was visible on the parchment. Once I finished the drawing, I handed it to you and watched you closely until you gave it back. Therefore, you did not draw the skull, and no one else was there to do it. Thus, it was not created by human hands. And yet, it was created.

“At this point in my thinking, I tried to recall, and did recall, with perfect clarity, every event that took place around that time. The air was cold (a fortunate and rare coincidence!), and a fire was burning in the fireplace. I was warm from exertion and sat by the table. You, however, had pulled a chair near the hearth. Just as I put the parchment into your hand, and as you were examining it, Wolf, the Newfoundland dog, came in and jumped onto your shoulders. With your left hand you petted him and held him back, while your right hand, holding the paper, dropped loosely between your knees, very near the flames. For a second, I thought the fire had ignited it and was about to warn you, but before I could say anything, you had pulled it away and were busy looking at it. When I weighed all these details, I did not doubt for an instant that heat had been the force that revealed the skull I saw drawn upon the paper. You know well that chemical substances exist, and have existed for ages, that allow one to write on paper or vellum so that the markings appear only when exposed to heat. Zaffre, dissolved in aqua regia and thinned with four times its weight in water, is sometimes used; it produces a green hue. The regulus of cobalt, dissolved in spirit of nitre, creates a red. These colors fade after the material cools, but reappear when heat is applied once more.

It was exactly the kind of slip that might be chosen for a note—a record of something to be long remembered and carefully kept.

“But,” I interrupted, “you say the skull was not on the parchment when you drew the beetle. How then do you connect the boat and the skull, since the latter, by your own admission, must have been drawn (God only knows how or by whom) after you sketched the beetle?”

“Ah, that is the heart of the mystery; although I had little trouble solving this part. My logic was sound and could lead to only one result. I reasoned like this: When I drew the beetle, no skull was visible on the parchment. When I finished the drawing, I gave it to you and watched you closely until you returned it. Therefore, you did not draw the skull, and no one else was there to do it. So, it was not done by human hands. And yet, it was done.

“At this point, I tried to remember, and I did remember clearly, every event from that time. The weather was chilly (a rare and lucky accident!), and a fire was burning in the hearth. I was warm from exercise and sat near the table. You, however, had pulled a chair close to the fireplace. Just as I handed you the parchment, and as you were looking at it, Wolf, the Newfoundland dog, came in and jumped on your shoulders. With your left hand, you petted him to keep him off, while your right hand, holding the parchment, dropped loosely between your knees, very close to the fire. For a moment, I thought the flames had caught it and was about to warn you, but before I could speak, you pulled it away and continued your inspection. Considering all these details, I did not doubt for a moment that heat had revealed the skull I saw on the parchment. You know that chemical substances have existed for a long time that allow one to write on paper or vellum so that the marks only appear when exposed to heat. Zaffre, dissolved in aqua regia and diluted with four times its weight in water, is sometimes used; it produces a green tint. Cobalt, dissolved in nitric acid, produces a red. These colors disappear after the material cools, but they reappear when heat is applied again.

Following this clue, I divided it this way: ‘A good glass in the Bishop’s hostel in the Devil’s seat—forty-one degrees and thirteen minutes—northeast and by north—main branch seventh limb east side—shoot from the left eye of the death’s-head—a bee-line from the tree through the shot fifty feet out.’”

“Even with this division,” I remarked, “I remain confused.”

I climbed to the top, then felt uncertain about my next move.

Quite correct.

There are.

I must concede all this. Nevertheless, there is an endless supply of mud shacks and wretched hovels. We cannot avoid noticing the wealth of grime in every gutter, and, were it not for the overwhelming scents of pagan incense, I am certain we would encounter a truly unbearable odor. Have you ever seen streets so painfully cramped, or buildings so impossibly high? What a gloom their shadows throw upon the earth! It is fortunate that the hanging lanterns in those perpetual colonnades remain lit all day; otherwise, we would suffer the darkness of Egypt during her period of ruin.

“It is surely a bizarre location! What is the purpose of that peculiar structure over there? Look! It rises higher than all the rest and sits to the east of what I assume is the king’s residence!”

That is the new Temple of the Sun, who is worshipped in Syria by the name Elah Gabalah. In time, a highly infamous Roman Emperor will establish this cult in Rome, and from it take the name Heliogabalus. I imagine you would enjoy a glimpse of the temple’s god. You need not gaze at the sky; his Sunship is not located there—at least, not the Sunship the Syrians revere. That deity resides inside that building. He is honored in the form of a massive stone pillar ending at the top in a cone or pyramid, which serves to represent Fire.

A few are charlatans. Others belong more specifically to the class of philosophers. The majority, however—especially those striking the commoners with sticks—are the chief courtiers of the palace, performing, as they are obligated to do, some praiseworthy jest of the monarch’s.

Frightening, if you insist; but not in the slightest bit hazardous. Each creature, if you care to notice, is trailing quite calmly behind its owner. THE MURDERS IN THE RUE MORGUE

What melody the Syrens performed, or what identity Achilles took when concealing himself among the women, while perplexing, are not entirely beyond speculation.

—Sir Thomas Browne.

The intellectual traits described as analytical are, in their own nature, difficult to analyze. We recognize them solely through their consequences. We understand, among other facts, that they provide their owner, when possessed to a high degree, with the most intense satisfaction. Just as a powerful man takes pride in his bodily strength, finding joy in activities that engage his muscles, so the analyst takes pride in the mental vigor that unravels complexities. He finds gratification in even the most minor tasks that utilize his skill. He enjoys puzzles, riddles, and secret symbols, demonstrating in his answers a level of sharpness that seems supernatural to the average observer. His conclusions, reached through the very heart and spirit of systematic thought, possess the distinct appearance of pure intuition.

The power of resolution is perhaps greatly strengthened by the study of mathematics, particularly that supreme branch which, unfairly and solely because of its reverse procedures, has been labeled analysis as if it were the only form. Yet, to perform calculations is not the same as to analyze. A chess player, for instance, performs the former without attempting the latter. Consequently, the game of chess, regarding its influence on the mind, is frequently misinterpreted. I am not composing a formal essay, but merely introducing a rather strange story with some scattered remarks; I will, therefore, take the opportunity to claim that the superior faculties of the reflective mind are more effectively and usefully engaged by the modest game of draughts than by the intricate triviality of chess. In the latter, where the pieces possess strange and varied movements, along with differing and shifting values, what is merely complicated is often wrongly assumed to be deep—a common mistake. The attention is here engaged with great intensity. If it wavers for a moment, a mistake occurs that leads to damage or loss. Since the potential moves are not only numerous but intricate, the likelihood of such errors increases; in nine instances out of ten, it is the player with greater focus rather than the one with more sharpness who triumphs. In draughts, conversely, where the moves are singular and possess little variety, the chances for carelessness are reduced, and since focus is left relatively free, any advantages gained by either side are won through superior insight. To be less theoretical, consider a game of draughts where the pieces are limited to four kings, and where, naturally, no mistakes are anticipated. It is clear that in this case, victory can be determined (assuming the players are matched) only by some sophisticated maneuver, the product of a significant mental effort. Lacking standard options, the analyst adopts the mindset of his rival, aligns himself with that perspective, and frequently perceives in an instant the only ways—sometimes indeed quite simple ones—by which he might lure the opponent into a mistake or force a miscalculation.

—Sir Thomas Browne.

The mental traits described as analytical are, in themselves, difficult to analyze. We only recognize them through their effects. We know, among other things, that they are always a source of intense enjoyment to those who possess them in abundance. Just as a strong person takes pride in their physical ability, delighting in exercises that engage their muscles, the analyst takes pride in the mental activity that disentangles problems. They find pleasure in even the most trivial tasks that put their talent to use. They are fond of enigmas, riddles, and hieroglyphics, displaying in their solutions a level of insight that seems supernatural to the average observer. Their results, achieved through the very essence of method, truly appear to be the product of intuition.

The faculty of resolution is perhaps strengthened by mathematical study, especially by that highest branch which, unfairly and merely because of its reverse operations, has been called analysis by way of distinction. Yet, to calculate is not the same as to analyze. A chess player, for instance, does the former without attempting the latter. It follows that the game of chess, in its effect on the mind, is widely misunderstood. I am not writing a formal essay here, but simply prefacing a somewhat unusual story with some random observations. I will, therefore, take this opportunity to assert that the higher powers of the reflective intellect are more effectively and usefully challenged by the modest game of checkers than by all the elaborate frivolity of chess. In the latter, where the pieces have different and strange movements with various, changing values, complexity is often mistaken for depth—a common error. Attention is called into play quite forcefully here. If it flags for an instant, a mistake is made that leads to damage or defeat. Because the possible moves are not only numerous but intricate, the chances for such oversights are multiplied; in nine cases out of ten, it is the more focused player, rather than the more acute one, who wins. In checkers, by contrast, where the moves are uniform and have little variation, the chances of oversight are reduced. Since mere attention is left relatively unemployed, any advantages gained by either party are the result of superior insight. To be less abstract, let us imagine a game of checkers where the pieces are reduced to four kings, and where, naturally, no oversight is expected. It is obvious that here, victory can be decided—assuming the players are equal—only by some sophisticated move, the result of a strong exertion of the intellect. Deprived of ordinary resources, the analyst enters into the spirit of their opponent, identifies with them, and frequently sees at a glance the only methods—sometimes indeed absurdly simple ones—by which they may lure the opponent into error or force them into a miscalculation.

The following day, the newspaper provided these supplementary details.

“The Rue Morgue Tragedy.—Numerous individuals have been questioned concerning this highly unusual and terrifying event” [The term ‘affaire’ does not yet carry the trivial connotation in France that it does for us], “yet nothing has emerged to clarify the situation. “Many other neighbors provided similar testimony. No one was mentioned as a frequent visitor to the house. It was unknown whether Madame L. and her daughter had any living relatives. The front window shutters were rarely opened. The rear ones were always closed, except for those in the large back room on the fourth floor. The house was in good condition and not very old.

“Isidore Musèt, a police officer, testifies that he was called to the house around three o’clock in the morning and found twenty or thirty people at the gate, trying to get in. He eventually forced it open with a bayonet, not a crowbar. He had little trouble opening it because it was a double gate and was not bolted at the top or bottom. The shrieking continued until the gate was forced, at which point it suddenly stopped. The sounds seemed to be the screams of someone—or several people—in great agony; they were loud and prolonged, not short and sharp. The witness led the way upstairs. Upon reaching the first landing, he heard two voices in a loud, angry argument—one a gruff voice, the other much shriller—a very strange voice.”

“We believe there is not yet the slightest clue to this horrible mystery.”

The next day’s newspaper provided these additional details:

“The Tragedy in the Rue Morgue.—Many people have been questioned regarding this extraordinary and frightening event” [the word ‘affaire’ does not yet carry the same light meaning in France that it does for us], “but nothing has come to light to explain it. We provide below all the relevant testimony gathered.

“Pauline Dubourg, a laundress, states that she has known both deceased women for three years, having done their laundry during that time. The old lady and her daughter seemed to have a good relationship and were very affectionate toward each other. They always paid well. She could not speak to their way of life or their income. She believed Madame L. earned money by telling fortunes. It was rumored that she had savings. She never encountered anyone else in the house when she picked up or dropped off the laundry. She was certain they employed no servants. There appeared to be no furniture in any part of the building except on the fourth floor.

“Pierre Moreau, a tobacconist, states that he has sold small amounts of tobacco and snuff to Madame L’Espanaye for nearly four years. He was born in the neighborhood and has always lived there. The deceased and her daughter had occupied the house where the bodies were found for more than six years. It was previously occupied by a jeweler, who rented the upper rooms to various people. The house belonged to Madame L. She became unhappy with how her tenant treated the property, so she moved in herself and refused to rent out any part of it. The old lady was somewhat senile. The witness had seen the daughter only five or six times over the six years. The two lived a very private life and were rumored to have money. He had heard neighbors say that Madame L. told fortunes, though he did not believe it. He had never seen anyone enter the door except for the old lady and her daughter, a porter once or twice, and a doctor eight or ten times.

The front window shutters were rarely opened. The rear shutters were always closed, except for those in the large back room on the fourth floor. The house was in good condition and not very old.

The witness led the way upstairs. Upon reaching the first landing, he heard two voices in a loud, angry argument—one was a gruff voice, and the other was much shriller—a very strange voice.”

I could only concur with the rest of Paris in viewing them as a riddle that defied solution. I perceived no way to track down the killer.

“We must not evaluate the methods,” Dupin remarked, “by this superficial investigation. The police of Paris, though highly praised for their sharpness, are merely sly. Their actions lack any system beyond the needs of the immediate moment. They put on a grand display of effort, yet these steps are often so poorly suited to their goals that they remind one of Monsieur Jourdain asking for his dressing gown to better hear the music. Their successes are sometimes startling, but they usually stem from basic hard work and energy. When those traits fail, their plans fall apart. Vidocq, for instance, was a persistent man who made good guesses. Yet, lacking disciplined thought, he constantly blundered through the sheer intensity of his focus. He blurred his sight by pressing the object too near. He might perceive one or two details with extreme clarity, but in doing so, he inevitably lost sight of the entire picture. Therefore, it is possible to be overly profound. Truth does not always reside in a deep well. Indeed, regarding the most significant knowledge, I suspect it is consistently found on the surface. The depth exists in the valleys where we search, rather than on the mountain peaks where it is actually discovered. The nature and origins of this mistake are well illustrated by observing the stars. To view a star by glancing at it—to look at it indirectly by turning the outer edges of the retina toward it, which are more sensitive to faint light than the center—is to see the star clearly and appreciate its brilliance, a brilliance that fades the more directly we stare at it. More light rays strike the eye in the latter instance, but the former allows for a more refined perception. Through excessive depth, we confuse and weaken our reasoning; it is even possible to make Venus disappear from the sky by staring at it too intently, too steadily, or too directly.

“Regarding these killings, let us conduct our own investigation before forming a judgment. The inquiry will provide us with some entertainment,” [I considered this a strange word to use, but I remained silent] “and furthermore, Le Bon once did me a favor for which I am grateful. We shall visit the scene ourselves. I am acquainted with G——, the Prefect of Police, and will easily secure the required authorization.”

The authorization was granted, and we traveled immediately to the Rue Morgue. It is one of those dreary streets situated between the Rue Richelieu and the Rue St. Roch. We arrived late in the day, as that district was quite far from our own. The residence was easily located, as a crowd of people remained on the opposite side of the street, staring at the shut windows with aimless curiosity.

I could only agree with all of Paris in viewing them as an unsolvable mystery. I saw no way that it would be possible to track down the murderer.

The aroma had not vanished for a single moment. Every connection in the sequence remained unbroken. I had pursued the mystery to its final conclusion, and that conclusion was the nail. It possessed, I maintain, every visual characteristic of its counterpart in the opposite window; yet this observation was entirely meaningless, however convincing it might appear, when weighed against the fact that the investigation ended precisely here. I remarked that there had to be a defect regarding the nail. Upon touching it, the head, along with a small portion of the shaft, detached into my hand. The remaining shank stayed lodged within the hole where it had snapped. The break was aged, as evidenced by the rust coating its edges, and had clearly been caused by a hammer strike that had partially driven the head into the top of the lower window frame. I meticulously returned the head to the depression from which I had removed it, restoring the appearance of an intact nail so that the crack was hidden. By activating the spring, I lifted the sash slightly; the head ascended with it, staying securely in place. I shut the window, and the illusion of a solid nail was restored.

“The mystery, to this extent, was solved. The murderer had fled through the window overlooking the bed. Whether by accident or design, the sash had fallen upon his departure and locked via the spring; the police had mistakenly identified this spring mechanism as a nail, leading them to believe further investigation was redundant.

“The subsequent issue concerns the method of descent. I had resolved this matter during our stroll around the structure. A lightning rod stands roughly five and a half feet from the window in question. It would have been unfeasible for anyone to reach the window from this rod, let alone gain entry. I noted, however, that the fourth-story shutters were of a specific style known to Parisian builders as ferrades—a design seldom used today, though common on historic homes in Lyons and Bordeaux. These shutters resemble standard doors, except the bottom half features a lattice or trellis pattern, providing a sturdy grip. These particular shutters measure at least three and a half feet wide. When we viewed them from the rear, they were both partially open, positioned at right angles to the wall. It is likely the police inspected the back of the building, but if they viewed these ferrades edge-on, they would have failed to notice their significant width or neglected to consider its importance. Having convinced themselves that no exit was possible from this side, they would have conducted only a superficial inspection.

The scent had never been lost for a moment. There was no break in the chain of evidence. I had traced the secret to its final conclusion—and that conclusion was the nail. It looked, I admit, exactly like its counterpart in the other window, but this fact was completely irrelevant when compared to the realization that the trail ended right here. 'There must be something wrong,' I said, 'with the nail.' I touched it, and the head, along with about a quarter-inch of the shank, came off in my fingers. The remainder of the shank stayed in the hole where it had snapped. The break was old, as the edges were covered in rust, and it had clearly been caused by a hammer blow that had partially embedded the head of the nail into the top of the lower sash. I carefully put the head back into the indentation where I had found it, and it looked like a perfect nail again—the crack was invisible. Pressing the spring, I gently raised the sash a few inches; the head rose with it, staying firmly in place. I closed the window, and the appearance of a solid nail was restored.

'The mystery, so far, was solved. The killer had escaped through the window overlooking the bed. Falling shut on its own as he left, or perhaps closed intentionally, it had locked via the spring; the police had mistaken the engagement of this spring for the nail, and thus concluded that no further investigation was needed.

'The next question concerns how he climbed down. I had already settled this in my mind while walking around the building with you. A lightning rod runs about five and a half feet from the window in question. It would have been impossible for anyone to reach the window from that rod, let alone enter it. I noticed, however, that the fourth-story shutters were of a specific type Parisian carpenters call ferrades—a style rarely used today, but often seen on old mansions in Lyons and Bordeaux. They are shaped like a standard door, except the bottom half is latticed, providing an excellent grip for the hands. In this case, these shutters are at least three and a half feet wide. When we saw them from the back of the house, they were both about half open, standing at right angles to the wall. The police likely examined the back of the building, but if they looked at these ferrades edge-on, they would not have noticed their significant width, or at least failed to consider it. Having decided that no one could have escaped that way, they naturally gave the area only a brief glance.'

However, I noticed that the fourth-story shutters were a specific type that Parisian carpenters call ferrades—a style rarely used today but common on old houses in Lyons and Bordeaux. We have no intention of harming you. I give you my word as a gentleman and a Frenchman that we mean you no injury. I am fully aware that you did not commit the horrors in the Rue Morgue. Nevertheless, it is impossible to deny that you are involved to some degree. Based on my previous remarks, you must realize that I possess sources of information regarding this case that you could never have imagined. The situation is this: you have done nothing that you could have prevented, and certainly nothing that makes you guilty. You did not even commit robbery, even though you could have done so without consequence. You have nothing to hide, and no reason to keep secrets. Conversely, you are obligated by every standard of honor to reveal everything you know. An innocent person is currently jailed, accused of a crime for which you can identify the true culprit.

The sailor had largely regained his composure while Dupin spoke, yet his initial bold demeanor had vanished entirely.

"So help me God!" he exclaimed after a short silence, "I will tell you everything I know about this matter, though I do not expect you to believe half of it; I would be a fool to think otherwise. Still, I am innocent, and I will confess everything even if it costs me my life."

His account was essentially as follows. He had recently traveled to the Indian Archipelago. A group he belonged to went ashore at Borneo and ventured into the interior for a pleasure trip. He and a companion had caught the orangutan. When his companion passed away, the creature became his sole property. After significant difficulty caused by the animal's stubborn aggression during the journey home, he finally managed to secure it in his Paris residence. To avoid drawing unwanted attention from his neighbors, he kept it hidden until it could heal from a foot injury caused by a ship splinter. His final plan was to sell the beast.

Returning home from a night of drinking on the morning of the murders, he discovered the animal in his bedroom, having escaped from an adjacent closet where it had been kept, as he believed, securely locked. Holding a razor and covered in lather, it sat before a mirror, trying to shave, having surely observed its owner performing the task through the closet keyhole. Frightened by the sight of such a dangerous tool held by a ferocious animal capable of using it, the man was momentarily paralyzed. However, he was used to calming the creature, even during its most violent outbursts, by using a whip, and he turned to this now. Upon seeing the whip, the orangutan immediately leaped through the bedroom door, ran down the stairs, and escaped into the street through an unfortunately open window.

The Frenchman pursued it in despair; the ape, still clutching the razor, would stop now and then to look back and gesture at its pursuer until the man had almost caught up to it.

The official notice announcing this bounty guaranteed a complete pardon to any partner in the crime who might testify against his associates. Furthermore, a private notice from a group of citizens was attached to these posters, promising an extra ten thousand francs beyond the sum pledged by the Prefecture. Consequently, the total reward reached thirty thousand francs, a truly remarkable amount given the victim's modest status and the common occurrence of such brutal acts in major urban centers.

Everyone assumed the mystery surrounding this homicide would be solved at once. However, while a few arrests were made that seemed promising, no evidence emerged to connect the suspects to the crime, and they were quickly released. Surprisingly, three weeks had elapsed since the body was found without any clarity, and even the rumors of these events, which had so disturbed the public, had not yet reached Dupin or me. We were occupied with studies that required our total focus, and nearly a month had passed since we had ventured out, welcomed a guest, or done more than skim the main political stories in a daily journal. The initial news of the killing was delivered to us by G— himself. He visited us on the afternoon of July 13, 18—, and stayed until deep into the night. He was frustrated by his inability to track down the killers. His standing—as he remarked with a distinctly Parisian manner—was in jeopardy. His very reputation was involved. The public was watching him, and he claimed there was no sacrifice he would not make to solve the enigma. He finished a rather odd address by praising what he called Dupin's intuition, and he offered him a straightforward and undoubtedly generous proposal, the specific details of which I cannot reveal, though they are irrelevant to my story.

My friend deflected the praise as best he could, yet he immediately agreed to the offer, even though its benefits were entirely conditional. With this decided, the Prefect began detailing his own theories, frequently pausing to offer lengthy remarks on the evidence, which we had not yet seen. He spoke at length and, without question, with great knowledge, while I offered a few ideas as the night dragged on slowly. Dupin, remaining motionless in his usual chair, was the picture of attentive courtesy. He wore his spectacles for the entire meeting, and an occasional look beneath those green lenses was enough to assure me that he was sleeping quite soundly, if quietly, during the seven or eight sluggish hours that passed before the Prefect finally left.

At dawn, I obtained a complete record of all the testimony gathered at the Prefecture, along with copies of every newspaper that had printed any significant information regarding this tragic matter.
