I do not feel at liberty to disclose the exact nature of this proposal, but it has no bearing on my story.

My friend deflected the compliment as best he could, but he accepted the offer immediately, even though its benefits were entirely conditional. Once this was settled, the Prefect began explaining his own theories, frequently interrupting himself with long comments on the evidence, which we had not yet seen. He spoke at length and, no doubt, learnedly, while I offered an occasional suggestion as the night dragged on. Dupin sat still in his usual armchair, the picture of respectful attention. He wore spectacles throughout the meeting, and an occasional glance beneath his green lenses convinced me that he was sleeping soundly, if silently, during the seven or eight slow-moving hours before the Prefect finally left.

In the morning, I obtained a full report of all the evidence from the police headquarters, along with copies of every newspaper that had published any significant information regarding this tragic affair.

No one crossed over. Within the Rue Pavée St. Andrée, nothing regarding the affair was spoken or overheard, even by those living in the same residence. M. St. Eustache, Marie’s suitor and fiancé who resided with her mother, testified that he remained unaware of the body’s discovery until the following morning, when M. Beauvais entered his room to inform him. Such a significant piece of news, it seems to us, was met with remarkable indifference.

In this fashion, the newspaper attempted to suggest a lack of concern among Marie’s kin, which contradicted the idea that they truly believed the remains were hers. The paper implied that Marie, aided by her associates, had left the city due to concerns regarding her reputation, and that these friends, upon finding a body in the Seine that looked somewhat like the girl, seized the chance to convince the public she had died. However, L’Etoile acted with premature haste. It was clearly demonstrated that no such indifference existed; the elderly woman was quite frail and so distressed that she could handle no responsibilities. Furthermore, St. Eustache, far from reacting calmly, was overwhelmed by sorrow and acted so wildly that M. Beauvais had to ask a relative to watch over him, keeping him from the disinterment proceedings. Additionally, while L’Etoile claimed the body was buried at public expense—asserting that the family rejected a generous offer for a private tomb and that no relatives attended the service—all these claims were effectively refuted. In a later edition, the paper tried to cast doubt upon Beauvais himself. The editor wrote:

“Now, the situation shifts. We are informed that once, while a Madame B. was visiting Madame Rogêt’s home, M. Beauvais, before departing, told her that a gendarme was expected, and that she must remain silent until he returned, leaving the matter to him. As things stand, M. Beauvais seems to hold the entire affair within his own control. One cannot move without encountering M. Beauvais; no matter where you turn, you run into him. For some reason, he decided that no one else should be involved in the investigation, and he has, by all accounts, pushed the male relatives aside in a very peculiar fashion. He appears to have been quite unwilling to let the family view the corpse.”

This suspicion directed at Beauvais gained some credibility from the following detail: a visitor to his office, shortly before the girl vanished and while he was away, noticed a rose placed in the door’s keyhole and the name “Marie” written upon a nearby slate.

Nobody went over. Nothing was said or heard about the matter on Rue Pavée St. Andrée that even reached the other people living in the same building. M. St. Eustache, Marie’s lover and fiancé, who boarded at her mother’s house, testified that he did not hear about the discovery of his fiancée’s body until the next morning, when M. Beauvais came into his room and told him. For a piece of news like this, it strikes us that it was received very calmly.

In this way, the newspaper tried to create the impression that Marie’s relatives were indifferent, which was inconsistent with the idea that these relatives believed the corpse was hers. Its insinuations amounted to this: that Marie, with the help of her friends, had left the city for reasons involving her reputation, and that these friends, upon finding a corpse in the Seine that somewhat resembled the girl, had taken the opportunity to convince the public that she was dead. But L’Etoile was once again too hasty. It was clearly proven that no such indifference existed; the old lady was extremely frail and so upset that she was unable to attend to any duties. St. Eustache, far from receiving the news calmly, was distracted by grief and acted so frantically that M. Beauvais convinced a friend and relative to look after him and keep him from attending the examination at the exhumation. Furthermore, although L’Etoile stated that the corpse was reburied at public expense—that a generous offer for a private burial was flatly refused by the family—and that no family member attended the ceremony—although, I say, all this was claimed by L’Etoile to support the impression it wanted to convey—all of it was satisfactorily disproven. In a later issue of the paper, an attempt was made to cast suspicion on Beauvais himself. The editor says:

“Now, then, the situation changes. We are told that on one occasion, while a Madame B. was at Madame Rogêt’s house, M. Beauvais, who was heading out, told her that a police officer was expected there, and that she, Madame B., must not say anything to the officer until he returned, but leave the matter to him.... In the current state of affairs, M. Beauvais seems to have the entire matter locked away in his head. A single step cannot be taken without M. Beauvais, for no matter which way you turn, you run into him.... For some reason, he decided that no one else should have anything to do with the proceedings but himself, and he has pushed the male relatives aside, according to their accounts, in a very strange manner. He seems to have been very much against letting the relatives see the body.”

Some credibility was given to the suspicion cast upon Beauvais by the following fact. A visitor at his office, a few days before the girl’s disappearance and while the office was empty, had noticed a rose in the keyhole of the door and the name “Marie” written on a slate hanging nearby.

No one went over. Nothing was said or heard about the matter in the Rue Pavée St. Andrée that even reached the people living in the same building. M. St. Eustache, Marie’s lover and fiancé who lived in her mother’s house, testified that he did not hear about the discovery of his fiancée’s body until the next morning, when M. Beauvais entered his room and told him. For news of this nature, it strikes us that he received it very calmly.

In this way, the newspaper tried to create the impression that Marie’s relatives were indifferent, which contradicted the idea that they believed the corpse was hers. Its suggestions amounted to this: that Marie, with the help of her friends, had left the city for reasons involving her reputation, and that these friends, upon finding a corpse in the Seine that somewhat resembled the girl, had used the opportunity to convince the public she was dead. But L’Etoile was too hasty. It was clearly proven that no such indifference existed; the old lady was extremely weak and so upset that she could not attend to any duties. St. Eustache, far from receiving the news calmly, was distracted by grief and acted so frantically that M. Beauvais convinced a friend and relative to look after him and keep him from attending the examination at the exhumation. Furthermore, although L’Etoile stated that the corpse was reburied at public expense—that the family had refused a generous offer for a private burial—and that no family member attended the ceremony—although, I say, all this was claimed by L’Etoile to support the impression it wanted to create—all of it was successfully disproven. In a later issue, the paper attempted to cast suspicion on Beauvais himself. The editor wrote:

“Now, the situation changes. We are told that on one occasion, while a Madame B. was at Madame Rogêt’s house, M. Beauvais, who was leaving, told her that a police officer was expected there. He instructed her not to say anything to the officer until he returned, but to leave the matter to him. In the current state of affairs, M. Beauvais seems to have the entire matter locked in his head. You cannot take a single step without M. Beauvais, because no matter where you turn, you run into him. For some reason, he decided that no one else should be involved in the proceedings, and according to the male relatives, he has pushed them aside in a very strange way. He seems to have been very opposed to letting the relatives see the body.”

The following fact gave some weight to the suspicion cast upon Beauvais. A visitor at his office, a few days before the girl disappeared and while Beauvais was away, had noticed a rose in the door’s keyhole and the name “Marie” written on a slate hanging nearby.

In my view, this piece demonstrates little more than the fervor of its author. We must remember that newspapers typically aim to stir up excitement or score a point rather than advance the truth. The latter is only sought when it aligns with the former. A publication that simply echoes common opinion, no matter how valid, gains no prestige with the crowd. The public considers only those who offer sharp contradictions to conventional wisdom to be deep thinkers. In logic, just as in literature, the epigram is the most instantly and widely valued, yet in both fields, it represents the lowest level of quality.

“My point is that the blend of wit and drama in the notion that Marie Rogêt survives, rather than any genuine credibility in the theory, is what prompted L’Etoile to propose it and ensured its warm public reception. Let us review the main points of this journal’s reasoning, while attempting to rectify the confusion with which it was initially presented.

“The author’s primary goal is to demonstrate, based on the short time between Marie’s vanishing and the discovery of the drifting body, that the corpse cannot be hers. Consequently, minimizing this duration becomes the reasoner’s immediate priority. In the reckless pursuit of this goal, he jumps to an unsupported assumption at the start. ‘It is absurd to imagine,’ he claims, ‘that the murder, if one occurred, could have been finished quickly enough for the killers to cast the body into the river before midnight.’ We naturally ask, why? Why is it absurd to think the murder happened within five minutes of the girl leaving her mother’s home? Why is it absurd to think it occurred at any other time of day? Murders have taken place at every hour. Yet, had the killing occurred at any moment between nine o’clock Sunday morning and a quarter to midnight, there would have been sufficient time to cast the body into the river before midnight. This premise essentially claims that the murder did not happen on Sunday at all; if we grant L’Etoile this assumption, we might as well allow it any freedom it desires. The paragraph starting ‘It is absurd to imagine,’ despite how it appears in L’Etoile, likely existed in the writer’s mind as: ‘It is absurd to imagine that the murder, if committed, could have been finished in time for the killers to cast the body into the river before midnight; it is absurd, we argue, to imagine all this, while simultaneously imagining (as we are determined to do) that the body was not cast in until after midnight’—a statement that is illogical on its own, though not as entirely ridiculous as the one that was printed.

“If my intention,” Dupin continued, “were simply to build a case against this portion of L’Etoile’s argument, I could safely leave it as it stands.”

To me, this article seems to prove little beyond the author's own zeal. We should remember that, generally, newspapers aim more to create a sensation—to make a splash—than to advance the truth. The latter is only pursued when it aligns with the former. A publication that merely agrees with common opinion, no matter how well-founded, earns no respect from the masses. The public only considers someone profound if they offer sharp contradictions to conventional wisdom. In logic, just as in literature, it is the witty remark that is most immediately and universally appreciated. In both fields, however, it is of the lowest order of merit.

“What I mean is that it is the blend of wit and melodrama in the idea that Marie Rogêt still lives, rather than any genuine plausibility, that suggested this theory to L’Etoile and secured it a favorable reception from the public. Let us examine the main points of this journal’s argument, while trying to avoid the incoherence with which it was originally presented.

“The writer’s first goal is to show, based on the short interval between Marie’s disappearance and the discovery of the floating corpse, that this body cannot be hers. Reducing this interval to the smallest possible timeframe immediately becomes the reasoner’s objective. In the reckless pursuit of this goal, he jumps to assumptions right from the start. ‘It is foolish to suppose,’ he says, ‘that the murder, if one was committed, could have been finished soon enough to allow her killers to throw the body into the river before midnight.’ We naturally ask, why? Why is it foolish to suppose the murder was committed within five minutes of the girl leaving her mother’s house? Why is it foolish to suppose it happened at any other time of day? Murders have occurred at all hours. But, had the murder taken place at any moment between nine o’clock Sunday morning and a quarter to midnight, there would still have been enough time to throw the body into the river before midnight. This assumption, then, amounts to this: that the murder was not committed on Sunday at all. If we allow L’Etoile to assume this, we might as well allow it any liberty it wants. The paragraph starting ‘It is foolish to suppose that the murder, etc.,’ however it appears in print in L’Etoile, can be imagined to have existed in the author’s mind like this: ‘It is foolish to suppose that the murder, if one was committed, could have been finished soon enough to allow her killers to throw the body into the river before midnight; it is foolish, we say, to suppose all this, and to suppose at the same time (as we are determined to suppose) that the body was not thrown in until after midnight’—a sentence that is inconsequential enough on its own, but not as utterly ridiculous as the one that was printed.

“If it were my goal,” Dupin continued, “merely to build a case against this part of L’Etoile’s argument, I could safely leave it where it stands.”

To me, this article proves little beyond the author's own enthusiasm. We should remember that newspapers generally aim to create a sensation or make a point rather than pursue the truth. They only seek the truth when it aligns with their desire for attention. A publication that simply agrees with common opinion—no matter how well-founded that opinion may be—gains no respect from the public. Most people only consider a person profound if they offer sharp contradictions to popular belief. In logic, just as in literature, the witty remark is the most immediately and universally appreciated. In both fields, however, it is of the lowest quality.

“What I mean is that it is the combination of wit and drama in the idea that Marie Rogêt still lives, rather than any real plausibility, that suggested this theory to L’Etoile and ensured it a positive reception from the public. Let us examine the main points of this journal’s argument, while trying to avoid the confusion with which it was originally presented.

“The writer’s first goal is to show, based on the short time between Marie’s disappearance and the discovery of the floating body, that the corpse cannot be Marie’s. Reducing this time interval to the smallest possible amount becomes the reasoner’s immediate goal. In the reckless pursuit of this, he jumps to assumptions right from the start. ‘It is foolish to suppose,’ he says, ‘that the murder, if it was indeed a murder, could have been completed soon enough to allow her killers to throw the body into the river before midnight.’ We naturally ask, why? Why is it foolish to suppose the murder was committed within five minutes of the girl leaving her mother’s house? Why is it foolish to suppose it happened at any other time of the day? Murders have occurred at all hours. But if the murder had taken place at any moment between nine o’clock Sunday morning and a quarter before midnight, there would still have been enough time to throw the body into the river before midnight. This assumption amounts to nothing more than claiming the murder did not happen on Sunday at all. If we allow L’Etoile to assume this, we might as well allow it any liberty it wants. The paragraph beginning ‘It is foolish to suppose,’ however it appears in print, can be imagined to have existed in the author’s mind as: ‘It is foolish to suppose that the murder could have been committed soon enough to allow the killers to throw the body into the river before midnight; it is foolish, we say, to suppose all this, and to suppose at the same time (as we are determined to suppose) that the body was not thrown in until after midnight’—a sentence that is illogical on its own, but not as completely absurd as the one that was printed.

“If my purpose,” continued Dupin, “were merely to argue against this passage from L’Etoile, I could safely leave it as it is.”

M. Beauvais, being no fool, would never have identified the body merely by the presence of hair on its arm. Every arm possesses hair. The broad statement made by L’Etoile is simply a distortion of the witness’s actual words. He must have referred to some distinct characteristic of that hair, whether regarding its hue, density, length, or placement.

“‘Her foot,’ the newspaper notes, ‘was petite—yet so are countless others. Her garter provides no evidence, nor does her shoe, as both are sold in bulk. The same applies to the blossoms in her hat. One detail M. Beauvais emphasizes is that the buckle on the discovered garter had been moved to tighten it. This signifies nothing, as many women prefer to adjust their garters at home to fit their legs rather than testing them at the shop.’ It is hard to believe the author of this argument is serious. If M. Beauvais, while seeking Marie’s remains, had found a body matching the missing girl’s general stature and build, he would have been justified—even ignoring her clothing—in concluding his search was successful. If, beyond those general physical traits, he had noticed a unique patch of hair on the arm that he had previously seen on the living Marie, his conviction would have been rightly reinforced; the degree of certainty would have grown in proportion to the rarity of that mark. If Marie’s feet were small, and the corpse’s feet were likewise small, the likelihood that the body was hers would increase not just arithmetically, but geometrically or cumulatively. Include shoes identical to those she wore when she vanished, and although such items are ‘sold in bulk,’ you increase the probability until it nears certainty. What might be insufficient evidence on its own becomes absolute proof through its corroborative context. Add to this flowers in the hat matching those the girl wore, and we require no further proof. If even one flower suffices, what then of two, three, or more? Each additional item provides multiple evidence—proof not merely added, but multiplied by hundreds or thousands. Now, find garters on the deceased identical to those the living girl wore, and it is nearly absurd to continue. Yet these garters were found tightened by a shifted clasp, exactly as Marie had adjusted her own shortly before departing. To doubt now is either insanity or deceit. L’Etoile’s claim that shortening garters is a common practice only highlights its own stubbornness in error. The elastic design of a clasp-garter proves that such adjustments are rare. Anything engineered to self-adjust seldom requires external modification.

M. Beauvais, being no fool, could never have identified the body based solely on hair on its arm. Every arm has hair. L’Etoile’s general description is merely a distortion of the witness’s actual words. He must have mentioned a specific peculiarity regarding that hair—perhaps its color, density, length, or placement.

“The newspaper claims, ‘Her foot was small—so are thousands of feet. Her garter is no proof at all—nor is her shoe—since shoes and garters are sold in bulk. The same applies to the flowers in her hat. One detail M. Beauvais strongly emphasizes is that the clasp on the recovered garter had been moved to tighten it. This means nothing; most women find it necessary to take garters home and adjust them to their own legs rather than trying them on at the store.’ It is hard to believe the writer is serious here. If M. Beauvais, while searching for Marie, had found a corpse matching her general size and appearance, he would have been justified—even without considering her clothing—in concluding his search was successful. If, beyond general size and shape, he had found a unique patch of hair on the arm that he had previously observed on the living Marie, his conviction would have been rightly strengthened; the increase in certainty would correspond to how unusual that mark was. If Marie’s feet were small and the corpse’s feet were also small, the probability that the body was hers would increase not just arithmetically, but geometrically or cumulatively. Add to this shoes like those she was known to wear on the day she vanished, and although such shoes are ‘sold in bulk,’ you increase the probability until it nears certainty. What would be no evidence of identity on its own becomes, through its corroborative context, the most solid proof. Provide us with flowers in the hat matching those worn by the missing girl, and we need look no further. If there is only one flower, we are satisfied—what then if there are two, three, or more? Each successive item is multiple evidence—proof not merely added to proof, but multiplied by hundreds or thousands. Now, if we find on the deceased garters like those the living girl wore, it is almost foolish to continue. But these garters were found to be tightened by a moved clasp, in the exact way Marie had tightened her own shortly before leaving home. It is now either madness or hypocrisy to doubt. What L’Etoile says regarding this shortening of the garters being a common occurrence only demonstrates its own stubbornness in error. The elastic nature of a clasp-garter proves that such an adjustment is unusual. Anything designed to self-adjust rarely requires external modification.

M. Beauvais is no fool, so he could not have identified the body simply by the hair on its arm. Every arm has hair. The newspaper L’Etoile must have misrepresented his words. He surely described a specific detail about that hair—perhaps its color, thickness, length, or placement.

“The journal claims that her small foot proves nothing, as thousands of feet are small. It argues that her garter and shoe are not evidence because they are sold in bulk, and the same applies to the flowers in her hat. M. Beauvais insists that the clasp on the found garter had been moved to make it tighter. The journal dismisses this, claiming women often adjust new garters at home rather than at the store. It is hard to believe the writer is serious. If M. Beauvais had found a body matching Marie’s general size and appearance, he would have been justified in believing he had found her, even without considering her clothing. If he also noticed a unique patch of hair on the arm that he had seen on the living Marie, his certainty would have grown in proportion to how unusual that mark was. If Marie’s feet were small and the corpse’s feet were also small, the probability that it was her would not just increase arithmetically, but geometrically. Add to this the shoes she was known to wear on the day she vanished, and even if those shoes are sold in packages, the probability becomes nearly certain. What might not be evidence on its own becomes solid proof when combined with other facts. If the flowers in the hat match those the missing girl wore, we need no further proof. If one flower is enough, what about two, three, or more? Each additional item is not just more evidence; it multiplies the certainty by hundreds or thousands. If we then find garters on the deceased like those the living girl wore, it is foolish to look further. But these garters were tightened by moving the clasp, just as Marie had tightened her own shortly before leaving home. To doubt now is either madness or dishonesty. The claim by L’Etoile that tightening garters is a common occurrence only shows their stubbornness in being wrong. The elastic nature of a clasp-garter proves that such adjustments are rare. Anything designed to adjust itself rarely requires manual alteration.

If that is the case, did she possess any when she was discovered? These are significant queries that the testimony has completely ignored; furthermore, there are additional points of equal importance that have received no notice. We must strive to find answers through our own investigation. The matter of St. Eustache requires a fresh look. I do not suspect this individual, yet we must act in an orderly fashion. We shall verify the truth of the sworn statements concerning his location on that Sunday. Documents of this nature are easily manipulated to create confusion. If everything proves accurate, however, we will remove St. Eustache from our list of suspects. His self-inflicted death, while potentially supporting suspicion if the affidavits were fraudulent, is not an inexplicable event or one that should divert us from our standard analytical path if no such deception exists.

“In the plan I now suggest, we will set aside the central elements of this tragedy and focus our attention on its periphery. A common mistake in inquiries like this is restricting the search to the immediate facts while ignoring secondary or circumstantial occurrences. It is a flaw in legal proceedings to limit testimony and debate to what seems directly pertinent. Nevertheless, experience and sound reasoning demonstrate that a vast, perhaps even the greater, part of the truth emerges from what appears irrelevant. It is by following the spirit, if not the exact letter, of this principle that modern science has learned to account for the unexpected. Perhaps you do not follow me. The record of human understanding has consistently shown that we owe our most numerous and significant breakthroughs to secondary, incidental, or accidental events; consequently, it has become essential, when looking toward future progress, to make substantial—even primary—allowances for discoveries that emerge by chance, far beyond the scope of typical expectations. It is no longer logical to base our vision of the future solely on the past. Chance is now accepted as part of the foundation. We treat luck as a subject for precise calculation. We apply the mathematical formulas of academia to the unforeseen and the unimagined.

“I reiterate that it is a fact that the majority of all truth has originated from the collateral; it is in keeping with the spirit of this principle that I wish to shift our investigation, in this instance, away from the well-worn and unproductive path of the event itself toward the surrounding circumstances. While you verify the authenticity of the affidavits, I will review the newspapers more broadly than you have managed so far. We have only scouted the area of inquiry thus far; however, it would be truly remarkable if a thorough examination of the public journals, as I intend, does not provide us with small details that will guide our search.”

Following Dupin’s advice, I conducted a careful review of the affidavit situation. The outcome was a solid belief in their accuracy and the resulting innocence of St. Eustache. Meanwhile, my companion busied himself with what appeared to me to be an entirely pointless examination of various newspaper archives.

If so, did she have any when she was found? These are important questions that the evidence has completely ignored, and there are others of equal significance that have received no attention. We must try to satisfy ourselves through personal investigation. The case of St. Eustache must be re-examined. I do not suspect this man, but let us proceed methodically. We will determine beyond a doubt the validity of the sworn statements regarding his whereabouts on Sunday. Statements of this nature are easily used to create confusion. If there is nothing wrong there, however, we will dismiss St. Eustache from our investigation. His suicide, while it would support suspicion if the statements were found to be deceitful, is not an inexplicable circumstance in the absence of such deceit, nor is it something that should cause us to deviate from our standard analysis.

“In what I am now proposing, we will discard the internal details of this tragedy and focus our attention on its periphery. A common error in investigations like this is limiting the inquiry to the immediate facts while completely disregarding collateral or circumstantial events. It is a bad practice of the courts to confine evidence and discussion to the limits of apparent relevance. Yet experience has shown, and true philosophy will always show, that a vast—perhaps the larger—portion of truth arises from what seems irrelevant. It is through the spirit of this principle, if not strictly its letter, that modern science has decided to account for the unforeseen. Perhaps you do not understand me. The history of human knowledge has so consistently shown that we owe our most numerous and valuable discoveries to collateral, incidental, or accidental events that it has become necessary, in any forward-looking view of progress, to make not just large, but the largest allowances for breakthroughs that arise by chance, entirely outside the range of ordinary expectation. It is no longer philosophical to base a vision of the future solely on what has happened in the past. Accident is now accepted as part of the foundation. We make chance a matter of precise calculation. We subject the unexpected and the unimagined to the mathematical formulas of the academy.

“I repeat that it is a fact that the greater portion of all truth has sprung from the collateral; and it is in accordance with the spirit of the principle behind this fact that I would divert the inquiry in this case from the well-trodden and so far fruitless ground of the event itself to the surrounding circumstances. While you verify the validity of the affidavits, I will examine the newspapers more broadly than you have done so far. We have only scouted the field of investigation, but it would be very strange if a comprehensive survey of the public press, as I propose, does not provide us with some small details that will establish a direction for our inquiry.”

Following Dupin’s suggestion, I made a careful examination of the matter of the affidavits. The result was a firm conviction of their validity and the subsequent innocence of St. Eustache. In the meantime, my friend occupied himself, with what seemed to me a completely pointless level of detail, in a scrutiny of the various newspaper files.

If so, did she have any when she was found? These are important questions that the evidence has not addressed at all; there are other equally significant points that have received no attention. We must try to find answers ourselves through personal investigation. The case of St. Eustache must be reviewed. I do not suspect this man, but let us proceed in an orderly fashion. We will confirm beyond any doubt whether the sworn statements regarding his whereabouts on Sunday are accurate. Statements like these can easily be used to create confusion. If there is nothing wrong there, however, we will remove St. Eustache from our investigation. His suicide, while it might support suspicion if the statements were proven false, is not an unexplainable event or one that should cause us to stray from our normal analytical path if those statements are honest.

“In what I am proposing now, we will ignore the central details of this tragedy and focus our attention on the surrounding circumstances. A very common mistake in investigations like this is limiting the inquiry to the immediate facts while ignoring related or circumstantial events. It is a bad habit of the courts to restrict evidence and discussion to what seems directly relevant. Yet experience has shown, and true philosophy will always show, that a vast—perhaps the largest—portion of the truth comes from what seems irrelevant. It is through the spirit of this principle, if not its exact wording, that modern science has decided to account for the unexpected. Perhaps you do not understand me. The history of human knowledge has consistently shown that we owe our most numerous and valuable discoveries to related, incidental, or accidental events. Consequently, it has become necessary, when looking toward future progress, to make large allowances for discoveries that arise by chance, far outside the range of normal expectations. It is no longer logical to base a vision of the future solely on what has happened in the past. We now accept chance as part of the foundation. We make luck a matter of precise calculation. We apply mathematical formulas to the unlooked-for and the unimagined.

“I repeat that it is a fact that the majority of all truth has come from the collateral; and it is in accordance with the principle behind this fact that I want to shift our inquiry, in this case, from the well-trodden and unproductive ground of the event itself to the surrounding circumstances. While you verify the validity of the sworn statements, I will examine the newspapers more broadly than you have done so far. Up to this point, we have only scouted the area of our investigation; but it would be very strange if a comprehensive survey of the public press, as I propose, does not provide us with some small details that will point us in the right direction.”

Following Dupin’s suggestion, I carefully examined the matter of the sworn statements. The result was a firm belief in their validity and, consequently, the innocence of St. Eustache. Meanwhile, my friend occupied himself with what seemed to me a completely pointless scrutiny of various newspaper files.

“You have noted that the prevailing belief regarding this tragic matter is, and has always been, that the young woman fell prey to a group of criminals. Generally, public sentiment should not be ignored under specific circumstances. When it emerges naturally—manifesting in a purely spontaneous fashion—we ought to view it as similar to that intuition which characterizes the unique individual of genius. In ninety-nine out of a hundred instances, I would accept its verdict. However, it is vital that we detect no clear signs of external influence. The view must be strictly the public’s own; distinguishing this is often exceptionally hard to discern and uphold. In this case, it seems to me that this ‘public opinion’ regarding a gang was prompted by the related incident described in the third of my excerpts. All of Paris is stirred by the discovery of Marie’s body, a girl who was young, attractive, and well-known. This corpse was found showing signs of violence, drifting in the river. Yet it is now revealed that, around the same time the girl was supposedly murdered, a similar assault, though less severe, was carried out by a group of young thugs against another young woman. Is it surprising that one confirmed atrocity would sway the public’s view of another, unknown one? This judgment needed guidance, and the known crime provided it quite conveniently! Marie, too, was discovered in the river; and it was upon this very river that the known assault took place. The link between the two events was so obvious that the real surprise would have been the public failing to notice and grasp it. But, in reality, the one crime known to have been committed this way serves, if anything, as proof that the other, occurring at nearly the same time, was not. It would truly be a miracle if, while one gang of ruffians was committing an unprecedented wrong in a specific area, another similar gang was engaged in a crime of the exact same nature, in a similar location, within the same city, under identical conditions, and using the same methods at the very same time! Yet, if not for this incredible series of coincidences, what is it that the accidentally formed opinion of the masses expects us to believe?

“Before we continue, let us examine the alleged site of the murder, in the brush at the Barrière du Roule. This thicket, while thick, was situated very near a public thoroughfare. Inside were three or four large rocks, creating a sort of seat with a backrest and footstool. On the top stone, a white petticoat was found; on the second, a silk scarf. A parasol, gloves, and a handkerchief were also discovered there. The handkerchief was marked with the name ‘Marie Rogêt.’ Pieces of clothing were visible on the surrounding branches. The ground was trodden, the shrubs were snapped, and there were clear signs of a fierce struggle.

“Despite the enthusiasm with which the press greeted the discovery of this thicket, and the universal agreement that it marked the exact location of the crime, it must be acknowledged that there was significant cause for skepticism.

“You have noted that the prevailing view regarding this tragic case is, and has been from the start, that the girl was the victim of a gang of thugs. Now, public opinion, under certain circumstances, should not be ignored. When it arises naturally—when it manifests in a strictly spontaneous way—we should view it as similar to the intuition that characterizes a person of genius. In ninety-nine out of a hundred cases, I would accept its verdict. However, it is vital that we find no clear signs of outside influence. The opinion must be strictly the public’s own, and that distinction is often extremely difficult to identify and uphold. In this instance, it seems to me that this ‘public opinion’ regarding a gang was prompted by the related event detailed in my third excerpt. All of Paris is stirred by the discovery of the corpse of Marie, a young, beautiful, and well-known girl. This body was found in the river, bearing signs of violence. But it has now been revealed that, at or around the same time the girl was supposedly murdered, a similar, though less severe, assault was committed by a gang of young ruffians against another young woman. Is it any wonder that one known atrocity would influence public judgment regarding the other unknown one? This judgment was waiting for direction, and the known outrage seemed to provide it so conveniently! Marie, too, was found in the river, and it was on this very river that the known assault occurred. The connection between the two events was so obvious that it would have been truly surprising if the public had failed to notice and seize upon it. But, in reality, the fact that one atrocity was known to be committed in that way is, if anything, evidence that the other, occurring at nearly the same time, was not. It would be a miracle if, while one gang of ruffians was committing an unheard-of crime in one location, another similar gang was engaged in a crime of the exact same nature, in a similar location, in the same city, under the same circumstances, and using the same methods at the exact same time! Yet, if not for this incredible series of coincidences, what is it that the public’s suggested opinion expects us to believe?

“Before we go further, let us consider the supposed scene of the murder in the thicket at the Barrière du Roule. This thicket, although dense, was very close to a public road. Inside were three or four large stones, forming a sort of seat with a back and footstool. On the top stone, a white petticoat was discovered; on the second, a silk scarf. A parasol, gloves, and a pocket handkerchief were also found there. The handkerchief bore the name ‘Marie Rogêt.’ Fragments of clothing were seen on the surrounding branches. The ground was trampled, the bushes were broken, and there was every sign of a violent struggle.

“Despite the enthusiasm with which the discovery of this thicket was greeted by the press, and the unanimous belief that it indicated the exact site of the crime, it must be admitted that there was some very good reason for doubt.”

“You have noted that the general consensus regarding this tragic case is, and has always been, that the girl was the victim of a gang of criminals. Now, public opinion, under certain conditions, should not be ignored. When it arises naturally—when it manifests in a strictly spontaneous way—we should view it as similar to that intuition which is the unique trait of a genius. In ninety-nine cases out of a hundred, I would trust its judgment. However, it is vital that we find no clear signs of outside influence. The opinion must be strictly the public’s own, and this distinction is often very difficult to identify and maintain. In this instance, it seems to me that this ‘public opinion’ regarding a gang was prompted by the related event detailed in my third excerpt. All of Paris is stirred by the discovery of the corpse of Marie, a young, beautiful, and well-known girl. This body was found bearing signs of violence, floating in the river. But it is now known that, at or around the same time the girl was supposedly murdered, a similar crime, though less severe, was committed by a gang of young thugs against another young woman. Is it any wonder that one known atrocity would influence public judgment regarding the other unknown one? This judgment was waiting for direction, and the known crime seemed to provide it perfectly! Marie was also found in the river, and it was on this very river that the known crime occurred. The connection between the two events seemed so obvious that it would have been more surprising if the public had failed to notice it. But, in reality, the fact that one crime was committed by a gang is, if anything, evidence that the other, occurring at nearly the same time, was not. It would be a miracle if, while one gang of thugs was committing an unheard-of crime in one location, another similar gang was engaged in a crime of the exact same nature, in a similar location, in the same city, under the same circumstances, and at the exact same time! Yet, if we do not believe in this incredible series of coincidences, what else is the public’s suggested opinion asking us to believe?

“Before we go further, let us consider the supposed scene of the murder in the thicket at the Barrière du Roule. This thicket, although dense, was very close to a public road. Inside were three or four large stones, forming a kind of seat with a back and a footstool. On the top stone, a white petticoat was discovered; on the second, a silk scarf. A parasol, gloves, and a pocket-handkerchief were also found there. The handkerchief bore the name, ‘Marie Rogêt.’ Fragments of clothing were seen on the surrounding branches. The ground was trampled, the bushes were broken, and there was every sign of a violent struggle.

“Despite the enthusiasm with which the discovery of this thicket was greeted by the press, and the unanimous belief that it indicated the exact scene of the crime, it must be admitted that there was some very good reason for doubt.

Should a fabric possess two edges—a pocket-handkerchief, for instance—and one wishes to remove a strip, then that single force suffices. However, we are dealing with a dress, which offers only a single edge. To rip a fragment from the middle, where no edge exists, would require a miracle performed by thorns, and no single thorn could manage it. Even with an edge available, two thorns would be required, one pulling in two separate directions and the other in one. This assumes the edge is not hemmed; if it were, the task becomes nearly impossible. We observe the significant hurdles to pieces being torn away by mere thorns, yet we are expected to accept that not just one, but several fragments were removed in this manner. One portion was even the hem of the dress! Another piece was part of the skirt, not the hem—meaning it was torn entirely out from the middle of the garment by thorns! These claims are difficult to credit; yet, taken together, they provide less cause for doubt than the startling fact that any murderers cautious enough to move the body would leave these items in the thicket. You have misunderstood me if you think I intend to deny this thicket as the location of the crime. A wrong or perhaps an accident might have occurred at Madame Deluc’s. Regardless, this is secondary. Our goal is not to locate the scene, but to identify the killers. My detailed arguments were intended, first, to expose the foolishness of Le Soleil’s rash assertions, and second, to lead you to further question whether this murder was the work of a gang.

We shall return to this by briefly mentioning the gruesome details provided by the surgeon at the inquest. It suffices to note that his published conclusions regarding the number of attackers have been rightly mocked as groundless by all respected Parisian anatomists. It is not that the situation could not have been as he suggested, but that there was no evidence for his inference—though was there not evidence for another?

Let us consider the signs of a struggle and ask what they are thought to prove. A gang. But do they not actually suggest the absence of one? What kind of struggle—so intense and prolonged as to leave marks everywhere—could occur between a frail, defenseless girl and a group of ruffians? A few firm grips would have ended it instantly.

If a fabric has two edges—for instance, a pocket handkerchief—and you want to tear off a strip, then, and only then, will a single force suffice. But in this case, we are dealing with a dress, which presents only one edge. Tearing a piece from the middle, where there is no edge, could only be done miraculously by thorns, and no single thorn could manage it. Even where an edge exists, two thorns would be required, one pulling in two different directions and the other in one. This assumes the edge is unhemmed. If it is hemmed, the task is nearly impossible. We can see the many significant obstacles to pieces being ‘torn off’ by simple ‘thorns,’ yet we are expected to believe that not just one, but many pieces were torn this way. One part was even the hem of the dress! Another piece was ‘part of the skirt, not the hem’—meaning it was torn completely out from the middle of the dress by thorns! These are things one can be forgiven for doubting; yet, taken together, they provide less reason for suspicion than the startling fact that the items were left in this thicket at all by murderers who were cautious enough to think about removing the body. You have misunderstood me, however, if you think I intend to deny that this thicket was the scene of the crime. There might have been a wrong committed here, or perhaps an accident at Madame Deluc’s. But this is a minor point. We are not trying to discover the location, but to identify the perpetrators of the murder. What I have presented, despite the detail I have used, is intended first to show the folly of the confident and reckless claims made by Le Soleil, and secondly, and primarily, to lead you, by the most logical path, to further consider whether this assassination was or was not the work of a gang.

“We will return to this question by briefly mentioning the revolting details provided by the surgeon at the inquest. It is only necessary to say that his published conclusions regarding the number of attackers have been rightly ridiculed as unjust and completely baseless by all the reputable anatomists in Paris. It is not that the situation could not have been as he inferred, but that there was no evidence for that inference—was there not much evidence for another?

“Let us now reflect on ‘the traces of a struggle,’ and let me ask what these traces are supposed to prove. A gang. But do they not actually prove the absence of a gang? What kind of struggle could have occurred—a struggle so violent and prolonged that it left ‘traces’ everywhere—between a weak, defenseless girl and a gang of ruffians? The silent grip of a few rough arms would have ended it all instantly.

If a piece of fabric has two edges—like a pocket-handkerchief—and you want to tear off a strip, then a single force will work. However, this case involves a dress, which only presents one edge. Tearing a piece from the middle, where there is no edge, would require a miracle involving thorns, and no single thorn could manage it. Even if an edge is available, you would need two thorns working in different directions. This assumes the edge is unhemmed; if it is hemmed, the task is nearly impossible. We can see the massive obstacles to tearing pieces off using only thorns, yet we are expected to believe that not just one, but many pieces were torn this way. One piece was even the hem of the dress! Another piece was part of the skirt, not the hem, meaning it was torn completely out of the middle of the fabric by thorns. It is understandable to doubt these claims. Yet, taken together, they are less suspicious than the fact that the murderers, who were careful enough to move the body, would leave these items in the thicket at all. You have misunderstood me if you think I am denying that the thicket was the scene of the crime. There might have been a struggle, or perhaps an accident at Madame Deluc’s. But that is a minor point. We are not trying to find the location, but to identify the murderers. I have presented these details, however minutely, first to show the foolishness of the reckless claims made by Le Soleil, and second, to lead you to reconsider whether this murder was truly the work of a gang.

We will return to this question by briefly mentioning the disturbing details provided by the surgeon at the inquest. It is enough to say that his published conclusions regarding the number of attackers have been rightly mocked as baseless by all the respected anatomists in Paris. It is not that he might not be right, but that there was no evidence for his conclusion—and was there not plenty of evidence for a different one?

Let us now consider the supposed traces of a struggle. What are these traces meant to prove? A gang. But do they not actually prove the absence of a gang? What kind of struggle could have occurred—a struggle so violent and long-lasting that it left traces everywhere—between a weak, defenseless girl and a gang of thugs? A few rough arms would have silenced her instantly, and it would have all been over.

I will provide just one more point against the theory of a gang; yet, to my mind, this single argument carries undeniable force. Given the substantial bounty offered and the promise of complete immunity for any informant, it is impossible to believe that some member of a group of common criminals would not have already exposed his partners. Every individual in such a group is driven less by a desire for money or freedom than by the terror of being double-crossed. He informs quickly and willingly to avoid being the one betrayed. The fact that the mystery remains unsolved is the strongest evidence that it is truly a secret. The grim details of this crime are known only to one or two living souls, and to God.

Let us now collect the sparse but definite results of our lengthy investigation. We have concluded that this was either a fatal accident at Madame Deluc’s residence or a murder committed in the woods near the Barrière du Roule by a lover, or perhaps a close, clandestine companion of the victim. This associate has a dark complexion. This skin tone, the irregularity in the bandage, and the sailor’s knot used to fasten the bonnet ribbon all suggest a seaman. His relationship with the victim, a lively but not destitute young woman, indicates he is of a higher status than a typical deckhand. The well-composed and pressing letters sent to the newspapers serve as strong support for this. The details of the initial disappearance, as reported by Le Mercurie, help link this sailor to the naval officer previously identified as having first introduced the unfortunate girl to a life of vice.

At this point, it is appropriate to examine the ongoing disappearance of the dark-skinned man. I must note that his complexion was notably swarthy; it was no ordinary darkness that served as the primary detail remembered by both Valence and Madame Deluc. But why is this man missing? Was he killed by the gang? If so, why do we only find evidence of the murdered girl? One would naturally assume the locations of the two crimes are the same. And where is his body? The killers would likely have disposed of both victims in the same manner. It might be argued that he is alive and refrains from coming forward out of fear of being accused of the murder. While this concern might influence him now—at this late stage—since testimony places him with Marie, it would have had no weight at the time of the crime. An innocent man’s first instinct would have been to report the violence and assist in identifying the perpetrators. This would have been the logical course of action. He had been seen with the girl. He had crossed the river with her in a public ferry. Even to a fool, denouncing the killers would have appeared the most certain and only way to clear himself of suspicion. We cannot imagine that on that fatal Sunday night, he was both innocent and unaware of the crime that had taken place.

I will add only one more argument against the theory of a gang, but to me, this point is completely undeniable. Given the large reward offered and the promise of full immunity for anyone who turns state’s evidence, it is impossible to believe that at least one member of a group of low-level criminals would not have betrayed his partners long ago. Each member of a gang in such a position is driven less by greed for the reward or a desire to escape than by the fear of being betrayed himself. He would betray his partners quickly and eagerly to avoid being betrayed first. The fact that the secret has not been revealed is the strongest possible proof that it is, in fact, a secret. The horrors of this dark crime are known only to one or two living people and to God.

Let us now summarize the limited but certain results of our long analysis. We have arrived at the conclusion that this was either a fatal accident at Madame Deluc’s house or a murder committed in the thicket at the Barrière du Roule by a lover, or at least by an intimate and secret associate of the victim. This associate has a dark complexion. This complexion, the snag in the bandage, and the sailor’s knot used to tie the bonnet ribbon all point to a seaman. His relationship with the victim—a lively but not low-class young woman—suggests he is of a higher rank than a common sailor. The well-written and urgent letters sent to the newspapers serve as strong corroboration. The details of the first elopement, as reported by Le Mercurie, tend to link this seaman with the naval officer who was first known to have led the unfortunate girl into a life of crime.

And here, it is appropriate to consider the continued absence of the man with the dark complexion. Let me note that his complexion was dark and swarthy; it was no ordinary tan that served as the only point of recognition for both Valence and Madame Deluc. But why is this man missing? Was he murdered by the gang? If so, why are there only traces of the murdered girl? One would naturally assume the two crimes took place in the same location. And where is his body? The killers would most likely have disposed of both victims in the same way. It might be argued that this man is still alive and is afraid to come forward for fear of being accused of the murder. This concern might affect him now—at this late stage—since evidence shows he was seen with Marie, but it would have had no weight at the time of the crime. An innocent man’s first instinct would have been to report the crime and help identify the criminals. Common sense would have dictated this. He had been seen with the girl. He had crossed the river with her in an open ferry. Even to a fool, reporting the killers would have seemed the surest and only way to clear himself of suspicion. We cannot assume that on the night of that fatal Sunday, he was both innocent and unaware of the crime that had been committed.

I will add only one more argument against the theory of a gang, but to me, this point is completely undeniable. Given the large reward offered and the promise of a full pardon for anyone who turns state’s evidence, it is impossible to believe that a member of a group of criminals would not have betrayed his partners long ago. Each person in such a group is not just greedy for the reward or desperate to escape; they are terrified of being betrayed themselves. They would betray their partners quickly to avoid being betrayed first. The fact that the secret has not been revealed is the strongest proof that it is, in truth, a secret. The horrors of this dark crime are known only to one or two living people and to God.

Let us summarize the small but certain results of our long analysis. We have concluded that this was either a fatal accident at Madame Deluc’s house or a murder committed in the thicket at the Barrière du Roule by a lover or a secret associate of the victim. This associate has a dark complexion. This complexion, the snag in the bandage, and the sailor’s knot used to tie the bonnet ribbon all point to a seaman. His relationship with the victim—a lively but not low-class young woman—suggests he is of a higher rank than a common sailor. The well-written and urgent letters sent to the newspapers support this. The story of the first elopement, as mentioned by Le Mercurie, helps connect this seaman to the naval officer who was first known to have led the unfortunate girl into a life of crime.

Here, it is appropriate to consider the continued absence of the man with the dark complexion. Let me note that his complexion was dark and swarthy; it was a distinct, unusual darkness that was the only thing remembered by both Valence and Madame Deluc. But why is this man missing? Was he murdered by the gang? If so, why are there only traces of the murdered girl? We would naturally assume the two crimes happened in the same place. And where is his body? In this account, while ostensibly describing the destiny of a Parisian working girl, the writer has meticulously tracked the core elements while merely mirroring the peripheral details of the actual homicide of Mary Rogers. Consequently, any reasoning derived from this fictional narrative applies equally to the reality; indeed, examining that truth was the primary goal. "The Mystery of Marie Roget" was written far from the site of the crime, utilizing only information provided by newspapers. As a result, the author missed details that could have been utilized had he been present at the location. It is worth noting, however, that the admissions of two individuals—including the Madame Deluc mentioned in the story—provided long after the work was published, fully validated not only the overarching deduction but every significant theoretical point used to reach that conclusion.

THE BALLOON-HOAX

[Incredible News via Express from Norfolk!—The Atlantic traversed in three days! A major victory for Mr. Monck Mason’s aerial craft!—Mr. Mason, Mr. Robert Holland, Mr. Henson, Mr. Harrison Ainsworth, and four companions have arrived at Sullivan’s Island, near Charleston, South Carolina, aboard the steerable balloon "Victoria," following a seventy-five-hour journey from shore to shore! Complete details of the trip! The following witticism, featuring the preceding headline in bold capital letters and filled with exclamation points, was first printed as a factual report in the "New York Sun" newspaper. It successfully served to provide sensational fodder for the curious public during the brief interval between Charleston mail deliveries. The demand for the "only paper with the story" was truly extraordinary; and, in truth, if some claim the "Victoria" did not actually complete the voyage described, it is hard to explain why she could not have done so.]

In this piece, under the guise of recounting the fate of a Parisian shopgirl, the author has followed the essential facts of the actual murder of Mary Rogers in minute detail, while merely paralleling the non-essential ones. Consequently, any argument based on the fiction applies to the truth, and investigating that truth was the objective. “The Mystery of Marie Roget” was written far from the scene of the crime, with no means of investigation other than what the newspapers provided. Thus, much escaped the writer that he could have utilized had he been on-site to visit the locations. It may be worth noting, however, that the confessions of two individuals—one of them the Madame Deluc of the story—made long after the publication, fully confirmed not only the general conclusion but absolutely all the primary hypothetical details used to reach it.

THE BALLOON-HOAX

[Astounding News by Express, via Norfolk!—The Atlantic crossed in three days! A major triumph for Mr. Monck Mason’s flying machine!—Arrival at Sullivan’s Island, near Charleston, S.C., of Mr. Mason, Mr. Robert Holland, Mr. Henson, Mr. Harrison Ainsworth, and four others in the steering balloon, “Victoria,” after a seventy-five-hour trip from land to land! Full details of the voyage!] The following witty piece, with the preceding headline in large capital letters and filled with exclamation points, was originally published as a factual report in the “New York Sun.” It successfully served the purpose of providing sensational news for curious readers during the few hours between Charleston mail deliveries. The demand for the “only paper that had the news” was truly extraordinary. In fact, if some people claim the “Victoria” did not actually complete the voyage described, it would be difficult to explain why she could not have done so.

We now continue with the record, as copied by Mr. Forsyth from the combined manuscripts of Mr. Monck Mason and Mr. Ainsworth. The main text is written by Mr. Mason, while a postscript is added daily by Mr. Ainsworth, who is currently drafting a more detailed and surely captivating report of the journey for future publication.

“THE JOURNAL.

“Saturday, April 6th.—Having completed all necessary preparations the previous evening, we began inflating the balloon at dawn. However, a dense fog clung to the silk, making it difficult to handle and delaying our progress until nearly eleven o’clock. We then cast off in high spirits, rising slowly and consistently on a gentle northerly breeze that carried us toward the British Channel. The lift was stronger than anticipated, and as we climbed above the cliffs into the sunlight, our ascent accelerated. Wishing to conserve gas early in the trip, I decided to continue climbing for the time being. We soon extended our guide-rope, but even after it cleared the ground, we kept rising quickly. The balloon remained remarkably stable and appeared magnificent. Ten minutes after departure, the barometer showed an altitude of 15,000 feet. The weather was excellent, and the sight of the landscape below—always romantic—was particularly majestic. Thick vapors filled the deep ravines, making them look like lakes, while the crags and peaks to the southeast were jumbled together like the mythical cities of the East. We were nearing the southern mountains, but our height was more than enough to clear them safely. We soared over the peaks shortly after, and Mr. Ainsworth and the sailors were struck by how low the mountains seemed from the car; great altitude in a balloon tends to flatten the terrain below into a nearly uniform surface. By half-past eleven, still traveling mostly south, we caught our first glimpse of the Bristol Channel, and fifteen minutes later, the coastal surf appeared directly under us as we moved out over the water. We decided to release enough gas to lower our guide-rope and its attached buoys into the sea. This was done at once, and we began a slow descent. Within twenty minutes, the first buoy touched the water, and after the second followed shortly after, our altitude stabilized. Eager to evaluate the rudder and screw, we immediately engaged both to shift our heading eastward toward Paris. The rudder allowed us to change direction instantly, positioning us nearly perpendicular to the wind; we then activated the screw’s spring and were pleased to see it drive us forward as intended.

We continue now with the journal, as transcribed by Mr. Forsyth from the joint manuscripts of Mr. Monck Mason and Mr. Ainsworth. The main body of the journal is in Mr. Mason’s handwriting, with a postscript added each day by Mr. Ainsworth, who is preparing and will soon release to the public a more detailed and undoubtedly thrilling account of the voyage.

“THE JOURNAL.

“Saturday, April 6th.—Having completed all preparations that might have hindered us the night before, we began the inflation at daybreak. However, a thick fog weighed down the silk folds and made the balloon difficult to handle, so we did not finish until nearly eleven o’clock. We then cast off in high spirits and rose gently but steadily on a light north breeze that carried us toward the English Channel. We found the lifting force greater than expected, and as we climbed higher, clearing the cliffs and entering the sunlight, our ascent became very rapid. I did not want to vent gas so early in the adventure, so I decided to keep climbing for the time being. We soon let out our guide-rope, but even after it cleared the ground, we continued to rise quickly. The balloon was exceptionally steady and looked beautiful. About ten minutes after takeoff, the barometer showed an altitude of 15,000 feet. The weather was remarkably clear, and the view of the landscape below—already romantic from any vantage point—was now truly magnificent. The many deep gorges looked like lakes due to the dense mist filling them, while the peaks and crags to the southeast, piled in chaotic confusion, resembled nothing so much as the giant cities of Eastern legend. We were approaching the mountains to the south, but our altitude was more than enough to clear them safely. In a few minutes, we soared over them impressively; Mr. Ainsworth and the sailors were surprised at how low the mountains appeared from the car, as great balloon altitudes tend to flatten the terrain below into a nearly level surface. At 11:30, still heading mostly south, we caught our first glimpse of the Bristol Channel; fifteen minutes later, the line of breakers on the coast appeared directly beneath us, and we were officially out at sea. We decided to release enough gas to lower our guide-rope, with its attached buoys, into the water. This was done immediately, and we began a gradual descent. In about twenty minutes, our first buoy touched the water, and after the second one dipped shortly after, our altitude remained steady. We were all eager to test the effectiveness of the rudder and screw, so we put them both to use immediately to shift our direction eastward, toward Paris. Using the rudder, we quickly made the necessary change in heading, bringing our course nearly perpendicular to the wind. We then engaged the screw’s spring and were pleased to find it propelled us forward just as we had hoped.

About ten minutes after takeoff, the barometer showed an altitude of 15,000 feet. The weather was remarkably fine, and the view of the landscape below—always romantic—was now especially magnificent. The deep gorges looked like lakes because they were filled with dense mist, while the peaks and crags to the southeast, piled in a chaotic jumble, resembled the giant cities of eastern legends. We were quickly approaching the mountains to the south, but our altitude was more than enough to clear them safely. In a few minutes, we soared over them easily. Mr. Ainsworth and the sailors were surprised at how low the mountains appeared from the car, as great height in a balloon tends to make the uneven ground below look almost flat. At 11:30, still heading nearly south, we caught our first glimpse of the Bristol Channel. Fifteen minutes later, the line of breakers on the coast appeared directly beneath us, and we were officially out at sea. We decided to release enough gas to lower our guide-rope, with its attached buoys, into the water. We did this immediately and began a gradual descent. In about twenty minutes, our first buoy touched the water, and after the second one dipped shortly after, our altitude remained steady. We were all eager to test the effectiveness of the rudder and screw, so we put them to use right away to change our direction toward the east, aiming for Paris. Using the rudder, we instantly made the necessary turn, bringing our course nearly perpendicular to the wind. We then engaged the screw’s spring and were delighted to find that it propelled us forward exactly as we had hoped.

The people living on the island and at the fort naturally crowded out to view the airship, yet it was extremely hard to convince anyone that the journey—the crossing of the Atlantic—had truly occurred. The anchor hooked at exactly 2:00 P.M., meaning the entire trip was finished in seventy-five hours, or slightly less, measuring from coast to coast. No major mishaps took place. No genuine peril was felt at any point. The balloon was deflated and fastened without difficulty; when the manuscript used for this account was sent from Charleston, the group remained at Fort Moultrie. Their future plans were not discovered, but we can confidently assure our audience that further details will be provided by Monday or, at the latest, the following day.

This is without doubt the most astonishing, captivating, and significant feat ever achieved or even tried by humanity. Speculating on what grand developments might follow would be pointless at this time.

(*1) Note.—Mr. Ainsworth has not tried to explain this occurrence, which is, however, entirely capable of being understood. A cord lowered from a height of 25,000 feet, straight down to the earth or sea, would create the vertical side of a right triangle, where the base would stretch from the right angle to the horizon, and the hypotenuse from the horizon to the balloon. Yet, 25,000 feet of height is negligible when measured against the breadth of the view. Stated differently, the base and hypotenuse of this theoretical triangle would be so vast relative to the vertical side that the two could be seen as nearly parallel. Consequently, the horizon of the traveler would seem to be even with the basket. However, because the spot directly under him appears, and is, far below him, it naturally seems to be far below the horizon as well. This creates the sense of concavity, and this perception must persist until the height reaches such a ratio to the breadth of the view that the apparent parallelism of the base and hypotenuse vanishes, at which point the actual curvature of the earth must become visible.

I have little to share regarding my homeland or my kin. Mistreatment and the passage of time have pushed me from the former and alienated me from the latter. Inherited riches provided me with an exceptional education, and a reflective disposition allowed me to organize the knowledge that my youthful studies had carefully accumulated. Above all, I found great pleasure in the works of German moralists, not because of any misguided praise for their passionate insanity, but because my disciplined way of thinking allowed me to easily spot their inaccuracies. I have frequently been criticized for the dryness of my intellect; a lack of imagination has been blamed on me as a fault, and my skeptical viewpoints have always made me well-known. In truth, a deep fondness for physical science has, I suspect, colored my thinking with a very typical mistake of this era—I refer to the tendency to attribute events, even those least suited to such an explanation, to the laws of that discipline.

The vessel’s massive frame was a somber, murky black, lacking any of the typical decorative carvings found on ships. A solitary line of brass cannons extended from her uncovered ports, reflecting the glare of countless battle-lanterns that swayed throughout the rigging. Yet, what filled us with the greatest dread and wonder was that she maintained a full spread of canvas directly against that unnatural ocean and that uncontrollable gale. Upon our initial sighting, only her prow was visible as she ascended sluggishly from the dark, terrifying abyss ahead. For a heartbeat of profound dread, she hung upon the dizzying crest, seemingly reflecting on her own grandeur, before she shook, swayed, and descended.

In that moment, an unexpected composure settled over my mind. Reeling as far toward the stern as possible, I awaited the impending destruction without fear. Our own ship had finally stopped fighting and was foundering, bow-first into the waves. The impact of the falling vessel struck her, therefore, in the section of her structure already submerged, and the unavoidable consequence was to cast me, with unstoppable force, onto the rigging of the foreign craft.

As I landed, the ship tacked and turned; I credited the resulting chaos for my remaining unseen by the sailors. With minimal effort, I crept unnoticed to the main hatch, which stood slightly ajar, and quickly discovered a chance to hide within the hold. My reasons for doing so are difficult to explain. A vague sense of dread, which had gripped me upon first observing the ship’s mariners, was likely the primary motive for my concealment. I was reluctant to expose myself to a group of beings who had presented, during my brief observation, so many elements of strange unfamiliarity, uncertainty, and alarm. I therefore decided it was wise to arrange a hiding spot in the hold. I accomplished this by shifting a few planks, creating a suitable refuge tucked between the vessel’s massive beams.

I had barely finished when footsteps in the hold compelled me to utilize my shelter. A man walked past my hiding place with a weak and wavering stride. Though his face remained hidden, I could discern his general demeanor. He displayed signs of extreme age and frailty. His knees shook under the weight of many years, and his entire body trembled beneath the burden. He mumbled to himself in a low, fractured voice, using a tongue I could not comprehend, while rummaging in a corner among a collection of odd-looking tools and weathered navigational charts. His behavior was a strange blend of the irritability of extreme old age and the grave majesty of a deity. Eventually, he returned to the deck, and I did not see him again.

A sentiment, for which I possess no label, has seized my spirit—an emotion that defies explanation, for which the teachings of the past are insufficient, and for which I fear the future will provide no answer.

Her massive hull was a deep, dingy black, lacking any of the usual ship carvings. A single row of brass cannons poked out from her open ports, reflecting the light from countless battle-lanterns swinging back and forth in the rigging. But what truly filled us with horror and amazement was that she held her course under full sail directly into the teeth of that supernatural sea and uncontrollable hurricane. When we first spotted her, only her bow was visible as she rose slowly from the dark, terrifying abyss behind her. For a moment of intense terror, she paused at the dizzying peak, as if contemplating her own grandeur, then she trembled, teetered, and came crashing down.

At that moment, I don’t know what sudden calm came over me. Staggering as far toward the stern as I could, I fearlessly awaited the destruction that was about to overwhelm us. Our own ship was finally giving up the fight, sinking bow-first into the sea. The impact of the descending mass struck our vessel in the section already submerged, and the inevitable result was that I was hurled with irresistible force onto the rigging of the stranger.

As I fell, the ship tacked and turned; I credited the resulting confusion for my escape from the crew’s notice. With little difficulty, I made my way unseen to the main hatch, which was partially open, and soon found a chance to hide in the hold. Why I did so, I can hardly say. A vague sense of awe, which had gripped my mind the moment I saw the ship’s navigators, was perhaps the reason for my hiding. I was unwilling to trust myself among a group of people who, in the brief glance I had caught, presented so many points of strange novelty, doubt, and apprehension. I therefore thought it best to arrange a hiding spot in the hold. I did this by removing a small section of the shifting-boards, creating a convenient retreat between the ship’s massive timbers.
