THE OVAL PORTRAIT

The castle my servant had broken into, rather than let me spend the night outdoors in my severely injured state, was one of those structures of mixed melancholy and majesty that have long loomed over the Apennines, both in reality and in the imagination of Mrs. Radcliffe. It seemed to have been deserted quite recently. We settled into one of the smallest, least opulent rooms, located in a distant turret. Its furnishings were ornate but worn and aged. The walls were covered in tapestries and adorned with various heraldic displays, along with a large collection of vibrant modern paintings in elaborate golden frames. These artworks, hanging not only on the main walls but also in the many alcoves created by the castle's strange design—these paintings, perhaps due to my early fever, captured my intense interest; I instructed Pedro to shut the heavy window shutters—as night had fallen—to ignite the candles of a tall candelabrum beside my bed, and to pull back the fringed black velvet curtains surrounding the bed. I desired this so I could surrender myself, if not to slumber, then to alternating between studying these images and reading a small book found on the pillow, which claimed to critique and explain them.

I read for a very long time, gazing with devotion and intensity. The hours passed swiftly and brilliantly until deep midnight arrived. The placement of the candelabrum bothered me, and reaching out with effort, rather than waking my sleeping servant, I adjusted it to cast its light more directly onto the book.

However, this movement had an entirely unexpected result. The light from the many candles now illuminated a niche in the room that had previously been cast into deep shadow by a bedpost. I thus beheld in clear light a portrait I had not noticed before. It was the likeness of a young woman transitioning into adulthood. I looked at the painting briefly, then shut my eyes. The reason for this was not immediately clear to me. Yet, while my eyes were closed, I reviewed my motive for doing so. It was an instinctive reaction to allow myself time to reflect—to confirm my sight had not misled me—to quiet and restrain my imagination for a more composed and certain look.

I have little time left to reflect on my fate! The circles are shrinking rapidly—we are plunging wildly into the grip of the whirlpool—and amidst the roaring, bellowing, and thundering of the ocean and the storm, the ship is shaking—oh God! and—sinking.

This official, however, has been completely bewildered; the root of his failure stems from the assumption that the Minister must be a simpleton because he has gained fame as a poet. All fools are poets; the Prefect senses this, and he simply commits a logical fallacy by concluding that all poets are fools.

“But is he truly a poet?” I inquired. “I am aware of two brothers, both of whom have achieved literary acclaim. I believe the Minister has authored scholarly works on the Differential Calculus. He is a mathematician, not a poet.”

“You are in error; I am well acquainted with him; he is both. As a poet and a mathematician, he would reason effectively; as a mere mathematician, he would have been unable to reason at all, and would have been entirely vulnerable to the Prefect.”

“Your views surprise me,” I remarked, “as they contradict the consensus of the world. You surely do not intend to disregard the established wisdom of centuries. Mathematical reasoning has long been considered the pinnacle of intellect.”

“‘Il y a à parièr,’” Dupin replied, citing Chamfort, “‘that every public notion, every accepted convention, is a folly, because it has suited the majority.’ The mathematicians, I admit, have endeavored to spread the popular misconception you mention, which remains an error despite its acceptance as truth. With a skill deserving a better purpose, for instance, they have applied the label ‘analysis’ to algebra. The French initiated this specific deception; yet if terminology holds any significance—if words gain meaning from their usage—then ‘analysis’ relates to ‘algebra’ about as much as, in Latin, ‘ambitus’ signifies ‘ambition,’ ‘religio’ means ‘religion,’ or ‘homines honesti’ describes a group of honorable men.”

“I perceive you have a dispute,” I said, “with certain Parisian algebraists; but please continue.”

“I challenge the utility, and therefore the merit, of reasoning developed in any mode other than purely abstract logic. Specifically, I contest the reasoning derived from mathematical study. Mathematics is the science of form and quantity; mathematical reasoning is simply logic applied to observations of form and quantity. The major mistake is assuming that even the truths of so-called pure algebra are abstract or universal truths. This error is so profound that I am amazed by how universally it has been accepted. Mathematical axioms are not axioms of universal truth. What holds true for relations—of form and quantity—is frequently entirely false regarding, for instance, morality. In that field, it is rarely true that the sum of the parts equals the whole. This axiom also fails in chemistry. It fails when considering motives; two motives, each of a specific value, do not necessarily possess a combined value equal to the sum of their individual parts. It is recalled that, in the standard telling of these stories, a certain king, having legitimate reason to distrust his queen, not only executes her but swears an oath, by his beard and the prophet, to marry the most attractive young woman in his realm each evening, only to hand her over to the headsman the following dawn. Having carried out this oath for many years with a religious precision and consistency that reflected well upon his piety and sound judgment, he was disturbed one afternoon—presumably while praying—by a visit from his grand vizier, whose daughter had conceived a plan. Her name was Scheherazade, and her intention was to either save the country from this tax on its beauty or die, as is customary for heroines, in the effort. Consequently, even though it was not a leap year—which adds to the merit of her sacrifice—she instructed her father, the grand vizier, to propose her hand to the king. The king readily accepted this offer—he had intended to take her anyway, having delayed only out of apprehension toward the vizier—but in doing so, he made it perfectly clear to everyone that, regardless of the vizier’s status, he had no intention of abandoning any part of his vow or his royal rights. Therefore, when the lovely Scheherazade insisted on marrying the king, and actually wed him despite her father’s wise counsel to the contrary—when she insisted on the marriage, whether he liked it or not—she did so with her beautiful dark eyes as wide open as the circumstances permitted. It appears, however, that this clever young woman, who had surely studied Machiavelli, had devised a subtle scheme. On their wedding night, she arranged, under some plausible excuse, for her sister to sleep on a couch close enough to the royal bed to allow for easy conversation. Shortly before dawn, she managed to rouse the monarch, her husband—who held no grudge against her, even though he planned to break her neck the next day—by engaging him with the deep intrigue of a tale (concerning a rat and a black cat, I believe) which she was whispering to her sister. When morning arrived, the story remained incomplete, and Scheherazade naturally could not finish it then, as it was time for her to rise and be strangled—a fate slightly more refined than hanging, but hardly more pleasant. The king’s curiosity, however, unfortunately outweighed his religious convictions, leading him to delay his vow until the following morning, in the hope of learning how the black cat and the rat fared in the end.

The sea, situated between the far-off island and the coast, possessed a highly peculiar quality. Even though a powerful gale was pushing toward the land at that moment—forcing a brig in the far distance to heave to under a double-reefed trysail, where it repeatedly vanished from view—there was no sign of a standard swell here. Instead, there was merely a brief, sharp, and turbulent clashing of water moving in every direction, regardless of the wind's orientation. Aside from the area right by the cliffs, there was very little foam.

“The Norwegians refer to that distant island as Vurrgh,” the elderly man continued. “The one in the middle is Moskoe. The island a mile to the north is Ambaaren. Over there are Islesen, Hotholm, Keildhelm, Suarven, and Buckholm. Further away, located between Moskoe and Vurrgh, are Otterholm, Flimen, Sandflesen, and Stockholm. Those are the actual names of these locations, though why anyone felt the need to name them at all is beyond both of our understandings. Do you perceive any sound? Do you notice any shift in the water?”

We had spent roughly ten minutes atop Helseggen, having climbed there from the inland part of Lofoden, meaning we had not seen the ocean until it suddenly appeared before us at the peak. As the man spoke, I began to hear a loud, steadily intensifying noise, reminiscent of the lowing of a massive herd of buffalo on an American prairie. Simultaneously, I noticed that the choppy nature of the ocean below us was quickly transforming into a current flowing toward the east. As I watched, this current gained incredible speed, its momentum growing more reckless with every passing second. Within five minutes, the entire sea, stretching as far as Vurrgh, was whipped into an uncontrollable rage; however, the primary disturbance was concentrated between Moskoe and the shore. There, the immense expanse of water, divided into countless clashing channels, erupted into a frantic state—heaving, boiling, hissing, and spinning into massive, countless whirlpools, all rushing eastward with a swiftness rarely seen in water unless it is falling down a steep cliff.

After a few more minutes, the scene underwent another drastic transformation. The overall surface became somewhat calmer, and the whirlpools vanished one by one, while massive lines of foam emerged where there had previously been none. Eventually, these streaks expanded over a wide area, merged together, adopted the spinning motion of the vanished vortices, and appeared to create the foundation for a much larger one. All at once, this formation took on a clear and precise shape, spanning a circle over a mile wide. The perimeter of the vortex was marked by a wide band of bright spray, yet not a single drop fell into the opening of the terrifying funnel. “It is impossible for anyone to comprehend the emotions I experienced at that instant. My entire body trembled as though I were suffering from a severe bout of fever. I understood his meaning behind that single word perfectly; I grasped exactly what he intended to convey. Carried by the gale that propelled us forward, we were heading directly toward the vortex of the Ström, and there was no escape for us.

“You must understand that when traversing the Ström channel, we typically steered far above the whirlpool, even during tranquil conditions, waiting patiently for the slack tide—yet now we were rushing straight toward the center of the abyss, and in the midst of such a tempest! ‘Perhaps,’ I reasoned, ‘we might arrive precisely at the slack—there is a sliver of hope in that thought’—but immediately I condemned myself for being foolish enough to entertain any optimism. I was fully aware that we were lost, even if we had been aboard a massive ninety-gun vessel.

“By this point, the initial intensity of the storm had subsided, or perhaps we simply felt it less while running before the wind; regardless, the waves, which had previously been suppressed by the gale and appeared flat and foamy, now rose into immense mountains. A remarkable transformation had also occurred in the sky. While the horizon remained pitch black in every direction, a circular opening of clear sky suddenly appeared directly above—as transparent as I have ever witnessed and of a vivid, deep blue—and through it, the full moon shone with a brilliance I had never before seen. It illuminated our surroundings with startling clarity—but, merciful God, what a sight it revealed!

“I attempted several times to address my brother, but for reasons I could not fathom, the roar had intensified to such a degree that he could not hear a single syllable, even though I shouted at the top of my lungs into his ear. Eventually, he shook his head, his face deathly pale, and raised a finger as if to command me to ‘listen!’

“At first, I failed to grasp his meaning, but then a terrifying realization struck me. I pulled my watch from my pocket. It had stopped. I examined its face by the moonlight, then wept as I cast it far into the sea. It had ceased running at seven o’clock! We had missed the slack tide, and the Ström’s whirlpool was raging at its peak intensity!

“When a vessel is sturdily constructed, balanced correctly, and not overburdened, the waves during a heavy storm, while running with the wind, seem to slide away beneath her—a phenomenon that appears quite odd to those unfamiliar with the sea—and this is termed ‘riding’ in nautical parlance.

“We had managed to ride the swells quite skillfully thus far; however, a massive wave suddenly caught us beneath the stern, lifting us upward—higher and higher—as if we were being carried into the heavens.

“No one will ever understand how I felt at that moment. I shook from head to toe as if I were suffering from a violent fever. I understood perfectly what he meant by that single word—I knew exactly what he wanted me to realize. With the wind currently driving us forward, we were headed straight for the vortex of the Maelström, and nothing could save us!

“You understand that when crossing the Maelström channel, we always traveled far above the whirlpool, even in the calmest weather, and then had to wait and watch carefully for the slack tide—but now we were being driven directly into the pool itself, and in a hurricane like this! ‘Surely,’ I thought, ‘we will arrive right around the slack—there is some small hope in that’—but the next moment I cursed myself for being such a fool as to dream of hope at all. I knew very well that we were doomed, even if we had been a massive ninety-gun warship.

“By this time the initial fury of the storm had faded, or perhaps we simply felt it less as we raced before it, but regardless, the seas, which had initially been flattened and frothing from the wind, now rose into absolute mountains. A strange change had also come over the sky. In every direction it remained pitch black, but almost directly overhead, a circular rift of clear sky suddenly opened—as clear as I have ever seen—a deep, bright blue—and through it, the full moon blazed with a brilliance I had never seen her wear before. She lit up everything around us with perfect clarity—but, oh God, what a scene it was to illuminate!

“I made a few attempts to speak to my brother—but, in a way I could not understand, the roar had increased so much that I could not make him hear a single word, even though I screamed at the top of my lungs into his ear. We had missed the time of the slack, and the whirlpool was in full fury!

I surely must have been feverish, as I even attempted to find diversion in guessing the comparative speeds at which various objects fell toward the churning spray beneath. I caught myself remarking that a particular pine tree would surely be the next to make the terrifying drop and vanish, yet I felt let down when the remains of a Dutch trading vessel passed it and sank first. Eventually, after making numerous such predictions and being wrong every time, this consistent failure to calculate correctly triggered a line of thought that caused my body to shake and my heart to pound violently once more.

It was not a fresh dread that troubled me, but rather the emergence of a more stimulating hope. This expectation stemmed partly from recollection and partly from what I was witnessing. I remembered the wide array of floating debris scattered along the Lofoden shore, which had been pulled into the Moskoe-ström and subsequently expelled. While the vast majority of these items were pulverized in a bizarre fashion—so worn and jagged that they looked as if they were embedded with slivers—I clearly recalled that some remained entirely undamaged. I could explain this discrepancy only by assuming that the battered pieces were the only ones fully pulled into the depths, while the others had either entered the vortex late in the tide cycle or descended so gradually that they failed to hit the bottom before the tide shifted. I reasoned that, in either scenario, they might be spun back up to the surface without suffering the same end as those drawn in sooner or swallowed more quickly. I also noted three significant points. First, larger objects generally descended faster. Second, between two items of equal size, the spherical one fell more quickly than any other shape. Third, between two items of equal size, the cylindrical one was pulled down more slowly. Since surviving, I have discussed this with a local schoolteacher, who provided the terms ‘cylinder’ and ‘sphere.’ He clarified—though I have lost the details—how my observations were the natural result of the shapes of the debris, demonstrating how a cylinder floating in a whirlpool resists suction more effectively than an object of equal volume in any other form.

One striking detail strongly reinforced these findings and made me eager to utilize them: with every rotation, we drifted past items like a barrel, a yardarm, or a ship’s mast, and many of these objects, which had been at our elevation when I first beheld the whirlpool’s marvels, were now far above us and appeared to have shifted very little from their initial position.

I must have been delirious, for I even tried to amuse myself by guessing the relative speeds at which various objects were falling toward the foam below. 'This fir tree,' I caught myself saying at one point, 'will surely be the next thing to take the terrifying plunge and vanish'—and then I was disappointed to see that the wreckage of a Dutch merchant ship overtook it and sank first. Eventually, after making several guesses of this kind and being wrong every time, this fact—the fact of my constant miscalculation—triggered a train of thought that made my limbs tremble again and my heart beat heavily once more.

It was not a new terror that affected me this way, but the dawn of a more exciting hope. This hope arose partly from memory and partly from current observation. I recalled the great variety of buoyant debris that littered the coast of Lofoden, having been pulled in and then spat out by the Moskoe-ström. By far the majority of these items were shattered in the most extraordinary way—so chafed and roughened that they appeared to be covered in splinters—but I distinctly remembered that there were some that were not disfigured at all. I could not explain this difference except by assuming that the roughened fragments were the only ones that had been completely absorbed—that the others had entered the whirlpool so late in the tide, or for some reason had descended so slowly after entering, that they did not reach the bottom before the tide turned. I thought it possible, in either case, that they might be whirled back up to the ocean's surface without suffering the fate of those that had been drawn in earlier or absorbed more rapidly. I also made three important observations. The first was that, as a general rule, the larger the objects were, the faster they descended; the second, that between two masses of equal size, one spherical and the other of any other shape, the sphere descended faster; the third, that between two masses of equal size, one cylindrical and the other of any other shape, the cylinder was absorbed more slowly. Since my escape, I have had several conversations on this subject with an old schoolteacher from the area, and it was from him that I learned the terms 'cylinder' and 'sphere.' He explained to me—though I have forgotten the explanation—how what I observed was, in fact, the natural consequence of the shapes of the floating fragments, and showed me how a cylinder, swimming in a vortex, offered more resistance to its suction and was drawn in with greater difficulty than an equally bulky body of any other shape.

The final form remains hidden from our basic senses, leaving us to observe only the outer casing that sheds away during decomposition; we do not see the core itself, though that core, like the shell, is perceptible to those who have attained the final state of existence.

_P._ You have frequently remarked that the mesmeric condition is quite similar to death. Why is that?

_V._ By saying it resembles death, I mean it resembles the final existence; when I am in a trance, the senses belonging to my basic life are suspended, and I experience the external world directly, without the need for physical organs, utilizing a medium that I will eventually use in my final, unorganized state.

_P._ Unorganized?

_V._ Indeed; organs are mechanisms that connect an individual to specific types and shapes of matter, while excluding others. Human organs are suited solely for our basic state; our final state, being unorganized, possesses boundless understanding in every respect except for one—the nature of God’s will—which is to say, the movement of unparticled matter. You can form a clear notion of the final body by imagining it as pure brain. It is not actually that, but such a thought will help you grasp its true nature. A glowing object causes vibrations in the luminiferous ether. These vibrations create corresponding ones within the retina, which then pass them to the optic nerve. The nerve transmits them to the brain, and the brain passes them to the unparticled matter that flows through it. The movement of this matter constitutes thought, with perception being its initial ripple. This is how the mind in our basic life interacts with the outside world, and this world is restricted for the basic life by the unique limitations of its organs. However, in the final, unorganized life, the external world reaches the entire body—which consists of a substance similar to brain, as I noted—with no interference other than an ether far more refined than the luminiferous one; the entire body vibrates in harmony with this ether, triggering the unparticled matter within it. Therefore, we must credit the nearly infinite perception of the final life to the lack of specialized organs. For basic beings, organs serve as the cages required to hold them until they are ready to fly.

_P._ You mention basic “beings.” Are there other thinking creatures besides humans?

_V._ The vast gathering of rare matter into nebulae, planets, suns, and other bodies that are neither nebulae, suns, nor planets, exists solely to provide sustenance for the specific organs of an infinite number of basic beings. Without the requirement for a basic stage preceding the final life, such bodies would not exist. Each one is inhabited by a unique type of organic, basic, thinking creature. In every case, the organs differ according to the characteristics of the environment. Upon death, or transformation, these creatures enter the final life—immortality—and, aware of all mysteries except for the one, they move and act everywhere through simple will: dwelling not within the stars, which appear to us as the only tangible things and for which we foolishly believe space was created, but within SPACE itself—that infinity whose truly substantial vastness consumes the shadows of the stars, erasing them as non-existent from the view of the angels.

The final body therefore eludes our basic senses, and we observe only the shell that drops away, in decaying, from the inner form; not that inner form itself. However, this inner form, as well as the shell, is perceptible to those who have already attained the ultimate life.

It was roughly five minutes before eight when I took the patient’s hand and asked him to confirm, as clearly as possible, to Mr. L—l whether he, M. Valdemar, was fully prepared for me to attempt to mesmerize him in his current state.

He answered weakly, yet clearly enough to be heard, “Yes, I wish to be. I fear you have mesmerized”—adding immediately after: “I fear you have waited too long.”

As he spoke, I began the motions that I had previously discovered were most successful in calming him. He was clearly affected by the initial sideways sweep of my hand across his brow; however, despite my greatest efforts, no further visible change occurred until shortly after ten o'clock, when Doctors D—— and F—— arrived as scheduled. I briefly described my intentions to them, and since they offered no resistance, noting that the patient was already in the throes of death, I continued without hesitation—though I switched from lateral movements to downward ones, focusing my gaze entirely on the sufferer’s right eye.

By then, his pulse could not be detected, and his respiration was labored and rattling, occurring at thirty-second intervals.

This state remained largely unchanged for fifteen minutes. At the end of that time, however, a natural yet very profound sigh emerged from the dying man’s chest, and the rattling quality of his breathing vanished, though the pauses between breaths remained the same. The patient’s limbs were cold as ice.

At five minutes to eleven, I observed clear evidence of the mesmeric effect. The vacant, rolling gaze of his eyes shifted to that look of restless internal focus seen only in cases of somnambulism, which is impossible to misinterpret. With several quick sideways passes, I caused his eyelids to tremble as if in the early stages of sleep, and with a few more, I shut them entirely. I was not content with this, however, and persisted with the movements energetically, applying the full force of my will, until I had entirely rigidified the limbs of the sleeper after arranging them in a seemingly comfortable posture.

By this time, his pulse was undetectable and his breathing was heavy, labored, and occurred at half-minute intervals.

At the end of this period, however, a natural but very deep sigh escaped the dying man’s chest, and the labored breathing stopped—that is to say, the harshness was no longer apparent, though the intervals remained the same. The patient’s limbs were ice cold.

At five minutes to eleven, I noticed clear signs of the mesmeric influence. The glassy, rolling look of his eyes shifted to that expression of uneasy internal focus seen only in cases of sleep-walking, which is impossible to mistake. With a few quick sideways passes, I made his eyelids quiver as if he were drifting into sleep, and with a few more, I closed them completely. I was not satisfied with this, however, and continued the movements vigorously, exerting my full will until I had completely stiffened the limbs of the sleeper after arranging them in a seemingly comfortable position.

He still claimed to be quite willing and even eager to have it done, and he urged me to begin immediately. A male and a female nurse were present, but I did not feel comfortable performing such a task with only these people as witnesses, in case something went wrong. I therefore delayed the operation until about eight the next night, when the arrival of a medical student I knew, Mr. Theodore L—l, relieved me of my concerns. I had originally intended to wait for the doctors, but I was persuaded to proceed, first by M. Valdemar’s urgent requests, and second by my belief that I had no time to lose, as he was clearly fading quickly.

Mr. L—l kindly agreed to my request that he take notes on everything that happened, and it is from his records that the following account is mostly condensed or copied word for word.

It was about five minutes before eight when I took the patient’s hand and asked him to state as clearly as he could to Mr. L—l whether he was fully willing for me to attempt to mesmerize him in his current state.

He replied weakly, yet clearly enough to be heard, “Yes, I wish to be. I fear you have mesmerized”—adding immediately afterward, “I fear you have waited too long.”

As he spoke, I began the hand motions that I had previously found most effective in calming him. He was clearly affected by the first sideways stroke of my hand across his forehead, but although I used all my strength, no further visible effect occurred until some minutes after ten o’clock, when Doctors D—— and F—— arrived as scheduled. I explained my plan to them briefly, and since they raised no objections, noting that the patient was already in the final stages of death, I proceeded without hesitation. I switched from sideways motions to downward ones, focusing my gaze entirely on the sufferer’s right eye.

By this time, his pulse could not be felt, and his breathing was heavy and rattling, occurring at half-minute intervals.

This condition remained almost unchanged for fifteen minutes. At the end of this time, however, a natural but very deep sigh escaped the dying man’s chest, and the rattling sound of his breathing stopped, though the intervals between breaths remained the same. His limbs were icy cold.

At five minutes before eleven, I noticed clear signs of the mesmeric influence. The glassy, unfocused look in his eyes changed to that expression of uneasy internal focus seen only in sleep-walking cases, which is impossible to mistake. With a few quick sideways motions, I made his eyelids quiver as if he were beginning to fall asleep, and with a few more, I closed them completely. I was not satisfied with this, however, and continued the movements vigorously, using all my willpower until I had completely stiffened the limbs of the sleeping man after arranging them in a comfortable position.

Pluto—that was the feline’s name—was my preferred companion and playfellow. I alone provided his meals, and he accompanied me throughout the residence. It was actually a struggle to keep him from trailing me along the public thoroughfares.

Our bond persisted in this fashion for a number of years, throughout which my overall disposition and nature—by way of the Demon Intemperance—had (I am ashamed to admit) undergone a fundamental shift for the worse. I became, as time passed, increasingly sullen, short-tempered, and indifferent to the emotions of those around me. I permitted myself to speak harshly to my spouse. Eventually, I even resorted to physical abuse against her. My other animals, naturally, were forced to endure the shift in my temperament. I not only ignored but mistreated them. Regarding Pluto, however, I still held enough affection to keep me from abusing him, as I felt no hesitation in harming the rabbits, the monkey, or even the dog whenever they happened to cross my path. Yet my malady intensified—for what sickness compares to Alcohol!—and eventually even Pluto, who was by then aging and thus somewhat irritable, began to suffer the consequences of my hostility.

One evening, arriving home heavily inebriated from one of my usual spots in town, I imagined the cat was shunning me. I grabbed him; when, startled by my aggression, he gave my hand a minor scratch with his fangs. A demonic rage immediately seized me. I was no longer myself. My true spirit seemed to vanish from my body at once, and a malevolence more than diabolical, fueled by gin, surged through every nerve of my being. I pulled a pen-knife from my vest, unfolded it, gripped the wretched creature by the neck, and intentionally sliced one of its eyes from the socket! I flush, I burn, I tremble as I record this wretched act.

As sanity returned with the dawn—once I had slept off the effects of the previous night’s indulgence—I felt a sensation of mixed horror and regret for the transgression I had committed; yet it was, at best, a weak and ambiguous emotion, and my soul remained unmoved. I sank back into debauchery and quickly submerged all recollection of the act in wine.

Meanwhile, the cat gradually healed. The empty eye socket certainly looked ghastly, but he no longer seemed to be in any pain. He wandered the house as he always had, but, as one might anticipate, he darted away in sheer panic whenever I drew near. I retained enough of my former self to feel initially saddened by this clear aversion from a creature that had once adored me. Yet this sentiment soon yielded to annoyance. And then arrived, as if to ensure my ultimate and permanent ruin, the spirit of PERVERSITY. Philosophy ignores this impulse. Yet I am no more certain that my soul exists than I am that perversity is a fundamental drive of the human spirit—one of the core, inseparable faculties or feelings that shape the character of Man. Who has not, countless times, caught themselves performing a wicked or foolish deed for no reason other than the knowledge that they ought not to?

Pluto—that was the cat’s name—was my favorite pet and playmate. I was the only one who fed him, and he followed me everywhere I went around the house. It was even difficult to keep him from trailing me through the streets.

Our friendship continued this way for several years, during which my general temperament and character—due to the influence of the demon Alcohol—had (I blush to admit it) undergone a radical change for the worse. I grew, day by day, moodier, more irritable, and more indifferent to the feelings of others. I allowed myself to use harsh language toward my wife. Eventually, I even became physically violent toward her. My pets, naturally, were made to feel the change in my disposition. I not only neglected them but mistreated them. For Pluto, however, I still held enough regard to keep me from abusing him, as I had no hesitation in abusing the rabbits, the monkey, or even the dog when they accidentally or affectionately got in my way. My original soul seemed to flee my body at once, and a more than fiendish malice, fueled by gin, thrilled through every fiber of my frame. I took a penknife from my waistcoat pocket, opened it, grabbed the poor beast by the throat, and deliberately cut one of its eyes from the socket! I blush, I burn, I shudder as I write down this damnable atrocity.

When my reason returned in the morning—once I had slept off the effects of the night’s drinking—I felt a sentiment half of horror, half of remorse, for the crime I had committed; but it was, at best, a weak and ambiguous feeling, and my soul remained untouched. I plunged back into excess and soon drowned all memory of the deed in wine.

He went about the house as usual, but, as might be expected, fled in extreme terror whenever I approached. Meanwhile, the cat slowly recovered. The empty eye socket looked frightening, it is true, but he no longer seemed to be in pain. And then came, as if to ensure my final and total downfall, the spirit of perverseness. Philosophy does not account for this spirit. Confident, nonetheless, in the hidden nature of my hiding spot, I experienced no discomfort at all. The officers invited me to join them during their investigation. They searched every single cranny and crevice. Eventually, for the third or fourth occasion, they went down into the basement. I did not tremble in the slightest. My pulse remained as steady as that of a person sleeping in purity. I paced the cellar from one side to the other. I crossed my arms across my chest and strolled about with ease. The police were completely convinced and readied themselves to leave. The joy within me was too intense to be contained. I was desperate to utter at least a single remark to celebrate, and to make their belief in my innocence doubly certain.

“Gentlemen,” I finally stated, as the group climbed the stairs, “I am pleased to have quieted your doubts. I wish you all good health, and perhaps a bit more politeness. By the way, gentlemen, this—this is a very sturdily built residence.” (In my frantic urge to speak casually, I hardly understood what I was saying at all.)—“I might say an exceptionally well-built residence. These walls—are you leaving, gentlemen?—these walls are put together quite solidly;” and here, driven by a sheer madness of defiance, I struck hard with a walking stick I carried upon that exact section of the brickwork behind which rested the body of my beloved wife.

But may God protect and save me from the clutches of the Devil! No sooner had the echo of my strikes faded into quiet, than I was met with a sound from inside the grave!—a cry, initially stifled and fragmented, like the weeping of a youngster, and then rapidly growing into one prolonged, intense, and steady shriek, completely unnatural and monstrous—a howl—a wailing scream, part terror and part victory, such as could have emerged only from hell, combined from the throats of the damned in their suffering and the devils who rejoice in their ruin.

It is useless to describe my own reflections. Fainting, I reeled toward the far wall. For a single moment, the group on the stairs stood frozen, overcome by extreme fear and wonder. The next instant, a dozen strong men were laboring at the wall. It collapsed entirely. The body, already significantly decomposed and covered in blood, stood upright before the eyes of the onlookers. Upon its head, with a red open mouth and a single fiery eye, sat the repulsive creature whose cunning had lured me into homicide, and whose revealing cry had delivered me to the executioner. I had entombed the beast within the grave!

THE FALL OF THE HOUSE OF USHER

Son cœur est un luth suspendu;
Sitôt qu’on le touche il résonne..

—De Béranger.

Throughout the entirety of a dreary, somber, and silent autumn day, when the clouds pressed heavily upon the sky, I had been traveling by myself, on horseback, across a remarkably bleak region; and finally arrived, as the evening shadows approached, within sight of the sorrowful House of Usher. I cannot explain why—but, upon the initial view of the structure, a feeling of unbearable sadness filled my soul.

Confident, however, in the secrecy of my hiding spot, I felt no embarrassment at all. The officers asked me to join them in their search. They left no nook or corner unexplored. Eventually, for the third or fourth time, they went down into the cellar. I did not twitch a muscle. My heart beat as calmly as that of someone sleeping in innocence. I walked the cellar from end to end. I folded my arms across my chest and paced back and forth easily. The police were completely satisfied and prepared to leave. The joy in my heart was too strong to contain. I was burning to say just one word to celebrate my triumph and to make their belief in my innocence doubly certain.

“Gentlemen,” I said at last, as the group climbed the stairs, “I am pleased to have put your suspicions to rest. I wish you all good health and a little more courtesy. By the way, gentlemen, this—this is a very well-built house.” (In my frantic desire to say something casual, I barely knew what I was saying.)—“I might say an excellently well-built house. These walls—are you leaving, gentlemen?—these walls are solidly put together;” and here, through sheer reckless bravado, I rapped heavily with a cane I held in my hand against the very section of brickwork behind which stood the corpse of my wife.

But may God protect and deliver me from the fangs of the devil! No sooner had the echo of my blows faded into silence than I was answered by a voice from within the tomb! It was a cry, at first muffled and broken, like the sobbing of a child, and then quickly swelling into one long, loud, and continuous scream, completely unnatural and inhuman—a howl—a wailing shriek, half of horror and half of triumph, such as might have risen only from hell, coming together from the throats of the damned in their agony and the demons that rejoice in their damnation.

It is foolish to speak of my own thoughts. Fainting, I staggered to the opposite wall. For one instant, the group on the stairs remained motionless, paralyzed by extreme terror and awe. In the next, a dozen strong arms were tearing at the wall. It collapsed entirely. The corpse, already badly decayed and clotted with blood, stood upright before the eyes of the spectators. Upon its head, with a red, open mouth and a single eye of fire, sat the hideous beast whose cunning had seduced me into murder, and whose revealing voice had condemned me to the hangman. I had walled the monster up inside the tomb!

During the course of a dull, dark, and silent day in the autumn, when the clouds hung low in the sky, I had been riding alone through a strangely dreary stretch of country. Finally, as evening approached, I found myself within view of the melancholy House of Usher. I do not know why, but with the first glimpse of the building, a sense of unbearable gloom filled my spirit.

The extreme intensification of his familiar features and their customary expression was so profound that I questioned the identity of the man before me. The deathly whiteness of his complexion and the extraordinary brilliance of his eyes particularly shocked and intimidated me. Furthermore, his silky hair had been left to grow without care, and as it drifted around his face in a wild, delicate web, I found it impossible to associate its exotic, intricate appearance with any ordinary human being.

I was immediately struck by a lack of coherence in my friend’s behavior, which I soon realized stemmed from his vain, weak attempts to suppress a chronic state of nervous trembling. I had been somewhat expecting this, given his correspondence, my memories of his childhood habits, and my own deductions regarding his unique physical and temperamental makeup. His movements shifted between sudden animation and gloomy withdrawal. His tone fluctuated rapidly from a shaky uncertainty—when his vitality seemed entirely suppressed—to that specific style of forceful brevity: a sudden, heavy, deliberate, and hollow-sounding delivery, much like the leaden, controlled, and perfectly measured guttural speech heard from a hopeless alcoholic or a chronic opium user during their moments of peak agitation.

He spoke in this manner about the purpose of my arrival, his sincere wish to see me, and the comfort he hoped I would provide. He discussed his illness at length, describing it as a hereditary, constitutional affliction for which he saw no cure, though he quickly qualified it as a mere nervous condition that would surely vanish soon. It manifested through a variety of strange sensations. Some of these, as he described them, both intrigued and confused me, though perhaps his choice of words and his narrative style played a role. He suffered from a morbid sensitivity of the senses; he could only tolerate the blandest food, could only wear clothing of a specific fabric, found the scent of all flowers stifling, felt tortured by even dim light, and could only endure certain sounds produced by stringed instruments without feeling intense dread.

I discovered he was a captive to a strange, singular form of fear. “I will die,” he declared, “I must perish in this wretched madness. This is how I shall be destroyed. I do not fear future events themselves, but rather their consequences. I tremble at the thought of any incident, however minor, that might affect this unbearable agitation of my soul. I do not fear danger itself, only its ultimate result—terror. In this weak, pathetic state, I know the time will come when I must lose both my life and my sanity in a battle against the grim specter of FEAR.”

Additionally, through fragmented and vague suggestions, I gathered another peculiar aspect of his mental state. He was bound by superstitious beliefs regarding the house he occupied, which he had not left for many years. He spoke of an influence whose supposed power was described in terms too indistinct to repeat—an influence that the specific architecture and composition of his ancestral home had, through long endurance, exerted over his spirit. He claimed the physical appearance of the gray walls and towers, along with the dark lake they overlooked, had eventually shaped the very essence of his existence.

The features I knew so well were now so exaggerated, and the expressions they usually conveyed so altered, that I doubted who I was addressing. The ghastly paleness of his skin and the unnatural brightness of his eyes startled and even intimidated me. His hair had been left to grow wild, and as it floated around his face like delicate gossamer, I struggled to associate its strange, intricate appearance with any ordinary human being.

It was not surprising that his state frightened me—that it spread to me. I felt the wild effects of his strange yet compelling superstitions gradually taking hold of me.

It was particularly when I went to bed late on the seventh or eighth night after Lady Madeline had been placed in the vault that I felt the full weight of these emotions. Sleep avoided my bed as the hours slowly slipped away. I fought to reason away the anxiety that controlled me. I tried to convince myself that much, if not all, of my feeling was caused by the unsettling effect of the room's somber furnishings—the dark, ragged curtains, which, stirred by the breath of a gathering storm, swayed erratically against the walls and rustled restlessly around the bed's ornaments. Yet my attempts were in vain. An uncontrollable shivering slowly spread through my body, and eventually, a weight of groundless dread settled upon my heart. Shaking this off with a gasp and a struggle, I sat up on my pillows and, staring intently into the deep gloom of the room, listened—I know not why, except that an instinct urged me—to certain faint and vague noises that arrived, during the lulls in the storm, at long intervals, from an unknown source. Overwhelmed by a profound sense of horror, inexplicable yet unbearable, I dressed quickly (for I knew I would not sleep again that night) and tried to rouse myself from the pathetic state I had fallen into by pacing rapidly back and forth across the room.

I had only walked a few times when a soft footfall on a nearby staircase caught my notice. I soon identified it as Usher's. A moment later, he knocked softly at my door and walked in, carrying a lamp. His face was, as always, deathly pale—but, in addition, there was a kind of frantic joy in his eyes—a clearly suppressed hysteria in his entire manner. His appearance terrified me—but anything was better than the isolation I had endured for so long, and I actually greeted his arrival as a comfort.

“And you have not seen it?” he asked suddenly, after gazing around the room for a few moments in silence—“you have not seen it?—but, wait! you shall.” Saying this, and having carefully shielded his lamp, he rushed to one of the windows and flung it wide open to the storm.

The violent force of the incoming gale nearly knocked us off our feet. It was, in truth, a stormy yet strikingly beautiful night, one uniquely singular in its terror and its splendor. A whirlwind had seemingly gathered its strength nearby; for there were frequent and fierce shifts in the wind's direction; and the extreme thickness of the clouds (which hung so low they seemed to press against the house's turrets) did not stop us from seeing the lifelike speed with which they raced from every direction against one another, without ever drifting away into the distance.

At other times, I was forced to conclude that his behavior was merely the inexplicable result of madness, for I would watch him stare into empty space for hours, with an expression of intense focus, as if listening to some imaginary sound. It is no surprise that his condition terrified me—that it began to affect me as well. I felt the wild influence of his strange, yet compelling, superstitions slowly but surely taking hold of me.

It was specifically while going to bed late on the seventh or eighth night after we had placed Lady Madeline in the vault that I felt the full weight of these emotions. Sleep would not come, even as the hours dragged on. I struggled to reason away the nervousness that had taken control of me. I tried to convince myself that much, if not all, of what I felt was due to the unsettling influence of the room’s gloomy furniture—the dark, tattered curtains, which were whipped into motion by the gusts of a rising storm, swaying erratically against the walls and rustling uneasily around the bed’s decorations. But my efforts were useless. An uncontrollable tremor gradually spread through my body, and eventually, a crushing sense of groundless fear settled upon my heart. Shaking this off with a gasp and a struggle, I sat up against my pillows and, peering intently into the deep darkness of the room, listened—I know not why, except that an instinct prompted me—to certain low, indistinct sounds that came at long intervals during the pauses in the storm, though I could not tell where they originated. Overpowered by an intense feeling of horror, inexplicable yet unbearable, I quickly put on my clothes (for I knew I would not sleep again that night) and tried to shake off my pathetic state by pacing rapidly back and forth across the room.

I had only taken a few turns when a light step on the nearby staircase caught my attention. I soon recognized it as Usher’s. A moment later, he knocked gently at my door and entered, carrying a lamp. His face was, as usual, deathly pale—but there was also a kind of manic joy in his eyes and a clearly suppressed hysteria in his entire demeanor. His appearance horrified me—but anything was better than the solitude I had endured for so long, and I even welcomed his presence as a relief.

“And you haven’t seen it?” he asked abruptly, after staring around the room in silence for a few moments. “You haven’t seen it? But wait! You shall.” Speaking this way, and having carefully shielded his lamp, he hurried to one of the windows and threw it wide open to the storm.

The violent force of the incoming gust nearly knocked us off our feet. It was, in truth, a stormy yet strikingly beautiful night, one uniquely singular in its terror and its beauty. A whirlwind had apparently gathered its strength nearby, for the wind shifted direction frequently and violently. The extreme density of the clouds—which hung so low they seemed to press against the house’s turrets—did not prevent us from seeing the lifelike speed with which they raced from all directions, colliding with one another without ever moving off into the distance.

At other times, I was forced to conclude that his behavior was simply the result of inexplicable madness, for I would watch him stare into empty space for hours, appearing to listen to imaginary sounds with the deepest attention. It is no surprise that his condition terrified me and began to affect my own mind. I felt the wild influence of his strange and haunting superstitions slowly but surely taking hold of me.

It was late at night, seven or eight days after we had placed Lady Madeline in the vault, that I felt the full weight of these emotions. Sleep would not come, and the hours dragged on. I tried to reason away the nervousness that had taken control of me. I attempted to convince myself that my feelings were caused by the gloomy furniture of the room—the dark, tattered curtains that swayed back and forth on the walls, moved by the rising storm, and rustled against the bed. But my efforts were useless. An uncontrollable tremor spread through my body, and eventually, a heavy, groundless sense of dread settled upon my heart. Shaking this off with a gasp, I sat up on my pillows and peered into the deep darkness of the room. “The man rested upon the stone, supporting his head with his hand, and gazed out at the wasteland. He peered down at the low, restless brush, up at the towering ancient trees, further toward the whispering sky, and into the blood-red moon. I remained hidden near the lilies, watching the man’s movements. He shook in his loneliness, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“The man shifted his focus from the heavens to the bleak Zaire River, the sickly yellow currents, and the ghostly ranks of water-lilies. He hearkened to the soft moans of the lilies and the low murmurs rising from them. I stayed concealed in my shelter, observing him. He shook in his loneliness, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“I then descended into the depths of the marsh, wading deep into the thicket of lilies, and summoned the hippopotami that lived in the swampy recesses. Hearing my cry, the hippopotami arrived with the behemoth at the base of the rock, roaring with terrifying intensity beneath the moonlight. I stayed concealed in my shelter, observing him. He shook in his loneliness, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“I then cast a curse of chaos upon the elements; a terrifying storm brewed in the sky where previously there had been no breeze. The heavens turned bruised and livid from the storm’s fury; rain lashed the man’s head, the river surged, the waters were churned into froth, the lilies wailed in their beds, the woods collapsed under the gale, thunder crashed, lightning struck, and the rock swayed to its base. I stayed concealed in my shelter, observing him. He shook in his loneliness, yet as the night faded, he remained seated on the rock.

“Enraged, I cast a curse of silence upon the river, the lilies, the wind, the woods, the sky, the thunder, and the sighs of the lilies. They became cursed and fell silent. I examined the inscriptions on the rock, and they had transformed; the characters now read SILENCE.

It was at that moment that Prince Prospero, driven to madness by fury and the humiliation of his brief moment of fear, dashed quickly through the six rooms, though no one dared follow him due to the paralyzing dread that had overcome them all. He held a naked blade high and had charged, with fierce haste, to within a few feet of the retreating figure, when the stranger, having reached the far end of the velvet-draped room, spun around to face his pursuer. A piercing cry rang out—the dagger fell, shining, onto the black rug, and immediately afterward, Prince Prospero collapsed, dead. Then, gathering the frantic bravery of desperation, a crowd of the partygoers surged into the dark chamber and grabbed the masked figure, whose tall form stood still and upright in the shadow of the ebony clock, only to gasp in absolute terror upon discovering that the burial shrouds and corpse-like mask they had handled so roughly contained no solid body.

And then the presence of the Red Death was recognized. He had arrived like a burglar in the night. One by one, the revelers collapsed in the blood-stained halls of their celebration, each expiring in the hopeless position in which they fell. The life of the ebony clock flickered out along with the last of the merrymakers. The flames in the tripods died away. And Darkness, Decay, and the Red Death maintained absolute control over everything.

THE CASK OF AMONTILLADO.

I had endured the thousand slights of Fortunato as best I could; but when he dared to insult me, I swore to have my revenge. You, who understand my character so well, will not imagine, however, that I voiced any threat. Eventually, I would have my vengeance; this was a matter firmly decided—but the very certainty of my resolve ruled out any possibility of danger. I had to punish, but I had to punish without suffering consequences. A wrong remains unavenged when the retribution strikes the person seeking it. It is also unavenged if the avenger fails to make his identity known to the one who committed the offense.

It must be clear that I had never given Fortunato any reason to question my friendship, either through my words or my actions. I continued to smile at him as I always had, and he failed to notice that my smile was now inspired by the thought of his destruction.

Fortunato possessed a vulnerability—though in other respects he was a man to be both respected and feared. He took great pride in his expertise regarding wine. Few Italians truly possess the spirit of a connoisseur. Mostly, their passion is feigned to fit the occasion—to deceive British and Austrian millionaires. In the arts of painting and jewelry, Fortunato, like his peers, was a fraud—but regarding vintage wines, he was genuine. In this area, I was much like him: I was quite knowledgeable about Italian wines myself and purchased them in large quantities whenever possible.

It was around twilight, one evening during the peak of the carnival madness, that I met my friend. He greeted me with great enthusiasm, as he had been drinking heavily. The man was dressed in motley. He wore a tight, multi-colored suit, and his head was topped with a conical cap adorned with bells.

It was then that Prince Prospero, driven mad by rage and the shame of his brief moment of cowardice, rushed through the six rooms, while no one followed him due to the paralyzing terror that had gripped them all. He held a drawn dagger high and had approached, in a sudden burst of speed, to within three or four feet of the retreating figure, when the stranger, having reached the end of the velvet room, turned abruptly to face his pursuer. A sharp cry rang out—and the dagger fell, gleaming, onto the black carpet, upon which Prince Prospero immediately collapsed, dead. Then, summoning the desperate courage of the hopeless, a crowd of the revelers threw themselves into the black room and seized the masked figure, whose tall form stood still and upright within the shadow of the ebony clock. They gasped in absolute horror to find that the burial shroud and corpse-like mask, which they had handled so roughly, contained no solid body.

And now, the presence of the Red Death was acknowledged. He had arrived like a thief in the night. One by one, the revelers fell in the blood-stained halls of their party, dying in the same desperate positions in which they collapsed. The life of the ebony clock flickered out along with that of the last party-goer. The flames in the tripods died. And Darkness, Decay, and the Red Death held total dominion over everything.

I discarded countless plans, as their execution carried a risk of being discovered. Eventually, while perusing some French memoirs, I encountered a story regarding a nearly lethal sickness that befell Madame Pilau, caused by a candle that had been inadvertently poisoned. The concept immediately captured my imagination. I was aware of my victim’s custom of reading while in bed. I also knew that his room was cramped and lacked proper ventilation. However, I will not bore you with unnecessary particulars. I need not recount the simple tricks through which I replaced the candle in his bedroom holder with a wax light of my own manufacture. The following morning, he was found deceased in his bed, and the coroner’s official finding was “Death by the visitation of God.”

Having come into his property, everything proceeded smoothly for me for several years. The possibility of being found out never crossed my mind. I had personally and meticulously destroyed the remnants of the lethal candle. I had left no trace of evidence that could lead to my conviction, or even to any suspicion of my guilt. It is impossible to describe the profound sense of contentment that filled my heart as I contemplated my total safety. For a very long time, I took pleasure in this feeling. It provided me with more genuine joy than all the material benefits I gained from my transgression. Yet, eventually, a time came when this pleasant sensation transformed, by almost invisible degrees, into a persistent and troubling thought. It was distressing because it was persistent. I could hardly banish it for a moment. It is quite typical to be bothered by the repetition in our minds of the chorus of a mundane tune or some unremarkable fragments from an opera. We are no less tormented even if the song itself is pleasant or the operatic melody is excellent. In this way, I eventually found myself constantly dwelling on my security, whispering to myself the phrase, “I am safe.”

One day, while walking through the streets, I caught myself muttering these familiar words aloud. In a moment of irritation, I altered them to: “I am safe—I am safe—yes—provided I am not foolish enough to confess!”

As soon as I uttered these words, I felt a freezing sensation spread to my heart. I had encountered these bouts of perversity before (the nature of which I have tried to clarify), and I recalled clearly that I had never managed to overcome their influence. Now, my own impulsive suggestion that I might be foolish enough to admit to the murder I had committed stood before me like the phantom of the man I had killed, gesturing toward my own destruction.

Initially, I attempted to cast off this mental nightmare. I walked briskly—then faster—and finally, I began to run. I felt an uncontrollable urge to scream. Each subsequent wave of thought filled me with fresh dread, for, unfortunately, I understood all too well that to think, in my circumstances, was to be ruined. I continued to increase my speed.

Every attempt was unsuccessful. Numerous individuals who had been most vigorous in the hunt were beginning to slacken their efforts, succumbing to a dark despair. There appeared to be minimal hope for the infant—and even less for the mother. Yet, from the depths of that shadowy alcove previously noted as a segment of the Old Republican jail, situated opposite the Marchesa’s lattice, a person wrapped in a mantle emerged into the light. Pausing briefly at the edge of the dizzying drop, he dove straight into the canal. A moment later, as he stood upon the marble paving beside the Marchesa holding the still-living child, his cloak, weighed down by the soaking water, came loose. It fell in layers around his feet, revealing to the astonished onlookers the elegant figure of a very young man whose name was then famous throughout most of Europe.

The rescuer uttered no sound. But the Marchesa! She would surely take her child now—clasp it to her breast—embrace its small body and cover it with kisses. Sadly, someone else’s arms took the infant from the stranger—someone else’s arms carried it away, unnoticed, into the palace. And the Marchesa! Her lip—her lovely lip quivers; tears well up in her eyes—those eyes which, like the acanthus of Pliny, are soft and nearly liquid. Yes, tears gather in those eyes, and look! The entire woman vibrates to her core, and the statue has awakened to life. The whiteness of her marble face, the rising of her marble chest, and the very purity of her marble feet are suddenly flooded with a wave of uncontrollable crimson. A faint tremor runs through her delicate frame, like a soft breeze in Naples moving over rich silver lilies in the meadow.

Why should that lady flush? There is no reply to this question, other than the fact that, in the frantic haste and panic of a mother’s heart, she had departed the privacy of her boudoir without putting on her slippers and had completely failed to drape her Venetian shoulders as was proper. What other explanation could exist for her blushing, for the look in those wild, pleading eyes, for the strange agitation of that heaving chest, or for the convulsive squeeze of that shaking hand—the hand that, as Mentoni entered the palace, brushed by chance against the stranger’s hand? What reason could there be for the low, the remarkably low pitch of those hollow words the lady spoke hastily while saying goodbye? “You have won,” she said, or perhaps the murmuring water misled me, “you have won—one hour past sunrise—we shall meet—so be it!”

The commotion had faded, the palace lights had vanished, and the stranger, whom I now identified, remained alone on the stones. He trembled with immense agitation, his gaze scanning the area for a gondola. I felt compelled to offer him the use of mine, and he accepted the courtesy. After securing an oar at the water-gate, we traveled together to his home, during which time he quickly regained his composure and discussed our previous brief acquaintance with apparent warmth.

Why should that lady blush? There is no answer to this, except that in the frantic haste and terror of a mother’s heart, she had left the privacy of her boudoir without putting on her slippers and had completely forgotten to drape her Venetian shoulders as was proper. What other possible reason could there be for her blushing, for the look in those wild, pleading eyes, for the unusual agitation of her heaving chest, or for the convulsive pressure of that trembling hand—the hand that, as Mentoni turned into the palace, accidentally brushed against the stranger’s? What reason could there be for the low, strangely quiet tone of the meaningless words she whispered hurriedly while saying goodbye? “You have won,” she said, or the murmurs of the water deceived me; “you have won—one hour after sunrise—we shall meet—so let it be!”

The commotion had died down, the lights inside the palace had faded, and the stranger, whom I now recognized, stood alone on the stones. He shook with intense agitation, his eyes scanning the area for a gondola. I could do no less than offer him the use of my own, and he accepted the gesture. Having secured an oar at the water-gate, we traveled together to his residence, while he quickly regained his composure and spoke of our previous brief acquaintance with apparent warmth.

Every effort was in vain. Many of those most active in the search were giving up, overcome by gloomy sorrow. There seemed little hope for the child—and even less for the mother. But then, from the dark alcove mentioned earlier as part of the Old Republican prison, facing the Marchesa’s window, a figure wrapped in a cloak stepped into the light. Pausing briefly at the edge of the steep drop, he plunged headlong into the canal. A moment later, he stood on the marble stones beside the Marchesa, holding the still-living child. His cloak, heavy with water, came undone and fell around his feet, revealing to the astonished crowd the graceful form of a very young man whose name was then famous throughout most of Europe.

The rescuer said nothing. But the Marchesa! Surely she would take her child, press it to her heart, hold it close, and shower it with affection. Alas, someone else took the child from the stranger, carrying it away unnoticed into the palace. And the Marchesa! Her beautiful lip trembled, and tears gathered in her eyes—eyes that were soft and almost liquid. Yes, tears filled those eyes, and suddenly the woman came to life, as if a statue had begun to breathe. The paleness of her marble face, the swelling of her chest, and the purity of her feet were suddenly flushed with a deep crimson. A slight shudder passed through her delicate frame, like a gentle breeze in Naples blowing over silver lilies in the grass.

Why should that lady blush? There is no answer, except that in the frantic haste and terror of a mother’s heart, she had left her private room without putting on her slippers and had forgotten to cover her shoulders with the proper shawl. What other reason could there be for her blushing, for the look in her wild, pleading eyes, for the unusual pounding of her heart, or for the convulsive pressure of her trembling hand—the hand that accidentally touched the stranger’s as Mentoni turned toward the palace? What reason could there be for the low, quiet tone of the meaningless words she whispered as she hurried to say goodbye? “You have conquered,” she said, or perhaps the sound of the water deceived me, “you have conquered—one hour after sunrise—we shall meet—so let it be!”

The commotion died down, the lights in the palace faded, and the stranger, whom I now recognized, stood alone on the stones. He shook with intense agitation, his eyes searching for a gondola. I could do no less than offer him mine, and he accepted the favor. After getting an oar at the water-gate, we traveled together to his home. He quickly regained his composure and spoke of our previous brief acquaintance with great apparent warmth.

“Come,” he finally remarked, pivoting toward a table of heavy, ornate silver, which held a few strangely colored goblets and two large Etruscan vases, crafted in the same unusual style as the one in the painting’s foreground, and containing what I took to be Johannisberger. “Come,” he said suddenly, “let us drink! It is early—but let us drink. It is truly early,” he added, lost in thought, as a golden cherub with a heavy hammer caused the room to echo with the first hour past dawn: “It is truly early—but what does it signify? Let us drink! Let us offer a libation to that solemn sun which these bright lamps and incense burners are so desperate to suppress!” After making me toast him with a full glass, he quickly downed several goblets of the wine in a row.

“To dream,” he went on, returning to his rambling manner of speaking, while holding one of the grand vases up to the brilliant glow of an incense burner, “to dream has been my life’s work. I have therefore constructed for myself, as you observe, a sanctuary of dreams. Could I have built a better one in the center of Venice? You see around you, it is true, a chaotic mix of architectural styles. The purity of Ionia is insulted by ancient designs, and Egyptian sphinxes lie stretched across golden rugs. Yet the result only disturbs the faint-hearted. Rules of location, and particularly of time, are the phantoms that keep people from appreciating the magnificent. I was once a decorator myself; but that height of absurdity has grown tiresome to my spirit. All this is now better suited to my needs. Like these arabesque incense burners, my soul is twisting in flames, and the madness of this setting is preparing me for the more intense visions of that realm of true dreams toward which I am now swiftly traveling.” He stopped suddenly, lowered his head to his chest, and appeared to be listening to a noise I could not detect. Finally, straightening his body, he gazed upward and recited the verses of the Bishop of Chichester:

“Stay for me there! I will not fail
To meet thee in that hollow vale.”

Immediately after, yielding to the wine’s influence, he collapsed at full length onto a couch.

A rapid footfall sounded on the stairs, followed quickly by a loud knock at the door. I was hurrying to prevent another interruption when a servant from Mentoni’s house rushed into the room, stammering in a voice thick with distress the disjointed words, “My mistress!—my mistress!—Poisoned!—poisoned! Oh, beautiful—oh, beautiful Aphrodite!”

Confused, I rushed to the couch and tried to wake the sleeper to the shocking news. But his body was stiff—his lips were pale—his once-bright eyes were fixed in death. I reeled back toward the table—my hand struck a shattered and scorched goblet—and the full, horrifying reality dawned upon me.

I was destined for the latter. My nerves had been shattered by prolonged suffering, to the point where the sound of my own voice made me shudder, rendering me a perfect candidate for the torment that lay ahead. Trembling in every part of my body, I felt my way back to the wall, determined to die there rather than face the horrors of the pits, which my mind now envisioned scattered throughout the cell. In a different state of mind, I might have had the bravery to finish my agony by leaping into one of these chasms, but I was now a complete coward. Furthermore, I could not erase from my memory what I had read regarding these pits—that a quick death was never part of their most gruesome design. Mental distress kept me awake for many hours, but eventually, I drifted off again. Upon waking, I discovered a loaf of bread and a pitcher of water beside me, just as before. A fierce thirst overwhelmed me, and I drained the container in one gulp. It must have been spiked, for I grew uncontrollably sleepy almost immediately. A profound slumber overcame me, resembling death itself. I have no idea how long it endured, but when I opened my eyes again, the surroundings were discernible. A strange, sulfurous glow, the source of which I could not initially identify, allowed me to perceive the scale and appearance of the dungeon. I had been significantly wrong about its size. The entire perimeter of the walls was no more than twenty-five yards. For a few minutes, this realization caused me unnecessary distress; how futile! What could matter less, given the dire situation surrounding me, than the simple measurements of my cell? Yet my mind fixated on these trivialities, and I occupied myself trying to explain the mistake I had made in my estimation. The reality finally dawned on me. During my initial exploration, I had walked fifty-two paces before I fell; I must have been within a step or two of the piece of fabric, meaning I had nearly completed the circuit of the room. I then slept, and upon waking, I must have retraced my path, leading me to believe the circuit was nearly twice its actual length. My mental confusion kept me from noticing that I had started my journey with the wall on my left and finished with it on my right. I had also been misled regarding the shape of the space. While feeling my way, I had encountered numerous angles, leading me to conclude it was highly irregular; such is the power of total darkness on someone waking from a stupor! The angles were merely a few shallow indentations or niches at irregular intervals. The prison was essentially square. What I had perceived as stone masonry now appeared to be iron or some other metal, formed into massive plates whose seams created the depressions. The entire surface of this metallic enclosure was crudely painted with all the grotesque and unsettling imagery that monastic superstition regarding the grave has produced.

I took this lesson deeply to heart. My every movement was surely under observation. Free!—I had merely avoided one agonizing death only to be handed over to something far worse. With that realization, I cast my eyes anxiously across the iron boundaries enclosing me. It was clear that something unusual—a transformation I could not initially grasp—had occurred within the room. For several minutes of hazy, shaking distraction, I occupied myself with useless, disjointed guesses. During this time, I first noticed the source of the sulfurous glow lighting the chamber. It came from a crack, roughly half an inch wide, running entirely around the prison at the bottom of the walls, which appeared, and were, entirely detached from the floor. I tried, though fruitlessly, to peer through the opening.

As I stood up from this effort, the secret of the room's change suddenly dawned on me. I had noted that while the outlines of the wall figures were clear enough, their colors seemed hazy and vague. These hues had now taken on, and were steadily gaining, a shocking and intense brightness, giving the ghostly, hellish images a look that might have shaken even stronger nerves than mine. Demon eyes, possessing a wild and ghastly life, stared at me from countless spots where none had been seen before, shining with the lurid gleam of a fire I could not force myself to believe was fake.

Fake!—Even as I inhaled, the scent of heated iron reached my nose! A stifling smell filled the cell! A deeper radiance settled with every second into the eyes watching my suffering! A more vivid shade of red spread across the painted scenes of blood. I panted! I struggled for air! There was no mistaking the intent of my torturers—oh! most cruel! oh! most devilish of men! I recoiled from the burning metal toward the middle of the room. Amidst thoughts of the fiery ruin that loomed, the memory of the well's coolness felt like a soothing balm. I ran to its lethal edge. I cast my straining gaze downward. The light from the glowing ceiling illuminated its deepest depths. Yet, for one frantic moment, my mind refused to grasp the meaning of what I saw. Finally, it forced—it fought its way into my consciousness—it seared itself into my trembling intellect. Oh! for a voice to cry out!—oh! horror!—oh! any horror but this! With a scream, I fled from the brink and hid my face in my hands—sobbing uncontrollably.

The Inquisitorial vengeance had been accelerated by my double escape, and there was to be no more playing with the King of Terrors.

For a short time, everyone stood frozen in shock, yet the pressing nature of the situation soon brought back their composure. It became clear that Mr. Stapleton still lived, though he had fainted. After being given ether, he recovered and quickly returned to health and his companions—though they were kept ignorant of his revival until there was no longer any risk of him falling ill again. One can imagine their amazement and overwhelming joy.

The most chilling aspect of this event, however, lies in Mr. S.’s own claims. He maintains that he was never entirely unconscious; he insists that, in a dim and muddled way, he perceived everything that occurred from the time his doctors declared him dead until he collapsed in the hospital. “I am alive” were the words he had struggled to speak in his desperation upon realizing he was in the dissecting room.

It would be simple to provide many more such accounts, but I will refrain, as we do not require them to prove that people are sometimes buried while still alive. When we consider how seldom we are able to uncover such cases, we must concede that they likely happen often without our knowledge. In fact, whenever a cemetery is disturbed for any significant reason, skeletons are almost always discovered in positions that provoke the most terrifying suspicions.

The suspicion is indeed dreadful, but the fate itself is far worse! It can be stated with certainty that no situation is better suited to cause the ultimate physical and mental agony than being interred while still breathing. The suffocating pressure on the chest, the foul odors rising from the moist soil, the shroud clinging to the skin, the tight confinement of the coffin, the total darkness, the crushing silence, and the unseen but felt presence of the grave worm—these horrors, combined with thoughts of the world above and the memory of loved ones who would rescue us if they only knew, yet who will never be informed of our plight, create a level of unbearable terror that even the boldest mind must shrink from. We know of no greater agony on Earth, and we can imagine nothing more hideous in the depths of Hell. Consequently, all stories on this subject possess a deep interest, which relies appropriately on our belief in their truth. What I am about to relate comes from my own direct and personal experience.

For many years, I have suffered from a strange condition that doctors, for lack of a better name, have labeled catalepsy. While the underlying causes and the precise nature of this ailment remain obscure, its outward symptoms are well recognized.
