After being given ether, he revived and was quickly restored to health and to the company of his friends—from whom, however, all knowledge of his resuscitation was withheld until a relapse was no longer a concern. Their wonder—their ecstatic astonishment—can be imagined.

The most thrilling detail of this incident, however, lies in what Mr. S. himself asserts. He declares that at no point was he entirely unconscious—that, dully and confusedly, he was aware of everything that happened to him, from the moment he was pronounced dead by his doctors to the moment he collapsed on the hospital floor. “I am alive,” were the misunderstood words he had tried to utter in his desperation upon realizing he was in the dissecting room.

It would be easy to multiply such stories, but I refrain, for we have no need of them to prove that premature burials occur. When we consider how rarely, by the nature of the case, we have the power to detect them, we must admit that they may frequently happen without our knowledge. In truth, a graveyard is rarely disturbed for any reason to any great extent without skeletons being found in positions that suggest the most fearful suspicions.

Fearful indeed is the suspicion, but the fate is more fearful! It can be stated without hesitation that no event is so terribly well-suited to inspire the height of physical and mental distress as burial before death. The unendurable pressure on the lungs, the stifling fumes from the damp earth, the clinging of the burial shroud, the rigid embrace of the narrow coffin, the blackness of absolute night, the silence like an overwhelming sea, and the unseen but palpable presence of the decay—these things, combined with thoughts of the air and grass above, the memory of dear friends who would rush to save us if only they knew our fate, and the consciousness that they can never be informed—that our hopeless lot is that of the truly dead—these considerations, I say, carry into the still-beating heart a degree of appalling and intolerable horror from which the most daring imagination must recoil. We know of nothing so agonizing on Earth; we can dream of nothing half so hideous in the depths of the lowest Hell. Thus, all narratives on this topic have a profound interest; an interest, however, which, through the sacred awe of the subject itself, very properly and uniquely depends on our conviction of the truth of the matter narrated. What I have now to tell is of my own actual knowledge—of my own positive and personal experience.

For several years, I had been subject to attacks of the singular disorder that physicians have agreed to call catalepsy, for lack of a more definitive title. Although both the immediate and underlying causes, and even the actual diagnosis of this disease, remain mysterious, its obvious and apparent nature is sufficiently well understood.

His words were unclear, but he definitely spoke them; the syllables were distinct. After speaking, he collapsed heavily to the floor.

For a few moments, everyone was frozen in awe, but the urgency of the situation soon helped them regain their composure. It was clear that Mr. Stapleton was alive, though he had fainted. After being given ether, he revived and quickly returned to health and the company of his friends. However, they were kept in the dark about his recovery until there was no longer any risk of him relapsing. Their wonder and joyful astonishment can be imagined.

The most thrilling detail of this incident, however, lies in what Mr. S. himself claims. He declares that he was never completely unconscious. He says that, although dully and confusedly, he was aware of everything that happened to him from the moment his doctors declared him dead until the moment he collapsed on the hospital floor. "I am alive" were the misunderstood words he had tried to say in his desperation upon realizing he was in the dissecting room.

It would be easy to list many more stories like these, but I will refrain. We do not need them to prove that people are sometimes buried alive. When we consider how rarely we are able to detect these cases, we must admit that they likely happen often without our knowledge. In fact, whenever a graveyard is disturbed for any reason, skeletons are frequently found in positions that suggest the most terrifying suspicions.

The suspicion is indeed fearful, but the fate itself is even worse! It can be stated without hesitation that no event is better suited to cause the ultimate physical and mental distress than being buried before death. The unbearable pressure on the lungs, the suffocating fumes from the damp earth, the clinging burial clothes, the rigid embrace of the narrow coffin, the absolute blackness of the night, the silence that overwhelms like a sea, and the unseen but felt presence of the grave worms—these things, combined with thoughts of the air and grass above, the memory of dear friends who would rush to save us if they only knew our fate, and the realization that they can never be informed—that our hopeless situation is that of the truly dead—these thoughts carry into a heart that is still beating a degree of appalling and intolerable horror from which the most daring imagination must recoil. We know of nothing so agonizing on Earth, and we can dream of nothing half as hideous in the depths of Hell. Therefore, all stories on this topic have a profound interest, an interest that, because of the sacred awe of the subject, depends on our belief that the story is true. What I am about to tell is from my own actual knowledge and personal experience.

For several years, I have suffered from attacks of a strange disorder that doctors have agreed to call catalepsy, for lack of a better name. Although the immediate and underlying causes, and even the exact diagnosis of this disease, remain mysterious, its obvious and apparent nature is well understood.

Evening drew near, and a tempest caught us. We found our only refuge in the cabin of a small vessel anchored in the current, which was filled with garden soil. We made do with the situation and stayed the night on the boat. I rested in one of the two bunks—and the bunks of a sloop of sixty or twenty tons hardly require a description. The one I used lacked any bedding. It was exactly eighteen inches wide, and the space between its base and the deck above was identical. I struggled immensely to wedge myself inside. Even so, I slept deeply, and my entire vision—for it was neither a dream nor a nightmare—sprang naturally from my physical surroundings, my usual patterns of thought, and the struggle I mentioned to gather my wits and recover my memory long after waking. The men who roused me were the sloop’s crew and some workers hired to unload it. The earthy scent originated from the cargo itself. The cloth around my jaw was a silk scarf I had wrapped around my head, lacking my usual nightcap.

The suffering I endured, however, was certainly, for that duration, equal to the experience of being buried alive. It was terrifying and unimaginably ghastly; yet, out of this evil came good, as the sheer intensity of the experience forced a necessary shift in my spirit. My soul gained strength and resilience. I ventured out. I engaged in active exercise. I inhaled the open air of the heavens. I focused on things other than death. I threw away my medical texts. I burned my copy of Buchan. I stopped reading Night Thoughts, pretentious nonsense about graveyards, or frightening stories like this one. In essence, I transformed into a new person and began living a real life. From that significant night, I cast off my fears of the grave forever, and with them disappeared the cataleptic condition, which perhaps had been less a result of those fears than their primary cause.

There are times when, even to the rational mind, the world of our sorrowful humanity can appear like a hell—but the human imagination is no Carathis, capable of exploring every cavern without consequence. Sadly, the dark army of burial terrors cannot be dismissed as entirely imaginary—but, like the demons who accompanied Afrasiab on his journey down the Oxus, they must be left to sleep, or they will consume us; they must be allowed to rest, or we are lost.

From birth until death, a wind of good fortune carried my friend Ellison. I do not use the term prosperity in a strictly material sense. I mean it as a synonym for happiness. The man I describe seemed destined to illustrate the philosophies of Turgot, Price, Priestley, and Condorcet—to prove through a single life what has been considered the impossible dream of those who believe in human perfection.

I read no more Night Thoughts, no nonsense about graveyards, and no ghost stories like this one. In short, I became a new man and lived a man’s life. From that memorable night, I dismissed my fears of the grave forever, and with them vanished the cataleptic disorder, which perhaps had been less the consequence than the cause of those fears.

During our conversation, my companion recited several excerpts from an author on the subject of landscape gardening who is considered to have handled the topic effectively:

“There are essentially only two modes of landscape gardening: the natural and the artificial. The former attempts to evoke the primitive charm of the countryside by aligning its methods with the local terrain, nurturing trees that complement the nearby slopes or flatlands, and identifying and applying those subtle connections of scale, ratio, and hue that, while obscured from the casual onlooker, are apparent everywhere to the seasoned naturalist. The outcome of the natural approach is manifested more in the lack of flaws and inconsistencies—in the dominance of a wholesome balance and arrangement—than in the production of any particular marvels or feats. The artificial mode possesses as many variations as there are individual preferences to satisfy. It maintains a general connection to the diverse architectural traditions. There are the grand paths and secluded spots of Versailles, the terraces of Italy, and a diverse, blended old English style that relates to domestic Gothic or Elizabethan architecture. Regardless of the criticisms leveled against the excesses of artificial landscape gardening, an infusion of deliberate art into a garden setting enhances its beauty significantly. This is satisfying to the sight, due to the display of structure and intent, and it also carries a moral weight. A terrace featuring an aged, mossy railing immediately conjures images of the graceful figures who walked there in past eras. Even the most minor display of art serves as proof of human attention and concern.”

“Based on my previous remarks,” Ellison stated, “you will grasp that I dismiss the notion, stated here, of restoring the landscape’s original beauty. The inherent beauty is never as profound as that which can be added. Naturally, everything hinges on choosing a location with potential. The comments regarding identifying and utilizing subtle relations of size, proportion, and color represent one of those vague linguistic habits that merely mask imprecise thinking. The quoted statement could signify anything or nothing, and it provides no real direction. The claim that the true achievement of the natural style is found more in the omission of faults and contradictions than in the generation of unique wonders is a concept better suited to the limited understanding of the masses than to the passionate visions of a creative genius. The negative value proposed belongs to that timid school of criticism which, in literature, would deify Addison. In reality, while the virtue defined by the simple avoidance of wrongdoing speaks directly to the intellect and can therefore be defined by regulations, the higher virtue, which burns within the act of creation, can only be understood through its outcomes. Rules apply only to the merits of restraint—to the virtues of omission. Beyond these, critical theory can only offer suggestions. We might be taught how to construct a ‘Cato,’ but we are told in vain how to conceive a Parthenon or an ‘Inferno.’ Once the work is finished, however—the miracle achieved—the ability to appreciate it becomes widespread. The sophists of the negative school, who, lacking the power to create, have mocked the act of creation, are now the most vocal in their praise.”

“There are essentially only two styles of landscape gardening: the natural and the artificial. This is pleasing partly to the eye, through the display of order and design, and partly for moral reasons. A terrace with an old, moss-covered railing immediately brings to mind the elegant figures who walked there in years past. Even the slightest display of art serves as evidence of care and human interest.”

Deeper within the estate, the view is blocked by a dense curtain of leaves. These details become apparent as the canoe slowly nears what I termed the vista's entrance. Upon approaching it, however, the chasm-like look disappears; a fresh passage from the bay emerges to the left, where the wall also curves, continuing to track the river's path. One cannot see far down this new channel, as the stream and the wall both veer left until they are obscured by foliage. The boat, however, drifts effortlessly into the twisting waterway, where the bank across from the wall mirrors the one in the previous straight stretch. High hills, occasionally reaching mountain heights and blanketed in wild, lush greenery, continue to enclose the landscape. Drifting forward with slightly increased speed, the traveler, after several sharp turns, finds his path seemingly blocked by a massive gate, or perhaps a door of polished gold, intricately carved and patterned, reflecting the sun's direct rays as it sets, with a brilliance that appears to set the surrounding woods ablaze. This gate is set into the high wall, which here seems to span the river perpendicularly. Moments later, it becomes clear that the main river continues in a wide, gentle curve to the left, with the wall following it, while a significant branch of the water diverges, flowing with a soft ripple beneath the door and vanishing from sight. The canoe enters this smaller channel and nears the gate. Its heavy wings open slowly with a musical sound. The boat passes between them and begins a swift descent into a massive amphitheater surrounded by purple mountains, their bases washed by a shining river along their entire perimeter. Suddenly, the entire Paradise of Arnheim reveals itself. A burst of captivating music fills the air, alongside an overwhelming scent of strange, sweet perfumes. There is a dreamlike visual blend of tall, thin Eastern trees, dense thickets, flocks of gold and crimson birds, lily-bordered lakes, and meadows filled with violets, tulips, poppies, hyacinths, and tuberoses. Interwoven lines of silver brooks run through it all, and rising chaotically from the center is a collection of semi-Gothic, semi-Saracenic buildings, appearing to hang miraculously in the air, sparkling in the crimson sunset with countless oriels, minarets, and spires, looking like the ghostly creation of Sylphs, Fairies, Genii, and Gnomes. A Pendant to “The Domain of Arnheim.” While walking through a couple of river counties in New York last summer, I found myself, as evening approached, rather uncertain about the route I was taking. The terrain was notably hilly, and my path, for the previous hour, had twisted so confusingly in its attempt to remain within the valleys that I could no longer tell which way the pleasant village of B— lay, where I had planned to spend the night. The sun had barely appeared throughout the day, which had nonetheless been uncomfortably hot.

Further back within the estate, the view is blocked by an impenetrable screen of foliage. These details are observed as the canoe gradually approaches what I have called the gate of the vista. As I draw closer, however, its chasm-like appearance vanishes; a new outlet from the bay is discovered to the left, in which direction the wall is also seen to sweep, still following the general course of the stream. The eye cannot see very far down this new opening, for the stream, accompanied by the wall, continues to bend to the left until both are swallowed up by the leaves.

The boat, nevertheless, glides magically into the winding channel; here, the shore opposite the wall is found to resemble the one opposite the wall in the straight vista. Lofty hills, rising occasionally into mountains and covered with wild, lush vegetation, still enclose the scene.

Floating gently onward, but with slightly increased speed, the voyager, after many short turns, finds his progress apparently blocked by a gigantic gate, or rather a door of polished gold, elaborately carved and decorated, reflecting the direct rays of the now fast-sinking sun with a brilliance that seems to wreath the entire surrounding forest in flames. This gate is set into the lofty wall, which here appears to cross the river at a right angle. In a few moments, however, it becomes clear that the main body of the water still sweeps in a gentle and extensive curve to the left, with the wall following it as before, while a stream of considerable volume, diverging from the main one, makes its way with a slight ripple under the door and is thus hidden from sight. The canoe enters the smaller channel and approaches the gate. Its heavy wings are slowly and musically opened. The boat glides between them and begins a rapid descent into a vast amphitheater entirely surrounded by purple mountains, whose bases are washed by a gleaming river throughout the full extent of their circuit. Meanwhile, the entire Paradise of Arnheim bursts into view. There is a gush of entrancing melody; there is an overwhelming sense of strange, sweet odor; there is a dream-like intermingling to the eye of tall, slender Eastern trees, dense shrubberies, flocks of golden and crimson birds, lily-fringed lakes, meadows of violets, tulips, poppies, hyacinths, and tuberoses, long intertangled lines of silver streamlets, and, rising confusedly from amid all, a mass of semi-Gothic, semi-Saracenic architecture, appearing to sustain itself by miracle in mid-air, glittering in the red sunlight with a hundred oriels, minarets, and pinnacles, and seeming the phantom handiwork of the Sylphs, Fairies, Genii, and Gnomes.

During a walking trip last summer through one or two of the river counties of New York, I found myself, as the day declined, somewhat confused about the road I was following. The land undulated very remarkably, and my path, for the last hour, had wound about so confusedly in its effort to stay in the valleys that I no longer knew in what direction lay the sweet village of B——, where I had decided to stop for the night. The sun had scarcely shone—strictly speaking—during the day, which had nevertheless been unpleasantly warm.

Further back in the domain, the view is blocked by a thick screen of leaves. These details are noticed as the canoe slowly approaches what I have called the gate of the vista. As I draw closer, however, its chasm-like appearance disappears; a new exit from the bay is revealed to the left, where the wall also curves, following the general path of the stream. The eye cannot see far down this new opening, as the stream and the wall both bend to the left until they are hidden by the foliage.

The boat, however, glides smoothly into the winding channel. Here, the shore opposite the wall looks just like the shore in the straight section of the vista. Tall hills, sometimes rising into mountains and covered in wild, lush vegetation, continue to enclose the scene.

Floating gently forward, but moving slightly faster, the traveler makes several short turns before his path seems blocked by a massive gate, or rather a door of polished gold. It is intricately carved and reflects the rays of the setting sun with a brilliance that makes the surrounding forest look as if it is on fire. This gate is set into the high wall, which appears to cross the river at a right angle. In a few moments, however, it becomes clear that the main body of water continues in a wide, gentle curve to the left, with the wall following it as before. Meanwhile, a smaller, significant stream branches off from the main one and flows with a light ripple under the door, disappearing from sight. The canoe enters this smaller channel and approaches the gate. Its heavy wings slowly open with a musical sound. The boat glides between them and begins a rapid descent into a vast amphitheater surrounded by purple mountains, whose bases are washed by a gleaming river. Suddenly, the entire Paradise of Arnheim appears. There is a burst of enchanting music and an overwhelming sense of strange, sweet scents. The scene is a dream-like mix of tall, slender Eastern trees, thick shrubberies, flocks of golden and crimson birds, lily-fringed lakes, and meadows filled with violets, tulips, poppies, hyacinths, and tuberoses. Long, tangled lines of silver streams run through it all, and rising from the center is a mass of semi-Gothic, semi-Saracenic architecture that seems to hang miraculously in mid-air. It glitters in the red sunlight with a hundred windows, minarets, and pinnacles, appearing as the phantom creation of Sylphs, Fairies, Genii, and Gnomes.

LANDOR’S COTTAGE

A Companion to “The Domain of Arnheim”

During a walking trip last summer through a few river counties in New York, I found myself, as the day ended, somewhat confused about the road I was taking. The land was remarkably hilly, and my path for the last hour had wound back and forth so confusingly in its attempt to stay in the valleys that I no longer knew which way to go to reach the pleasant village of B—, where I had planned to stay for the night. The sun had barely shone all day, which had nevertheless been unpleasantly warm.

From that point on, my word became absolute within the home; while still at an age when most youngsters have barely left their infancy, I was granted the freedom to follow my own desires, effectively becoming the sovereign of my own conduct.

My primary memories of schooling are linked to a sprawling, sprawling Elizabethan estate situated in a hazy English hamlet, a place filled with immense, twisted trees and buildings of extreme antiquity. Truly, that aged town was a dreamlike and tranquil setting. Even now, in my imagination, I sense the invigorating coolness of its shaded paths, breathe in the scent of its countless gardens, and feel a renewed, inexplicable joy at the low, resonant tolling of the church bell, which would shatter the quiet of the dim air surrounding the sleeping, intricate Gothic steeple every hour.

It provides me with as much satisfaction as I can currently hope to find to linger on the smallest details of that school and its affairs. Drowning in sorrow—sorrow that is, unfortunately, all too genuine—I hope to be forgiven for finding solace, however brief or slight, in these wandering recollections. These memories, though entirely mundane and perhaps even absurd, take on a special significance in my mind because they are tied to a time and place where I first sensed the vague warnings of the fate that would eventually consume me. Let me, therefore, recall them.

The building, as I noted, was ancient and asymmetrical. The property was vast, surrounded by a tall, sturdy brick wall topped with mortar and jagged glass. This fortress-like barrier defined our boundaries; we only saw what lay beyond it three times a week—once on Saturday afternoons, when we were escorted by two teachers for short, group walks through nearby fields, and twice on Sundays, when we were marched in the same rigid fashion to attend morning and evening services at the village's single church. The head of our school served as the minister of this church. With what profound amazement and confusion I used to watch him from our distant gallery pew as he climbed the pulpit with slow, grave steps! This holy man, with his expression of calm kindness, his sleek and flowing clerical vestments, and his wig, so precisely powdered, stiff, and enormous—could this truly be the same person who, just before, had wielded a rod with a sour expression and in stained clothing to enforce the harsh rules of our school? Oh, a massive contradiction, far too strange to ever resolve!

At one corner of the heavy wall stood an even heavier gate. It was reinforced with iron studs and topped with sharp metal spikes. How deeply it filled us with dread! It was only opened for those three scheduled departures and returns I mentioned; each time its massive hinges groaned, we found a wealth of mystery—a subject for serious discussion or even deeper contemplation.

The large grounds were uneven in shape, featuring several deep alcoves. Three or four of the largest of these served as our playground. It was flat and surfaced with fine, packed gravel.

From that point on, my word was law in the house; and at an age when few children have stopped needing supervision, I was left to follow my own desires, becoming, in every way but name, the master of my own behavior.

My earliest memories of school life are tied to a large, sprawling Elizabethan house in a misty English village, where there were countless massive, twisted trees and where every building was incredibly old. Truly, that venerable town was a dreamlike and soothing place. Even now, in my imagination, I feel the refreshing chill of its deeply shaded avenues, breathe in the scent of its many shrubberies, and feel a fresh, indefinable thrill at the deep, hollow sound of the church bell, which broke the stillness of the dim air every hour with a sudden, sullen roar, right where the intricate Gothic steeple sat embedded and asleep.

It gives me perhaps as much pleasure as I can currently experience to dwell on the minute details of that school and its affairs. Steeped in misery as I am—misery that is, unfortunately, all too real—I hope to be forgiven for seeking relief, however slight and temporary, in the indulgence of a few rambling memories. These details, though utterly trivial and even ridiculous on their own, take on an added importance in my mind because they are connected to a time and place where I recognize the first vague warnings of the destiny that later overshadowed me so completely. Let me remember, then.

The house, as I have said, was old and irregular. The grounds were vast, and a high, solid brick wall, topped with mortar and broken glass, surrounded the entire property. This prison-like barrier marked the edge of our world; beyond it, we saw nothing except three times a week—once every Saturday afternoon, when, accompanied by two teachers, we were allowed to take short group walks through the nearby fields, and twice on Sunday, when we were marched in the same formal way to the morning and evening services at the village's only church. The head of our school was the pastor of this church. With what deep wonder and confusion I used to watch him from our distant pew in the gallery as he climbed the pulpit with slow, solemn steps! This reverend man, with his demurely kind face, his glossy, flowing clerical robes, and his wig, so precisely powdered, so rigid, and so vast—could this really be the same person who, just a short time before, had been administering the school’s harsh laws with a sour expression, dirty clothes, and a ruler in his hand? Oh, what a massive paradox, far too monstrous to solve!

At a corner of that heavy wall stood an even heavier gate. It was reinforced and studded with iron bolts and topped with jagged iron spikes. What a deep sense of awe it inspired! It was never opened except for the three scheduled departures and arrivals I already mentioned; then, in every creak of its mighty hinges, we found a wealth of mystery—a world of subject matter for serious conversation or even more serious reflection.

The large enclosure was irregular in shape, with many spacious alcoves. Three or four of the largest ones made up the playground. It was flat and covered with fine, hard gravel.

From that time on, my word was law in the house. At an age when few children have stopped needing guidance, I was left to follow my own will and became, in every way but name, the master of my own actions.

I could never determine how his keen insight first realized that such a trivial matter would irritate me, yet once he knew, he made a habit of tormenting me with it. I had long harbored a dislike for my unrefined surname and its common, perhaps even vulgar, first name. These sounds were poison to me, and when another William Wilson arrived at the school on my first day, I resented him for carrying the name, and felt even more repulsed by the name itself because a stranger now possessed it. This meant the name would be spoken twice as often, he would be constantly near me, and our daily school affairs would inevitably become confused due to this detestable coincidence.

This sense of annoyance intensified whenever any detail highlighted a physical or moral likeness between my rival and me. While I had not yet realized we were the same age, I noticed we shared the same height and possessed a striking similarity in our physical build and facial features. I was also irritated by rumors of a family connection that had begun to circulate among the older students. In short, nothing disturbed me more—though I carefully hid my agitation—than any suggestion that we were alike in mind, appearance, or status. In reality, I had no reason to think that anyone other than Wilson himself had noticed or commented on this resemblance. It was clear that he observed it as closely as I did, and his ability to turn such a situation into a source of torment could only be credited, as I previously noted, to his exceptional perceptiveness.

His strategy, which involved perfecting an imitation of me, extended to both his speech and his behavior; he performed this role with remarkable skill. Copying my clothing was simple, and he adopted my walk and general demeanor without trouble. Even my voice did not escape him, despite his natural vocal limitation. He naturally avoided my louder tones, but the pitch was identical, and his peculiar whisper became an exact reflection of my own.

I will not attempt to explain how deeply this precise portrayal troubled me, as it could hardly be called a mere caricature. My only comfort was that the imitation seemed apparent to me alone, leaving me to suffer only the knowing, mocking smiles of my namesake. Content with having achieved his goal, he seemed to relish his secret triumph, showing no interest in the public recognition his clever efforts might have easily earned. For many months, I was baffled as to why the rest of the school failed to notice his design or join in his mockery. Perhaps the subtle nature of his mimicry made it difficult to detect, or more likely, I was spared further embarrassment by the skill of the copyist, who ignored the superficial details that the dull-witted might notice, choosing instead to present the very essence of his original for my private frustration.

After a convincing display of hesitation, and only once my persistent refusals had provoked him into sharp remarks that made my eventual agreement seem like a response to his irritation, I finally gave in. The outcome merely confirmed how completely my victim was ensnared; within an hour, he had increased his losses fourfold. For a while, his face had been fading from the ruddy flush caused by the wine, but now, to my surprise, it turned a truly ghastly shade of pale. I say surprise because Glendinning had been described to my careful inquiries as immensely rich, and the amounts he had lost so far, while substantial, should not have troubled him deeply, let alone affected him so severely. I assumed he was simply overcome by the alcohol he had consumed, and, more to protect my own reputation among my peers than from any altruistic concern, I was about to demand that we stop the game. However, comments from the crowd beside me and a cry of absolute despair from Glendinning made me realize I had brought about his complete ruin under conditions that, while making him a target for everyone’s pity, should have shielded him from the malice of even a demon.

It is hard to say how I might have acted next. The pathetic state of my victim had cast a mood of awkward somberness over everyone, and for a few moments, a deep silence reigned, during which I felt my face burn under the many intense looks of contempt or blame directed at me by the less depraved members of the group. I will even admit that a crushing sense of anxiety was briefly removed from my chest by the sudden and startling interruption that followed. The large, heavy double doors of the room were suddenly flung wide open with such forceful speed that every candle in the room was extinguished as if by a spell. As their flames died, we could just make out that a stranger had walked in, roughly my height and wrapped tightly in a cloak. The darkness was absolute, however, and we could only sense his presence among us. Before any of us could shake off the intense shock caused by this intrusion, we heard the newcomer speak.

“Gentlemen,” he said, in a quiet, clear, and unforgettable whisper that sent a chill to the very core of my being, “Gentlemen, I offer no excuse for this conduct, for in acting this way, I am merely performing a duty. You are surely unaware of the true nature of the man who has tonight won a significant amount of money from Lord Glendinning at écarté. I will therefore provide you with a quick and certain method to gain this essential knowledge. Please take a moment to inspect the inner linings of his left sleeve cuff, as well as the various small packets that can be discovered in the rather large pockets of his embroidered morning gown.”

With a well-acted show of reluctance, and only after my repeated refusals had provoked him into some angry words that made my eventual agreement seem like a response to his irritation, did I finally give in. The result, naturally, only proved how completely the prey was in my trap: in less than an hour, he had quadrupled his debt. A tall glass—or so I initially perceived in my bewilderment—now occupied a space where nothing had been visible previously; as I approached it in a state of intense fright, my own reflection, yet with a face deathly white and stained with gore, moved toward me with a weak and unsteady stride.

So it seemed, I maintain, but that was not the truth. It was my foe—it was Wilson, who stood before me in the throes of his final moments. His disguise and mantle rested on the ground where he had cast them aside. “You have triumphed, and I surrender. Yet, from this moment on, you are likewise dead—dead to the world, to heaven, and to hope! You existed within me—and, in my passing, observe through this reflection, which is your own, how completely you have destroyed yourself.”

THE TELL-TALE HEART.

True!—anxious—I have been and remain dreadfully anxious; but why do you insist that I am insane? The malady had honed my perceptions—not ruined—not blunted them. Above all, my sense of hearing was sharp. I perceived every sound in the heavens and on the earth. I perceived many things in hell. How, then, could I be mad? Listen! And notice how soundly—how composedly I can relate the entire tale to you.

It is not possible to explain how the notion first took root in my mind; but once it was formed, it pursued me night and day. There was no specific motive. There was no anger. I held the old man dear. He had never harmed me. He had never offered me an insult. I had no craving for his wealth. I believe it was his eye! Yes, that was it! He possessed the eye of a vulture—a light blue gaze, obscured by a film. Whenever it rested upon me, my blood turned to ice; and so, by increments—very slowly—I resolved to end the old man’s life, and thereby free myself from that eye for good.

Now, here is the crux. You believe me insane. Madmen understand nothing. But you ought to have witnessed me. You should have seen how sagely I acted—with what prudence—with what foresight—with what deceit I carried out my plan! I was never more affectionate toward the old man than during the entire week leading up to his murder. And every night, near midnight, I lifted the latch of his door and swung it open—oh, so carefully! And then, once I had created an opening wide enough for my head, I inserted a dark lantern, completely shut, so that no beam escaped, and then I pushed my head inside. Oh, you would have chuckled to observe how craftily I inserted it! I moved it gradually—very, very gradually, so as not to interrupt the old man’s slumber.

The brief moment I looked away was apparently enough to cause a physical change in the layout at the far end of the room. A large mirror—or so it seemed to me in my confusion—now stood where none had been visible before. As I approached it in absolute terror, my own reflection, but with features pale and stained with blood, stepped forward to meet me with a weak and unsteady gait.

That is how it appeared, I say, but it was not the truth. It was my antagonist—it was Wilson, who stood before me in the agony of his final moments. His mask and cloak lay on the floor where he had discarded them. Not a single thread of his clothing, nor a line in the distinct and unique features of his face, failed to be an exact duplicate of my own.

It was Wilson, but he no longer spoke in a whisper, and I could have imagined that I was speaking myself as he said:

“You have conquered, and I surrender. Yet, from now on, you are also dead—dead to the world, to heaven, and to hope! You existed in me, and in my death, see by this image, which is your own, how completely you have murdered yourself.”

Alas! Her thinness was extreme, and not a trace of her former self remained in any feature of her outline. My intense gaze finally settled upon her face.

The forehead was lofty, extremely pale, and remarkably calm; the once black hair hung partially across it, shading the sunken temples with countless curls, now a bright yellow, clashing strangely in their whimsical style with the pervasive sadness of her expression. Her eyes were dull, devoid of light, and appeared to lack pupils, causing me to recoil instinctively from their glassy gaze to examine her thin, withered lips. They parted, and in a smile of strange significance, the teeth of the transformed Berenice were revealed to me slowly. I wish to God I had never seen them, or that, having seen them, I had perished!

The sound of a closing door startled me, and looking up, I realized my cousin had left the room. Yet, from the chaotic chamber of my mind, the white and spectral image of those teeth had not departed, nor could it be banished. Not a spot on their surface, not a blemish on their enamel, not a notch in their edges escaped being branded into my memory by that brief moment of her smile. I perceived them now even more clearly than I had then. The teeth! The teeth! They were present everywhere, visible and tangible before me; long, slender, and strikingly white, with pale lips twisting around them, just as they had appeared during that first horrific revelation. Then the full intensity of my obsession seized me, and I fought fruitlessly against its bizarre and overpowering force. Amidst the many objects of the outside world, I could think of nothing but the teeth. I craved them with a frantic intensity. All other concerns and interests were swallowed up by their singular contemplation. They alone occupied my mind, and in their solitary existence, they became the core of my mental life. I examined them in every light. I rotated them into every position. I scrutinized their features. I focused on their distinct traits. I reflected on their structure. I pondered the change in their nature. I trembled as I imagined them possessing a sensitive and sentient quality, and even when separated from the lips, a capacity for moral expression. It has been aptly remarked of Mademoiselle Salle that “all her steps were sentiments,” and I believed even more earnestly of Berenice that all her teeth were ideas. Ideas! Ah, that was the foolish notion that ruined me! Ideas! Ah, that was why I desired them so desperately! I felt that only by possessing them could I regain my peace and return to sanity.

Thus the evening ended for me—and then the darkness arrived, lingered, and passed—and the day broke once more—and the mists of a second night were now collecting—yet I remained sitting still in that lonely room—still lost in thought—and still the phantom of the teeth held its terrifying dominance, as it drifted with vivid, hideous clarity amidst the shifting lights and shadows of the room.

The sound of a closing door startled me, and looking up, I realized my cousin had left the room. But from the disordered chamber of my brain, the white and ghastly image of those teeth had not departed, and could not be driven away. Not a speck on their surface, not a shade on their enamel, not a notch in their edges—nothing had escaped being branded into my memory during that brief smile. I saw them now even more clearly than I had then. The teeth! The teeth! They were here, there, and everywhere, visibly and palpably before me; long, narrow, and intensely white, with her pale lips writhing around them, just as they had been in that first terrible moment. Then the full fury of my obsession took hold, and I struggled in vain against its strange and irresistible influence. Among the many objects of the external world, I had no thoughts but for the teeth. I longed for them with a frenzied desire. All other matters and interests were absorbed into their singular contemplation. They alone were present to my mind's eye, and they, in their sole individuality, became the essence of my mental life. I examined them in every light. I turned them in every position. I surveyed their characteristics. I dwelt upon their peculiarities. I pondered their structure. I mused upon the change in their nature. I shuddered as I imagined them possessing a sensitive and sentient power, and even when separated from the lips, a capacity for moral expression. It has been well said of Mademoiselle Salle that "all her steps were sentiments," and I more seriously believed of Berenice that all her teeth were ideas. Ideas! Ah, here was the idiotic thought that destroyed me! Ideas! Ah, that was why I coveted them so madly! I felt that possessing them was the only thing that could restore my peace by returning me to reason.

And so the evening closed in on me—and then the darkness came, lingered, and passed—and the day dawned again—and the mists of a second night were now gathering—and still I sat motionless in that solitary room—and still I sat buried in meditation—and still the phantom of the teeth maintained its terrible dominance, as it floated with vivid, hideous clarity amidst the changing lights and shadows of the room.

Alas! She was extremely thin, and not a trace of her former self remained in any line of her figure. My burning gaze finally settled on her face.

Her forehead was high, very pale, and remarkably calm. Her once jet-black hair fell partially over it, shadowing her hollow temples with countless ringlets that were now a vivid yellow. These curls clashed strangely with the deep sadness of her expression. Her eyes were lifeless, dull, and seemed to lack pupils. I instinctively recoiled from their glassy stare to look at her thin, shrunken lips. They parted, and in a smile of strange meaning, the teeth of the changed Berenice slowly revealed themselves to me. I wish to God that I had never seen them, or that, having seen them, I had died!

The sound of a door closing startled me, and when I looked up, I realized my cousin had left the room. But the white, ghastly image of those teeth had not left my disordered mind, and I could not drive it away. Every speck on their surface, every shade on their enamel, and every notch in their edges had been branded into my memory during that brief smile. I saw them now even more clearly than I had seen them then. The teeth! They were here, there, and everywhere, visible and tangible before me; long, narrow, and intensely white, with her pale lips writhing around them just as they had in that first terrible moment. Then, the full fury of my obsession took hold, and I struggled in vain against its strange, irresistible power. Among all the objects in the world, I could think only of the teeth. I longed for them with a frantic desire. All other matters and interests were consumed by this single focus. They alone were present to my mind, and they became the entire essence of my mental life. I examined them in every light. I turned them in every position. I surveyed their features. I dwelled on their peculiarities. I pondered their shape. I mused on the change in their nature. I shuddered as I imagined them possessing a sensitive, sentient power, and even a capacity for moral expression, independent of the lips. It has been well said of Mademoiselle Salle that “all her steps were sentiments,” and I more seriously believed of Berenice that “all her teeth were ideas.” Ideas! Ah, that was the foolish thought that destroyed me! Ideas! That was why I coveted them so madly! I felt that possessing them was the only thing that could restore my peace and return me to reason.

And so the evening closed in on me—then the darkness came, stayed, and went—the day dawned again—the mists of a second night began to gather—and still I sat motionless in that solitary room, buried in thought, while the phantom of the teeth maintained its terrible hold, floating with vivid, hideous clarity amidst the changing lights and shadows of the chamber.

The blossom of the Aristolochia Clematitis features a tubular corolla that ends in a strap-shaped limb and swells into a sphere at its foundation. The interior of the tube is lined with rigid, downward-facing bristles. The spherical section houses the pistil, comprised of a germen and stigma, encircled by stamens. Still, despite his unhappiness, he is not so consumed by his own sorrow that he fails to take a keen interest in the plans of others. He has often spoken with me about my own, which I have shared with him quite openly. He listened carefully to all my reasoning regarding my potential success and to every small detail of the steps I had taken to ensure it. Encouraged by the empathy he showed, I was easily persuaded to speak from the heart, to express the intense passion of my spirit, and to declare, with all the fervor that moved me, how willingly I would give up my wealth, my life, and all my hopes to advance my undertaking. The life or death of a single man would be a trivial cost for the knowledge I pursued, and for the mastery I would gain and pass on over the natural forces that oppose our kind. As I talked, a dark shadow fell across my listener’s face. At first, I noticed him trying to hide his feelings; he covered his eyes with his hands, and my voice faltered and died away as I watched tears stream from between his fingers; a sob escaped his heaving chest. I stopped; eventually, he spoke in fragmented tones: “Wretched man! Are you afflicted with my same madness? Have you also tasted that intoxicating potion? Listen to me; let me tell you my story, and you will cast that cup away from your lips!”

These words, as you might suppose, greatly stirred my interest; however, the fit of sorrow that had gripped the stranger exhausted his fragile strength, and many hours of rest and quiet talk were required to help him regain his calm.

Having mastered the intensity of his emotions, he seemed to look down on himself for being controlled by such passion; and, suppressing the grim rule of despair, he guided me back to talking about my own life. He inquired about the history of my youth. The story was brief, yet it stirred up many lines of thought. I talked of my longing to find a friend, of my craving for a deeper connection with another mind than I had ever experienced, and stated my belief that a person could claim little joy without enjoying such a gift.

“I concur,” the stranger answered; “we are unfinished beings, only partially formed, if someone wiser, better, and more precious than ourselves—as a true friend should be—does not offer his help to refine our weak and flawed natures. I once possessed a friend, the most noble of all people, and am therefore qualified to judge the nature of friendship. You have hope, the world lies ahead of you, and you have no reason for despair. But I—I have lost everything and cannot start my life over.”

As he spoke, his face took on an expression of quiet, permanent sadness that moved me deeply. Yet he remained silent and soon withdrew to his cabin.

Deeply aware of their obligations to the life they had created, and driven by a constant, affectionate spirit, my parents raised me with such gentle care that every lesson in patience, kindness, and restraint felt like a continuous stream of happiness. For a considerable time, I remained their sole focus. Although my mother had long hoped for a daughter, I stayed their only child. When I was five, during a trip outside Italy, they spent a week by Lake Como. Their generous nature frequently led them to visit the homes of the impoverished. For my mother, this was not merely an obligation but a profound necessity and passion; recalling her own past hardships and the aid she had received, she felt compelled to act as a protector for the suffering. On one excursion, a desolate cottage tucked away in a valley caught their attention, where the sight of several poorly dressed children revealed extreme destitution. One day, while my father was away in Milan, my mother took me to visit this dwelling. She discovered a hardworking peasant couple, worn down by labor and worry, sharing a meager meal with five hungry children. One child in particular captivated my mother. She seemed entirely different from the others. While the four siblings were dark-eyed and rugged, this girl was delicate and fair. Her hair shone like bright gold, and despite her ragged attire, it gave her an air of nobility. With a broad, clear forehead, serene blue eyes, and a face radiating sweetness and sensitivity, she appeared to be a creature of a higher order, marked by a heavenly grace. Observing my mother’s fascination, the peasant woman quickly shared the girl’s story. She was not their biological child, but the daughter of a Milanese nobleman and a German mother who had passed away during childbirth. The infant had been left with these kind people to be nursed when they were more prosperous and had only one child of their own. The girl’s father was a man devoted to the memory of Italy’s past greatness—one of those restless souls fighting for his country’s freedom—who eventually fell victim to its political instability. Whether he had perished or remained trapped in an Austrian prison was unknown. His assets were seized, leaving his daughter an orphan in poverty. She remained with her foster family, flourishing in their humble home like a garden rose amidst wild, dark brambles. Upon returning from Milan, my father discovered a child playing with me in our villa’s hall who was more beautiful than any painted angel—a being who seemed to glow with light, moving with a grace more agile than a mountain chamois.

Deeply aware of their responsibility toward the life they had created, and driven by the active spirit of tenderness that motivated them both, it is easy to imagine that while every hour of my childhood provided a lesson in patience, charity, and self-control, I was guided so gently that my life felt like one continuous experience of joy.

Chapter 3

Upon reaching seventeen, my parents decided I should enroll at the University of Ingolstadt. I had previously studied in Geneva, but my father deemed it essential for my development that I experience traditions beyond those of my homeland. My departure was scheduled soon, but before that day arrived, the initial tragedy of my existence took place—a harbinger, in a sense, of my coming wretchedness.

Elizabeth had contracted scarlet fever; her condition was grave, and she faced extreme peril. Throughout her sickness, many urged my mother to avoid nursing her. She initially bowed to our pleas, but upon learning that her favorite’s life was threatened, she could no longer suppress her worry. She tended to the sickbed; her vigilant care overcame the virulence of the disease—Elizabeth was rescued, yet the results of this rashness proved deadly for her savior. By the third day, my mother fell ill; her fever displayed the most frightening signs, and the expressions of her doctors signaled the worst outcome. On her deathbed, the courage and kindness of this finest of women did not waver. She placed Elizabeth’s hand in mine. “My children,” she declared, “my greatest hopes for future joy rested on the vision of your marriage. This expectation will now serve as comfort for your father. Elizabeth, my dear, you must take my place for my younger children. Oh, I lament that I am torn from you; and, blessed and cherished as I have been, is it not difficult to leave you all? Yet these thoughts are unworthy of me; I will strive to accept death with grace and will hold to the hope of reuniting with you in another realm.”

She passed away peacefully, her face showing love even in death. I need not detail the emotions of those whose closest bonds are severed by that most permanent of evils, the emptiness that fills the soul, and the hopelessness visible on the face. It takes so long for the mind to accept that someone seen daily, whose very life seemed woven into our own, has vanished forever—that the radiance of a cherished eye has dimmed and the tone of a voice so well-known and precious to the ear has fallen silent, never to be heard again. These are the musings of the initial days; but when the passage of time confirms the truth of the loss, then the true sharpness of sorrow begins. Yet who has not had some dear relationship torn away by that harsh hand? And why should I recount a grief that everyone has experienced, and must experience?

Her victory was signaled by an unusual peace and joy of spirit that followed my abandonment of those old, recently tormenting studies. In this way, I was taught to link evil with their pursuit and happiness with their neglect.

It was a powerful exertion of the spirit of good, yet it proved ineffective. Destiny was too strong, and its unchangeable laws had decreed my complete and terrible ruin.

When I reached the age of seventeen, my parents decided I should become a student at the University of Ingolstadt. I had previously attended schools in Geneva, but my father believed it necessary for the completion of my education that I become familiar with customs other than those of my native country. My departure was therefore scheduled for an early date, but before the appointed day could arrive, the first misfortune of my life occurred—a sign, as it were, of my future misery.

Elizabeth had contracted scarlet fever; her illness was severe, and she was in great danger. During her sickness, many arguments were used to persuade my mother to refrain from nursing her. She had initially yielded to our pleas, but when she heard that the life of her favorite was threatened, she could no longer control her anxiety. She attended her sickbed; her watchful care triumphed over the severity of the disease—Elizabeth was saved, but the consequences of this imprudence were fatal to her savior. On the third day, my mother fell ill; her fever was accompanied by the most alarming symptoms, and the expressions of her doctors predicted the worst outcome. On her deathbed, the strength and kindness of this best of women did not desert her. She joined the hands of Elizabeth and myself. “My children,” she said, “my firmest hopes for future happiness were placed on the prospect of your marriage. This expectation will now be the consolation of your father. Elizabeth, my love, you must take my place for my younger children. Alas! I regret that I am taken from you; and, happy and beloved as I have been, is it not hard to leave you all? But these are not thoughts befitting me; I will try to resign myself cheerfully to death and will hold onto the hope of meeting you in another world.”

She died calmly, and her face expressed affection even in death. I need not describe the feelings of those whose dearest bonds are torn by that most irreparable evil, the void that presents itself to the soul, and the despair shown on the face. It takes so long before the mind can convince itself that she whom we saw every day and whose very existence seemed a part of our own can have departed forever—that the brightness of a beloved eye can have been extinguished and the sound of a voice so familiar and dear to the ear can be hushed, never to be heard again. These are the reflections of the first days; but when the passage of time proves the reality of the loss, then the true bitterness of grief begins. Yet from whom has that harsh hand not torn away some dear connection? And why should I describe a sorrow which all have felt, and must feel?

My victory was marked by an unusual sense of peace and joy that followed my decision to give up my old, tormenting studies. In this way, I learned to connect evil with pursuing those studies and happiness with ignoring them. It was a powerful effort of my better nature, but it failed. Destiny was too strong, and its unchangeable laws had already decreed my total and terrible destruction.

Chapter 3

When I turned seventeen, my parents decided I should attend the university of Ingolstadt. I had previously studied in Geneva, but my father believed it was necessary for my education to experience customs outside my native country. My departure was scheduled for soon after, but before that day arrived, the first tragedy of my life occurred—a sign, perhaps, of my future misery.

Elizabeth had caught scarlet fever; her illness was severe, and she was in great danger. During her sickness, many people tried to persuade my mother not to care for her. She initially listened to our pleas, but when she heard that the life of her favorite was at risk, she could no longer control her worry. She tended to Elizabeth’s sickbed, and her watchful care overcame the severity of the disease. Elizabeth was saved, but the consequences of this choice were fatal for her savior. On the third day, my mother fell ill. Her fever showed alarming symptoms, and the doctors’ expressions predicted the worst. On her deathbed, the strength and kindness of this wonderful woman did not leave her. She joined the hands of Elizabeth and me. “My children,” she said, “my greatest hopes for the future were based on the prospect of your marriage. This expectation will now be a comfort to your father. Elizabeth, my love, you must take my place for my younger children. I regret that I am being taken from you; and, happy and loved as I have been, is it not hard to leave you all? But these thoughts are not right for me now; I will try to accept death cheerfully and hope to meet you again in another world.”

She died calmly, and her face showed affection even in death. I do not need to describe the feelings of those whose closest bonds are broken by such an irreparable loss, the emptiness that fills the soul, and the despair shown on the face. These are the thoughts of the first few days; but when time proves the reality of the loss, the true bitterness of grief begins. Yet, who has not had that rough hand tear away a dear connection? And why should I describe a sorrow that everyone has felt, and must feel?

How much has been accomplished, the spirit of Frankenstein cried—I shall achieve even more; following the paths already established, I will blaze a fresh trail, investigate hidden forces, and reveal the most profound secrets of existence to mankind.

I did not sleep that night. My mind was in a state of chaotic rebellion; I sensed that harmony would eventually emerge, yet I lacked the ability to bring it about. Gradually, as day broke, slumber arrived. Upon waking, the thoughts of the previous night seemed like a mere dream. All that persisted was a firm decision to resume my former studies and dedicate myself to a discipline for which I felt I had an innate aptitude. That same day, I called upon M. Waldman. His private demeanor was even gentler and more engaging than his public one, for the air of gravity he maintained during his lectures was replaced at home by immense warmth and friendliness. I provided him with much the same summary of my past interests as I had offered his colleague. He listened intently to my brief account of my studies and smiled at the mention of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, yet he showed none of the disdain that M. Krempe had displayed. He remarked that these were individuals whose tireless dedication provided modern scholars with the bedrock of their understanding. They had left us the simpler duty of renaming and organizing the facts they had largely helped to uncover. The efforts of brilliant men, even when misguided, rarely fail to eventually benefit humanity. I heard his words, which were spoken without arrogance or pretense, and then replied that his talk had dispelled my biases against modern chemists; I spoke with the careful restraint and respect a student owes his teacher, taking care not to reveal—as my youthful inexperience would have made me embarrassed to do—the fervor that drove my planned endeavors. I asked for his guidance regarding which texts I should acquire.

I am pleased, M. Waldman stated, to have found a student; provided your diligence matches your talent, I have no doubt you will succeed. Chemistry is the field of natural science where the most significant progress has occurred and will continue to occur; that is why I have made it my primary focus, though I have not ignored other scientific disciplines. A person would be a poor chemist if they focused solely on that area of study. If you truly desire to become a man of science rather than a mere dabbler in experiments, I suggest you study every aspect of natural philosophy, mathematics included.

He then led me into his laboratory, described the functions of his various instruments, advised me on what equipment I should obtain, and pledged that I might use his own once I had progressed sufficiently to avoid damaging them. He also provided the reading list I had asked for, and I departed.

Chapter 5

It was on a bleak November night that I witnessed the fruition of my efforts. With an intensity bordering on agony, I gathered the tools of life about me, hoping to spark existence into the inanimate object resting at my feet. It was already one o'clock in the morning; rain tapped gloomily against the glass, and my candle was nearly spent when, by the dim glow of the dying flame, I watched the creature's dull yellow eye open; it gasped, and a spasmodic movement shook its body.

How might I articulate my feelings regarding this disaster, or how depict the miserable being I had labored with such immense effort to construct? His limbs were well-proportioned, and I had chosen features I considered beautiful. Beautiful! Good heavens! His sallow skin barely concealed the underlying muscles and veins; his hair was a glossy, flowing black, and his teeth were pearly white; yet these details only created a more grotesque contrast with his watery eyes, which appeared nearly the same shade as the murky white sockets holding them, his withered skin, and his straight, dark lips.

The various circumstances of existence are not as fickle as the human heart. I had toiled for nearly two years with the singular goal of breathing life into a corpse. For this, I had sacrificed my sleep and well-being. I had pursued it with a passion that far surpassed reason; but now that I had succeeded, the charm of my vision evaporated, and breathless dread and revulsion overwhelmed me. Unable to bear the sight of the creature I had brought to life, I fled the room and spent a long time pacing my bedroom, unable to calm my mind enough to rest. Eventually, exhaustion replaced the turmoil I had previously felt, and I collapsed onto the bed in my clothes, attempting to find a brief period of oblivion.

My father did not criticize me in his letters, but he did note my silence by asking about my activities more specifically than he had before. Winter, spring, and summer passed while I worked, but I did not notice the blossoms or the budding leaves—sights that had previously brought me immense joy—so deeply was I absorbed in my task. Every night I was weighed down by a low-grade fever, and I became painfully nervous; the sound of a falling leaf startled me, and I avoided other people as if I had committed a crime. Sometimes I grew alarmed at the wreck I realized I had become; only the energy of my goal sustained me. My work would soon end, and I believed that exercise and recreation would then drive away the early signs of illness; I promised myself both once my creation was complete.

“Hardly anything has shifted, aside from the growth of our beloved children, since your departure. The azure lake and the peaks covered in snow remain constant; I believe our peaceful residence and our satisfied spirits are governed by those same unalterable principles. My small tasks occupy my hours and provide entertainment, and I find my efforts repaid by the sight of only cheerful, gentle countenances nearby. Since you went away, only one alteration has occurred within our small domestic circle. Do you recall the circumstances under which Justine Moritz joined our household? You likely do not; I shall therefore recount her background briefly. Madame Moritz, her mother, was a widow with four offspring, Justine being the third. This girl had always been her father’s darling, yet through an odd stubbornness, her mother could not tolerate her and, following M. Moritz’s passing, mistreated her severely. My aunt noticed this, and when Justine reached the age of twelve, she convinced the mother to permit the girl to reside with us. The democratic systems of our nation have fostered more modest and joyful customs than those found in the vast kingdoms surrounding us. Consequently, there is a smaller divide between the various ranks of its citizens; as the working classes are neither so destitute nor so scorned, their behavior is more polished and virtuous. A domestic worker in Geneva does not signify the same thing as one in France or England. Justine, welcomed into our home in this manner, acquired the responsibilities of a servant, a status which, in our lucky land, does not imply a lack of knowledge or a surrender of one’s human worth.

“Justine, you might recall, was a great favorite of yours; I remember you once noted that if you were in a foul mood, a single look from Justine could banish it, for the same reason Ariosto provides regarding the beauty of Angelica—she appeared so sincere and joyful. My aunt developed a deep fondness for her, which prompted her to provide an education better than what she had originally planned. This kindness was entirely returned; Justine was the most appreciative little soul imaginable: I do not suggest she made any verbal declarations—I never heard one escape her—but you could perceive by her gaze that she nearly worshipped her guardian. Although her nature was lighthearted and in many ways impulsive, she nonetheless observed every movement of my aunt with the utmost care. She viewed her as the standard of all virtue and strove to copy her way of speaking and her habits, so that even today she frequently brings her to my mind.

“When my dearest aunt passed away, everyone was too consumed by their own sorrow to observe poor Justine, who had cared for her throughout her sickness with the most worried devotion. Poor Justine was quite unwell; yet further hardships were in store for her.

“One after another, her siblings passed away; and her mother, save for her disregarded daughter, was left without children. The woman’s conscience was burdened; she began to suspect that the passing of her favorites was a divine punishment to rebuke her favoritism.

My native land, my cherished home! Only one born here could describe the joy I felt in seeing your rivers, your peaks, and above all, your beautiful lake once more.

However, as I neared my destination, sorrow and dread returned to haunt me. Night descended; as the dark mountains faded from view, my spirits sank further. The landscape seemed a sprawling, shadowy theater of malice, and I sensed vaguely that I was fated to be the most miserable of men. How right my premonition was, though I failed in one regard: the suffering I feared was not even a fraction of the agony I was truly meant to bear.

It was pitch black when I reached the outskirts of Geneva. The city gates were locked, forcing me to spend the night in Secheron, a village half a league away. The sky was clear, and since I could not sleep, I decided to visit the site of poor William’s murder. Unable to enter the city, I took a boat across the lake to reach Plainpalais. During the crossing, I watched lightning dance across the peak of Mont Blanc in exquisite patterns. The tempest seemed to draw near quickly, and upon reaching the shore, I climbed a small hill to watch its advance. It moved closer; the clouds gathered, and I felt the first heavy drops of rain, which soon turned into a torrential downpour.

I left my position and continued walking, even as the darkness and the gale intensified, and thunder roared with terrifying force above me. The sound reverberated off the Salêve, the Juras, and the Savoy Alps. Brilliant lightning blinded me, turning the lake into a vast sheet of flame, followed by moments of absolute blackness until my vision adjusted. The storm, as frequently happens in Switzerland, seemed to strike from several directions at once. The most intense activity centered north of the city, over the water between the Belrive promontory and the village of Copêt. Another storm lit up the Jura with pale flickers, while a third obscured and occasionally revealed the Môle, a sharp mountain to the east.

As I observed this beautiful yet frightening spectacle, I hurried forward. This magnificent celestial conflict lifted my mood; I wrung my hands and cried out, “William, sweet angel! This is your funeral, this is your requiem!” As I spoke, I noticed a shape in the shadows slipping from behind a cluster of trees nearby. I stood motionless, staring hard; I could not be wrong. A bolt of lightning lit up the figure, revealing its form clearly. Its immense size and the grotesque, inhuman distortion of its features told me instantly that it was the wretch, the foul demon, to whom I had granted life. Why was he there? Could he be—I trembled at the thought—the killer of my brother?

The landscape seemed like a vast, dim scene of evil, and I sensed that I was destined to become the most miserable of human beings. Sadly, my prediction was accurate, though I failed in one regard: in all the misery I imagined and feared, I did not conceive even a fraction of the anguish I was actually destined to endure.

It was completely dark when I reached the outskirts of Geneva. The town gates were already locked, so I had to spend the night at Secheron, a village half a league from the city. The sky was clear, and since I could not sleep, I decided to visit the place where my poor William had been murdered. Because I could not pass through the town, I had to cross the lake by boat to reach Plainpalais. During this short trip, I watched lightning dance across the summit of Mont Blanc in beautiful patterns. The storm seemed to approach quickly, and upon landing, I climbed a low hill to watch its progress. It advanced; the sky grew cloudy, and I soon felt rain falling in large, slow drops, though the intensity quickly increased.

I left my seat and walked on, even as the darkness and the storm grew more intense by the minute. Thunder crashed terrifically over my head, echoing from the Salêve, the Juras, and the Alps of Savoy. Vivid flashes of lightning dazzled my eyes, illuminating the lake until it looked like a vast sheet of fire; then, for an instant, everything plunged into pitch-black darkness until my eyes adjusted. The storm, as often happens in Switzerland, seemed to be occurring in several parts of the sky at once. The most violent part hung directly north of the town, over the section of the lake between the Belrive promontory and the village of Copêt. Another storm lit up the Jura mountains with faint flashes, while a third darkened and occasionally revealed the Môle, a peaked mountain to the east of the lake.

As I watched this beautiful yet terrifying tempest, I walked on with hurried steps. This magnificent war in the sky lifted my spirits. I clasped my hands and exclaimed aloud, “William, dear angel! This is your funeral, this is your song of mourning!” As I spoke, I noticed a figure in the gloom creeping from behind a cluster of trees nearby. I stood frozen, staring intently; I could not be mistaken. A flash of lightning illuminated the object, clearly revealing its shape. Its gigantic stature and the deformity of its appearance, more hideous than anything human, instantly told me it was the wretch, the filthy demon, to whom I had given life. What was he doing there? Could he be—I shuddered at the thought—the murderer of my brother?

Life, even if it is just a collection of pain, is precious to me, and I will defend it. Remember, you have made me stronger than yourself; I am taller than you, and my joints are more flexible. But I will not be tempted to fight you. I am your creature, and I will be gentle and obedient to my natural master if you will also do your part, which you owe me. Oh, Frankenstein, do not be fair to everyone else while trampling only on me, to whom your justice, and even your mercy and affection, are most due. Remember that I am your creature; I should be your Adam, but instead I am the fallen angel, whom you drive away from joy for no wrongdoing. Everywhere I see happiness, from which I alone am permanently excluded. I was benevolent and good; misery made me a fiend. Make me happy, and I will be virtuous again.”

“Get out! I will not listen to you. There can be no connection between us; we are enemies. Leave, or let us test our strength in a fight, where one of us must fall.”

“How can I move you? Will no pleas cause you to look kindly upon your creature, who begs for your goodness and compassion? Believe me, Frankenstein, I was benevolent; my soul glowed with love and humanity; but am I not alone, miserably alone? You, my creator, loathe me; what hope can I expect from your fellow humans, who owe me nothing? They reject and hate me. The desert mountains and dreary glaciers are my refuge. I have wandered here for many days; the ice caves, which are the only things I do not fear, are my home, and the only one that humans do not begrudge me.”

“Devil,” I shouted, “do you dare come near me? Are you not afraid of the fierce revenge I will take on your miserable head? Get away, you vile insect! Or rather, stay, so I can crush you into dust! Oh, how I wish that by ending your miserable life, I could bring back the victims you have so wickedly murdered!”

“I expected this reaction,” the demon said. “All people hate the wretched; how much more must I be hated, since I am more miserable than any other living thing! Yet you, my creator, hate and reject me, your creature, to whom you are bound by ties that can only be broken by the death of one of us. You intend to kill me. How dare you play with life like this? Do your duty toward me, and I will do mine toward you and the rest of humanity. If you agree to my terms, I will leave you and everyone else in peace; but if you refuse, I will feed the hunger of death until it is satisfied with the blood of your remaining friends.”

“You hated monster! You fiend! The tortures of hell are too mild a punishment for your crimes. Wretched devil! You blame me for creating you; come on, then, so I can put out the spark of life I so carelessly gave you.”

My rage was limitless; I lunged at him, driven by every feeling that can turn one being against the life of another.

He easily dodged me and said,
