I have wandered here for many days; the ice caves, which are the only things I do not fear, are my home, and the only one that humans do not begrudge me.”

I attempted to prepare my meals similarly, laying them upon the glowing coals. I discovered that the berries were ruined by this process, while the roots and nuts were significantly better.

“Provisions, however, grew rare, and I frequently passed entire days hunting fruitlessly for a handful of acorns to quiet my hunger. Once I realized this, I decided to abandon the location I had occupied until then, hoping to find a place where my modest needs might be more readily met. During this migration, I deeply mourned the loss of the fire I had acquired by chance, as I possessed no method to recreate it. I dedicated several hours to contemplating this problem, but I eventually had to abandon any hope of replacing it; wrapping myself in my cloak, I headed through the forest toward the setting sun. I spent three days wandering before finally reaching open land. A heavy snowfall had occurred the previous night, leaving the fields a uniform white; the scene was bleak, and my feet grew numb from the cold, wet substance blanketing the earth.

“It was roughly seven in the morning, and I desperately sought sustenance and protection; eventually, I spotted a small cabin on a hill, clearly constructed for a shepherd’s use. This was a novel sight, and I inspected the building with intense interest. Seeing the door ajar, I stepped inside. An elderly man sat nearby, tending a fire while cooking his morning meal. Hearing a sound, he turned, saw me, and let out a piercing scream before bolting from the hut, sprinting across the fields with a swiftness his frail body seemed unable to sustain. His appearance, unlike any I had encountered, and his sudden flight left me somewhat bewildered. Yet, I was captivated by the cabin; here, the rain and snow could not reach me; the floor was dry; and it seemed to me as wonderful and heavenly a sanctuary as Pandemonium must have appeared to the demons of hell following their torments in the lake of fire. I ravenously consumed the leftovers of the shepherd’s meal, which included bread, cheese, milk, and wine—though I disliked the latter. Then, exhausted, I curled up in some straw and drifted off to sleep.

“It was midday when I woke, and drawn by the warmth of the sun shining brilliantly on the white landscape, I resolved to resume my journey. Stowing the remainder of the peasant’s food in a bag I discovered, I walked across the fields for several hours until I reached a village at dusk. How wondrous this seemed! The huts, the tidy cottages, and the grand houses captured my admiration in turn. The garden vegetables, along with the milk and cheese visible in cottage windows, tempted my hunger. I stepped into one of the finer dwellings, but I had barely crossed the threshold when the children began to scream and a woman collapsed in a faint. The entire village was stirred; some people ran, while others assaulted me, until, badly battered by stones and various projectiles, I fled to the open fields and timidly sought shelter in a small, empty shed, which looked miserable compared to the grand structures I had just witnessed in the village.

I tried, therefore, to prepare my food in the same way, placing it on the hot coals. I discovered that the berries were ruined by this process, but the nuts and roots were much improved.

“Food, however, became scarce, and I often spent the entire day searching unsuccessfully for a few acorns to ease the pangs of hunger. When I realized this, I decided to leave the place I had lived in until then, to look for somewhere where my few needs would be more easily met. During this migration, I deeply regretted the loss of the fire I had obtained by accident, as I did not know how to recreate it. I spent several hours seriously considering this problem, but I was forced to give up all attempts to replace it. Wrapping myself in my cloak, I headed across the woods toward the setting sun. I spent three days wandering and finally discovered open country. A heavy snowfall had occurred the night before, and the fields were a uniform white; the sight was bleak, and I found my feet chilled by the cold, damp substance covering the ground.

“It was about seven in the morning, and I longed for food and shelter; eventually, I spotted a small hut on a hill, which had clearly been built for the convenience of a shepherd. This was a new sight to me, and I examined the structure with great curiosity. Finding the door open, I went inside. An old man sat near a fire, preparing his breakfast. He turned when he heard a noise, and upon seeing me, he shrieked loudly. Leaving the hut, he ran across the fields with a speed his frail body hardly seemed capable of. His appearance, unlike anything I had ever seen before, and his flight surprised me somewhat. But I was enchanted by the hut; here, the snow and rain could not get in; the ground was dry; and it seemed to me as exquisite and divine a retreat as Pandemonium appeared to the demons of hell after their suffering in the lake of fire. I greedily devoured the remnants of the shepherd’s breakfast, which consisted of bread, cheese, milk, and wine; I did not like the wine, however. Then, overcome by fatigue, I lay down in some straw and fell asleep.

“It was noon when I woke up, and drawn by the warmth of the sun, which shone brightly on the white ground, I decided to resume my travels. Placing the remains of the peasant’s breakfast in a bag I found, I walked across the fields for several hours until I arrived at a village at sunset. How miraculous this seemed! The huts, the neater cottages, and the stately houses captured my admiration in turn. The vegetables in the gardens and the milk and cheese I saw placed in the windows of some cottages tempted my appetite. I entered one of the better ones, but I had barely stepped inside the door before the children screamed and one of the women fainted. The whole village was stirred up; some people fled, while others attacked me, until, badly bruised by stones and various other projectiles, I escaped to the open country and fearfully took refuge in a low hovel, which was completely bare and looked wretched compared to the palaces I had seen in the village.

I tried to prepare my food in the same way, placing it on the hot coals. I discovered that the berries were ruined by this, but the nuts and roots were much improved.

Food, however, became scarce, and I often spent the entire day searching unsuccessfully for a few acorns to satisfy my hunger. When I realized this, I decided to leave the place where I had been living to find somewhere where my few needs would be more easily met. However, I was delighted by the hut; the snow and rain could not get inside, the ground was dry, and it seemed as wonderful and divine a retreat to me as Pandemonium appeared to the demons of hell after their suffering in the lake of fire. I greedily ate the leftovers of the shepherd’s breakfast, which included bread, cheese, milk, and wine; I did not like the wine, however. Then, exhausted, I lay down in some straw and fell asleep.

It was noon when I woke up. Attracted by the warmth of the sun, which shone brightly on the white ground, I decided to continue my travels. I put the remains of the shepherd’s breakfast into a bag I found and walked across the fields for several hours until I reached a village at sunset. How miraculous it looked! The huts, the tidy cottages, and the grand houses all caught my attention. The vegetables in the gardens and the milk and cheese I saw in the cottage windows tempted my appetite. I entered one of the nicer homes, but I had barely stepped inside before the children screamed and one of the women fainted. The entire village was stirred up; some people ran away, while others attacked me. After being badly bruised by stones and other objects, I escaped into the open country and fearfully took refuge in a low, empty shed that looked wretched compared to the houses I had seen in the village.

A significant amount of time passed before I identified a primary source of the distress felt by this kind family: they were living in a state of severe poverty. Their diet was limited to garden vegetables and the milk from a single cow, which produced very little in the winter months when they could barely afford to feed the animal. I believe they frequently endured the sharp pains of hunger, particularly the two younger residents, who would often set meals before the elderly man while keeping nothing for themselves.

This display of affection touched me deeply. I had previously made a habit of stealing from their supplies at night to feed myself, but upon realizing that my actions caused them suffering, I stopped and instead relied on berries, nuts, and roots gathered from the nearby forest.

I also found another way to lighten their burdens. I noticed the young man spent much of his day gathering firewood, so I began taking his tools at night—having quickly learned how to operate them—to provide enough fuel to last them for several days.

I recall that the first time I did this, the young woman seemed quite amazed when she opened the door to find a large stack of wood waiting. She exclaimed in a loud voice, and the young man came to join her, showing equal astonishment. I was pleased to see that he did not head to the woods that day, choosing instead to spend his time fixing the cottage and working in the garden.

Eventually, I made a discovery of even greater importance. I realized these people had a way of sharing their thoughts and emotions through spoken language. I observed that their words could evoke joy or sorrow, smiles or tears, in those who heard them. This seemed like a divine ability, and I was eager to master it. Yet, every effort I made was met with failure. Their speech was rapid, and because the words seemed unrelated to physical objects, I could find no way to decode their meaning. Through intense focus, however, and after remaining in my shelter for several months, I identified the terms for common items; I learned to use the words fire, milk, bread, and wood. I also discovered the names of the cottagers. The young man and his companion had multiple names, while the old man was simply called father. The girl was known as sister or Agatha, and the youth as Felix, brother, or son. I cannot express the joy I felt when I grasped the meaning of these sounds and could speak them myself. I identified several other words, such as good, dearest, and unhappy, though I could not yet fully understand or utilize them.

I passed the winter in this fashion. The gentle nature and beauty of the family made me feel very close to them; when they were sad, I felt downcast, and when they were happy, I shared in their delight.

They gestured in ways I could not grasp, yet I observed that her arrival brought cheer to the home, lifting their gloom just as the sun clears away the dawn fog. Felix appeared especially joyful, greeting his Arabian visitor with radiant smiles. Agatha, ever kind, kissed the beautiful newcomer’s hands and gestured toward her brother, which I interpreted as a sign that he had been miserable before her arrival. Several hours went by in this manner, with their faces showing a happiness I could not fathom. Soon, I realized from the repeated sounds the stranger mimicked that she was attempting to acquire their tongue; I immediately resolved to utilize these same lessons for my own benefit. The visitor mastered about twenty words during that initial session; while I already knew most of them, I gained knowledge from the remainder.

“As evening fell, Agatha and the Arabian went to bed. Upon parting, Felix kissed the stranger’s hand and bid her, ‘Good night, sweet Safie.’ He remained awake much longer to talk with his father, and from the frequent mention of her name, I surmised that their charming guest was the topic of their discussion. I desperately longed to grasp their meaning, focusing all my mental energy toward that goal, yet I found it entirely beyond my reach.

“The following morning, Felix departed for his labor, and once Agatha had completed her customary chores, the Arabian sat at the old man’s feet. She took up his guitar and played melodies so captivatingly lovely that they immediately brought tears of both sadness and joy to my eyes. She sang, her voice rising and falling in a melodic flow, much like a woodland nightingale.

“Upon finishing, she handed the instrument to Agatha, who initially hesitated. She played a modest tune, singing along in gentle tones, though lacking the stranger’s extraordinary skill. The old man seemed enchanted, speaking words that Agatha tried to interpret for Safie, seemingly conveying that her music provided him with immense pleasure.

“The days continued as tranquilly as before, with the only change being that joy had replaced the sorrow on my friends’ faces. Safie remained constantly cheerful; she and I progressed quickly in our linguistic studies, and within two months, I could understand the majority of what my protectors said.

“Meanwhile, the dark earth became blanketed in grass, and the verdant banks were dotted with countless flowers, delightful to both smell and sight, appearing like pale, glowing stars amidst the moonlit trees. The sun grew hotter, the nights turned clear and mild, and my nightly wanderings became a source of great joy, though they were significantly curtailed by the sun’s late setting and early rising. I never dared to venture out in daylight, terrified of encountering the same hostility I had faced in the first village I visited.

“I dedicated my days to intense study so that I might learn the language more quickly. I can claim that I advanced faster than the Arabian, who understood very little and spoke in fragmented phrases, whereas I could grasp and replicate nearly every word spoken.”

They made many gestures that I did not understand, but I saw that her presence brought happiness to the cottage, clearing away their sorrow just as the sun burns off the morning mist. Felix seemed especially happy and welcomed his Arabian visitor with delighted smiles. Agatha, ever gentle, kissed the hands of the lovely stranger and pointed toward her brother, using signs that I interpreted to mean he had been miserable until she arrived. She took his guitar and played melodies so entrancingly beautiful that they immediately drew tears of sorrow and joy from my eyes. She sang, her voice flowing in a rich cadence that swelled and faded like a nightingale in the woods.

When she finished, she handed the guitar to Agatha, who initially declined. She played a simple tune, her voice accompanying it in sweet tones, though unlike the stranger’s wondrous performance. The old man seemed enraptured and said words that Agatha tried to explain to Safie, appearing to express that her music gave him the greatest delight.

The days passed as peacefully as before, with the only change being that joy had replaced sadness on the faces of my friends. Safie was always cheerful; she and I improved rapidly in our language skills, so that within two months I began to understand most of the words spoken by my protectors.

Safie explained that her mother was a Christian Arab captured and enslaved by the Turks; her striking beauty captured the heart of Safie’s father, who eventually wed her. The young woman described her mother with great fervor, noting that, having been born free, she despised the servitude she now endured. She educated her daughter in her own faith and encouraged her to seek intellectual growth and a spirit of autonomy denied to women under the laws of Muhammad. Though her mother passed away, her teachings remained fixed in Safie’s mind, who felt repulsed by the idea of returning to Asia to be confined within a harem, restricted to childish diversions that did not suit a spirit now shaped by lofty thoughts and a desire for virtue. The possibility of wedding a Christian and living in a land where women held a recognized place in society delighted her.

The date for the Turk’s execution was set, yet the night before, he escaped his cell and was many miles from Paris by dawn. Felix had obtained travel documents for his father, his sister, and himself. He had earlier shared his scheme with his father, who assisted in the ruse by leaving their home under the guise of traveling and hiding with his daughter in a secluded section of Paris.

Felix guided the escapees across France to Lyons and over Mont Cenis to Leghorn, where the merchant intended to wait for a good chance to travel into Turkish territory.

Safie decided to stay with her father until he left, and before that time, the Turk reaffirmed his vow that she would marry her rescuer; Felix stayed with them, awaiting that moment, and meanwhile cherished the company of the Arabian, who showed him the most sincere and gentle devotion. They communicated through an interpreter, and occasionally through glances, while Safie sang the beautiful melodies of her homeland for him.

The Turk permitted this closeness and fostered the young lovers' hopes, though he secretly harbored different intentions. He detested the thought of his daughter marrying a Christian, yet he dreaded Felix’s anger should he seem unenthusiastic, knowing he remained vulnerable to his rescuer if he chose to report him to the Italian authorities. He considered countless schemes to maintain the deception until it was no longer required, intending to secretly take his daughter away upon his departure. His designs were aided by reports arriving from Paris.

The French government was furious at the escape of their prisoner and worked tirelessly to find and punish his benefactor. Felix’s conspiracy was soon uncovered, leading to the imprisonment of De Lacey and Agatha. This news reached Felix, shattering his blissful state. His elderly, sightless father and kind sister were suffering in a foul prison while he remained in the open air, enjoying the company of the woman he loved.

She taught her daughter the principles of her faith and encouraged her to seek intellectual growth and an independent spirit, things forbidden to women under Islamic law. For some time, I was lost in speculation as to the cause, but yesterday an idea occurred to me, and if it is correct, I urge you to admit it. Keeping quiet on such a matter would not only be useless but would bring triple the misery upon us all.”

I shook violently at his opening remarks, and my father continued—

“I admit, my son, that I have always looked forward to your marriage with our dear Elizabeth as the foundation of our family happiness and the support of my final years. You were attached to each other from your earliest childhood; you studied together and seemed, in temperament and interests, perfectly suited to one another. But human judgment is so blind that what I thought were the best factors for my plan may have actually ruined it. Perhaps you view her as a sister, without any desire for her to be your wife. Or perhaps you have met someone else you love, and, feeling bound by honor to Elizabeth, this internal conflict is causing the intense misery you seem to feel.”

“My dear father, please set your mind at ease. I love my cousin tenderly and sincerely. I have never met any woman who inspires such deep admiration and affection in me as Elizabeth does. My future hopes and prospects are entirely tied to the expectation of our marriage.”

“Hearing your feelings on this subject, my dear Victor, gives me more pleasure than I have felt in a long time. If you feel this way, we will certainly be happy, no matter how current events may cast a shadow over us. But it is this shadow that seems to have taken such a strong hold on your mind that I wish to clear away. Tell me, therefore, if you have any objection to an immediate wedding. We have been unfortunate, and recent events have pulled us away from the everyday peace suitable for my age and health. You are younger; yet I do not imagine, possessing a comfortable fortune as you do, that an early marriage would interfere with any future plans for success or service you may have made.”

A real change had occurred in me. My health, which had been failing, was now much improved, and my spirits rose whenever I was not thinking of my unhappy promise. My father noticed this change with pleasure and began to consider how he might remove the remaining traces of my sadness, which would occasionally return in fits, casting a dark shadow over my brighter moments. At those times, I sought complete solitude. I spent entire days alone on the lake in a small boat, watching the clouds and listening to the waves, feeling quiet and listless. However, the fresh air and bright sun usually helped me regain some composure, and upon returning, I greeted my friends with a readier smile and a lighter heart.

We traveled to the burial site of the renowned Hampden and the battlefield where that patriot perished. Briefly, my spirit rose above its wretched and degrading anxieties to reflect upon the noble concepts of freedom and martyrdom that these locations commemorate. For a fleeting moment, I felt bold enough to cast aside my shackles and survey my surroundings with a liberated and noble outlook, yet the metal had scarred my skin, and I collapsed once more into my pathetic state, shaking and devoid of hope.

We departed Oxford with sorrow and traveled to Matlock, our subsequent stop. The landscape surrounding this village bore a resemblance to the vistas of Switzerland, though everything appeared on a more modest level, and the verdant hills lacked the crowning distant white Alps that invariably grace the pine-covered peaks of my homeland. We toured the remarkable cavern and the small displays of natural history, where the artifacts were arranged similarly to the exhibits in Servox and Chamounix. The mention of that latter name by Henry caused me to shudder, and I hurried to leave Matlock, as it was linked to that horrific event.

Moving north from Derby, we spent two months in Cumberland and Westmorland. I could almost imagine I was back among the Swiss peaks. The small drifts of snow still clinging to the northern slopes, the lakes, and the rushing of the stony brooks were all cherished and recognizable sights to me. In this place, we also formed friendships that nearly tricked me into feeling content. Clerval’s joy was correspondingly larger than my own; his intellect flourished among gifted individuals, and he discovered within himself greater potential and depth than he could have supposed possible while in the company of those less capable. “I could spend my days here,” he remarked to me; “and among these peaks, I would hardly miss Switzerland or the Rhine.”

Yet he discovered that the existence of a wanderer involves significant hardship alongside its pleasures. One’s emotions are constantly strained; just as one begins to settle into tranquility, one is compelled to abandon the source of that comfort for something novel, which then demands attention, only to be forsaken for further new experiences.

We had barely toured the diverse lakes of Cumberland and Westmorland and grown fond of some of the local residents when the time for our meeting with our Scottish acquaintance drew near, and we departed to continue our journey. Personally, I felt no regret. I had by then ignored my vow for quite some time, and I dreaded the consequences of the demon’s frustration. He might stay in Switzerland and unleash his retribution upon my kin. This thought haunted and plagued me during every interval when I might have otherwise found rest and calm. I awaited my correspondence with frantic eagerness; if the letters were late, I was wretched and besieged by countless terrors; and when they finally appeared, bearing the handwriting of Elizabeth or my father, I scarcely possessed the courage to open them and learn my destiny. At times, I imagined the monster trailed me and might hasten my negligence by slaying my traveling companion.

I exited the chamber and, after turning the key, swore a serious oath to myself that I would never return to my work; then, with shaking legs, I retreated to my own quarters. I was solitary; no one was nearby to scatter the shadows or lift the nauseating weight of my most horrifying thoughts.

Many hours went by, and I stayed by my window watching the ocean; it was nearly still, as the breezes had died down, and the entire world rested beneath the gaze of the tranquil moon. Only a handful of fishing boats dotted the surface, and occasionally a soft wind carried the murmurs of the fishermen hailing each other. I sensed the quiet, though I barely grasped its absolute depth, until my hearing was suddenly caught by the splashing of oars by the coast, and a figure stepped onto the land near my dwelling.

Moments later, I caught the groan of my door, as if someone were trying to unlatch it quietly. I shook from top to bottom; I had a foreboding of who it might be and wanted to wake one of the laborers who lived in a hut nearby; yet I was seized by that feeling of paralysis, so common in terrifying nightmares, when you try in vain to escape a looming threat, and I remained fixed where I stood.

Soon I caught the noise of pacing in the hallway; the door swung wide, and the creature I feared stood there. Closing the door, he drew near and spoke in a muffled tone,

“You have ruined the task you started; what is your plan? Do you truly intend to violate your word? I have suffered labor and agony; I departed Switzerland with you; I skulked along the banks of the Rhine, through its willow-covered isles and across the peaks of its mountains. I have lived for many months on the moors of England and in the wilds of Scotland. I have suffered immense exhaustion, freezing temperatures, and starvation; do you dare to crush my expectations?”

“Leave! I am breaking my word; I will never fashion another being like you, matching your ugliness and malice.”

“Slave, I once debated with you, but you have shown yourself undeserving of my patience. Keep in mind that I possess strength; you think you are unhappy, but I can render you so miserable that the sun will be loathsome to you. You are my maker, but I am your superior; submit!”

“The time for my hesitation has ended, and the moment for your authority has passed. Your menaces cannot sway me to commit an evil deed; instead, they solidify my resolve not to build you a partner in crime. Should I, in cold blood, unleash upon the world a fiend whose joy is found in slaughter and sorrow? Go! I am resolute, and your speech will only fuel my fury.”

The creature perceived my resolve in my expression and ground his teeth in the futility of his rage. “Must every man,” he shouted, “find a spouse for his heart, and every animal have its partner, while I remain solitary? I possessed capacity for love, and it was met with loathing and contempt. Human! You may despise me, but take heed! Your days will be spent in terror and grief, and soon the strike will land that must tear your joy away forever.”

I left the room, locked the door, and made a solemn vow to myself never to resume my work; then, with shaking legs, I went to my own room. I was alone; no one was nearby to clear away the gloom or relieve me from the sickening weight of my most terrifying thoughts.

Several hours passed, and I stayed by my window looking out at the sea; it was almost still, for the winds were quiet, and all of nature rested under the gaze of the calm moon. Only a few fishing boats dotted the water, and occasionally a soft breeze carried the sound of voices as the fishermen called to each other. I felt the silence, though I was barely aware of how deep it was, until my ear was suddenly caught by the sound of oars paddling near the shore, and someone landed close to my house.

A few minutes later, I heard my door creak, as if someone were trying to open it quietly. I trembled from head to toe; I had a premonition of who it was and wanted to wake one of the peasants who lived in a cottage nearby, but I was overcome by that feeling of helplessness often experienced in nightmares, when you try in vain to escape an approaching danger, and I was frozen in place.

I have lived for many months on the moors of England and in the wilds of Scotland. I have endured immense fatigue, cold, and hunger; do you dare destroy my hopes?”

“Leave! I am breaking my promise; I will never create another like you, equal in deformity and wickedness.”

“Slave, I reasoned with you before, but you have proven yourself unworthy of my patience. Remember that I have power; you think you are miserable, but I can make you so wretched that the light of day will be hateful to you. You are my creator, but I am your master; obey!”

“My time of hesitation is over, and the time for your power has ended. Your threats cannot move me to commit an act of wickedness; instead, they confirm my decision not to create a partner in vice for you. Should I, in cold blood, unleash upon the earth a demon who delights in death and misery? Leave! I am firm, and your words will only fuel my rage.”

My entire existence seemed like a phantom; at times, I questioned if it were truly factual, as it lacked the weight of reality in my mind. These were my initial thoughts, though I eventually discovered that Mr. Kirwin had treated me with great benevolence. He had arranged for the finest room in the jail to be readied for me—though even the best was miserable—and he was the one who had secured the doctor and the nurse. Admittedly, he rarely visited, for while he sincerely wished to alleviate the suffering of all people, he preferred not to witness the torment and frantic ravings of a murderer. Consequently, he stopped by occasionally to ensure I was not being ignored, but his stays were brief and infrequent.

One day, as I was slowly mending, I sat in a chair with my eyes partially closed and my face pale as a corpse. I was consumed by sorrow and despair, frequently pondering whether it would be better to embrace death than to persist in a world filled with such misery for me. At one point, I contemplated confessing to the crime and accepting the legal punishment, even though I was less innocent than poor Justine had been. I was lost in these musings when the door to my room swung open and Mr. Kirwin walked in. His face showed pity and kindness; he pulled a chair near mine and spoke to me in French,

“I worry that this environment is very distressing for you; is there anything I can do to improve your situation?”

“I appreciate your offer, but your suggestions mean nothing to me; there is no solace on this earth that I am able to accept.”

“I realize that the compassion of a stranger offers little relief to someone burdened by such an unusual tragedy. Nevertheless, I hope you will soon leave this dismal place, as I am sure evidence will be found to clear you of this criminal accusation.”

“That is my smallest worry; through a series of bizarre occurrences, I have become the most wretched of men. Harassed and tormented as I am and have been, how could death be a misfortune for me?”

“Indeed, nothing could be more tragic or agonizing than the strange circumstances that have recently unfolded. You were cast upon this shore, known for its kindness, by some unexpected mishap, only to be immediately apprehended and accused of homicide. The first thing you beheld was the corpse of your companion, killed in such an inexplicable fashion and positioned, as if by some demon, directly in your way.”

I turned away in disgust from the woman who could say something so heartless to someone who had just been saved from the brink of death. I felt weak and unable to process everything that had happened. My entire life felt like a dream; I sometimes doubted if it were actually real, as it never felt truly tangible to me.

Chapter 22

The sea voyage concluded. Upon landing, we traveled to Paris. I quickly realized my vigor was spent and that I required rest before proceeding. My father’s devotion was tireless, yet he remained ignorant of the root of my agony, attempting misguided cures for my hopeless condition. He encouraged me to find diversion among people. I loathed the sight of humanity. No, not loathed! They were my kin, my peers, and I felt drawn even to the most unsightly among them as if they were divine, heavenly beings. Yet, I believed I had no place in their company. I had unleashed a foe upon them who delighted in their slaughter and thrived on their suffering. If they understood my wicked deeds and the atrocities stemming from my actions, they would surely despise me and drive me from the earth.

My father eventually conceded to my wish to shun society, attempting to dispel my misery through various reasonings. At times, he assumed I was deeply wounded by the humiliation of facing a murder accusation, and he tried to convince me that such pride was pointless.

“Alas! Father,” I replied, “you understand me so poorly. Humanity, with all its emotions and desires, would truly be debased if a wretch like me possessed pride. Justine, poor, unfortunate Justine, was as guiltless as I, yet she faced the same accusation; she perished for it, and I am responsible—I killed her. William, Justine, and Henry—they all died because of me.”

During my incarceration, my father had frequently heard me make these claims. When I confessed, he sometimes seemed to crave an account, while at other times he appeared to view it as the product of madness, believing that during my sickness, such notions had taken root in my mind and persisted into my recovery. I evaded any clarification and remained steadfastly mute regarding the creature I had fashioned. I was convinced I would be deemed insane, which alone would have silenced me. Furthermore, I could not bring myself to reveal a mystery that would overwhelm my listener with alarm and instill unnatural terror within his heart. Consequently, I suppressed my desperate longing for comfort and remained quiet when I would have sacrificed everything to share the lethal truth. Nevertheless, phrases similar to those I have noted would escape me involuntarily. I could provide no context for them, yet their veracity partially eased the weight of my enigmatic sorrow.

Toward morning, I was gripped by a nightmare; I felt the monster’s hands around my throat and could not break free; groans and screams echoed in my ears. My father, who was watching over me, noticed my agitation and woke me. The crashing waves were all around, the sky was cloudy above, and the monster was gone. A sense of safety—a feeling that a temporary truce had been called between the present moment and the inevitable, disastrous future—gave me a kind of calm forgetfulness, something the human mind is naturally prone to experience.

Shortly after I reached home, my father discussed my impending wedding to Elizabeth. I stayed mute.

“Do you possess some other affection?”

“None at all. I adore Elizabeth and anticipate our marriage with joy. Set the date, therefore; and upon that day, I shall dedicate my existence, for life or death, to my cousin’s joy.”

“My dear Victor, do not talk in such a way. Great tragedies have struck us, yet we must hold tighter to the survivors and shift our love for the departed to those still with us. Our group will be limited but tightly knit by bonds of affection and shared suffering. Once time has dulled your grief, fresh and precious responsibilities will emerge to fill the void left by those we have so harshly lost.”

These were my father’s teachings. Yet, the memory of the warning haunted me; you cannot be surprised that, given the demon’s absolute power in his murderous acts, I viewed him as nearly unstoppable, and when he declared, “I shall be with you on your wedding-night,” I considered the predicted outcome inevitable. Still, death seemed no tragedy if it meant losing Elizabeth, so I agreed with a satisfied and even bright expression that if my cousin agreed, the wedding should occur in ten days, thereby, as I supposed, finalizing my destiny.

Merciful God! Had I for a moment grasped the infernal purpose of my monstrous enemy, I would have exiled myself forever from my homeland and roamed the world as a lonely wanderer rather than agreeing to this wretched union. Yet, as if wielding sorcery, the creature had obscured his true designs from me; and while I believed I was merely arranging my own demise, I was accelerating the death of a much more precious victim.

As the date for our marriage approached, whether from fear or a sense of foreboding, my spirit faltered. I hid my emotions behind a facade of cheerfulness that delighted my father, though it barely fooled the observant and sensitive gaze of Elizabeth. She anticipated our marriage with calm satisfaction, tempered by a slight anxiety, born of past tragedies, that this seemingly certain happiness might vanish like a phantom, leaving behind only profound and eternal sorrow.

Arrangements for the ceremony were underway, well-wishers visited, and everything appeared joyful. I buried the dread consuming me deep within and participated with apparent sincerity in my father’s schemes, even though they might only function as the stage dressing for my own ruin. Through my father’s efforts, a portion of Elizabeth’s legacy had been returned to her by the Austrian authorities. A modest estate on the banks of Lake Como was now hers.

It is fine for the unfortunate to be resigned, but for the guilty, there is no peace. The agony of remorse poisons the comfort that might otherwise be found in indulging one’s grief.

I secured passage on that same boat, yet he vanished, I know not how.

Throughout the wilderness of Tartary and Russia, though he consistently eluded me, I have perpetually pursued his path. Occasionally, villagers, terrified by this monstrous vision, alerted me to his direction; at other times, he himself—fearing that I might lose his trail and succumb to despair—left behind signs to lead me. Snow fell upon me, and I observed the impression of his massive footprint upon the white expanse. To you who are just beginning your journey, for whom worry is novel and suffering is foreign, how could you grasp what I have endured and continue to endure? Cold, deprivation, and weariness were the minor hardships I was fated to suffer; I was plagued by some demon and bore my own private hell with me. Nevertheless, a benevolent spirit accompanied and steered my path, and when I complained the most, it would suddenly deliver me from seemingly impossible obstacles. At times, when my body, overcome by starvation, collapsed from exhaustion, a meal was provided for me in the wilderness that revived and encouraged me. The food was certainly simple, the kind eaten by local peasants, yet I do not doubt it was placed there by the spirits I had called upon for help. Frequently, when the land was parched and the sky cloudless, and I was withered by thirst, a small cloud would darken the heavens, release the few drops that restored me, and then disappear.

For this reason, I will keep myself alive. To carry out this precious revenge, I will look upon the sun again and walk on the green grass of the earth, which otherwise would disappear from my sight forever. I call upon you, spirits of the dead, and you, wandering agents of vengeance, to assist and guide me in my mission. I lunged toward the spot where the sound came from, but the devil slipped from my grasp. Suddenly, the broad disk of the moon rose and shone directly upon his ghastly, distorted form as he fled with more than human speed.

I pursued him, and for many months, this has been my task. Guided by a small clue, I followed the winding Rhone, but in vain. The blue Mediterranean appeared, and by a strange coincidence, I saw the fiend enter a ship bound for the Black Sea at night to hide. I booked passage on the same vessel, but he escaped, I know not how.

Across the wilds of Tartary and Russia, although he still evaded me, I have always followed his trail. Sometimes the peasants, frightened by this horrific apparition, told me of his path; sometimes he himself, fearing that I would despair and die if I lost all trace of him, left some mark to guide me. Snow fell upon my head, and I saw the print of his huge step on the white plain. To you, just beginning life, to whom worry is new and agony unknown, how can you understand what I have felt and still feel? Cold, hunger, and fatigue were the least of the pains I was destined to endure; I was cursed by some devil and carried my own eternal hell with me. Yet, a spirit of good still followed and directed my steps, and when I complained the most, it would suddenly rescue me from seemingly impossible difficulties. Sometimes, when my body, overcome by hunger, collapsed from exhaustion, a meal was prepared for me in the wilderness that restored and energized me. The food was indeed coarse, the kind the local peasants ate, but I do not doubt it was placed there by the spirits I had invoked to help me. Often, when everything was dry, the sky cloudless, and I was parched with thirst, a small cloud would dim the sky, shed the few drops that revived me, and vanish.

To achieve this, I will keep myself alive. To carry out this precious revenge, I will look at the sun again and walk on the green grass of the earth, which otherwise would disappear from my eyes forever. I call upon you, spirits of the dead, and you, wandering agents of vengeance, to help and guide me in my task. Let that cursed and hellish monster drink deeply of agony; let him feel the despair that now torments me.

I had begun my plea with solemnity and an awe that almost convinced me the ghosts of my murdered friends heard and approved of my dedication, but fury took hold of me as I finished, and rage choked my voice.

I was answered through the silence of the night by a loud and fiendish laugh. It rang in my ears long and heavily; the mountains echoed it, and I felt as if all of hell surrounded me with mockery and laughter. Surely, in that moment, I would have been driven to madness and destroyed my miserable life, if not for the fact that my vow was heard and I was saved for vengeance. The laughter died away, and then a well-known and hated voice, apparently close to my ear, spoke to me in a clear whisper, “I am satisfied, miserable wretch! You have decided to live, and I am satisfied.”

I darted toward the spot where the sound came from, but the devil escaped my grasp. Suddenly, the broad disk of the moon rose and shone directly upon his ghastly and distorted shape as he fled with more than human speed.

I pursued him, and for many months this has been my task. Guided by a small clue, I followed the winding path of the Rhone, but in vain. The blue Mediterranean appeared, and by a strange chance, I saw the fiend enter a ship at night and hide himself on a vessel bound for the Black Sea. I booked passage on the same ship, but he escaped, I do not know how.

In the wilds of Tartary and Russia, although he still evaded me, I have always followed his trail. Sometimes the peasants, scared by this horrible apparition, told me which way he went; sometimes he himself, fearing that I would despair and die if I lost his trail, left some mark to guide me. Snow fell on my head, and I saw the print of his huge foot on the white plain. To you who are just starting life, to whom worry is new and agony unknown, how can you understand what I have felt and still feel? Cold, hunger, and fatigue were the least of the pains I was destined to endure; I was cursed by some devil and carried my own personal hell with me. Yet, a spirit of good still followed and directed my steps, and when I complained the most, it would suddenly pull me out of seemingly impossible difficulties. Sometimes, when my body, overcome by hunger, collapsed from exhaustion, a meal was prepared for me in the wilderness that restored and energized me. The food was simple, like what the local peasants ate, but I do not doubt that it was placed there by the spirits I had called upon to help me. Often, when everything was dry, the sky was cloudless, and I was parched with thirst, a small cloud would dim the sky, shed the few drops that revived me, and then vanish.

In this fashion, many dreadful hours went by; a number of my dogs perished, and I was on the verge of collapsing from the weight of my suffering when I spotted your ship anchored, offering me the prospect of rescue and survival. I had no idea that ships ever ventured this far north and was stunned by the sight. I promptly broke apart a section of my sledge to fashion oars, and through this immense exertion, I managed to propel my ice raft toward your vessel. I had resolved that if you were traveling south, I would still commit myself to the mercy of the ocean rather than give up my goal. I hoped to persuade you to provide me with a boat so I could continue chasing my foe. However, you were heading north. You rescued me when my strength was spent, and I would have soon succumbed to my many trials, meeting a death I still fear, for my mission remains incomplete.

Oh! When will my guiding spirit, in leading me to the fiend, grant me the peace I crave so deeply; or must I perish while he continues to live? If I do, promise me, Walton, that he will not get away, that you will hunt him down and fulfill my need for retribution by killing him. Do I dare request that you take up my journey and endure the miseries I have faced? No; I am not that self-centered. Still, after I am gone, if he should show himself, if the agents of justice should lead him to you, swear that he shall not survive—swear that he will not prevail over my gathered sorrows and live on to increase his tally of dark deeds. He is articulate and convincing, and at one time his speech even held sway over my heart; but do not believe him. His spirit is as demonic as his appearance, brimming with deceit and devilish spite. Do not listen to him; invoke the names of William, Justine, Clerval, Elizabeth, my father, and the miserable Victor, and drive your blade into his chest. I will linger nearby and guide the steel to its mark.

Walton, continuing.

August 26th, 17—.

You have perused this bizarre and terrifying account, Margaret; and does your blood not run cold with dread, just as mine does even now? At times, overcome by sudden torment, he could not proceed with his story; at other moments, his voice, fractured yet sharp, struggled to articulate words so filled with pain. His handsome and expressive eyes were at times ignited with fury, then softened into dejected grief and dimmed by boundless misery. Occasionally he mastered his features and tone, recounting the most ghastly events in a calm voice, hiding any sign of distress; then, like an erupting volcano, his face would abruptly shift to a look of the most intense rage as he screamed curses at his tormentor.

His narrative is coherent and delivered with the appearance of absolute sincerity, yet I admit to you that the correspondence of Felix and Safie, which he displayed to me, and the sighting of the creature from our deck, provided me with a stronger belief in the reality of his tale than his declarations, however sincere and logical they seemed. Such a monster, then, truly exists!

In this way, many terrifying hours passed. Several of my dogs died, and I was on the verge of collapsing under the weight of my suffering when I saw your ship anchored, offering me hope for rescue and survival. I had no idea that ships ever traveled this far north and was stunned by the sight. I quickly broke apart part of my sledge to build oars, and through this, with immense exhaustion, I managed to steer my ice raft toward your vessel. I had decided that if you were heading south, I would still risk the mercy of the sea rather than give up my goal. I hoped to convince you to give me a boat so I could continue chasing my enemy. But you were heading north. You brought me aboard when my strength was gone; otherwise, I would have soon succumbed to my many hardships and died—a death I still fear, because my task remains unfinished.

Oh, when will my guiding spirit lead me to the demon and grant me the rest I crave? Or must I die while he continues to live? If I do, swear to me, Walton, that he will not escape, that you will hunt him down and satisfy my vengeance by killing him. Do I dare ask you to take on my journey and endure the suffering I have faced? No, I am not that selfish. Yet, when I am dead, if he appears, or if the forces of justice lead him to you, swear that he will not live. Swear that he will not triumph over my endless suffering and survive to add to his list of dark crimes. He is eloquent and persuasive; his words once even swayed my own heart, but do not trust him. His soul is as hellish as his appearance, filled with treachery and demonic malice. Do not listen to him. Call out the names of William, Justine, Clerval, Elizabeth, my father, and the wretched Victor, then drive your sword into his heart. I will hover nearby and guide the blade true.

Walton, continuing.

Walton, continuing.

August 26th, 17—.

Margaret, you have read this strange and terrifying story. Does your blood not run cold with horror, just as mine does now? Sometimes, overcome by sudden agony, he could not continue his tale. At other times, his voice was broken and piercing, struggling to speak words filled with anguish. His fine, lovely eyes would light up with anger, then soften into downcast sorrow, finally dimming with infinite misery. Sometimes he controlled his expression and tone, relating the most horrible events with a calm voice, hiding any sign of agitation. Then, like an erupting volcano, his face would suddenly shift into an expression of wild rage as he shrieked curses at his persecutor.

His story is coherent and told with the appearance of simple truth. Yet, I admit to you that the letters from Felix and Safie, which he showed me, and the sight of the monster from our ship, convinced me of the truth of his narrative more than his own earnest claims. Such a monster, then, truly exists!

Addressing the crew, he asked, "What is your intent? What do you ask of your leader? Have you so quickly abandoned your mission? Did you not label this a magnificent voyage? And why was it magnificent? Not because the path was calm like a tropical ocean, but because it was fraught with peril and dread, because each new event demanded your resilience and bravery, because you were meant to face and conquer the dangers and death that encompassed us. That is what made this a noble and honorable pursuit. You were to be celebrated as saviors of humanity, your names revered as those of courageous men who faced mortality for honor and the common good. Yet now, at the first hint of risk—or, if you prefer, the first significant test of your mettle—you retreat, satisfied to be remembered as men lacking the stamina to withstand cold and hardship; poor, shivering souls who fled back to their comfortable hearths. Such a retreat requires no such elaborate preparation; you need not have traveled this far and forced your captain to endure the humiliation of failure simply to demonstrate your cowardice. Oh! Act like men, or even better than men. Remain steadfast in your intentions and immovable as stone. This ice is not as unyielding as your hearts; it is changeable and will succumb if you command it. Do not go home to your families bearing the mark of shame. Return as victors who have battled and triumphed, never knowing the meaning of retreat." 

He delivered these words with a tone perfectly tuned to the shifting emotions of his address, his gaze filled with such noble ambition and heroism, that it is no surprise his men were stirred. They exchanged glances, speechless. I intervened, instructing them to withdraw and contemplate his words, stating that I would not force them further north if they firmly opposed it, but that I trusted their resolve would return upon reflection. 

They withdrew, and I turned to my companion, who had fallen into a state of exhaustion, appearing nearly lifeless. 

I cannot foresee the outcome, yet I would prefer death to a shameful retreat with my mission incomplete. Still, I fear that will be my destiny; the crew, lacking any sense of honor or glory, cannot be expected to endure these trials willingly. 

September 7th. 

The decision is final; I have agreed to turn back unless we are destroyed. My aspirations are shattered by their timidity and hesitation; I return empty-handed and defeated. It takes more stoicism than I possess to endure this unfairness with grace. 

September 12th. 

It is over; I am heading back to England. My dreams of service and renown are gone; I have lost my friend. Nevertheless, I will attempt to recount these painful events to you, my dear sister; and as I am carried toward England and toward you, I will not lose heart. 

On September 9th, the ice began to shift, and distant sounds like thunder echoed as the floes fractured and splintered in every direction.

Turning toward the men, he said, "What do you mean? What do you demand of your captain? Are you so easily swayed from your mission? Did you not call this a glorious expedition? And why was it glorious? Not because the path was smooth and calm like a southern sea, but because it was filled with danger and terror, because every new event demanded your fortitude and courage, because danger and death surrounded it, and these you were to face and overcome. For this reason, it was a glorious and honorable undertaking. You were to be hailed as benefactors of your species, your names adored as those of brave men who faced death for honor and the benefit of humanity. And now, look, at the first thought of danger, or, if you prefer, the first mighty and terrifying test of your courage, you shrink away and are content to be remembered as men who lacked the strength to endure cold and peril; and so, poor souls, they were chilly and returned to their warm firesides. Why, that does not require this level of preparation; you did not need to come this far and drag your captain into the shame of defeat merely to prove yourselves cowards. Oh! Be men, or be more than men. Stay true to your purposes and firm as a rock. This ice is not made of the same material as your hearts; it is changeable and cannot withstand you if you declare that it shall not. Do not return to your families with the mark of disgrace on your brows. Return as heroes who have fought and conquered and who do not know what it is to turn their backs on the enemy."

He spoke this with a voice so modulated to the different emotions expressed in his speech, with an eye so full of lofty ambition and heroism, that can you wonder these men were moved? They looked at one another and were unable to reply. I spoke; I told them to retire and consider what had been said, that I would not lead them further north if they strongly desired the contrary, but that I hoped their courage would return with reflection.

They retired and I turned toward my friend, but he was sunk in exhaustion and almost lifeless.

How all this will end, I do not know, but I would rather die than return shamefully, my purpose unfulfilled. Yet I fear such will be my fate; the men, unsupported by ideas of glory and honor, can never willingly continue to endure their present hardships.

September 7th.

The die is cast; I have consented to return if we are not destroyed. Thus are my hopes blasted by cowardice and indecision; I come back ignorant and disappointed. It requires more philosophy than I possess to bear this injustice with patience.

September 12th.

It is over; I am returning to England. I have lost my hopes of utility and glory; I have lost my friend. But I will endeavor to detail these bitter circumstances to you, my dear sister; and while I am carried toward England and toward you, I will not despair.

September 9th, the ice began to move, and roarings like thunder were heard at a distance as the islands split and cracked in every direction.

I told them I would not lead them further north if they strongly desired otherwise, but that I hoped their courage would return upon reflection.

September 7th.

The decision is made; I have agreed to return if we are not destroyed. My hopes are ruined by cowardice and indecision; I return ignorant and disappointed. It requires more philosophy than I possess to bear this injustice with patience.

September 12th.

It is over; I am returning to England. I have lost my hopes of being useful and achieving glory; I have lost my friend. But I will try to describe these bitter circumstances to you, my dear sister; and while I am carried toward England and toward you, I will not lose hope.

From that point on, wickedness served as my virtue. Driven to this extreme, I had no alternative but to align my character with the path I had voluntarily taken. Finishing my devilish plan turned into an unquenchable obsession. And now it is finished; there lies my final victim!”

Initially, I felt moved by his displays of sorrow; however, remembering Frankenstein’s warnings about his gift for rhetoric and persuasion, and looking once more at the dead body of my companion, my anger flared up again. “Monster!” I exclaimed. “It is convenient for you to arrive here and weep over the destruction you have caused. You set fire to a collection of structures, and once they have burned down, you sit amidst the debris and mourn their collapse. You deceitful demon! If the man you grieve were still alive, he would remain the target, he would again become the prey, of your wicked retribution. You feel no genuine compassion; you only lament because the target of your malice is no longer within your reach.”

“Oh, that is not the case—not at all,” the creature broke in. “Though that must be the conclusion you draw from the apparent intent of my deeds. Yet I do not ask for shared feelings in my wretchedness. I can never find any empathy. When I originally looked for it, I wanted to share the love of virtue, the sensations of joy and warmth that filled my entire existence. But now that virtue has faded into a ghost, and those joys and affections have transformed into harsh and sickening despair, where should I look for understanding? I am prepared to endure my pain in solitude for as long as it lasts; when I perish, I am perfectly content that hatred and shame should burden my legacy. My imagination was once comforted by visions of goodness, renown, and pleasure. I once mistakenly trusted that I would encounter individuals who, overlooking my physical appearance, would cherish me for the noble traits I was prepared to reveal. I was sustained by lofty ideas of integrity and loyalty. But now, wrongdoing has lowered me below the lowliest beast. No sin, no harm, no malice, no suffering can be compared to mine. When I review the terrifying list of my transgressions, I cannot accept that I am the same being whose mind was once occupied by grand and elevated dreams of the beauty and splendor of righteousness. Yet it is true; the fallen angel turns into a spiteful devil. Even that adversary of God and humanity had companions and allies in his isolation; I am entirely alone.

“You, who claim to be Frankenstein’s friend, appear to understand my offenses and his tragedies. But in the account he provided, he could not capture the years and months of agony I spent languishing in helpless rage. For while I ruined his prospects, I did not fulfill my own longings. They remained forever intense and hungry; I continued to crave love and companionship, and I was continually rejected. Was there no unfairness in this? Am I to be considered the sole offender, when all of humanity committed wrongs against me? Why do you not despise Felix, who cast his acquaintance from his home with insults?”

From that point on, evil became my good. Driven this far, I had no choice but to adapt my nature to the path I had willingly chosen. Completing my demonic plan became an insatiable passion. And now it is finished; there lies my final victim!”

I was initially moved by his expressions of misery; yet, when I recalled what Frankenstein had said about his powers of eloquence and persuasion, and when I looked again at the lifeless body of my friend, my indignation was rekindled. “Wretch!” I said. “It is convenient that you come here to whine over the destruction you have caused. You throw a torch into a group of buildings, and when they are consumed, you sit among the ruins and lament their fall. Hypocritical fiend! If the man you mourn were still alive, he would still be the target, once again the prey, of your cursed vengeance. It is not pity that you feel; you only lament because the victim of your malice is now beyond your reach.”

“Oh, it is not like that—not like that,” the being interrupted. “Yet that must be the impression you get from the apparent purpose of my actions. I do not seek shared feelings in my misery. I can never find sympathy. When I first sought it, it was the love of virtue, the feelings of happiness and affection with which my whole being overflowed, that I wished to share. But now that virtue has become a shadow to me, and happiness and affection have turned into bitter, loathing despair, in what should I seek sympathy? I am content to suffer alone for as long as my suffering lasts; when I die, I am satisfied that hatred and disgrace should burden my memory. Once, my imagination was soothed by dreams of virtue, fame, and enjoyment. Once, I falsely hoped to meet beings who, forgiving my outward appearance, would love me for the excellent qualities I was capable of revealing. I was nourished by high thoughts of honor and devotion. But now, crime has degraded me below the lowest animal. No guilt, no mischief, no malice, no misery can be compared to mine. When I review the frightening list of my sins, I cannot believe I am the same creature whose thoughts were once filled with sublime and transcendent visions of the beauty and majesty of goodness. But it is true; the fallen angel becomes a malignant devil. Yet even that enemy of God and man had friends and associates in his desolation; I am alone.

“You, who call Frankenstein your friend, seem to have knowledge of my crimes and his misfortunes. But in the details he gave you, he could not sum up the hours and months of misery I endured, wasting away in helpless passions. For while I destroyed his hopes, I did not satisfy my own desires. They were forever burning and craving; I still desired love and companionship, and I was still rejected. Was there no injustice in this? Am I to be considered the only criminal, when all of humanity sinned against me? Why do you not hate Felix, who drove his friend from his door with insults?”

But it is true; the fallen angel becomes a malignant devil. Yet even that enemy of God and man had friends and companions in his isolation; I am alone.

“You, who call Frankenstein your friend, seem to know of my crimes and his misfortunes. But in the account he gave you, he could not describe the hours and months of misery I endured while wasting away in helpless rage. For while I destroyed his hopes, I did not satisfy my own desires. They were always intense and craving; I still wanted love and companionship, and I was still rejected. Was there no injustice in this? Am I to be considered the only criminal, when all of humanity sinned against me? Why do you not hate Felix, who drove his friend from his door with insults?”

With my thoughts entirely consumed by these notions, I felt as though spellbound, lingering near the future residence of the young Earl. I observed the ongoing renovations and remained near the unloading carts as various luxury items, transported from London, were removed and carried into the estate. It was the former Queen’s intention to envelop her son in princely opulence. I witnessed luxurious rugs and silk draperies, golden decorations, intricately worked metals, ornate furnishings, and every accessory of high status being positioned so that only regal splendor would meet the eyes of one born of royal lineage. I observed these things, then shifted my attention to my own humble attire. What was the source of this disparity? It stemmed from nothing but betrayal, deceit, and a complete abandonment of noble empathy and kindness by the prince’s father. Surely, he too, whose veins carried the mingled blood of his haughty mother—he, the recognized center of the nation’s riches and aristocracy—had been instructed to utter my father’s name with contempt and to mock my legitimate requests for support. I attempted to convince myself that all this magnificence was merely a more obvious disgrace, and that by positioning his gold-threaded standard next to my worn and ragged flag, he was demonstrating not his greatness, but his moral decay. Nevertheless, I felt envious. His stable of fine horses, his expensive weaponry, the acclaim he received, the adoration, the eager attendants, his elevated status and high reputation—I viewed these as things violently stolen from me, and I envied them all with a fresh and agonizing resentment.

To add to my mental distress, Perdita, the idealistic Perdita, appeared to come alive with excitement when she informed me that the Earl of Windsor was soon to arrive.

“And this brings you joy?” I remarked, sullenly.

“It truly does, Lionel,” she answered; “I am quite eager to see him; he is the heir to our monarchs, the foremost nobleman in the country: everyone admires and cherishes him, and they claim that his status is his least significant virtue; he is noble, courageous, and kind.”

“You have mastered a fine lesson, Perdita,” I stated, “and you recite it so faithfully that you overlook the actual evidence we possess regarding the Earl’s character; his generosity toward us is evident in our abundance, his courage in the security he provides, his kindness in the attention he grants us. His status is his least significant virtue, you claim? In truth, all his virtues stem solely from his position; because he is wealthy, he is labeled generous; because he is influential, courageous; because he is well-attended, he is kind. Let them label him so, let all of England believe him to be such—we know the truth—he is our adversary—our stingy, cowardly, haughty adversary; if he possessed even a fraction of the virtues you attribute to him, he would treat us fairly, if only to demonstrate that if he must attack, it should not be against a defeated opponent. His father wronged my father—his father, secure upon his throne, dared to scorn the man who only lowered himself when he chose to associate with that royal ingrate. We, the descendants of the one and the other, are destined to be enemies as well. He shall discover that I am capable of feeling my wrongs; he shall learn to fear my retribution!”

With my mind completely consumed by these thoughts, I was, as if under a spell, drawn to haunt the future home of the young Earl. I watched the progress of the renovations and stood by the unloading wagons as various luxury items, brought from London, were taken out and moved into the mansion. It was part of the former Queen’s plan to surround her son with princely magnificence. I saw expensive carpets and silk curtains, gold ornaments, intricately embossed metals, ornate furniture, and all the trappings of high status arranged so that nothing but royal splendor would meet the eyes of someone of royal descent. I looked at these things, then turned my gaze to my own shabby clothes. Where did this difference come from? It came from nothing but ingratitude, dishonesty, and the prince’s father abandoning all noble sympathy and generous feeling. No doubt he, too, whose blood was mixed with that of his proud mother—the acknowledged center of the kingdom’s wealth and nobility—had been taught to speak my father’s name with disdain and to mock my legitimate claims to protection. I tried to convince myself that all this grandeur was merely a more glaring disgrace, and that by planting his gold-woven flag beside my tarnished and tattered banner, he was proclaiming not his superiority, but his own degradation. Yet, I envied him. His stable of beautiful horses, his weapons of costly craftsmanship, the praise he received, the adoration, the ready servants, his high status and high esteem—I viewed them all as things forcibly stolen from me, and I envied them with a new and tormenting bitterness.

To add to my frustration, Perdita, the idealistic Perdita, seemed to wake up to reality with excitement when she told me that the Earl of Windsor was about to arrive.

“And this pleases you?” I remarked, moodily.

“It truly does, Lionel,” she replied. “I really look forward to seeing him. He is the descendant of our kings, the highest noble in the land. Everyone admires and loves him, and they say that his rank is his least significant merit; he is generous, brave, and friendly.”

“You have learned your lesson well, Perdita,” I said, “and you repeat it so word-for-word that you forget the actual evidence we have of the Earl’s virtues. His generosity to us is obvious in our abundance, his bravery in the protection he provides, and his friendliness in the attention he pays us. His rank is his least merit, you say? Why, all his virtues are derived solely from his position. Because he is rich, he is called generous; because he is powerful, brave; because he is well-served, he is friendly. Let them call him that; let all of England believe it—we know him. He is our enemy—our stingy, cowardly, arrogant enemy. If he possessed even a fraction of the virtues you attribute to him, he would treat us justly, if only to show that if he must strike, it should not be against a fallen foe. His father wronged my father—his father, secure on his throne, dared to despise the man who only lowered himself when he deigned to associate with that royal ingrate. We, the descendants of the one and the other, must be enemies as well. He will find that I can feel my injuries; he will learn to fear my revenge!”

The penmanship was remarkably elegant; even I, who lacked affection for her and possessed little expertise in such things, could recognize the refinement in her stationery and how it was folded. Her tone conveyed significant warmth, appreciation, and gentleness, yet lacked romantic passion. Evadne was two years Adrian’s senior, and at eighteen, who truly falls for someone so much younger? I contrasted her calm letters with Adrian’s fervent ones. His spirit seemed to pour into his writing, the pages pulsing with the vitality of the love that defined his existence. The act of writing drained him, and he would often cry over the letters, overwhelmed by the intensity of the feelings they stirred within him.

Adrian’s character was reflected in his face, and he was far too transparent to ever practice deception. Evadne insisted that their romance remain hidden from his mother, and after some resistance, he eventually agreed. It was a futile promise; his behavior soon gave the secret away to the observant former queen. With the cautious discretion that defined her, she kept her knowledge to herself but moved quickly to distance her son from the captivating Greek woman. He was dispatched to Cumberland, yet the secret communication channel Evadne had established remained undetected. Consequently, the separation intended to drive them apart only strengthened their connection. To me, he spoke constantly of his beloved Ionian. Her homeland, its history, and its recent conflicts were all imbued with her brilliance and virtue. He accepted his exile only because she demanded it; otherwise, he would have proclaimed his devotion to all of England and defied his mother’s disapproval with unwavering resolve. Evadne’s feminine caution recognized that asserting his intentions would be pointless until he was older and more influential. Perhaps she also harbored a secret reluctance to publicly commit to someone she did not love—at least, not with the fiery intensity she believed she might one day experience with someone else. He followed her instructions and spent a year in Cumberland.

Several months after I had come back to England, I left London one day to go see my sister. Her company was my primary comfort and joy, and my mood invariably lifted at the prospect of meeting her. Her talk was filled with sharp observations and insight; within her delightful garden nook, fragrant with the most aromatic blossoms and decorated with impressive plaster casts, ancient urns, and her own reproductions of masterpieces by Raphael, Correggio, and Claude, I imagined I had found an enchanted sanctuary, untouched and unreachable by the clamorous disputes of statesmen or the shallow interests of the fashionable world. On this particular visit, my sister was not by herself, and I immediately knew her guest: it was Idris, the woman who had been the unseen focus of my intense, obsessive devotion.

With what appropriate words of awe and pleasure, with what select phrasing and gentle cadence, can I introduce the most beautiful, intelligent, and virtuous of women? How can a meager collection of terms capture the radiant glow that encircled her, or the countless charms that attended her constantly? The initial impression upon seeing that lovely face was one of absolute kindness and sincerity; honesty rested on her forehead, artlessness in her gaze, and divine warmth in her smile. Her slender, tall form swayed with the grace of a poplar in a western breeze, and her movement, like that of a goddess, resembled a winged angel recently descended from the heavens; the pearly pale tone of her skin was flushed with a pure tint, and her tone of voice was like the soft, muted melody of a flute. It is perhaps simplest to explain by way of comparison. I have described my sister’s virtues, yet she was entirely different from Idris. Perdita, even when she felt affection, remained guarded and shy; Idris was open and trusting. The former withdrew into isolation to protect herself from hurt and sorrow, while the latter moved through the world in the light of day, convinced that no one would cause her harm. Wordsworth once likened a beloved woman to two beautiful things in nature, though his verses always struck me as a contrast rather than a likeness:

A violet by a mossy stone
 Half hidden from the eye,
Fair as a star when only one
 Is shining in the sky.

Such a violet was the sweet Perdita, hesitant to expose herself to the breeze, shrinking from notice yet revealed by her own merits, and rewarding those who discovered her in her secluded path with a thousand graces. Idris was like that star, positioned in solitary brilliance within the soft crown of a mild evening; prepared to illuminate and please the world below, protected from every impurity by her immense distance from all that was not, like her, related to the divine.

I discovered this vision of loveliness in Perdita’s alcove, engaged in serious discussion with its occupant. When my sister spotted me, she stood up, took my hand, and remarked, “He has arrived, just as we hoped; this is Lionel, my brother.” Idris stood as well, turned her sky-blue eyes toward me, and with a unique elegance stated, “You scarcely require an introduction; we possess a portrait, greatly treasured by my father, which identifies you at once. Verney, you will recognize this connection, and as my brother’s friend, I feel I can rely upon you.”

Then, with eyes moist with tears and a quivering voice, she went on, “Dear friends, do not consider it odd that now, upon my first visit to you, I seek your help and share my hopes and anxieties with you.”
