I perused the chronicles of Greece, Rome, and England’s past, all while observing the movements of the woman I loved. At night, I could discern her silhouette against the walls of her room; by day, I watched her in her garden or riding through the park with her usual associates. I felt the spell would shatter if I were discovered, yet hearing the melody of her voice brought me joy. I attributed her beauty and peerless virtues to every heroine I read about—she was Antigone, leading the sightless Oedipus to the Eumenides’ grove and performing the burial rites for Polynices; she was Miranda in Prospero’s secluded cave; she was Haidee upon the shores of the Ionian island. I was delirious with intense, passionate devotion; yet a pride as fierce as flame possessed me, keeping me from revealing my feelings through any gesture or utterance.

Meanwhile, as I indulged in these opulent mental feasts, a common laborer would have scorned the meager sustenance I occasionally pilfered from the forest squirrels. I admit I was frequently tempted to return to the illicit habits of my youth and strike down the nearly tame pheasants that sat in the trees, fixing their bright gazes upon me. Yet they belonged to Adrian and were the charges of Idris; thus, although my hunger-sharpened imagination suggested they would be better suited for my kitchen spit than for the forest foliage, I nevertheless restrained my arrogant impulses and refrained from eating, choosing instead to dine on sentiment and dream fruitlessly of the sweet morsels I could not obtain while awake.

Yet, at this juncture, the entire structure of my life was on the verge of transformation. The neglected, orphaned son of Verney was about to be connected to the social order by a golden link and to participate in all the responsibilities and bonds of existence. Miracles were to be performed on my behalf, with the gears of society pushed backward through immense effort. Listen, reader, as I recount this marvelous story!

One afternoon, as Adrian and Idris rode through the woods with their mother and their usual companions, Idris pulled her brother away from the group and abruptly inquired, “What has become of your friend, Lionel Verney?”

“You can see his home from this very spot,” Adrian answered, gesturing toward my sister’s cottage.

How could I say to this generous person, “Support me in idleness. You who have dedicated your intellect and fortune to the benefit of humanity, should you so misdirect your efforts as to maintain a strong, healthy, and capable man in uselessness?”

And yet I dared not ask him to use his influence so that I might obtain an honorable living for myself—for then I would have been forced to leave Windsor. I hovered constantly around the walls of its castle and beneath its shaded thickets; my only companions were my books and my loving thoughts. I studied the wisdom of the ancients and gazed at the happy walls that sheltered the beloved of my soul. My mind was nonetheless idle. I pored over the poetry of old times; I studied the metaphysics of Plato and Berkeley. I read the histories of Greece and Rome, and of England’s past, while watching the movements of the lady of my heart. At night I could see her shadow on the walls of her apartment; by day I watched her in her flower garden or riding in the park with her usual companions. I thought the charm would be broken if I were seen, but I heard the music of her voice and was happy. I gave each heroine I read about her beauty and matchless excellence—such was Antigone when she guided the blind Oedipus to the grove of the Eumenides and performed the funeral rites for Polynices; such was Miranda in the unvisited cave of Prospero; such was Haidee on the sands of the Ionian island. I was mad with an excess of passionate devotion, but a pride as untamable as fire defined my nature and prevented me from betraying myself by word or look.

In the meantime, while I pampered myself with such rich mental feasts, a peasant would have disdained my meager fare, which I sometimes stole from the squirrels of the forest. On other occasions, we undertook journeys lasting several days, traversing the land to see locations celebrated for their scenery or historical significance. Idris possessed a character that was notably candid, gentle, and loving. Her disposition remained consistently pleasant; while she stood firm and determined regarding matters close to her heart, she was accommodating toward those she held dear. Perdita’s nature was perhaps less flawless, yet joy and affection refined her temperament and lessened her inherent reticence. She possessed a sharp and broad intellect alongside a lively imagination; she was honest, magnanimous, and sensible. Adrian, that incomparable kindred spirit, that delicate and noble man who loved everyone and was loved in return, appeared fated never to discover the partner meant to finalize his contentment. He frequently departed from our company to roam the forests alone or navigate his small boat, accompanied only by his volumes. He was often the most cheerful member of our group, yet he was also the sole person prone to bouts of melancholy; his fragile physique seemed burdened by the heaviness of existence, and his spirit appeared to reside within his frame rather than be truly joined to it. I was scarcely more committed to my Idris than to her brother, and she regarded him as her mentor, her companion, and the benefactor who had ensured the realization of her fondest desires. Raymond, the driven and unsettled Raymond, paused at the midpoint of life’s journey, satisfied to abandon his grand designs for power and renown to join our ranks as one of the wildflowers. His domain was Perdita’s heart, and his subjects were her thoughts; she loved him, revered him as a superior entity, and served him with obedience. No task, no act of devotion, and no period of vigilance felt burdensome to her when it concerned him. She would withdraw from our group to observe him, weeping with happiness at the thought that he belonged to her. She built a sanctuary for him within her soul, where every faculty acted as a priestess dedicated to his worship. At times she might be temperamental or fickle, yet her remorse was profound, her reconciliation complete, and even this volatility suited a man not naturally inclined to drift aimlessly through life.

Throughout the initial year of their union, Perdita bore Raymond a beautiful daughter. It was fascinating to observe in this tiny likeness the exact features of her father. She possessed the same slightly scornful mouth and triumphant expression, the same perceptive eyes, the same forehead and auburn hair; even her hands and slender fingers mirrored his. How precious she was to Perdita! As time went on, I likewise became a father, and our small children, our sources of amusement and joy, awakened a multitude of fresh and exquisite emotions.

Years unfolded in this manner—many years. CHAPTER VII.

After ensuring our friend was settled into his new position, we directed our attention toward Windsor. The proximity of this location to London served to lessen the feeling of a distressing parting when we left Raymond and Perdita. We said our goodbyes to them at the Protectoral Palace. It was quite charming to watch my sister embrace the spirit of the role, attempting to occupy her position with appropriate grace. Her inner pride and modest demeanor were now more conflicted than ever. Her shyness was genuine, stemming from that anxiety about not being valued correctly, that slight disregard for the world’s indifference, which also defined Raymond. Yet Perdita thought of others more consistently than he did; part of her reserve came from a desire to spare those around her from feeling inferior—a sentiment that never occurred to her. Given her background and upbringing, Idris would have been better suited for the rituals of ceremony, but the very naturalness that made such tasks easy for her also made them monotonous, whereas, despite every obstacle, Perdita clearly relished her role. She was too occupied with fresh ideas to feel much sorrow when we left; she bid us a warm farewell and vowed to visit soon, yet she did not lament the events that necessitated our departure. Raymond’s energy was limitless; he was unsure how to utilize his newly acquired authority; his mind teemed with schemes; he had settled on none as of yet—but he vowed to himself, his companions, and the public that the period of his Protectorship would be marked by some deed of extraordinary brilliance. Thus, we discussed them and reflected on life as we returned to Windsor Castle with a smaller party. We experienced immense relief at our departure from political chaos and pursued our seclusion with renewed enthusiasm. We were not lacking in tasks, but my restless nature was now directed solely toward intellectual pursuits, and I discovered that rigorous study served as a perfect remedy to soothe the agitation of spirit that, in idleness, would surely have plagued me.

She felt in his embrace a comforting assurance that as long as he had her, he would not lose hope. “This is kind,” she cried; “this is noble, my own beloved! Oh, do not fear disgrace or poverty while you have your Perdita; do not fear sorrow while our child lives and smiles. Let us go wherever you wish; the love that travels with us will prevent any regrets.”

Locked in his arms, she spoke these words and tilted her head back, searching his eyes for agreement—they were sparkling with indescribable delight. “Why, my little Lady Protector,” he said playfully, “what is this you are saying? And what elaborate plan have you woven of exile and obscurity, when a brighter, gold-threaded future is what you should truly be considering?”

He kissed her forehead, but the impulsive girl, feeling a bit sorry at his triumph and agitated by a sudden shift in her thoughts, hid her face in his chest and wept. He comforted her, instilling his own hopes and desires into her, and soon her face beamed with sympathy. How incredibly happy they were that night! How full, even to the point of overflowing, was their sense of joy!

Joy is, at its peak, the sibling of virtue. While sorrow and kindness can coexist—a pairing that authors have frequently enjoyed portraying for its poignant, human resonance—absolute happiness is a quality of celestial beings, and those who attain it seem angelic. It has been claimed that fear is the progenitor of faith, even the kind of faith that drives followers to offer human sacrifices at its shrines. It is the source of imagination and the guardian of poetry, granting a benevolent wisdom to the tangible workings of the universe and transforming the earth into a sanctuary beneath the vault of heaven. This blend of joy, goodness, and faith resided within Perdita’s soul.

Throughout the five years we spent as a circle of content individuals at Windsor Castle, my sister frequently spoke of her blissful circumstances. During the five years we spent together as a group of happy people at Windsor Castle, my sister frequently spoke of her blissful life. Because of our long-standing habits and natural affection, she chose me over Adrian or Idris to share in her overflowing delight. Perhaps, despite our apparent differences, some secret similarity born of our shared blood led to this preference. Often at sunset, I walked with her along the quiet, shaded forest paths and listened with joyful sympathy. Security gave her love dignity, and the certainty of being fully loved left her with no unfulfilled wishes. The birth of her daughter, a miniature version of her Raymond, completed her contentment and created a sacred, unbreakable bond between them. Sometimes she felt proud that he had chosen her over the prospect of a crown. Sometimes she remembered the sharp pain she felt when he had hesitated in his choice. Yet, this memory of past unhappiness only served to increase her present joy. What had been difficult to win was now entirely possessed and twice as dear. She would watch him from a distance with the same rapture—or rather, a far more exuberant rapture—that one might feel after surviving a storm to finally reach a desired port. She would hurry toward him to feel the reality of her happiness more certainly in his arms. This warmth of affection, combined with her deep understanding and brilliant imagination, made her incredibly precious to Raymond.

“I am a vessel,” she mused, “a vessel overflowing with the most bitter spirit of despair. Goodbye, Perdita! Goodbye, unfortunate girl! You shall never behold yourself in this state again; opulence and riches have abandoned you; in your profound destitution, you might even covet the lot of a wandering pauper; I am truly homeless! I dwell upon a desolate wasteland, vast and endless, yielding neither blossoms nor fruit; in its center stands a lonely crag to which you, Perdita, are tethered, watching the bleak horizon extend into the distance.”

She flung wide her casement, which faced the royal gardens. Illumination and shadow contended, and the eastern sky was marked with streaks of pink and gold. A single star flickered within the brightening expanse. The morning breeze, blowing coolly across the damp foliage, surged into the stifling chamber. “Everything continues,” Perdita reflected, “everything advances, fades, and expires! Once the midday has waned and the exhausted day has guided its steeds to their evening stables, the celestial fires emerge from the East, traveling their habitual route, climbing and descending the heavens. When their journey concludes, the sundial begins to cast a wavering shadow toward the west; the eyes of the day open, and the birds, blossoms, awakened greenery, and crisp winds stir; finally, the sun emerges, ascending the heights of the sky in grand procession. Everything progresses, transforms, and perishes, save for the awareness of agony within my shattering heart.”

“Yes, everything progresses and transforms: why should it surprise me, then, that love has traveled toward its sunset, and that the master of my existence has altered? We label the heavenly lights constant, yet they roam across that expanse, and if I gaze once more where I looked an hour prior, the aspect of the infinite firmament has shifted. The foolish moon and fickle planets alter their wandering dance each night; the sun itself, monarch of the heavens, periodically abandons his seat, surrendering his realm to darkness and winter. Nature ages, trembling in her failing members—creation has fallen into insolvency! Why wonder, then, that shadow and mortality have guided the light of your existence, O Perdita, toward ruin!”

CHAPTER IX.

Such were the melancholy and chaotic reflections of my unfortunate sister once she grew certain of Raymond’s unfaithfulness. Both her merits and her flaws ensured that the wound would remain unhealed. Her devotion to me, her brother, as well as to Adrian and Idris, was subordinate to the dominant passion of her soul; even her maternal affection derived much of its intensity from the joy she found in recognizing Raymond’s traits and expressions in her child’s face. She had been guarded and even harsh during her youth; however, love had mellowed the sharpness of her temperament, and her marriage to Raymond had allowed her gifts and emotions to blossom; with the former betrayed and the latter vanished, she reverted somewhat to her former nature. The intense pride of her character, dormant during her period of bliss, stirred, piercing her heart with its serpent’s sting; her inner humility intensified the poison’s effect; she had felt elevated in her own eyes while honored by his devotion: what value did she possess now that he had cast her from that position? She had taken pride in having captured and kept him—yet another had claimed him from her, and her triumph was as frigid as a quenched coal.

“I am a vessel,” she thought, “a vessel filled to the brim with the most bitter essence of despair. Goodbye, Perdita! Goodbye, poor girl! You will never see yourself like this again; luxury and wealth are no longer yours. In your extreme poverty, you might even envy the homeless beggar; I am truly without a home. I live on a barren desert, vast and endless, which produces neither fruit nor flower. In the center stands a solitary rock to which you, Perdita, are chained, watching the dreary landscape stretch out into the distance.”

She threw open her window, which overlooked the palace garden. Light and darkness were battling, and the eastern horizon was streaked with rose and gold. Only one star trembled in the depths of the brightening atmosphere. The morning air, blowing freshly over the dewy plants, rushed into the heated room. “Everything continues,” Perdita thought, “everything moves forward, decays, and perishes. When noon has passed and the weary day has driven its team to their western stalls, the fires of heaven rise from the East, moving along their accustomed path, ascending and descending the sky. When their course is complete, the sundial begins to cast an uncertain shadow westward; the eyelids of day open, and birds, flowers, the startled vegetation, and the fresh breeze awaken. Finally, the sun appears and climbs the heights of heaven in a majestic procession. Everything proceeds, changes, and dies, except for the sense of misery in my bursting heart.”

“Yes, everything proceeds and changes. Why should it be a surprise, then, that love has reached its sunset and that the lord of my life has changed? We call the heavenly lights fixed, yet they wander across that plain, and if I look again where I looked an hour ago, the face of the eternal heavens has altered. The foolish moon and inconstant planets vary their erratic dance nightly; the sun itself, sovereign of the sky, occasionally deserts his throne and leaves his dominion to night and winter. Nature grows old and shakes in her decaying limbs—creation has gone bankrupt! Why wonder, then, that eclipse and death have led the light of your life to destruction, O Perdita!”

The meeting hall stood empty, and the throngs of people who once served as instruments for his many schemes were now ignored. Revelry, and even debauchery, became the standard practice. Perdita watched the growing chaos with alarm. For an instant, she believed she might hold back the tide and persuade Raymond to listen to her logic. It was a futile wish. Her time of influence had vanished. He heard her with arrogance and answered with scorn; if she managed to stir his conscience, the only result was that he looked for a way to numb the pain through mindless excess. With her characteristic vigor, Perdita then tried to fill his role. Their marriage, still outwardly intact, allowed her to accomplish a great deal, yet no woman could ultimately fix the Protector’s mounting neglect. He acted as if gripped by a fit of madness, disregarding all decorum, structure, and responsibility, surrendering himself entirely to indulgence. News of these bizarre events came to us, and we were unsure how to proceed to bring our friend back to his senses and his nation, when Perdita unexpectedly arrived. She described the development of this tragic transformation and begged Adrian and me to travel to London to try and curb the worsening situation. Tell him, she exclaimed, tell Lord Raymond that I will no longer bother him with my presence. He does not need to throw himself into this ruinous debauchery to repel me or force me to leave. That goal is now met; he shall never see me again. But let me, as my final request, find in the acclaim of his fellow citizens and the success of England that the choice of my youth was correct. While riding to the city, Adrian and I debated Raymond’s behavior and his failure to live up to the promise of lasting greatness he had previously shown us. My companion and I had been raised with the same belief, or rather, I was his student in the conviction that unwavering commitment to principle was the sole path to glory, and that a constant focus on the common good was the only honorable goal for human ambition. Although we shared these views, we disagreed on how to apply them. Anger sharpened my criticism, and I condemned Raymond’s actions harshly. Adrian was more gentle and thoughtful. He acknowledged that my principles were ideal, but he rejected the idea that they were the only ones. Referencing the scripture that there are many mansions in my father’s house, he argued that the ways to achieve goodness or greatness are as varied as the temperaments of men, of whom it could be said, as of the leaves in the woods, that no two are identical. We rushed to the Protector’s palace. We discovered Raymond in his dining chamber with six companions; the wine was circulating freely, and it had already significantly clouded the judgment of one or two of them.

The entire Turkish army, followed by the Greeks, had moved eastward; only the dead remained in my area. From the top of the mound, I looked all around—everything was silent and deserted.

The last rays of the setting sun shone from behind the distant peak of Mount Athos. The Sea of Marmora still sparkled under the light, while the coast of Asia beyond was half-hidden in a hazy mist. Many helmets, bayonets, and swords, dropped from lifeless hands, reflected the fading light; they lay scattered everywhere. From the east, a flock of ravens, long-time residents of Turkish cemeteries, flew toward their feast as the sun disappeared. This hour, sad yet peaceful, has always seemed to me the time when we are most naturally drawn to connect with higher powers; our human harshness fades, and a gentle calm fills the soul. But now, surrounded by the dying and the dead, how could a murderer feel peace or think of heaven? During the busy day, my mind had been a slave to the situation created by others; historical pride, hatred of the enemy, and military excitement had controlled me. Now, I looked at the evening star as it hung softly and calmly in the orange sunset. I turned to the corpse-strewn earth and felt ashamed of my species. Perhaps the peaceful skies felt the same, for they quickly covered themselves in mist, speeding up the short twilight common in the south. Heavy clouds drifted up from the southeast, and red, murky lightning flashed from their dark edges. The rushing wind disturbed the clothing of the dead and turned cold as it passed over their icy bodies. Darkness gathered, and the objects around me became blurry. I climbed down from my position and struggled to guide my horse to avoid the slain.

Suddenly, I heard a piercing scream. A figure seemed to rise from the ground and flew quickly toward me, sinking to the earth again as it drew near. It all happened so fast that I struggled to pull back my horse so it would not trample the fallen person. The individual wore a soldier’s uniform, but the exposed neck and arms, along with the continuous screaming, revealed that a woman was disguised in this way.

In this manner shall I confront the contagion; and even if no soul joins me, I shall hoist the Greek flag upon the summit of St. Sophia.”

Logic proved useless against such intense emotions. I attempted in vain to demonstrate that the arrival of winter would clear the infected atmosphere and renew the Greeks’ resolve. “Speak not of any season but the present!” he exclaimed. “I have seen my final winter, and the year 2092 shall be inscribed upon my sepulcher. I already perceive,” he added, gazing upward with sorrow, “the boundary and the sheer precipice of my life, from which I shall fall into the dark enigma of the hereafter. I am ready, provided I leave a path of brilliance so vivid that even my harshest foes cannot obscure it. I owe this to Greece, to you, to my remaining Perdita, and to myself, the sacrifice of ambition.”

Our conversation was broken off by a servant, who reported that Raymond’s officers were gathered in the council hall. He asked me to ride through the encampment in the interim to assess and report on the soldiers’ morale; he then departed. I had been stirred to the limit by the day’s events, and now even more so by Raymond’s fervent words. How fragile is human reason! He charged the Greeks with being superstitious, yet what term would he apply to the belief he placed in Evadne’s prophecies? I traveled from the palace of Sweet Waters to the field where the camp was situated, discovering its occupants in a state of unrest. The arrival of several men bearing new accounts of wonders from the fleet, the embellishments added to existing rumors, and stories of ancient omens and terrifying reports of entire territories devastated by the plague this year, frightened and preoccupied the troops. Order vanished; the military force disintegrated. Each soldier, previously a component of a vast entity acting only in concert with others, now reverted to the solitary individual nature intended, concerned only with his own survival. They slipped away initially in ones and twos, then in larger groups, until, without interference from their commanders, entire battalions took the path toward Macedonia.

Around midnight, I went back to the palace and looked for Raymond; he was solitary and seemingly tranquil—a composure born of a firm commitment to a specific course of action. He listened to my report regarding the army’s collapse with serenity, then remarked, “You are aware, Verney, of my unwavering resolve not to abandon this location until Stamboul is openly ours in the light of day. If the men currently with me fear to accompany me, others of greater bravery can be located. Depart before dawn, deliver these messages to Karazza, and supplement them with your own pleas that he dispatch his marines and naval troops to me; if I can secure just one regiment to support me, the others would naturally follow. Let him provide this regiment. I shall await your arrival by noon tomorrow.”

I felt this was a weak strategy, yet I promised him my compliance and dedication. I left him to secure a brief period of sleep. As dawn arrived, I was prepared for my journey.

“Lionel, you must depart for England; go back to gentle Idris and beloved Adrian. Return, and treat my orphaned daughter as your own child within your home. A torrent of weeping concluded her mournful speech. I had anticipated some irrational suggestion, so I stayed quiet for a moment, gathering my resolve to better challenge her fanciful plan. “You are harboring gloomy reflections, my dear Perdita,” I remarked, “and I am not surprised that your sound judgment is temporarily clouded by intense sorrow and a troubled mind. Even I feel an attachment to this final residence of Raymond’s; yet, we are obligated to depart.”

“I foresaw this,” Perdita exclaimed; “I knew you would regard me as a foolish, irrational girl. Do not deceive yourself; I commanded that this cottage be constructed, and I will stay here until the moment comes when I may join him in his more joyful abode.”

“My dearest girl!”

“What is so peculiar about my intention? I could have misled you; I might have spoken of staying here for only a few months. In your eagerness to arrive at Windsor, you would have departed, and I could have followed my course without any argument or conflict. However, I scorned such deception; or rather, in my despair, it was my sole comfort to unburden my soul to you, my brother and only friend. You will not oppose me, will you? You understand how stubborn your miserable, grief-stricken sister can be. Take my daughter with you; distract her from scenes and memories of sadness; let youthful joy return to her heart and brighten her eyes. “You express your intent, Perdita,” I answered, “with forceful language, yet that intent is self-centered and beneath you. Will you commune any less with his departed spirit while you nurture and observe the rare virtues of his child? You have suffered a grievous trial, and I do not marvel that a sensation bordering on madness should push you toward harsh and illogical fantasies. Nevertheless, a home filled with affection awaits you in your native England. An unusual report reached us from the East, which might have been dismissed as unbelievable had it not been confirmed by numerous observers across various regions of the globe. It was claimed that on the twenty-first of June, an hour before midday, a black sun appeared. This orb, matching the size of the true sun but dark and distinct, with rays composed of shadow, rose from the west. Within an hour, it reached the zenith and obscured the radiant source of daylight. Darkness descended upon every land—a sudden, absolute, and lightless night. Stars emerged, casting their feeble glow upon the earth, now bereft of its sun. Before long, however, the gloomy sphere moved away from the sun and drifted toward the eastern sky. As it descended, its murky beams intersected with the brilliant rays of the sun, muting or warping them. The shadows cast by objects took on bizarre and terrifying forms. Wild creatures in the forests were startled by the unfamiliar shapes projected onto the ground. They fled in aimless terror, while the populace was gripped by even greater alarm at the upheaval that drove lions into populated streets. Powerful eagles, suddenly struck blind, plummeted into marketplaces, while owls and bats emerged to greet the premature night. Eventually, this object of terror dipped below the horizon, casting shadowy rays into the otherwise bright atmosphere until the very end. This was the account relayed from Asia, the eastern reaches of Europe, and Africa as far west as the Gold Coast. Regardless of the story's veracity, its consequences were undeniable. Throughout Asia, from the Nile's banks to the Caspian shores, and from the Hellespont to the Sea of Oman, a sudden terror took hold. Men crowded into mosques, while veiled women hurried to gravesites, bringing gifts for the departed in hopes of safeguarding the living. The plague was eclipsed by this new dread ignited by the black sun; even as the death toll rose and the streets of Ispahan, Pekin, and Delhi were littered with victims of the pestilence, people walked past, staring at the ominous heavens, indifferent to the corpses beneath them. Christians flocked to their churches; young Christian women, even during the festival of roses, dressed in white with shimmering veils, moved in lengthy processions toward their sacred sites, filling the air with hymns. Occasionally, a cry of lamentation would erupt from a grieving soul in the throng, causing others to look upward, imagining they could see the sweeping wings of angels passing over the world, mourning the calamities destined to befall humanity.

In the sun-drenched regions of Persia, the densely populated cities of China, the fragrant groves of Cashmere, and along the southern Mediterranean coast, such events transpired. Even in Greece, the legend of the dark sun intensified the anxiety and hopelessness of the perishing masses. We, situated on our misty island, were distant from the peril, and the only factor that made these catastrophes feel relevant to us was the constant arrival of ships from the east, packed with refugees, primarily English. The Muslims, though deeply affected by the fear of death, remained united; they reasoned that if they were destined to perish—and death would find them just as easily on the desolate ocean or in distant England as it would in Persia—they preferred that their remains rest in soil sanctified by the relics of the faithful.

I have delayed this long on the outer edge, the eroding sandbar extending into the river of life, playing with the specter of death. For this long, I have comforted my spirit by looking back on former joy, back when hope existed. Why not remain this way forever? I am not eternal, and the narrative of my life could be drawn out until my final days. Yet the identical feeling that initially drove me to depict scenes filled with fond memories now urges me to move forward. The same longing within this beating, fervent heart that compelled me to document my wandering youth, my peaceful maturity, and my soul’s deepest passions, now makes me shrink from further hesitation. I must finish my task.

So here I remain, as I mentioned, by the rapid currents of passing years, and now I must depart! Unfurl the canvas and pull the oars, rushing past looming, dark cliffs and down steep rapids, toward the sea of emptiness I have arrived at. Yet, one moment, a single brief pause before I leave the bank—once more, let me imagine myself as I was in 2094 at my home in Windsor, let me shut my eyes and pretend that the vast branches of its oaks still provide me shade, with its castle walls nearby. Let my imagination paint the happy scene of the twentieth of June, just as my pained heart remembers it even now.

Events had drawn me to London, where I heard reports that signs of the plague had appeared in the city’s hospitals. I traveled back to Windsor; my expression was somber, my spirit burdened. I walked into the Little Park, as I usually did, through the Frogmore gate, heading toward the Castle. Much of the land had been turned over to farming, with patches of potatoes and grain spread throughout. Rooks called out noisily in the branches overhead; mingling with their harsh sounds, I caught a cheerful melody. It was Alfred’s birthday. The youth, the Eton students, and the children of local families were holding a festive fair, to which all the local residents were welcomed. The park was dotted with tents, their bright colors and vibrant flags fluttering in the sun, contributing to the festive atmosphere. On a stage built below the terrace, several of the younger guests were dancing. I rested against a tree to watch them. The musicians performed the lively eastern melody by Weber from Abon Hassan; its spirited notes made the dancers move with ease, while the spectators tapped their feet in rhythm without realizing it.

The panic caused more harm than the disease and its natural consequences. Winter was welcomed as a universal and reliable healer. The browning woods, swollen rivers, evening mists, and morning frosts were greeted with gratitude. The effects of the purifying cold were felt immediately, and the weekly death tolls decreased. Many of our visitors departed; those whose homes were far to the south fled our northern winter with joy, seeking their native lands, confident in their abundance even after such a terrifying ordeal. We breathed easily again. We did not know what the coming summer would bring, but the present months were ours, and our hopes for an end to the plague were high.

CHAPTER VI.

I have lingered this long on the outer bank, the eroding sandbar stretching into the stream of life, toying with the shadow of death. For this long, I have cradled my heart in memories of past happiness, back when hope still existed. Why not stay this way forever? I am not immortal, and the thread of my history could be spun out to the very end of my life. But the same feeling that first led me to portray scenes filled with tender memories now urges me to hurry on. The same yearning of this warm, beating heart that compelled me to record my wandering youth, my serene adulthood, and the passions of my soul, now makes me recoil from further delay. I must finish my work.

Here I stand, as I said, beside the swift waters of the passing years, and now, away! Spread the sail and pull the oars, rushing past dark, looming cliffs and down steep rapids, straight to the sea of desolation I have reached. Yet, one moment—one brief interval before I push off from the shore—let me once again imagine myself as I was in 2094 at my home in Windsor. Let me close my eyes and pretend that the massive branches of its oaks still shade me, with its castle walls nearby. Let my imagination depict the joyous scene of the twentieth of June, just as my aching heart recalls it even now.

Circumstances had called me to London, where I heard rumors that symptoms of the plague had appeared in the city's hospitals. I returned to Windsor with a clouded brow and a heavy heart. I entered the Little Park, as was my custom, through the Frogmore gate on my way to the Castle. A large portion of these grounds had been turned over to farming, and patches of potato fields and corn were scattered about. Rooks cawed loudly in the trees above, and mixed with their hoarse cries, I heard a lively strain of music. It was Alfred’s birthday. The young people, the Eton students, and the children of the local gentry were holding a mock fair, to which all the local people were invited. The park was dotted with tents, their bright colors and gaudy flags waving in the sunshine, adding to the festive atmosphere. On a platform built beneath the terrace, a number of the younger guests were dancing. I leaned against a tree to watch them. The band played the wild eastern melody by Weber from Abon Hassan; its lively notes gave wings to the dancers' feet, while the spectators tapped along to the rhythm without even realizing it.

The panic caused more harm than the disease and its natural symptoms. Winter was welcomed as a universal and reliable healer. The browning woods, swollen rivers, evening mists, and morning frosts were greeted with gratitude. The effects of the purifying cold were felt immediately, and the weekly death tolls decreased. Many of our visitors left; those whose homes were far to the south fled happily from our northern winter, returning to their native lands, confident of finding plenty even after such a frightening ordeal. We breathed easily again. We did not know what the coming summer would bring, but the present months were ours, and our hopes that the plague would end were high.

I have lingered this long on the outer edge, the eroding bank that stretched into the stream of life, playing with the shadow of death. For this long, I have comforted my heart by looking back on past happiness, back when hope still existed. Why could it not stay that way forever? I am not immortal, and the thread of my history could be spun out to the very end of my life. However, the same feeling that first led me to describe scenes filled with tender memories now tells me to hurry on. The same longing of this warm, beating heart that made me record my wandering youth, my peaceful adulthood, and the passions of my soul now makes me pull back from further delay. I must finish my work.

Here then I stand, as I said, beside the swift waters of the passing years, and now I must go! Spread the sail and pull the oars, hurrying past dark, looming cliffs and down steep rapids, even to the sea of desolation I have reached. Yet, one moment, one brief interval before I leave the shore—once more, let me imagine myself as I was in 2094 at my home in Windsor. Let me close my eyes and imagine that the massive branches of its oaks still shade me, with its castle walls nearby. Let my imagination paint the joyous scene of the twentieth of June, just as my aching heart recalls it even now.

I entered the Little Park, as was my custom, at the Frogmore gate on my way to the Castle. A large part of these grounds had been turned into farmland, and strips of potato patches and corn were scattered here and there. Rooks cawed loudly in the trees above; mixed with their hoarse cries, I heard a lively strain of music. It was Alfred’s birthday. The young people, the Eton students, and the children of the local gentry were holding a mock fair, to which all the country people were invited. The park was dotted with tents, whose bright colors and gaudy flags waving in the sunshine added to the cheerfulness of the scene. On a platform built beneath the terrace, a number of the younger guests were dancing. I leaned against a tree to watch them. The band played the wild eastern melody by Weber from Abon Hassan; its lively notes gave wings to the feet of the dancers, while the onlookers tapped their feet to the rhythm without even realizing it.

The concept itself was magnanimous and grand, yet his modest demeanor and complete lack of self-righteousness made his deed infinitely more moving. I intended to refuse his plea, but having witnessed the benevolence he spread, I realized his intentions were immovable; thus, with a burdened spirit, I agreed to his request. He squeezed my hand with warmth, stating, “Thank you; you have freed me from a difficult predicament and remain, as always, my dearest friend. Goodbye—I must depart for a few hours. Go and speak with Ryland. Even though he is abandoning his duties in London, he could prove immensely useful in the north of England by welcoming and aiding travelers, and helping to provide the capital with sustenance. I implore you to stir him toward a sense of responsibility.”

Adrian departed, as I later discovered, to continue his routine of visiting infirmaries and examining the congested districts of London. I found Ryland significantly changed since his visit to Windsor. Constant dread had sallowed his skin and withered his frame. I informed him of the evening’s agenda, and a smile eased his tense features. He was eager to leave; every day he anticipated catching the contagion, and every day he struggled against the gentle pressure of Adrian’s insistence that he stay. Once Adrian was officially named his deputy, he intended to flee to safety. Influenced by this thought, he attended to my words, and, buoyed by the imminent prospect of his escape, he began discussing the strategies he would implement in his home county, momentarily abandoning his firm intention to isolate himself from all contact within his private estate.

That evening, Adrian and I traveled to Westminster. During the journey, he reminded me of my instructions, yet, remarkably, I arrived at the chamber without having contemplated my objective. Adrian waited in the coffee-room while I, following his wishes, took my place in St. Stephen’s. An unusual quiet hung over the hall. I had not been there since the time of Raymond’s protectorate, an era marked by high attendance, powerful oratory, and spirited debate. The benches were largely deserted; the seats traditionally held by hereditary peers were empty, though representatives from the city and commercial hubs were present, alongside a few landowners and those seeking a political career. The house first addressed a petition from the Lord Protector, requesting that they name a deputy to serve during his unavoidable absence.

A hush fell over the room until a member approached me, whispering that the Earl of Windsor had sent word that I was to propose his election, as the individual originally selected for the role was absent. Only then did I grasp the true weight of my responsibility, and I felt crushed by the situation I had accepted. Ryland had abandoned his position out of terror of the pestilence, and because of that same fear, Adrian faced no opposition.

The very idea was generous and noble, but beyond that, his modest manner and complete lack of self-righteousness made his actions ten times more moving. I wanted to refuse his request, but I had seen the good he spread; I felt that his resolve was unshakable, so with a heavy heart, I agreed to do as he asked. He grasped my hand affectionately. "Thank you," he said. "You have relieved me of a painful dilemma, and you are, as you have always been, my best friend. Farewell—I must leave you for a few hours now. Go and speak with Ryland. Although he is abandoning his post in London, he could be of great service in the north of England by receiving and assisting travelers and helping to supply the capital with food. I beg you to awaken him to some sense of duty."

Adrian left me, as I later learned, for his daily task of visiting the hospitals and inspecting the crowded districts of London. I found Ryland much changed, even from when he had visited Windsor. Constant fear had sallowed his complexion and made him look withered. I told him about the evening's business, and a smile relaxed his tense muscles. He wanted to go; every day he expected to catch the plague, and every day he was unable to resist the gentle pressure of Adrian’s insistence that he stay. The moment Adrian was legally elected his deputy, he would escape to safety. With this in mind, he listened to everything I said, and, buoyed by the near prospect of his departure, he began discussing the plans he would adopt in his own county, forgetting for the moment his cherished resolution to isolate himself from all contact within his estate.

The city members were there, along with representatives from commercial towns, a few landowners, and not many of those who entered parliament to build a career. I had previously toured the estates and country homes, frequently discovering residents driven by genuine kindness and prepared to offer significant support for the well-being of their tenants. Yet, this proved insufficient. The deep connection born from shared aspirations, anxieties, experiences, and goals was absent. The impoverished realized that the wealthy held distinct methods of survival unavailable to them, such as isolation and, to the extent possible, a life free from worry. They could not trust the wealthy, instead turning with increased reliance to the assistance and counsel of their own peers. Consequently, I determined to travel between villages, identifying the local leaders and, by organizing their efforts and broadening their perspectives, enhancing their influence and utility among their fellow villagers. Numerous shifts occurred in these informal leadership roles: removals and resignations were common, while passionate youths, hungry for activity and indifferent to peril, replaced the elderly and cautious. Frequently, a voice that everyone heeded was abruptly stilled, a supportive hand grew cold, a compassionate gaze shut, and the villagers grew more terrified of the death that had claimed a prominent figure, turning to dust the heart that had cared for them and reducing to absolute nothingness the mind constantly engaged in plans for their benefit.

Anyone working for humanity must frequently encounter ingratitude, nourished by corruption and stupidity, growing from the seeds they have planted. Death, which in our youth had moved across the world like a nocturnal thief, now emerged from its underground tomb, empowered and draped in dark banners, as a conqueror. Many perceived a supreme Providence seated above his deputy throne, directing his arrows and guiding his path, and they lowered their heads in submission or at least compliance. Others saw only a random event; they attempted to trade fear for indifference, diving into debauchery to escape the painful pangs of extreme dread. Thus, while the virtuous, wise, and careful were engaged in charitable work, the winter lull created different results among the young, the reckless, and the immoral. Throughout the colder months, there was a widespread migration to London seeking entertainment; social constraints weakened; many who were previously poor had become wealthy; many had lost the parents who served as their moral guardians and guides. It would have been futile to resist these urges with obstacles, which would only have pushed those driven by them toward more harmful excesses. The theaters remained open and crowded; dances and late-night celebrations were popular—in many of these, propriety was abandoned, and the vices previously associated with a sophisticated society were magnified. The scholar abandoned his texts, the artist his studio: life’s serious pursuits had vanished, but the diversions persisted; pleasure could be extended to the very edge of the grave. All artificial pretenses vanished—death ascended like the night, and, shielded by its dark shadows, the blush of modesty, the reserve of pride, and the decorum of prudery were often discarded as pointless masks. This was not the case everywhere. Among nobler souls, suffering and terror, the dread of permanent separation, and the profound astonishment caused by this unprecedented disaster, tightened the bonds of family and friendship.

I had previously visited the manor houses and estates of the wealthy, often finding the residents driven by the purest benevolence, ready to offer their full support for the well-being of their tenants. But this was insufficient. The deep connection born of shared hopes, fears, experiences, and goals was missing. The poor realized that the rich had different means of survival than they did—seclusion and, as much as circumstances allowed, freedom from worry. They could not rely on them, instead turning with tenfold dependence to the help and advice of their own peers. I decided, therefore, to travel from village to village, seeking out the local leaders, and by organizing their efforts and broadening their perspectives, increase both their influence and their usefulness to their fellow villagers. Many changes also occurred in these spontaneous local elections: removals and resignations were frequent, while, in place of the old and prudent, passionate youths would step forward, eager for action and indifferent to danger. Often, too, the voice everyone listened to was suddenly silenced, the helping hand turned cold, the sympathetic eye closed, and the villagers feared even more the death that had claimed a chosen victim, turning to dust the heart that had beaten for them, and reducing to total silence the mind forever occupied with plans for their welfare.

Whoever works for humanity must often find ingratitude, fueled by vice and folly, growing from the seeds they have sown. Death, which in our younger days had walked the earth like a thief in the night, now rose from his subterranean vault, armed with power and dark banners, arriving as a conqueror. Many saw, seated above his throne, a supreme Providence directing his arrows and guiding his progress, and they bowed their heads in resignation or at least in obedience. Others saw only a random accident; they tried to trade terror for recklessness, plunging into debauchery to avoid the agonizing pangs of deep anxiety. Thus, while the wise, the good, and the prudent were occupied with acts of kindness, the winter lull produced different effects among the young, the thoughtless, and the vicious. During the colder months, there was a general rush to London in search of amusement—the bonds of public opinion loosened; many who were previously poor were now rich—many had lost the parents who served as the guardians of their morals, their mentors, and their restraints. It would have been useless to oppose these impulses with barriers, which would only have driven those motivated by them to more harmful indulgences. The theaters were open and crowded; dances and midnight festivals were popular—in many of these, decorum was violated, and the evils that had previously clung to an advanced state of civilization were doubled. The student left his books, the artist his studio: the occupations of life were gone, but the amusements remained; enjoyment could be prolonged to the very edge of the grave. All artificial pretenses disappeared—death rose like night, and, protected by its murky shadows, the blush of modesty, the reserve of pride, and the decorum of prudery were frequently cast aside as useless veils. This was not universal. Among better natures, anguish and dread, the fear of eternal separation, and the profound wonder produced by unprecedented calamity drew the ties of family and friendship closer.
