Anyone who works for humanity often finds that ingratitude, fueled by vice and foolishness, grows from the seeds they have planted. Death, which in our youth had moved like a thief in the night, now rose from his grave with great power, like a conqueror with a dark banner. Many saw a supreme Providence directing his arrows and guiding his path, and they bowed their heads in resignation or obedience. Others saw only random chance; they tried to trade their terror for recklessness, plunging into wild behavior to escape their worst fears. Thus, while the wise and good were busy with acts of kindness, the winter months had different effects on the young and thoughtless. During the cold season, there was a general rush to London for entertainment. Public standards weakened; many who were once poor were now rich, and many had lost the parents who had guided and restrained them. It would have been useless to try to stop these impulses, as that would only have driven people toward more harmful indulgences. The theaters were open and crowded, and dances and midnight festivals were popular. In many of these, proper behavior was ignored, and the problems usually associated with advanced civilization doubled. Students left their books and artists left their studios; the serious work of life was gone, but the amusements remained, allowing people to pursue pleasure right up to the edge of the grave. All artificial pretenses vanished. Death rose like the night, and under its dark shadows, modesty, pride, and social decorum were often cast aside as useless veils. This was not true for everyone, however. Among better people, the anguish, the fear of permanent separation, and the awe caused by this unprecedented disaster only strengthened the bonds of family and friendship.

I proceeded to Idris, formulating believable justifications for spending the entire day at the Castle, while attempting to smooth the signs of worry from my face. Happily, she was not by herself. I discovered Merrival, the astronomer, in her company. His perspective on humanity was far too distant to notice the daily tragedies, and he existed amidst the plague oblivious to its presence. This unfortunate man, as knowledgeable as La Place yet as innocent and shortsighted as a youth, had frequently neared starvation—he, his wan wife, and their many children—all while he neither experienced hunger nor noticed their suffering. His celestial theories consumed him; he scribbled equations in coal upon the exposed walls of his attic. He would trade a hard-won coin or a piece of clothing for a book without hesitation. He neither heard his offspring weeping nor noticed his partner’s gaunt appearance, and the height of disaster was to him merely like a night of cloud cover, when he would have sacrificed much to witness a heavenly event. His wife was one of those remarkable individuals, found only among women, whose devotion could not be lessened by hardship. Her spirit was split between immense reverence for her spouse and gentle concern for their children; she attended to him, labored for them, and never uttered a grievance, even though sorrow turned her existence into a prolonged, somber reverie.

He had first approached Adrian by asking to view certain planetary movements through his telescope. His destitution was readily apparent and subsequently eased. He frequently expressed gratitude for the volumes we provided and for access to our equipment, yet he never mentioned his improved living quarters or his changed situation. His wife confirmed that he had noticed no difference, save for the absence of the children from his workspace, and to her immense astonishment, he actually lamented this unusual silence.

He arrived then to inform us that he had finished his treatise on the pericyclical movements of the Earth’s axis and the precession of the equinoctial points. If a Roman from the Republican era had been resurrected to discuss the upcoming selection of a laurel-crowned consul or the final conflict with Mithridates, his notions would not have been more detached from the present than Merrival’s discourse. Humanity, no longer possessing a desire for connection, expressed its thoughts through visible symbols, yet no readers remained. While everyone, having discarded their weapons, stood alone against their shield awaiting the pestilence, Merrival spoke of the condition of mankind six millennia into the future. He might have just as well provided us with a commentary describing the unknown and inconceivable features of the beings who would then inhabit the abandoned home of humanity. We lacked the cruelty to disillusion the elderly man, and when I entered, he was reciting sections of his manuscript to Idris, inquiring what rebuttal could be offered to this or that argument.

Idris could not suppress a grin as she heard him. She had already learned from him that his family remained living and healthy. Although she was not prone to forgetting the precarious edge of time upon which she stood, I could see that she was briefly entertained by the disparity between the narrow focus we had long maintained regarding human existence and the vast, sweeping strides with which Merrival traversed a coming eternity.

I then went to Idris, inventing plausible excuses along the way for staying at the Castle all day, and trying to smooth the signs of worry from my forehead. Fortunately, she was not alone. I found Merrival, the astronomer, with her. He was far too focused on the long-term view of humanity to pay attention to the daily tragedies, and he lived in the middle of the contagion without even realizing it existed. This poor man, as learned as La Place but as innocent and shortsighted as a child, had often been on the verge of starvation—he, his pale wife, and their many children—while he neither felt hunger nor noticed their suffering. His astronomical theories consumed him; calculations were scribbled in coal on the bare walls of his attic. A hard-earned coin or a piece of clothing would be traded for a book without a second thought. He neither heard his children crying nor noticed his wife’s emaciated frame, and the height of the disaster was to him merely like a cloudy night when he would have given his right hand to observe a celestial event. His wife was one of those remarkable beings, found only among women, whose capacity for love could not be diminished by misfortune. Her mind was split between boundless admiration for her husband and tender anxiety for her children; she waited on him, worked for them, and never complained, even though her worries made her life one long, melancholy dream.

He had introduced himself to Adrian by asking to observe some planetary motions through his telescope. His poverty was easily spotted and relieved. He often thanked us for the books we lent him and for the use of our instruments, but he never mentioned his new home or his change in circumstances. His wife assured us that he had not noticed any difference, except for the absence of the children from his study, and to her infinite surprise, he actually complained about this unusual quiet.

He had come now to announce the completion of his Essay on the Pericyclical Motions of the Earth’s Axis and the precession of the equinoctial points. If an ancient Roman from the Republic had returned to life and talked about the upcoming election of a consul or the last battle with Mithridates, his ideas would not have been more out of touch with the times than Merrival’s conversation. People, no longer having an appetite for connection, kept their thoughts to themselves; there were no readers left. While everyone else, having cast aside their weapons, waited alone for the plague, Merrival talked about the state of mankind six thousand years in the future. He might have just as well provided a commentary describing the unknown and unimaginable features of the creatures who would then occupy the abandoned home of humanity. We did not have the heart to disillusion the poor old man, and at the moment I walked in, he was reading parts of his book to Idris, asking what answer could be given to this or that argument.

Idris could not help but smile as she listened. She had already gathered from him that his family was alive and healthy. Although she was not prone to forgetting the precarious edge of time on which she stood, I could see that she was amused for a moment by the contrast between the narrow view of human life we had taken for so long and the giant leaps with which Merrival paced through a coming eternity.

I then went to Idris, inventing plausible excuses to stay at the Castle all day while trying to hide the worry on my face. Fortunately, she was not alone. I found Merrival, the astronomer, with her. He was so focused on the big picture of humanity that he ignored the daily tragedies, living through the plague as if it did not exist. This poor man, as learned as La Place but as innocent and shortsighted as a child, had often nearly starved along with his pale wife and many children, yet he never felt hunger or noticed their suffering. His astronomical theories consumed him; he scrawled calculations in coal on his attic walls and traded his last coin or his clothes for books without a second thought. He did not hear his children crying or notice his wife’s thin, sickly frame. To him, the worst disasters were no more significant than a cloudy night that prevented him from observing the stars. His wife was one of those remarkable women whose love is never diminished by misfortune. Her mind was split between deep admiration for her husband and tender concern for her children; she served him and worked for them without complaint, even though her life had become a long, sad dream of hardship.

He had first introduced himself to Adrian by asking to use his telescope to observe planetary movements. We easily noticed his poverty and helped him. He often thanked us for the books and instruments we lent him, but he never mentioned his improved living conditions. His wife told us that he had not even noticed the change, except for the fact that the children were no longer in his study, and to her great surprise, he actually complained about the unusual quiet.

He had come now to announce that he had finished his essay on the earth's axis and the equinoxes. If an ancient Roman had returned to life to discuss a consul election or a battle from centuries ago, his ideas would not have been more out of place than Merrival’s conversation. People no longer cared for intellectual pursuits; they were simply waiting for the plague to take them. Yet, while everyone else faced their end, Merrival talked about the state of humanity six thousand years in the future. He might as well have been describing the appearance of the creatures who would eventually replace mankind. We did not have the heart to tell the poor old man the truth. When I arrived, he was reading parts of his book to Idris, asking for her opinion on his arguments.

Idris could not help but smile as she listened. She had already learned from him that his family was alive and healthy. Although she never forgot the dangerous situation we were in, I could see that she was briefly amused by the contrast between our narrow focus on survival and the way Merrival looked toward a distant eternity.

At this time, Adrian and Idris reunited following a year-long absence. Adrian had been engaged in a difficult and taxing endeavor. Having encountered every variety of human suffering, he consistently discovered that his abilities were insufficient and his assistance largely ineffective. Nevertheless, his inner resolve, vigor, and passionate determination kept him from succumbing to despair. He appeared transformed, as if virtue—more effective than any mythical potion—had granted him renewed vitality. Idris barely identified the delicate individual, once seemingly swayed by a light summer wind, in this vigorous man whose heightened sensitivity actually equipped him better to serve as a guide for a nation in turmoil. 

Idris’s condition was quite different. She remained silent, yet a profound sense of dread had taken root within her. She had become gaunt and pallid, her eyes frequently welled with tears, and her speech was faint and fractured. She attempted to mask the transformation she knew her brother would notice, but her efforts failed; when they were alone, she released her pent-up anguish and shared her anxieties. She vividly detailed the constant worry that relentlessly consumed her spirit, likening this persistent anticipation of disaster to the vulture that preyed upon Prometheus. Driven by this perpetual agitation and the exhausting struggle to suppress it, she explained that her physical being felt as though it were operating at an unsustainable pace, rapidly wearing itself out. Her rest offered no relief, for her waking thoughts—partially restrained by logic and the sight of her healthy children—morphed into nightmarish visions where her worst fears came to pass. She saw no prospect for improvement, hoping only that death might claim her before she suffered the agony of losing those she cherished. She tried to shield me from her misery, but upon seeing her brother after so long, she could not contain her sorrow, pouring out her heart to the sympathetic Adrian with the intense imagery born of suffering. 

Her stay in London only deepened her distress by revealing the full scope of the plague’s destruction. The city barely resembled a populated place; thick grass grew in the thoroughfares, squares were overgrown with weeds, buildings were boarded up, and a profound silence hung over the formerly bustling districts. Even amidst this ruin, Adrian maintained order, ensuring that life continued according to established laws and traditions—human structures persisting even as natural ones failed, and while the population dwindled, property rights remained inviolate. It was a somber realization; despite the reduction in chaos, it felt like a pathetic farce. All thoughts of entertainment, such as plays or celebrations, had vanished.

On this occasion, Adrian and Idris reunited after being apart for nearly a year. Adrian had been busy completing a difficult and painful task. He had encountered every kind of human suffering, always finding his abilities insufficient and his help of little use. Yet, his inner purpose, energy, and passionate resolve prevented him from sinking into sorrow. He seemed reborn, and his virtue, more powerful than any ancient magic, granted him health and strength. Idris barely recognized the fragile person who once seemed ready to break in a summer breeze; he was now an energetic man whose very depth of feeling made him better suited to lead storm-tossed England.

It was not the same for Idris. She did not complain, but a deep sense of fear had taken root in her heart. She had grown thin and pale, her eyes filled with involuntary tears, and her voice was weak and trembling. She tried to hide the changes she knew her brother would notice, but the effort failed. When alone with him, she gave way to a burst of uncontrollable grief, venting her anxieties and sorrow. She vividly described the constant worry that consumed her soul with endless hunger, comparing this sleepless anticipation of disaster to the vulture that fed on the heart of Prometheus. Under the pressure of this constant tension and the exhausting struggle to hide it, she felt as if her body’s internal systems were running at double speed, rapidly burning themselves out. Sleep offered no rest, for her waking thoughts—restrained by reason and the sight of her healthy children—transformed into wild nightmares where all her terrors and fears were realized. She saw no hope or relief for this state, wishing only for the grave to claim her before she had to endure a thousand living deaths by losing those she loved. Fearing she would cause me pain, she tried to hide the extent of her misery, but meeting her brother after such a long absence, she could not hold back her woe. With the vivid imagination that always accompanies suffering, she poured out her heart to her beloved and sympathetic Adrian.

Her visit to London only increased her anxiety by revealing the full extent of the plague’s destruction. The city barely looked inhabited; thick grass grew in the streets, the squares were overgrown with weeds, houses were boarded up, and silence and loneliness defined the busiest parts of town. Yet, amidst this desolation, Adrian had maintained order. Everyone continued to live according to law and custom—human institutions surviving even as natural ones failed, and while the population dwindled, property remained sacred. It was a melancholy realization; despite the reduction of chaos it provided, it struck the heart as a miserable mockery. All thoughts of entertainment, theaters, or festivals had vanished.

Adrian and Idris met on this occasion after being separated for nearly a year. Adrian had been busy with a difficult and exhausting task. He had encountered every kind of human suffering, and he had always found his own abilities insufficient and his help of little use. Yet, his inner purpose, his energy, and his passionate determination prevented him from falling into despair. He seemed like a new person, and his virtue, more powerful than any magical potion, gave him health and strength. Idris barely recognized the fragile person who once seemed to bend in the summer breeze in this energetic man, whose very depth of feeling made him better able to act as a leader for storm-tossed England.

It was not the same for Idris. She did not complain, but a deep sense of fear had taken root in her heart. She had grown thin and pale, her eyes were filled with involuntary tears, and her voice was weak and low. She tried to hide the changes she knew her brother would notice, but her efforts failed. When she was alone with him, she gave way to her fears and sorrow with a burst of uncontrollable grief. She described vividly the constant worry that ate into her soul with a hunger that never ended; she compared this gnawing, sleepless expectation of disaster to the vulture that fed on the heart of Prometheus. Under the influence of this constant stress and the endless struggle to fight and hide it, she said she felt as if the internal mechanisms of her body were working at double speed and were quickly burning themselves out. Sleep brought no rest, for her waking thoughts—which were kept in check by reason and the sight of her healthy, happy children—turned into wild nightmares where all her terrors came true. There was no hope or relief for this state, she felt, unless the grave should soon claim her, allowing her to die before she suffered a thousand living deaths by losing those she loved. Fearing she would cause me pain, she hid the extent of her misery as best she could, but meeting her brother after such a long absence, she could not hold back her grief. With all the vivid imagination that accompanies misery, she poured out her heart to her beloved and sympathetic Adrian.

Her visit to London only increased her anxiety by showing her the full extent of the damage caused by the plague. The city barely looked inhabited anymore; thick grass grew in the streets, the squares were overgrown with weeds, the houses were boarded up, and silence and loneliness defined the busiest parts of town. Yet, in the middle of this desolation, Adrian had maintained order, and everyone continued to live according to law and custom. Human institutions survived even as natural ones failed, and while the population was being wiped out, property remained sacred. It was a sad thought, and despite the reduction in chaos it provided, it felt like a miserable mockery. All thoughts of pleasure, theaters, and festivals had vanished.

The bustling eastern district of the city remained quiet, or revealed only spots where warehouses had been rummaged through, driven by greed or inquisitiveness: bundles of expensive Indian merchandise, costly shawls, gems, and spices lay scattered across the ground. In certain spots, the owner had guarded their inventory until the end, perishing in front of locked entrances. The heavy church doors groaned on their hinges, with a few corpses resting upon the stone floors. A miserable woman, the unloved casualty of common violence, had drifted into the dressing room of an aristocrat, draped herself in fine clothing, and expired before the glass that showed her transformed reflection. Ladies whose soft feet had rarely trod the ground in their pampered lives had escaped their residences in terror, only to become lost in the city's grimy lanes and perish at the doors of the destitute. My heart grew weary at the diverse miseries on display; witnessing such grim transformations, my spirit was pained by the dread of what might happen to my dear Idris and our children. Would they, outliving Adrian and me, be left without guardians in this world? Thus far, only the mind had been tormented—could I indefinitely delay the moment when the fragile body and sensitive nerves of my beloved, raised in luxury and rank, would be assaulted by starvation, suffering, and sickness? It would be better to perish immediately—better to drive a dagger into her chest, still unmarred by harsh misfortune, and then bury it in my own! Yet, no; during eras of suffering, we must battle our fates and endeavor not to be conquered by them. I refused to surrender, determined to shield my loved ones from grief and agony until my final breath; if I were eventually defeated, it would not be without honor. I remained at the barricade, opposing the adversary—the intangible, unseen enemy that had besieged us for so long—and as of yet, he had created no opening: it had to be my duty to ensure he did not, by burrowing in secret, erupt within the very sanctuary of love where I offered my daily devotions. The appetite of Death seemed sharpened by the dwindling supply of his victims: or perhaps it was simply that before, when survivors were numerous, the dead were tallied with less urgency? Now, every life was a treasure, every breathing human being worth far more than the finest carved statues; and the daily, indeed hourly, decline in our population struck the heart with nauseating despair. This summer saw our hopes extinguished; the ship of civilization was destroyed, and the broken raft, bearing the remaining few across a sea of sorrow, was split and battered by storms. Humanity existed only in pairs or trios; the individual human, who might slumber, wake, and carry out basic biological needs, remained; but humanity, inherently fragile yet mightier in collective groups than the wind or the sea; humanity, the conqueror of nature, the master of creation, the equal of deities, existed no more. Farewell to the scenes of patriotism, to the devotion to freedom and the deserved rewards of noble ambition!—farewell to the packed parliament, resonant with the wisdom of counselors, whose decrees were sharper than a blade forged in Damascus!

The busy eastern district of the city was silent, or at most, one could see where warehouses had been ransacked rather than looted, driven by a mix of greed and curiosity; bales of expensive Indian goods, valuable shawls, jewels, and spices lay scattered across the floors. In some spots, the owners had guarded their stock until the end, dying before their locked gates. The heavy church doors creaked on their hinges, and a few bodies lay on the pavement. A wretched woman, a victim of common brutality, had wandered into the dressing room of a high-born lady, adorned herself in fine clothing, and died before the mirror that reflected her transformed appearance to no one but herself. Women whose delicate feet had rarely touched the ground in their life of luxury had fled their homes in terror, only to lose their way in the squalid city streets and die on the doorsteps of the poor. My heart sickened at the variety of misery on display, and as I witnessed these grim changes, my soul ached with fear for what might happen to my beloved Idris and our children. Would they, surviving Adrian and me, be left without protection in this world? So far, only the mind had suffered—could I delay the moment when the delicate frame and sensitive nerves of my wife, raised in prosperity and wealth, would be ravaged by famine, hardship, and disease? It would be better to die at once—better to plunge a dagger into her chest while she remained untouched by grim adversity, and then drive it into my own! But no; in times of misery, we must fight our destinies and strive not to be defeated by them. I would not give in, but would resolutely defend my loved ones against sorrow and pain until my final breath; and if I were eventually defeated, it would not be without a fight. I stood in the breach, resisting the enemy—the intangible, invisible foe that had besieged us for so long—and as yet, he had made no progress. It had to be my mission to ensure he did not secretly undermine us and burst into the very sanctuary of the love I served daily. Death’s hunger seemed sharper now as his food supply dwindled; or perhaps it was simply that when there were more survivors, the dead were counted less eagerly. Now, every life was a gem, every breathing human being worth far more than the finest sculpture; the daily, even hourly, decline in our numbers struck the heart with sickening misery. This summer extinguished our hopes; the vessel of society was wrecked, and the shattered raft carrying the few survivors across the sea of misery was torn apart by the storm. Humanity existed only in twos and threes; the individual might still sleep, wake, and function, but man—once weak alone yet more powerful in groups than the wind or ocean, the conqueror of the elements, the lord of nature, the equal of gods—existed no longer. Farewell to the patriotic scene, to the love of liberty, and to the well-earned rewards of virtuous ambition! Farewell to the crowded senate, echoing with the advice of the wise, whose laws were sharper than a Damascus blade!

The busy eastern part of the city was silent. You could only see where warehouses had been ransacked, driven by greed or curiosity; bales of expensive goods from India, valuable shawls, jewels, and spices lay scattered across the floors. In some spots, the owners had guarded their property until the end, dying before their locked gates. The heavy church doors creaked on their hinges, and a few bodies lay on the pavement. A wretched woman, a victim of common brutality, had wandered into the dressing room of a wealthy lady; she had put on fine clothes and died in front of the mirror, which reflected her changed appearance to no one but herself. Women whose delicate feet had rarely touched the ground in their luxury had fled their homes in terror, only to lose their way in the city's squalid streets and die on the doorsteps of the poor. My heart sickened at the variety of misery I saw, and as I witnessed these grim changes, my soul ached with fear for my beloved Idris and our children. Would they, if they outlived Adrian and me, be left without protection in the world? So far, only the mind had suffered—could I delay the time when the delicate body and sensitive nerves of my wife, raised in rank and wealth, would be attacked by famine, hardship, and disease? It would be better to die at once—better to plunge a dagger into her heart while she was still untouched by misery, and then into my own! But no; in times of suffering, we must fight against our fate and try not to be defeated by it. I would not give up; I would defend my loved ones against sorrow and pain until my final breath. If I were defeated in the end, it would not be without a fight. I stood in the breach, resisting the enemy—the invisible, untouchable foe that had besieged us for so long. He had not yet broken through, and it was my duty to ensure he did not secretly undermine us and burst into the sanctuary of love where I worshipped daily. Death’s hunger seemed sharper now that his victims were fewer; or perhaps it was just that when more people survived, the dead were not counted as closely. Now, every life was a gem, and every breathing human was worth far more than the finest sculpture. The daily, even hourly, drop in our numbers filled my heart with sickening misery. This summer destroyed our hopes; the ship of society had wrecked, and the broken raft carrying the few survivors across the sea of misery was torn apart by the storm. People existed only in twos and threes. Man, the individual who could sleep, wake, and perform basic functions, remained; but man, who is weak alone yet more powerful than the wind or ocean when gathered in numbers—man, the conqueror of the elements, the lord of nature, and the equal of gods—existed no longer.

Farewell to the patriotic scene, to the love of liberty, and to the well-earned rewards of virtuous ambition! Farewell to the crowded senate, filled with the voices of wise leaders whose laws were sharper than a Damascus sword!

For an instant, I was incapacitated, my head drooping from a wave of intense sickness; then, regaining my senses, I leaped up, pushed the creature away, and rushed up the stairs into the room my family occupied. A faint glow revealed Alfred resting on a sofa; Clara, shaking and whiter than fresh snow, had lifted him onto her arm, offering him a drink of water. It was clear to me that no vitality remained in that broken body; his face was stiff, his eyes were vacant, and his head had slumped backward. I lifted him from her, placed him gently down, pressed a kiss to his cold lips, and attempted to speak in a futile whisper, though the loudest roar of artillery would have been unable to reach him in his spiritual dwelling.

And what of Idris? The news that she had departed to find me and had not come back was terrifying, while the wind and rain hammered against the glass and howled about the residence. Furthermore, the nauseating symptoms of the plague were intensifying within me; I had no time to waste if I hoped to find her again. I climbed onto my horse and rode out to search, imagining her voice in every breeze, burdened by fever and sharp agony.

I traveled through the dark, rainy, and winding streets of deserted London. My son was dead at home; the roots of a fatal illness had taken hold of me; I went to find my beloved Idris, now wandering by herself, while the rain poured from the sky like a waterfall to drench her head in freezing moisture and her delicate limbs in numbing cold. A woman stood on a doorstep and shouted to me as I rode by. It was not Idris, so I hurried on, until a sudden realization—a mental replay of what I had glimpsed but not registered—convinced me that another figure, slender, elegant, and tall, was standing there, clutching the person who held her up. Within moments, I was at the side of the petitioner, and in moments, I caught the collapsing Idris in my embrace. Lifting her, I set her upon the horse; she lacked the power to hold herself up, so I climbed on behind her and pulled her tightly against my chest, enveloping her in my cloak, while her companion, whose familiar but altered face—it was Juliet, the Duke of L—'s daughter—could earn from me at this moment of terror nothing more than a brief look of pity. She grabbed the loose reins and guided our compliant horse toward home. Can I admit it? That was the final instant of my joy; yet I was joyful. Idris was destined to perish, for her spirit was shattered: I was destined to perish, for I had contracted the pestilence; the world was a wasteland; optimism was insanity; existence had wedded mortality; they were unified; but, as I held my fading love, as I felt that my own end was near, I delighted in the bliss of holding her once more; repeatedly I kissed her and held her to my heart.

We reached our residence. I helped her off the horse, carried her upstairs, and entrusted her to Clara so that her soaked clothing could be replaced.

For a moment I was overwhelmed, my head bowed by aching nausea; then, as my senses returned, I jumped up, pushed the wretch away, and dashed up the stairs, entering the room where my family usually stayed. A dim light revealed Alfred on a couch; Clara, trembling and paler than the whitest snow, had lifted him onto her arm, holding a cup of water to his lips. I saw clearly that no spark of life remained in that ruined form; his features were rigid, his eyes glazed, and his head had fallen back. I took him from her, laid him down gently, kissed his cold little mouth, and turned to speak in a whisper that would have been useless, as even the loudest thunder could not have reached him in his spiritual home.

And where was Idris? The news that she had gone out to find me and had not returned was terrifying, while the rain and driving wind rattled against the window and roared around the house. Added to this, the sickening sensation of the disease took hold of me; there was no time to lose if I ever wanted to see her again. I mounted my horse and rode out to search for her, imagining I heard her voice in every gust, weighed down by fever and aching pain.

I rode through the dark and rain in the labyrinthine streets of unpeopled London. My child lay dead at home; the seeds of a fatal disease had taken root in my chest; I went to find Idris, my beloved, now wandering alone while the rain poured from the sky like a waterfall to soak her dear head in chill damp and her fair limbs in numbing cold. A woman stood on a doorstep and called out to me as I galloped past. It was not Idris, so I rode on quickly until a kind of second sight—a realization of what I had seen but not registered—made me certain that another figure, thin, graceful, and tall, stood clinging to the person supporting her. In a minute I was beside the woman, and in a minute I received the sinking Idris into my arms. Lifting her up, I placed her on the horse; she lacked the strength to support herself, so I mounted behind her and held her close to my chest, wrapping my riding cloak around her, while her companion, whose well-known but changed face (it was Juliet, daughter of the Duke of L—) could at this moment of horror receive from me nothing more than a passing glance of pity. She took the loose rein and led our obedient horse toward home. Dare I admit it? That was the last moment of my happiness, but I was happy. Idris had to die, for her heart was broken; I had to die, for I had caught the plague; the earth was a scene of desolation; hope was madness; life had married death; they were one. Yet, while supporting my fainting love and knowing I would soon die, I reveled in the joy of holding her once more; again and again I kissed her and pressed her to my heart.

We arrived at our home. I helped her dismount, carried her upstairs, and left her in Clara’s care so her wet clothes could be changed.

For a moment I was overwhelmed, my head bowed by a sickening nausea. Then, as my senses returned, I jumped up, pushed the wretch away, and ran up the stairs into the room where my family usually stayed. A dim light revealed Alfred on a couch. Clara, trembling and paler than snow, was holding him up and offering him a cup of water. I saw clearly that no life remained in that broken body; his features were stiff, his eyes were glazed, and his head had fallen back. I took him from her, laid him down gently, kissed his cold little mouth, and tried to whisper to him, though no sound, not even the loudest thunder, could have reached him in his new, spiritual home.

And where was Idris? The news that she had gone out to look for me and had not returned was terrifying, especially with the rain and wind clattering against the windows and roaring around the house. On top of this, the sickening feeling of the plague was growing inside me. I knew I had to act quickly if I ever wanted to see her again. I mounted my horse and rode out to find her, imagining I heard her voice in every gust of wind, all while suffering from fever and pain.

I rode through the dark, rainy, and empty streets of London. My child was dead at home, and the seeds of the fatal disease had taken root in me. I went to find my beloved Idris, who was wandering alone while the rain poured down like a waterfall, soaking her head and chilling her limbs. A woman stood on a doorstep and called out as I galloped past. It was not Idris, so I rode on quickly. Then, a sudden realization—a memory of something I had seen but not fully registered—made me certain that another figure, thin, graceful, and tall, was standing there, clinging to the person who supported her. In a moment, I was beside them, and in another, I held the collapsing Idris in my arms. I lifted her onto the horse, but she was too weak to sit up, so I mounted behind her, held her close to my chest, and wrapped my cloak around her. Her companion, whose familiar but changed face I recognized as Juliet, the daughter of the Duke of L—, received only a brief, compassionate glance from me in that moment of horror. She took the loose reins and led our horse back home. Can I admit it? That was the last moment of my happiness, yet I was happy. Idris had to die because her heart was broken; I had to die because I had caught the plague. The earth was a wasteland, hope was insanity, and life had joined with death. Yet, as I held my fainting love and knew I would soon die, I delighted in having her with me once more. I kissed her again and again, pressing her to my heart.

We arrived home. I helped her dismount, carried her upstairs, and left her in Clara’s care so her wet clothes could be changed.

Her bravery faltered solely when she anticipated danger for her parent, or worried that mortality might hinder her from fulfilling the obligations to which she was steadfastly committed.

Upon leaving Windsor for London, as a preliminary to our ultimate departure, we called upon Lucy and coordinated the strategy for her and her mother’s relocation. Lucy felt regret at the compulsion that forced her to abandon her familiar lanes and village, and to transport a frail parent from her domestic comforts into the desolate expanse of a depopulated world; yet, she was too accustomed to hardship, and possessed too gentle a disposition, to yield to complaints regarding the unavoidable.

Later events, specifically my sickness and that of Idris, pushed her from our thoughts; we eventually recalled her only to assume that she was among the few who had traveled from Windsor to accompany the emigrants, and that she had already reached Paris. Consequently, when we reached Rochester, we were astonished to receive a letter from this model of endurance, delivered by a man who had just arrived from Slough. His report stated that, while traveling from his home and passing through Datchet, he was startled to observe smoke rising from the inn’s chimney; assuming he would find fellow travelers gathered there, he knocked and was let in. The only occupants were Lucy and her mother; the latter had lost the use of her limbs due to a bout of rheumatism, and thus, one by one, all the remaining local residents had departed, leaving them solitary. Lucy begged the man to remain with her; she believed her mother would recover in a week or two, allowing them to depart, but insisted they would surely die if abandoned in such a helpless and isolated state. The man explained that his wife and children were already among the emigrants, and he therefore felt it impossible to stay. As a final measure, Lucy gave him a letter for Idris, to be handed to her wherever he might encounter us. He fulfilled this request, and Idris received the following note with great feeling:

“HONOURED LADY,

“I am certain you will recall and sympathize with me, and I dare to hope you will aid me; what other prospect remains? Forgive my style of writing, as I am quite distraught. A month ago, my dear mother lost the use of her limbs. She is improving, and I am certain that in another month she would be capable of traveling, in the manner you were so generous as to suggest you would organize for us. But now everyone has departed—everyone—and as they left, each person remarked that perhaps my mother would be recovered before we were entirely forsaken. Three days ago, I visited Samuel Woods, who had stayed until the end due to his newborn child; having a large family, I hoped I might convince them to wait a little longer for us, but I discovered the house empty. I have not seen a single person since, until this kind man arrived. —What will become of us? My mother is unaware of our situation; she is so unwell that I have concealed it from her.

Her courage only faltered when she feared danger for her parent, or worried that death might stop her from fulfilling the duties to which she was completely committed.

Lucy was saddened by the necessity of leaving her native lanes and village, and by having to drag an infirm parent from the comforts of home into the desolate, depopulated wilderness; however, she was too well-tempered by hardship and possessed too sweet a nature to complain about what was unavoidable.

Later events, including my illness and that of Idris, pushed her from our thoughts; we only remembered her eventually, assuming she was among the few who had traveled from Windsor to join the emigrants and was already in Paris. When we arrived at Rochester, therefore, we were surprised to receive a letter from this exemplary woman, delivered by a man who had just come from Slough. He explained that while traveling from his home through Datchet, he was surprised to see smoke rising from the inn’s chimney. Thinking he might find companions for his journey there, he knocked and was let in. There was no one in the house but Lucy and her mother; the latter had lost the use of her limbs due to a bout of rheumatism, and so, one by one, all the remaining local inhabitants had departed, leaving them behind. Lucy begged the man to stay with her, promising that in a week or two her mother would be better and they could set out, but that they would surely perish if left so helpless and alone. The man replied that his wife and children were already among the emigrants, and he felt it was impossible for him to stay. As a final resort, Lucy gave him a letter for Idris, to be delivered wherever he might find us. He fulfilled this task, and Idris received the following letter with deep emotion:

“HONOURED LADY,

“I am certain you will remember and pity me, and I dare hope you will assist me; what other hope remains? Please forgive my writing, as I am so bewildered. A month ago, my dear mother lost the use of her limbs. She is already improving, and in another month, I am sure she would be able to travel in the way you kindly offered to arrange for us. But now everyone is gone—everyone—and as they left, each said that perhaps my mother would be better before we were completely deserted. I have not seen a soul since, until this good man arrived. What will become of us? My mother does not know our situation; she is so ill that I have hidden it from her.”

The man replied that his wife and children were already among the emigrants, so he felt it was impossible for him to stay. Please pardon my writing, as I am so bewildered. A month ago, my dear mother lost the use of her limbs. She is already improving, and I am certain that in another month she would be able to travel in the way you kindly offered to arrange for us. But now everyone is gone—everyone. Three days ago, I went to Samuel Woods, who had stayed until the end because of his newborn child. Since they were a large family, I thought I could persuade them to wait a little longer for us, but I found their house empty. I have not seen a soul since, until this good man arrived.
