She wished to share her anxieties with her son, hoping to find comfort in his empathy or strength in his dismissal of her dark predictions. While walking with him along the shore on her first day in Dover, she was cautiously steering their talk toward these concerns, driven by the nervous intensity of her deep emotions, when a courier arrived carrying my message about our brief return to Windsor. He provided a verbal report on our condition, mentioning that despite Lady Idris’s brave and cheerful spirit, he feared she might not survive the journey to Windsor. “Indeed,” the Countess replied, “your assessment is correct; she is nearing her end!” As she uttered these words, her gaze was locked on a tomb-like opening in the cliffside, where she claimed—and later insisted to me with great gravity—that she witnessed Idris walking languidly toward the cavern. Idris had her back turned, her head bowed, and wore her usual white gown, though a light, gauze-like veil draped over her blonde hair like a faint, translucent fog. She appeared sorrowful, as if surrendering to an irresistible force, and she walked submissively into the shadows of the cave, disappearing from sight. “If I were prone to hallucinations,” the elderly woman continued, “I might question my own vision and dismiss my belief; yet I dwell in a world of facts, and I am certain that what I beheld was real. From that instant, I found no peace; I would have given anything to see her once more before she passed, even though I knew it was likely impossible. I set out for Windsor at once, and although I was told we were moving quickly, our pace felt agonizingly slow, as if every delay were designed to torment me. I blamed you, pouring out my burning frustration upon you. It was not a surprise, though it was a sharp, painful blow, when you showed me her final resting place; no words can capture the loathing I felt for you then, as the successful obstacle to my deepest desire. I looked upon her, and my rage, hatred, and unfairness vanished at her coffin, replaced by a remorse—merciful God, that I should suffer it!—that will persist as long as my memory and heart remain.” To heal such guilt, and to ensure that this newfound love and gentleness did not yield the same bitter results as her previous hostility, I dedicated myself to comforting the repentant woman. Our group was a somber one; everyone was consumed by grief for what could not be undone, and the loss of his mother cast a shadow even over young Evelyn’s playfulness. Beyond this lay the uncertainty of the future. Before committing to any major, self-chosen shift, the mind wavers, alternating between hopeful anticipation and a recoil from challenges that suddenly appear more terrifying than ever. A spontaneous shiver passed through me at the thought that, within another day, we might have traversed the sea and begun that bleak, endless, and sorrowful journey which, only a short while ago, I had viewed as the only possible escape from our misery.

She wanted to share her fears with her son, hoping to find comfort in his empathy or strength in his dismissal of her dark premonitions.

On the day she arrived in Dover, she walked with him along the beach. With the hesitation typical of someone prone to intense, dramatic emotions, she was gradually steering the conversation toward her concerns when a messenger arrived with my letter, announcing our sudden return to Windsor. He gave a verbal update on how he had left us, adding that despite Lady Idris’s cheerful spirit, he feared she might not survive the journey to Windsor. “Indeed,” the Countess replied, “your fears are correct; she is on the verge of death!”

As she spoke, her gaze was fixed on a tomb-like hollow in the cliff. She swore to me with absolute solemnity that she saw Idris walking slowly toward the cave. Idris was turned away from her, her head bowed, wearing the white dress she usually favored, though a thin, crape-like veil covered her golden hair, obscuring her like a faint, transparent mist. She appeared dejected, as if submissively yielding to an irresistible force; she walked into the dark opening and vanished.

“If I were prone to hallucinations,” the venerable lady continued, “I might doubt my own eyes and dismiss my belief in what I saw. But I live in a world of reality, and I have no doubt that what I witnessed existed outside of my own mind. From that moment, I could find no peace. I would have given anything to see her one last time before she died. I knew it was unlikely, yet I had to try. I left for Windsor immediately. Even though we traveled quickly, our progress felt agonizingly slow, as if every delay were designed specifically to torment me. I blamed you, heaping my burning impatience upon your head. It was no surprise, though it was an agonizing blow, when you pointed to her final resting place. Words cannot describe the hatred I felt for you at that moment—you, the triumphant obstacle to my deepest desire. I saw her, and my anger, hatred, and injustice died at her bier, replaced by a remorse—great God, that I should feel it—that will last as long as my memory and feelings endure.”

To heal such remorse, and to ensure that this newfound love and tenderness did not produce the same bitter fruit as her previous hatred and harshness, I dedicated all my efforts to soothing the elderly penitent. Our group was a somber one; everyone was consumed by regret for what could not be undone. Even young Evelyn’s natural cheerfulness was overshadowed by the absence of his mother. Added to this was the uncertainty of the future. Before committing to any major, voluntary change, the mind wavers, alternating between soothing itself with hopeful expectations and recoiling from obstacles that suddenly seem more terrifying than ever before. An involuntary tremor ran through me when I realized that, in another day, we might have crossed the sea and begun that hopeless, endless, and sorrowful wandering which, only a short time ago, I had viewed as the only relief our situation could offer.

She wanted to share her fears with her son, hoping to find comfort in his sympathy or courage in his dismissal of her dark predictions. On her first day in Dover, she walked with him along the beach. With the hesitation typical of someone prone to intense, exaggerated emotions, she was slowly steering their conversation toward her concerns when a messenger arrived with my letter announcing our temporary return to Windsor. He gave a verbal update on our condition, adding that despite Lady Idris’s cheerful spirit, he feared she might not reach Windsor alive. “It is true,” the Countess replied, “your fears are correct; she is about to die!” As she spoke, she stared at a tomb-like hollow in the cliff. She solemnly insisted to me that she saw Idris walking slowly toward the cave. Idris was turned away from her with her head bowed, wearing her usual white dress, though a thin, veil-like fabric covered her golden hair, obscuring her like a faint, transparent mist. She appeared dejected, as if obediently yielding to a higher power; she submissively entered the cave and vanished into the darkness. “If I were prone to hallucinations,” the elderly lady continued, “I might doubt my own eyes and question my judgment. But I live in a world of reality, and I do not doubt that what I saw was real. From that moment, I could find no rest. I felt that seeing her one last time before she died was worth my very life. I knew I might not succeed, but I had to try. I left for Windsor immediately. Although I was assured we were traveling quickly, our progress felt like a snail’s pace, and every delay seemed designed specifically to torment me. I blamed you, heaping my burning impatience upon your head. It was not a disappointment, but an agonizing pang, when you pointed to her final resting place. Words cannot express the hatred I felt for you at that moment, as you stood as the triumphant obstacle to my deepest wishes. I saw her, and my anger, hate, and injustice died at her bier, replaced by a remorse—great God, that I should feel it!—that will last as long as my memory and feelings endure.” To heal such remorse and to ensure that this newfound love and gentleness did not produce the same bitter fruit as her previous hate and harshness, I dedicated all my efforts to soothing the repentant woman. Our group was a melancholy one; everyone was filled with regret for what could not be undone. The absence of his mother cast a shadow even over young Evelyn’s natural cheerfulness. Added to this was the uncertainty of the future. Before committing to any major, voluntary change, the mind wavers, alternating between hopeful expectation and recoiling from obstacles that suddenly appear more frightening than ever before. An involuntary tremor ran through me when I realized that, in another day, we might have crossed the sea and begun that hopeless, endless, and sad journey which, only a short time ago, I had viewed as the only relief for our sorrow.

Eventually, his voice was heard and heeded; the throng retreated, leaving only the leaders gathered near him. I had previously watched Lord Raymond traverse his ranks; his triumphant expression and regal bearing commanded universal deference and compliance. Adrian’s presence and impact were quite different. His slender frame, his passionate gaze, and his movements—suggesting entreaty rather than command—demonstrated that affection, devoid of dread, granted him authority over the spirits of a crowd that understood he never retreated from peril and was driven solely by concern for the common good. No division remained between the two factions that had recently been prepared to spill one another’s blood, for while neither would defer to the other, both readily submitted to the Earl of Windsor.

One group, however, stood apart, isolated from the rest, failing to share in the delight sparked by Adrian’s appearance or to absorb the spirit of reconciliation that descended like dew upon the softened hearts of their fellow citizens. Leading this gathering was a heavy, somber individual whose malevolent gaze observed the grim countenances of his followers with gloating satisfaction. They had remained idle until now, but noticing they were overlooked in the general celebration, they moved forward with menacing motions. Our allies had engaged in a sort of reckless dispute; they only required the realization that their interests were identical for them to unite. Their shared hostility had been a fleeting blaze compared to the smoldering resentment they both harbored toward these dissenters, who claimed a segment of the future world to fortify and entrench themselves, emerging with terrifying raids and dreadful proclamations against the ordinary inhabitants of the earth. The initial movement of this small band of the chosen reignited their fury; they seized their weapons and awaited only their commander’s sign to begin the assault, when the distinct resonance of Adrian’s voice commanded them to withdraw. With a confused rumble and a hasty retreat, much like a tide receding noisily from the shores it recently submerged, our friends complied. Adrian rode alone into the gap between the rival groups; he neared the hostile commander as if inviting him to follow his lead, but his gesture was ignored, and the chief moved forward, accompanied by his entire troop. Many women were among them, appearing even more fervent and determined than the men. They crowded around their leader as if to protect him, while loudly showering him with every holy title and term of adoration. Adrian encountered them midway; they stopped. “What,” he inquired, “are you searching for? Do you demand anything from us that we decline to provide, and that you feel compelled to seize through combat and violence?”

His inquiries were met with a collective shout, in which only the terms election, sin, and the red right arm of God were distinguishable.

Adrian gazed pointedly at their leader, remarking, “Are you unable to quiet your followers? Mine, you observe, heed my commands.”

The man responded with a glare; then, perhaps apprehensive that his people might overhear the discussion he anticipated, he ordered them to step back and moved forward alone. “I inquire once more,” Adrian stated, “what is it you demand of us?”

His voice was finally heard and obeyed; the crowd stepped back, leaving only the leaders gathered around him. I had seen Lord Raymond ride through his ranks; his victorious expression and majestic presence commanded universal respect and obedience. Adrian’s appearance and influence were quite different. His slender frame, his intense gaze, and his gestures—more pleading than commanding—proved that love, rather than fear, gave him authority over the hearts of a multitude who knew he never shied away from danger and was motivated only by concern for the common good. There was no longer any distinction between the two factions that had recently been ready to shed each other’s blood, for although neither would submit to the other, both readily obeyed the Earl of Windsor.

One group, however, remained isolated, failing to share in the joy of Adrian’s arrival or to absorb the spirit of peace that settled like dew upon the softened hearts of their countrymen. Leading this assembly was a heavy, dark-featured man whose malicious eyes surveyed the stern expressions of his followers with gloating delight. They had been inactive until now, but seeing themselves ignored in the general celebration, they advanced with threatening gestures. Our friends had been fighting in a kind of reckless rivalry; they only needed to be told their cause was the same for it to become so. Their mutual anger had been like a straw fire compared to the slow-burning hatred they both felt for these separatists, who claimed a portion of the world for themselves, fortifying their position to launch fearful raids and terrifying condemnations against the ordinary people of the earth. The initial advance of this small army of the "elect" reignited their rage; they grabbed their weapons and waited only for their leader’s signal to attack, when the clear tones of Adrian’s voice were heard, ordering them to fall back. With confused murmurs and a hurried retreat—like a wave receding noisily from the sands it had just covered—our friends obeyed. Adrian rode alone into the space between the opposing groups; he approached the hostile leader as if inviting him to do the same, but his gesture was ignored, and the chief advanced, followed by his entire troop. There were many women among them who seemed more eager and resolute than their male companions. They crowded around their leader as if to shield him, while loudly bestowing upon him every sacred title and epithet of worship. Adrian met them halfway; they halted. "What," he asked, "do you seek? Is there anything you require from us that we refuse to give, and that you are forced to acquire through arms and warfare?"

His questions were answered by a general cry, in which the words "election," "sin," and "the red right arm of God" were the only ones that could be heard.

Adrian looked directly at their leader, saying, "Can you not silence your followers? Mine, you see, obey me."

The man answered with a scowl, and then, perhaps fearful that his people might overhear the debate he expected to follow, he ordered them to fall back and advanced by himself. "What, I ask again," said Adrian, "do you require of us?"

His voice was finally heard and obeyed; the crowd stepped back, leaving only the leaders around him. I had seen Lord Raymond ride through his lines, his look of victory and majestic presence earning the respect and obedience of everyone. Adrian’s appearance and influence were quite different. His slender frame, his passionate expression, and his gestures—which seemed more like pleas than commands—proved that love, not fear, gave him power over the hearts of the crowd. They knew he never backed away from danger and was motivated only by a desire for the general good. There was no longer any distinction between the two groups that had recently been ready to shed each other’s blood. Although neither side would submit to the other, they both readily obeyed the Earl of Windsor.

One group, however, remained isolated. They did not share in the joy caused by Adrian’s arrival, nor did they feel the spirit of peace that settled like dew upon the softened hearts of their countrymen. Leading this group was a heavy, dark-looking man whose malicious eyes watched his followers’ stern expressions with gloating delight. They had been inactive until now, but seeing themselves forgotten in the general celebration, they moved forward with threatening gestures. Our friends had been fighting each other in a pointless dispute, but they only needed to be told their cause was the same for it to become so. Their mutual anger had been like a straw fire compared to the slow-burning hatred they both felt for these separatists. These people had claimed a portion of the world for themselves, fortifying their position to launch frightening attacks and terrifying threats against ordinary people. The first advance of this group of 'the elect' reawakened the others' rage. They grabbed their weapons and waited only for their leader’s signal to attack, but Adrian’s clear voice commanded them to fall back. With confused murmurs and a hurried retreat—like a wave noisily pulling back from the sand it had just covered—our friends obeyed. Adrian rode alone into the space between the opposing groups. He approached the hostile leader, as if asking him to do the same, but his look was ignored, and the chief advanced, followed by his entire troop. Many women were among them, appearing more eager and determined than the men. They crowded around their leader as if to shield him, loudly calling him by sacred titles and names of worship. Adrian met them halfway, and they stopped. “What,” he asked, “do you seek? Is there anything you require from us that we refuse to give, and that you feel forced to take by force and warfare?”

His questions were answered by a general shout, in which only the words 'election,' 'sin,' and 'the red right arm of God' could be heard.

Adrian looked directly at their leader, saying, “Can you not silence your followers? Mine, you see, obey me.”

The man answered with a scowl. Perhaps fearing that his people might hear the debate he expected to follow, he ordered them to fall back and stepped forward alone. “I ask you again,” said Adrian, “what do you require of us?”

Whenever our numbers became separated by night, the vanguard would pause at dawn for the rear guard to catch up. We were scheduled to gather at each of the previously noted major towns, where the leading officers would convene to deliberate on our collective welfare. As I had stated, I traveled in the lead, while Adrian brought up the rear. His mother stayed with him, keeping Clara and Evelyn under her care. With our arrangement settled, I set out. My intention was to travel only as far as Fontainebleau initially, where Adrian would meet me in a few days before I continued my journey further toward the east.

My friend traveled a few miles with me out of Versailles. He seemed melancholy, and with a tone of unusual hopelessness, he voiced a wish that we might reach the Alps quickly, adding a futile lament that we were not already there. I remarked that we could simply accelerate our pace if he found the current, slower schedule so disagreeable.

He answered that it was too late for such changes. A month prior, we had been in control of our own fates, but now—he turned his face away from me, and though the deepening dusk already obscured his features, he averted his gaze further as he added that a man had perished from the plague the previous night.

He spoke in a muffled tone, then suddenly wrung his hands and cried out that the final hour was approaching us with great speed. He compared its arrival to stars fading before the sun, claiming it would destroy us. He had tried his best, he said, clutching at the wheels of the plague’s chariot with weak, desperate hands, but it dragged him along as it crushed the lives of everyone scattered across the path of existence. He wished it were finished and that we had all reached the grave together.

His eyes filled with tears as he continued, lamenting that the tragedy would repeat itself again and again. He spoke of the agony of hearing the dying groan and the survivors wail, and of witnessing the final pangs that seemed to contain an eternity within a fleeting moment. He questioned why he was spared, asking why he, the infected sheep of the flock, was not struck down among the first. He found it incredibly difficult for a human being to bear what he was enduring.

Until this moment, Adrian had carried out his self-appointed duty with a brave spirit and a profound sense of purpose. I had watched him with deep respect and a vain wish to emulate him. I offered him some words of sympathy and support. He covered his face with his hands, struggling to regain his composure, and prayed that God would not let his heart fail or his courage falter for a few more months. He begged that the sights of unbearable suffering would not drive him to madness or cause his fragile heart to break. He had accepted it as his fate to lead the remnants of humanity until death ended his rule, and he bowed to that destiny.

He apologized to me, Verney, for causing me distress, promising to complain no more. He claimed to be himself again, or perhaps even better than he had been.

If the groups were separated at night, those in the front would wait for the rear in the morning. We were all to gather at each of the major towns mentioned previously, where a council of the primary officers would meet for the common good. As I mentioned, I went first, while Adrian went last. His mother, with Clara and Evelyn under her care, stayed with him. With our order established, I set out. My plan was to travel no further than Fontainebleau at first, where Adrian would join me in a few days before I continued my journey eastward.

My friend traveled a few miles with me from Versailles. He was sad, and in a tone of unusual hopelessness, he prayed for our quick arrival in the Alps, expressing a futile regret that we were not already there. "In that case," I remarked, "we can speed up our pace; why stick to a plan whose slow progress you already dislike?"

"No," he replied, "it is too late now. A month ago, we were in control of our own fates; now—" he turned his face away from me; even though the gathering twilight had already hidden his expression, he turned it further still as he added, "a man died of the plague last night!"

He spoke in a muffled voice, then suddenly clasped his hands and exclaimed, "The final hour for us all is approaching rapidly; just as the stars disappear before the sun, so will its arrival destroy us. I have done my best; with grasping hands and weak strength, I have tried to hold back the chariot of the plague, but it drags me along as it moves like a Juggernaut, crushing the life out of everyone who litters the highway of existence. I wish it were over—I wish that once its procession was finished, we had all entered the grave together!"

Tears streamed from his eyes. "Again and again," he continued, "the tragedy will play out; I must hear the groans of the dying and the wailing of the survivors once more; I must witness the agony that, in its finality, packs an eternity into a fleeting moment. Why am I kept for this? Why, as the tainted sheep of the flock, am I not struck down among the first? It is hard, very hard, for a human being to endure all that I am enduring!"

Until now, Adrian had fulfilled his self-appointed task with an undaunted spirit and a deep sense of duty and worth. I had watched him with reverence and a useless desire to emulate him. I offered a few words of encouragement and sympathy. He hid his face in his hands, and as he struggled to calm himself, he cried out, "For a few months, just for a few more months, God, do not let my heart fail or my courage break; do not let the sights of unbearable misery drive this half-crazed brain to madness or cause this fragile heart to beat against its cage until it bursts. I have believed it to be my destiny to guide and rule the last of the human race until death ends my leadership; and to this destiny, I submit."

"Forgive me, Verney, I am causing you pain, but I will complain no longer. Now I am myself again, or rather, I am better than myself."

If our numbers were separated at night, those in the front would wait for those in the rear the next morning. We were all to gather at each of the large towns mentioned previously, where a group of the main officers would meet to discuss our general welfare. As I mentioned, I went first, while Adrian went last. His mother, with Clara and Evelyn under her care, stayed with him. With our order established, I set out. My plan was to travel no further than Fontainebleau at first, where Adrian would join me in a few days before I continued my journey further east.

My friend traveled with me for a few miles outside of Versailles. He was sad, and in a tone of unusual hopelessness, he prayed that we would arrive in the Alps quickly, expressing a useless regret that we were not already there. “In that case,” I remarked, “we can speed up our march; why stick to a plan whose slow pace you already dislike?”

“No,” he replied, “it is too late now. A month ago, we were in control of our own fates; now—” he turned his face away from me. Even though the gathering twilight had already hidden his expression, he turned further away as he added, “A man died of the plague last night!”

He spoke in a muffled voice, then suddenly clasped his hands and exclaimed, “The final hour for us all is approaching very quickly. Just as the stars vanish before the sun, the plague’s approach will destroy us. I have done my best; with grasping hands and weak strength, I have tried to hold back the chariot of the plague, but it drags me along with it. Like a Juggernaut, it crushes the life out of everyone who litters the road of existence. I wish it were over—I wish that once the procession was finished, we had all entered the grave together!”

Tears streamed from his eyes. “Again and again,” he continued, “this tragedy will be repeated; again I must hear the groans of the dying and the wailing of those who survive; again I must witness the suffering that, in its finality, packs an eternity into a brief moment of existence. Why am I kept for this? Why, as the tainted sheep of the flock, am I not struck down among the first? It is hard, very hard, for a person born of woman to endure all that I must endure!”

Until now, Adrian had carried out his self-imposed duty with a brave spirit and a strong sense of responsibility and worth. I had watched him with respect and a useless desire to be like him. I now offered a few words of encouragement and sympathy. He hid his face in his hands, and while he tried to calm himself, he cried out, “For a few months, just a few months more, O God, do not let my heart fail or my courage break; do not let the sights of unbearable misery drive this half-crazed brain to madness, or cause this weak heart to beat against its chest until it bursts. I have believed it to be my destiny to guide and rule the last of the human race until death ends my leadership; and to this destiny, I submit.”

“Forgive me, Verney, I am causing you pain, but I will complain no longer. Now I am myself again, or rather, I am better than myself.”

We had anticipated this juncture with intensity, as the most arduous segment of our bleak trek was finished, and Switzerland lay ahead. Still, how could we celebrate a triumph so incomplete? Were these forlorn creatures, trudging by in mournful lines, the final survivors of a human race that once, like a deluge, covered and claimed the entire globe? It had flowed pure and unobstructed from its original mountain spring at Ararat, expanding from a tiny brook into a massive, constant river, with generations pouring forth without end. Consistent yet varied, it surged toward the consuming sea, whose hazy banks we now approached. Humanity was merely nature’s toy when it first emerged from the empty void into the light; yet intellect birthed power and wisdom, and, adorned with these, mankind claimed stature and command. It was no longer just the earth’s gardener or a shepherd of flocks; it possessed a grand and stately presence, boasting a lineage, noble forebears, a collection of portraits, carved memorials, and documented titles.

All of this had vanished, now that the sea of death had swallowed the waning tide and its fountainhead had withered. We had initially said goodbye to the order of existence that, having endured for millennia, seemed permanent—that structure of governance, submission, commerce, and social life that had shaped our spirits and abilities for as long as we could remember. Then, we bid farewell to national fervor, the arts, prestige, lasting renown, and the very concept of a homeland. We watched every hope of reclaiming our former state depart—every expectation, save for the faint desire to rescue our own lives from the ruins of the past. To save them, we had abandoned England—England, no more; for without its people, what title could that desolate isle hold? We held tightly to whatever governance and discipline might best protect us, hoping that if a small settlement could survive, it might eventually suffice to revive the lost fellowship of humanity.

But the end has arrived! We are all destined to die, leaving neither survivor nor successor to the vast legacy of the world. We must all perish! The human species must vanish; its frame of delicate craftsmanship; the miraculous apparatus of its senses; the magnificent symmetry of its divine limbs; its mind, the enthroned ruler of these; all must cease. Will the earth maintain its position among the stars; will it continue its steady orbit around the sun; will the seasons shift, the trees dress themselves in foliage, and blossoms release their scent, all in isolation? Will the peaks stand firm, and rivers continue their descent toward the great deep; will the tides ebb and flow, and breezes stir the natural world; will animals graze, birds soar, and fish glide, once man, the master, owner, observer, and chronicler of all these wonders, has vanished as if he had never existed? Oh, what a cruel irony! Surely death is not truly death, and humanity is not gone, but has simply transformed into other forms, beyond the reach of our senses.

We had anticipated this moment with eagerness, for we had finished the most difficult part of our bleak journey, and Switzerland was close by. Yet, how could we celebrate any achievement that felt so incomplete? Were these miserable people, worn out and wretched as they moved in a sorrowful procession, the only survivors of the human race, which had once spread like a flood to possess the entire earth? It had flowed clearly and steadily from its original mountain source at Ararat, growing from a tiny stream into a vast, constant river, generation after generation flowing on without end. It grew, changing yet remaining the same, and swept toward the consuming ocean, whose dim shores we had now reached. Humanity had been a mere plaything of nature when it first emerged from the empty void into the light; but thought brought forth power and knowledge, and, equipped with these, the human race took on dignity and authority. It was no longer just the gardener of the earth or the shepherd of its flocks; it carried an imposing and majestic presence; it had a lineage and famous ancestors; it had its gallery of portraits, its commemorative inscriptions, its records, and its titles.

This was all finished now that the ocean of death had swallowed the slowing tide, and its source had dried up. We had first said goodbye to the way of life that, having existed for thousands of years, had seemed eternal; the system of government, obedience, commerce, and social interaction that had shaped our hearts and abilities for as long as we could remember. Then we had bid farewell to patriotic passion, the arts, reputation, lasting fame, and the very concept of a country. We saw the end of all hope of restoring our former state—all expectation, except for the weak desire to save our individual lives from the wreckage of the past. To preserve these, we had left England—England, which was no more; for without its people, what name could that barren island claim? We clung desperately to whatever rules and order could best protect us, trusting that if a small colony could be saved, it would eventually be enough to restore the lost community of mankind.

But the game is over! We must all die, leaving neither survivor nor heir to the vast inheritance of the earth. We must all die! The human species must perish; its exquisitely crafted frame, the wondrous mechanism of its senses, the noble proportions of its godlike limbs, and its mind, the enthroned king of all these, must perish. Will the earth still keep its place among the planets; will it still travel with silent regularity around the sun; will the seasons change, the trees dress themselves in leaves, and flowers release their fragrance in solitude? Will the mountains remain unmoved, and streams still flow downward toward the vast abyss; will the tides rise and fall, and the winds blow across the natural world; will beasts graze, birds fly, and fish swim, when man, the lord, possessor, observer, and recorder of all these things, has vanished as if he had never existed? Oh, what a mockery this is! Surely death is not truly death, and humanity is not extinct, but has merely transformed into other shapes, beyond our ability to perceive.

We had looked forward to this moment with eagerness, for we had finally finished the most difficult part of our bleak journey, and Switzerland was close. Yet, how could we celebrate an achievement that felt so incomplete? Were these miserable people, worn out and wretched as they moved in a sorrowful procession, the only survivors of the human race? Humanity had once spread across the entire earth like a flood. It had flowed clearly from its original mountain source at Ararat, growing from a tiny stream into a vast, constant river, generation after generation. It had changed and diversified as it swept toward the final ocean, whose dim shores we were now reaching. At first, humanity was merely a plaything of nature as it emerged from the void into the light; but thought brought power and knowledge. Clad in these, the human race gained dignity and authority. It was no longer just a gardener of the earth or a shepherd of its flocks; it carried an imposing and majestic presence, with a history, famous ancestors, galleries of portraits, monuments, and official records.

All of this was over now that the ocean of death had swallowed the slowing tide and dried up its source. We had first said goodbye to the way of life that had existed for thousands of years and seemed eternal—the systems of government, obedience, trade, and family life that had shaped our hearts and minds for as long as we could remember. Then, we said farewell to patriotism, the arts, reputation, lasting fame, and the very idea of a country. We saw the end of all hope for restoring our old way of life, leaving only the weak expectation of saving our individual lives from the wreckage of the past. To preserve these, we had left England—an England that was no more, for without its people, what name could that barren island claim? We held tightly to whatever rules and order could best protect us, hoping that if a small colony could survive, it might eventually restore the lost community of mankind.

But the game is over! We must all die, leaving no survivors or heirs to inherit the earth. We must all die! The human species must perish; our bodies of exquisite workmanship, the wondrous mechanism of our senses, the noble proportions of our godlike limbs, and our minds—the throned kings of all these—must perish. Will the earth still keep its place among the planets? Will it still travel with steady regularity around the sun? Will the seasons change, the trees grow leaves, and the flowers shed their fragrance in solitude? Will the mountains remain unmoved, the streams continue their downward course toward the vast abyss, the tides rise and fall, and the winds blow through nature? Will beasts graze, birds fly, and fish swim when man—the lord, possessor, observer, and recorder of all these things—has passed away as if he had never existed? Oh, what a mockery this is! Surely death is not truly the end, and humanity is not extinct, but has merely passed into other forms that we can no longer perceive.

Amber flashes of lightning flickered across the immense summit of Mont Blanc, as mute as the frozen stone they lit; everything appeared stark, untamed, and majestic, while the humming of the pine trees provided a soft contrast to the rugged grandeur. At times, the splitting and descent of glacial boulders pierced the atmosphere; at others, the roar of an avalanche crashed upon us. In regions of smaller scale, nature reveals her vitality through the leaves of forests, the sprouting of grass, and the quiet babbling of winding brooks; here, possessed of colossal traits, the waterfall, the tempest, and the movement of heavy currents reveal her force. Such was the cemetery, such the funeral song, and such the everlasting assembly that attended our companion’s burial.

It was not merely the human body we had laid within this permanent tomb, whose final rites we were observing. With this final casualty, the Plague departed from the world. Death had never lacked instruments to extinguish life, and we, diminished and frail as we had grown, remained vulnerable to every other arrow contained in his overflowing quiver. Yet, the pestilence was gone from our midst. For seven years, it had reigned supreme across the planet; it had traversed every corner of our vast world; it had permeated the air, which serves as a mantle for all living beings—the people of Europe, the opulent Asian, the dark-skinned African, and the independent American had all been conquered and annihilated by it. Its savage rule reached its conclusion here within the stony valley of Chamounix.

Recurring visions of suffering and agony, the results of this sickness, no longer defined our existence—the term plague ceased to echo in our hearing—the manifestation of the plague etched upon the human face no longer confronted our sight. From that instant, I witnessed the plague no more. It surrendered its dominion and cast aside its royal staff amidst the icy crags that enclosed us. It left solitude and stillness as the joint inheritors of its realm.

My current emotions are so intertwined with those of the past that I cannot determine if the awareness of this shift reached us while we stood upon that barren ground. It appears to me that it did; that a shadow seemed to lift from above us, that a burden was removed from the atmosphere; that from then on we inhaled more easily and held our heads with a measure of our former freedom. Yet we did not possess hope. We were struck by the conviction that our species had reached its end, but that the plague would not be our executioner. The future was like a powerful river, along which a bewitched vessel is carried, whose human pilot understands that the visible threat is not the one he must dread, yet that peril is near; and who drifts, filled with dread, beneath overhanging cliffs and through murky, turbulent waters—observing in the distance even more bizarre and harsh forms, toward which he is inevitably drawn. What was to be our fate? Oh, for some Delphic prophecy, or Pythian priestess, to declare the mysteries of what is to come! Oh, for some Oedipus to decipher the puzzle of the merciless Sphinx! Such an Oedipus I was destined to be—not one interpreting a verbal trick, but one whose own excruciating torments and grief-stricken existence were to serve as the tools to expose the mysteries of fate and uncover the significance of the enigma, the resolution of which concluded the chronicle of mankind.

Yellow lightning flickered around the massive dome of Mont Blanc, as silent as the snow-covered rock it lit up; everything was bare, wild, and majestic, while the singing of the pines in melodic murmurs added a gentle interest to the rugged grandeur. Now the splitting and falling of icy rocks tore through the air; now the thunder of an avalanche crashed into our ears. In regions with less dramatic features, nature reveals her living power through the foliage of trees, the growth of grass, and the soft bubbling of winding streams; here, gifted with giant attributes, the torrent, the thunderstorm, and the flow of massive waters display her activity. Such was the graveyard, such the requiem, and such the eternal congregation that attended our companion’s funeral!

It was not just the human form we had placed in this eternal tomb, whose funeral rites we were now observing. With this final victim, the plague vanished from the earth. Death had never lacked weapons to destroy life, and we, few and weak as we had become, were still exposed to every other arrow in his full quiver. But the pestilence was gone from among them. For seven years it had held full sway over the earth; it had walked every corner of our spacious globe; it had mingled with the atmosphere, which wraps around all our fellow creatures like a cloak—the inhabitants of native Europe, the luxurious Asiatic, the swarthy African, and the free American had all been defeated and destroyed by it. Its barbaric tyranny came to an end here in the rocky valley of Chamonix.

Recurring scenes of misery and pain, the results of this sickness, were no longer a part of our lives—the word plague no longer rang in our ears—the appearance of plague embodied in the human face no longer stood before our eyes. From this moment, I saw the plague no more. It abdicated its throne and stripped itself of its imperial scepter among the ice rocks that surrounded us. It left solitude and silence as the joint heirs of its kingdom.

My current feelings are so mixed with the past that I cannot say whether the knowledge of this change visited us as we stood on that barren spot. It seems to me that it did; that a cloud seemed to pass from over us, that a weight was lifted from the air; that from then on we breathed more freely and raised our heads with some portion of our former liberty. Yet we did not hope. We were struck by the feeling that our race was run, but that the plague would not be our destroyer. The coming time was like a mighty river, down which a charmed boat is driven, whose mortal steersman knows that the obvious peril is not the one he needs to fear, yet that danger is near; and who floats, awe-struck, under looming precipices, through dark and murky waters—seeing in the distance even stranger and harsher shapes, toward which he is irresistibly pulled. What would become of us? Oh, for some Delphic oracle or Pythian priestess to utter the secrets of the future! Oh, for some Oedipus to solve the riddle of the cruel Sphinx! Such an Oedipus I was to be—not guessing a word puzzle, but one whose agonizing pangs and sorrow-tainted life were to be the tools used to lay bare the secrets of destiny and reveal the meaning of the enigma whose explanation closed the history of the human race.

Observe us now within our fragile dwelling, surrounded by ravenous, crashing billows and lashed by gales. In the pitch-black east, two immense cloud formations, drifting in opposing directions, collided; lightning flashed, and the deep thunder rumbled. Once more in the south, the clouds answered, and the jagged bolt of fire streaking across the dark heavens revealed to us the terrifying masses of clouds, now joined and obscured by the rising swells. Merciful God! And we are solitary—we three—alone—the only inhabitants of the ocean and the world, and we three are destined to die! The immense cosmos, its countless spheres, and the stretches of infinite land we had abandoned—the vastness of the endless sea surrounding us—narrowed in my vision; they, along with everything they held, diminished to a single point, specifically our pitching vessel, which carried the remnants of noble humanity.

A spasm of hopelessness passed over Adrian’s affectionate features, while he muttered through clenched teeth, “Yet they shall be rescued!” Clara, struck by a mortal dread, pallid and shaking, moved toward him—he gazed at her with a reassuring grin—“Are you afraid, dear girl? Oh, do not be afraid, we will reach the coast soon!”

The gloom kept me from observing the shifts in her expression; however, her tone was distinct and gentle as she answered, “Why would I be afraid? Neither the ocean nor the tempest can injure us, unless powerful fate or the master of fate forbids it. Furthermore, the sharp terror of outliving either of you is absent here—a single death will embrace us together.”

In the meantime, we furled all our canvas, except for a jib; and, as soon as it was safe, we altered our direction, sailing with the gale toward the Italian coast. The black night blended all things; we barely perceived the white foam of the lethal waves, except when lightning created a fleeting midday, consuming the darkness, exposing our peril, and returning us to intensified night. We remained quiet, except when Adrian, acting as helmsman, offered a hopeful remark. Our small craft responded to the tiller remarkably well, gliding along the summits of the waves as if it were a child of the ocean, and the furious mother protected her threatened offspring.

I remained at the bow, observing our path; when abruptly I listened to the waters crashing with renewed intensity. We were surely near the coast—simultaneously I shouted, “Turn about!” and a wide flash of lightning filling the sky revealed for an instant the flat beach ahead, exposing even the sands and the stunted, mud-covered patches of reeds that grew at the high-tide line. Once more it was dark, and we caught our breath with the relief one might feel who, while pieces of volcanic rock darken the atmosphere, watches a massive boulder strike the earth directly at his feet. We were uncertain how to proceed—the surf here, there, and everywhere surrounded us—they bellowed, crashed, and cast their loathed spray upon our faces. With significant effort and hazard, we eventually managed to change our heading and steered away from the coast. I encouraged my companions to ready themselves for the destruction of our small boat and to secure themselves to an oar or spar that might serve to keep them afloat. I was personally a skilled swimmer—the mere sight of the ocean usually stirred in me feelings similar to those a huntsman experiences when he listens to a pack of hounds in full pursuit; I delighted in feeling the waves envelop me and attempt to overwhelm me, while I, master of my own movements, navigated this way or that, despite their furious assaults.

Shall it come to pass? We were accustomed to anticipate death with trembling, yet was that not simply because its destination remained hidden? Far more frightening, and significantly more veiled, was the revealed path of my solitary future. I snapped my staff and cast it aside. I required no instrument to measure the incremental progress of my existence, as my restless mind forged divisions far different from those governed by the stars. Reflecting upon the era that had passed since I became solitary, I refused to label the spasms of suffering that truly defined it as mere days or hours.

I covered my countenance with my palms. The chirping of fledgling birds settling for the night and their movement within the branches disrupted the quiet evening air; crickets sang, and the aziolo called out periodically. My reflections had centered on death, yet these noises whispered of life. I raised my gaze; a bat circled overhead. The sun had descended behind the serrated mountain peaks, and the pale, crescent moon appeared, silvery and bright against the amber sunset, joined by a single radiant star that extended the twilight. A group of cattle wandered through the valley below, unguided, toward their watering hole. The breeze stirred the grass, and the olive groves, softened into gentle shapes by the moonlight, set their sea-green hues against the deep chestnut leaves. Indeed, this remains the earth; there is no alteration, no destruction, no tear within her lush surface. She persists in her rotation, alternating between night and day across the heavens, even though humanity no longer inhabits or beautifies her. Why could I not lose myself as those creatures do, and cease to endure the frantic turmoil of my agony? Yet, alas, what a fatal chasm separates their condition from my own! Do they not possess companions? Do they not each have a mate, their beloved offspring, and a home that, while unspoken to us, is surely cherished and enriched in their perception by the community nature has provided? I alone am solitary; I, upon this small summit, observing the plains and mountain hollows, the sky and its celestial inhabitants, listening to every vibration of the earth, the atmosphere, and the whispering tide. I alone cannot share my numerous thoughts with a companion, nor rest my aching head upon a beloved breast, nor draw from meeting eyes a divine essence that surpasses the legendary nectar of the deities. Shall I not then lament? Shall I not denounce the lethal mechanism that has harvested the children of men, my kin? Shall I not cast a curse upon every other creature of nature that dares to exist and find pleasure, while I remain to live and suffer?

Oh, no! I shall train my grieving heart to share in your delights; I will find happiness because you possess it. Continue to exist, you innocent ones, the favored children of nature; I am not so different from you. My being is formed of nerves, pulse, brain, joints, and flesh, and you are structured by those same principles. I possess something beyond this, yet I shall label it a flaw rather than a gift if it leads me to despair while you remain content. I scaled the shed and slipped through my window just as dawn began to break. My fresh garments were covered in grease and mud, and I was exhausted.

CHAPTER III.

I received a stern lecture the next morning from Miss Watson regarding my attire; however, the widow refrained from scolding, choosing instead to wipe away the dirt and oil. She appeared so saddened that I felt inclined to try and act properly for a time. Miss Watson then led me into a closet to pray, yet nothing resulted from it. She instructed me to pray daily, promising that whatever I requested would be granted. That was not the case, however. I tested it. I once obtained a fishing line, but lacked the hooks. It was useless without them. I attempted to secure the hooks on several occasions, but it never succeeded. Eventually, I asked Miss Watson to pray on my behalf, but she called me a fool. She never explained why, and I could not fathom the reason.

I sat alone in the woods once and pondered the matter deeply. I reasoned that if a person could receive anything they prayed for, why did Deacon Winn not recover the money he lost on pork? Why could the widow not retrieve her stolen silver snuffbox? Why could Miss Watson not gain weight? No, I concluded, there is nothing to it. I mentioned this to the widow, and she explained that the things one could obtain through prayer were “spiritual gifts.” This was confusing, but she clarified that I must assist others, prioritize their needs constantly, and never consider myself. I assumed this included Miss Watson. I retreated to the woods to contemplate this for a long while, but I could see no benefit in it—except for the other people; so, I finally decided to stop worrying and let it be. Sometimes the widow would pull me aside and speak of Providence in a way that sounded appealing; yet, the following day, Miss Watson would intervene and ruin it all. I figured there must be two Providences, and a poor fellow might fare well with the widow’s version, but if he fell under Miss Watson’s, there was no hope for him. I thought it through and decided I would belong to the widow’s if he wanted me, though I could not see how he would benefit, given how uneducated, low-down, and mean I was.

Father had not been spotted for over a year, which was a relief; I had no desire to see him again. He used to beat me whenever he was sober and could catch me, though I usually hid in the woods whenever he was nearby. Well, around this time, he was discovered drowned in the river, roughly twelve miles upstream, or so the townspeople claimed.

I climbed up the shed and crept into my window just before daybreak. My new clothes were covered in grease and clay, and I was exhausted.

At times he thinks he will depart, and then again he believes he will remain. The wisest course is to remain calm and allow the old man to follow his own path. A pair of angels are circling him. One is luminous and white, while the other is dark. The white one encourages him to behave for a short spell, then the black one intervenes and ruins everything. CHAPTER V.

I had closed the door. Then I pivoted and there he stood. I was habitually terrified of him, as he whipped me so frequently. I assumed I was frightened now, as well; but within a moment I realized I was mistaken—that is, following the initial shock, so to speak, when my breathing faltered because his presence was so unforeseen; but immediately afterward I perceived I was not afraid of him in any meaningful way.

He was nearly fifty, and he appeared every bit of it. His hair was lengthy, matted, and oily, hanging down so that his eyes gleamed through as if he were peering from behind vines. It was entirely black, without a trace of gray; the same was true of his long, tangled beard. There was no color in his complexion, where it was visible; it was white; not like the complexion of any other man, but a white that would sicken a person, a white that would make one’s skin creep—a tree-toad white, a fish-belly white. Regarding his attire—merely tatters, that was all. He had one ankle resting upon the other knee; the boot on that foot was split, and a pair of his toes protruded, which he flexed occasionally. His hat rested upon the floor—a battered black slouch with the crown collapsed, like a lid.

I remained observing him; he sat there observing me, with his chair tipped back slightly. I placed the candle down. I observed the window was raised; so he had climbed in by way of the shed. He continued to examine me from head to toe. Eventually he remarked:

“Fancy clothes—very. You fancy yourself quite the important person, don’t you?”

“Perhaps I am, perhaps I am not,” I replied.

I anticipated finding someone lying inside, as individuals frequently acted that way to deceive others, popping up to mock any fellow who rowed a boat out to investigate. However, that was not the case here. It was truly a drifting canoe, so I climbed aboard and rowed it to the bank. I figured my father would be pleased to see it, as it was valued at ten dollars. Yet, when I reached the shore, my father was nowhere to be seen. As I maneuvered the craft into a small, vine-and-willow-covered creek, a new thought occurred: I decided to conceal it well. Instead of fleeing into the woods when I eventually ran away, I would travel fifty miles downriver, settle in one spot, and avoid the hardship of traveling on foot.

It was quite near the cabin, and I constantly imagined I heard my father approaching, but I managed to hide the boat. I stepped out and peered around some willow bushes to see him down the path, aiming his rifle at a bird. He had not noticed anything.

By the time he arrived, I was busy retrieving a trotline. He scolded me briefly for my sluggishness, but I claimed I had fallen into the river, which explained my delay. I knew he would notice my wet clothes and start questioning me. We collected five catfish from the lines and returned home.

While we rested after breakfast to recover our strength, both of us being exhausted, I pondered that if I could devise a method to prevent my father and the widow from pursuing me, it would be more reliable than relying on luck to get far away before they noticed my absence; after all, anything could happen. I saw no solution for a while, but eventually, my father sat up to drink more water and remarked, "Next time a man comes prowling around here, you wake me up, you hear? That fellow was up to no good. I would have shot him. Next time, you wake me up, you hear?"

He then lay back down and resumed sleeping, but his words provided the exact idea I needed. I told myself that I could now arrange things so that no one would consider chasing me.

Around noon, we got up and walked along the riverbank. The water was rising rapidly, carrying plenty of driftwood. Soon, a section of a log raft appeared, consisting of nine logs lashed together. We took the skiff out and towed it to shore. Then we ate dinner. Anyone other than my father would have waited to see what else the day might bring, but that was not his way. Nine logs sufficed; he had to head straight to town to sell them. He locked me inside, took the skiff, and departed with the raft in tow around three-thirty. I ignited my pipe, enjoyed a lengthy smoke, and continued my observation. The ferry was drifting with the river, and I anticipated a chance to identify the passengers as it passed, since it would steer near the spot where the bread had floated. Once it drew reasonably close, I extinguished my pipe, moved to the location where I had retrieved the bread, and stretched out behind a log in a small clearing. Through a gap in the log’s branches, I could peer out.

Eventually, the boat arrived, drifting so near that they could have extended a gangplank and stepped onto the bank. Nearly everyone was aboard: Pap, Judge Thatcher, Bessie Thatcher, Jo Harper, Tom Sawyer, his elderly Aunt Polly, Sid, Mary, and many others. Everyone was discussing the murder, but the captain interrupted, saying, "Keep a sharp lookout; the current pulls in closest here, and perhaps he has washed up and become caught in the brush along the bank. I certainly hope so."

I did not share that hope. They all gathered at the rails, leaning over almost directly above me, remaining silent and watching intently. I had a clear view of them, but they could not spot me. Then the captain shouted, "Stand back!" and the cannon fired a blast so close that the sound deafened me and the smoke nearly blinded me, leading me to believe I was finished. Had they loaded it with bullets, I suspect they would have secured the corpse they were seeking. Fortunately, I realized I was unharmed. I assumed they had reached the end and were abandoning the search. However, they persisted a while longer. Some young birds appeared, fluttering a few feet at a time before landing. Jim claimed it indicated rain was coming. He explained that when his father was gravely ill, someone caught a bird, and his grandmother predicted his father would pass away, which is exactly what happened.

Jim also warned against counting items intended for dinner, as it invited misfortune. The same applied to shaking a tablecloth after sunset. He added that if a beekeeper passed away, the bees had to be informed before dawn, or they would grow weak, stop working, and perish. Jim insisted bees wouldn’t sting fools, though I doubted that, having tested it myself many times without getting stung.

I had heard of some of these omens, but not all. Jim was an expert on signs and claimed to know nearly everything. I remarked that most signs seemed to involve bad luck, so I asked if there were any for good fortune. He replied:

Each evening around ten o’clock, I would sneak to the bank near some small town to purchase a few cents’ worth of bacon, meal, or other provisions. Occasionally, I would also snatch a chicken that seemed to be resting poorly. My father always insisted that one should grab a chicken whenever possible, arguing that if you didn’t need it, someone else surely would, and a kind act is never forgotten. I never knew my father to turn down a chicken for himself, but that was his usual justification.

Before dawn, I would creep into fields to take a pumpkin, a watermelon, a cantaloupe, or some fresh corn. My father claimed it was not wrong to borrow items if you intended to pay for them eventually, though the widow maintained that was merely a polite term for theft and that no respectable person would engage in it. Jim suggested that both the widow and my father were partially correct, so we decided the best approach was to select a few items from our list to stop taking, which would make borrowing the rest acceptable. We spent an entire night drifting down the river debating whether to give up watermelons, cantaloupes, or other produce. By morning, we reached a satisfactory agreement to stop taking persimmons and crabapples. We felt uneasy before this decision, but afterwards, we were at ease. I was pleased with the outcome, as crabapples are never tasty, and the persimmons would not be ready for several months.

Occasionally, we would shoot a waterfowl that rose too early or stayed out too late. All things considered, we were eating quite well.

On the fifth night past St. Louis, a severe storm struck after midnight, bringing intense thunder and lightning while rain fell in heavy sheets. We remained inside our shelter and allowed the raft to drift. Whenever lightning illuminated the scene, we could see a wide, straight stretch of river flanked by tall, rocky cliffs. Eventually, I called out, “Look there, Jim!” It was a steamboat that had wrecked against a rock, and we were drifting directly toward it. The lightning revealed the vessel clearly; it was tilted, with a portion of the upper deck above the surface. During the flashes, I could see every detail, including the chimney stays and a chair near the large bell with an old slouch hat draped over it.

Given the late hour, the storm, and the general sense of mystery, I felt exactly as any boy would upon seeing that mournful, lonely wreck sitting in the middle of the river. I wanted to board her and explore a bit. I said, “Let’s land on her, Jim.”

Jim was strongly opposed at first. He replied, “I don’t want to go fooling around any wreck.”

Every night now, I would sneak ashore around ten o’clock at some small village to buy ten or fifteen cents’ worth of cornmeal, bacon, or other food. Sometimes I would grab a chicken that wasn’t roosting comfortably and take it with me. My father always said to take a chicken whenever you had the chance, because if you didn’t want it yourself, you could easily find someone who did, and a good deed is never forgotten. I never saw my father when he didn’t want the chicken himself, but that is what he used to say anyway.

In the mornings before dawn, I would slip into cornfields and borrow a watermelon, a muskmelon, a pumpkin, some fresh corn, or things like that. My father always said it wasn’t wrong to borrow items if you intended to pay them back eventually, but the widow insisted that was just a polite term for stealing and that no decent person would do it. Jim thought the widow was partly right and my father was partly right, so he suggested the best approach would be for us to pick two or three items from our list and agree not to borrow them anymore, which he figured would make it acceptable to borrow the rest. We talked it over all night while drifting down the river, trying to decide whether to stop taking watermelons, cantaloupes, muskmelons, or something else. By daybreak, we had settled it to our satisfaction and decided to stop taking crabapples and persimmons. We hadn’t felt quite right before that, but everything was comfortable now. I was happy with the outcome, too, because crabapples are never good, and the persimmons wouldn’t be ripe for another two or three months.

We occasionally shot a waterfowl that got up too early in the morning or didn’t go to bed early enough in the evening. All things considered, we lived quite well.

He remarks:

“I didn’t realize there were so many of them. I’ve hardly heard of any, except for old King Solomon, unless you count the kings found in a deck of cards. How much money does a king earn?”

“Earn?” I reply; “why, they receive a thousand dollars monthly if they choose; they can take as much as they desire; everything is their property.”

“Isn’t that grand? And what are they required to do, Huck?”

“They don’t do anything! Honestly, what are you saying? They simply lounge about.”

“No; is that the truth?”

“Certainly it is. They just sit around—except, perhaps, when there is a war; then they head off to battle. But otherwise they just idle away; or go falconry—just hunting and sp— Shh!—did you hear a sound?”

We hurried out to investigate; but it was nothing more than the splashing of a steamboat’s paddle wheel far down the river, rounding the bend; so we returned.

“Yes,” I continue, “and at other times, when things are boring, they argue with the parliament; and if everyone doesn’t act exactly as they wish, they chop their heads off. But mostly they spend their time in the harem.”

“In the what?”

“Harem.”

“What is a harem?”

“The location where he houses his wives. Don’t you know about a harem? Solomon possessed one; he had about a million wives.”

“Why, yes, that’s true; I—I had forgotten. A harem is a boarding-house, I suppose. Most likely they have noisy times in the nursery. And I imagine the wives argue quite a bit; and that increases the commotion. Yet they claim Solomon was the wisest man who ever lived. I don’t believe that. Because why: would a wise man choose to live in the middle of such a racket all the time? No—indeed he wouldn’t. A wise man would go and build a boiler factory; and then he could shut down the boiler factory whenever he wanted to rest.”

“Well, but he was the wisest man, regardless; because the widow told me so, herself.”

“I don’t care what the widow says, he wasn’t a wise man at all. He had some of the most ridiculous habits I’ve ever seen. Do you know about that child he was going to cut in two?”

“Yes, the widow told me the whole story.”

“Well, then! Wasn’t that the most absurd idea in the world? You just take and consider it for a moment. There is the stump, there—that is one of the women; here is you—that is the other one; I am Solomon; and this dollar bill is the child. Both of you claim it. What do I do? Do I go around among the neighbors and discover which one the bill belongs to, and hand it over to the rightful owner, safely, the way anyone with any sense would? No; I take and chop the bill in two, and give half of it to you, and the other half to the other woman. That is how Solomon intended to treat the child. Now I want to ask you: what is the use of that half a bill?—you can’t buy anything with it. And what use is half a child? I wouldn’t give a damn for a million of them.”

“But hang it, Jim, you’ve completely missed the point—darn it, you’ve missed it by a thousand miles.”

“I am, am I? Well, tell me this: didn’t you carry the rope out in the canoe to tie it to the sandbar?”

“No, I didn’t. What sandbar? I haven’t seen any sandbar.”

“You haven’t seen any sandbar? Listen here, didn’t the line snap and the raft go rushing down the river, leaving you and the canoe behind in the mist?”

“What mist?”

“Why, the mist! The mist that has been around all night. And didn’t you shout, and didn’t I shout, until we got confused among the islands and one of us got lost, and the other was just as good as lost because he didn’t know where he was? And didn’t I crash into a bunch of those islands and have a terrible time and nearly drown? Now isn’t that true, boss—isn’t it? You answer me that.”

“Well, this is beyond me, Jim. I haven’t seen any mist, or any islands, or any troubles, or anything. I’ve been sitting here talking with you all night until you fell asleep about ten minutes ago, and I suppose I did the same. You couldn’t have gotten drunk in that time, so naturally you’ve been dreaming.”

“Confound it, how am I going to dream all that in ten minutes?”

“Well, hang it all, you did dream it, because none of it actually occurred.”

“But, Huck, it’s all just as clear to me as—”

“It doesn’t matter how clear it is; there’s nothing to it. I know, because I’ve been here the whole time.”

Jim didn’t say anything for about five minutes, but sat there pondering it. Then he says:

“Well, then, I suppose I did dream it, Huck; but I’ll be darned if it isn’t the most intense dream I’ve ever had. And I’ve never had a dream before that exhausted me like this one.”

“Oh, well, that’s fine, because a dream can tire a person out completely sometimes. But this was a remarkable dream; tell me the whole thing, Jim.”

So Jim began and told me the entire story exactly as it occurred, though he embellished it quite a bit. Then he said he had to begin to “interpret” it, because it was sent as a warning. He explained that the first sandbar represented a man who would try to help us, but the current was another man who would pull us away from him. The shouts were warnings that would come to us occasionally, and if we didn’t try hard to comprehend them, they would lead us into misfortune instead of keeping us safe. The group of sandbars were troubles we would encounter with argumentative people and all sorts of nasty folks, but if we kept to ourselves and didn’t talk back or provoke them, we would survive and emerge from the mist into the large, clear river, which was the free States, and wouldn’t have any more difficulties.

It had grown quite dark just after I boarded the raft, but it was brightening up again now.

“Oh, well, that’s all interpreted well enough as far as it goes, Jim,” I say; “but what do these things represent?”

“I am, am I? Well, you answer me this: Didn’t you take the line out in the canoe to tie it to the sandbar?”

“No, I didn’t. What sandbar? I haven’t seen any sandbar.”

“You haven’t seen any sandbar? Look here, didn’t the line pull loose and the raft go humming down the river, leaving you and the canoe behind in the fog?”

“What fog?”

“Why, the fog! The fog that’s been around all night. And didn’t you shout, and didn’t I shout, until we got mixed up in the islands and one of us got lost and the other was just as good as lost, because he didn’t know where he was? And didn’t I crash into a bunch of those islands and have a terrible time and almost get drowned? Now isn’t that so, boss—isn’t it so? You answer me that.”

“Well, this is too much for me, Jim. I haven’t seen any fog, or any islands, or any troubles, or anything. I’ve been sitting here talking with you all night until you went to sleep about ten minutes ago, and I suppose I did the same. You couldn’t have gotten drunk in that time, so of course you’ve been dreaming.”

“Darn it, how am I going to dream all that in ten minutes?”

“Well, hang it all, you did dream it, because none of it happened.”

“But, Huck, it’s all just as clear to me as—”

“It doesn’t make any difference how clear it is; there’s nothing to it. I know, because I’ve been here the whole time.”

Jim didn’t say anything for about five minutes, but sat there thinking it over. Then he says:

“Well, then, I suppose I did dream it, Huck; but I’ll be darned if it isn’t the most powerful dream I’ve ever had. And I’ve never had a dream before that tired me out like this one.”

“Oh, well, that’s all right, because a dream does tire a person out sometimes. But this one was a real intense dream; tell me all about it, Jim.”

So Jim went to work and told me the whole thing right through, just as it happened, only he embellished it quite a bit. Then he said he had to start in and interpret it, because it was sent as a warning. He said the first sandbar stood for a man who would try to do us some good, but the current was another man who would pull us away from him. The shouts were warnings that would come to us every now and then, and if we didn’t try hard to understand them, they would just lead us into bad luck, instead of keeping us out of it. The group of sandbars represented troubles we were going to get into with quarrelsome people and all kinds of mean folks, but if we minded our own business and didn’t talk back and aggravate them, we would pull through and get out of the fog and into the big clear river, which was the free States, and wouldn’t have any more trouble.

It had clouded up pretty dark just after I got onto the raft, but it was clearing up again now.

“Oh, well, that’s all interpreted well enough as far as it goes, Jim,” I says; “but what do these things stand for?”

"I am, am I? Well, you answer me this: Didn't you take the line out in the canoe to tie it to the sandbar?"

"No, I didn't. What sandbar? I haven't seen any sandbar."

"You haven't seen any sandbar? Look here, didn't the line pull loose and the raft go rushing down the river, leaving you and the canoe behind in the fog?"

"What fog?"

"Why, the fog! The fog that has been around all night. And didn't you shout, and didn't I shout, until we got mixed up in the islands and one of us got lost and the other was just as good as lost, because he didn't know where he was? And didn't I crash into a lot of those islands and have a terrible time and almost get drowned? Now isn't that so, boss—isn't it so? You answer me that."

"Well, this is too much for me, Jim. I haven't seen any fog, or any islands, or any troubles, or anything. I have been sitting here talking with you all night until you went to sleep about ten minutes ago, and I suppose I did the same. You couldn't have gotten drunk in that time, so of course you have been dreaming."

"Darn it, how am I going to dream all that in ten minutes?"

"Well, hang it all, you did dream it, because none of it happened."

"But, Huck, it is all just as clear to me as—"

"It doesn't make any difference how clear it is; there is nothing to it. I know, because I have been here the whole time."

Jim didn't say anything for about five minutes, but sat there thinking it over. Then he said:

"Well, then, I suppose I did dream it, Huck; but I'll be surprised if it isn't the most powerful dream I ever had. And I have never had a dream before that tired me out like this one."

"Oh, well, that is all right, because a dream does tire a person out sometimes. But this one was a remarkable dream; tell me all about it, Jim."

So Jim went to work and told me the whole thing from start to finish, just as it happened, only he added quite a bit to it. Then he said he must start in and interpret it, because it was sent as a warning. He said the first sandbar stood for a man who would try to do us some good, but the current was another man who would get us away from him. The shouts were warnings that would come to us every now and then, and if we didn't try hard to understand them, they would just lead us into bad luck instead of keeping us out of it. The group of sandbars represented troubles we were going to get into with quarrelsome people and all kinds of mean folks, but if we minded our own business and didn't talk back or aggravate them, we would pull through and get out of the fog and into the big clear river, which was the free States, and wouldn't have any more trouble.

It had clouded up pretty dark just after I got onto the raft, but it was clearing up again now.

"Oh, well, that is all interpreted well enough as far as it goes, Jim," I said; "but what do these things stand for?"

We pushed off into the darkness on our raft. Anyone who still doubts the folly of handling a snakeskin, after all the trouble it caused us, will surely be convinced after reading what happened next. The best way to acquire a canoe is from rafts moored along the bank, but we found none, so we drifted for over three hours. The night turned gray and hazy, which is nearly as difficult as fog, making it impossible to discern the river's contours or see very far. It grew late and silent until a steamboat approached. We ignited our lantern, assuming they would spot us. Usually, upstream vessels avoid us by following the sandbars and seeking calmer water near the reefs, but on nights like this, they plow straight up the main channel.

We heard her heavy thumping before we could clearly see her. She headed directly toward us. Steamboats often do this to see how close they can get without making contact; sometimes the paddlewheel clips an oar, and the pilot just leans out to laugh, feeling quite clever. This one approached rapidly, and we realized she intended to graze us, yet she showed no sign of veering away. She was a massive vessel moving at high speed, resembling a dark cloud rimmed with glowing lights. Suddenly, she loomed large and terrifying, her furnace doors gaping like red-hot teeth, her immense bow and guards hanging directly above us. A shout rang out, followed by the jingling of engine-room bells, a flurry of swearing, and the hiss of escaping steam. As Jim leaped off one side and I off the other, she crashed directly through our raft.

I dove deep, intending to reach the riverbed, as I needed to ensure the thirty-foot paddlewheel had ample space to pass over me. I could typically hold my breath for a minute, but this time I suspect I stayed under for ninety seconds. Then, feeling as though I might burst, I shot toward the surface. I broke through up to my armpits, cleared the water from my nose, and gasped for air. Naturally, the current was powerful, and the steamboat resumed its engines only ten seconds after stopping, as they had little regard for those on rafts. She churned away upstream, vanishing into the gloom, though I could still hear her.

I called out for Jim repeatedly, but received no reply. I grabbed a passing plank while treading water and began pushing it toward the bank. Realizing the current was pulling toward the left, indicating I was in a river crossing, I adjusted my course accordingly.
