One depicted a girl at a window gazing at the moon, tears streaming down her face; she held an open letter marked with black wax, pressing a chained locket to her lips, while the caption read, “And Art Thou Gone Yes Thou Art Gone Alas.” These were fine drawings, I suppose, yet they never appealed to me, as they gave me the jitters whenever I felt low. Everyone mourned her passing, for she had planned many more such works, and one could tell by her existing efforts what had been lost. Still, I figured that with her temperament, she was likely better off in the cemetery. She was laboring on what was considered her masterpiece when she fell ill, praying daily and nightly to survive until its completion, but that opportunity never arrived. It showed a girl in a long white dress standing on a bridge railing, prepared to leap, her hair loose, looking moonward with tears on her cheeks; she had two arms crossed over her chest, two extended forward, and two more reaching toward the moon—the plan was to determine which pair looked best and erase the rest; but, as I mentioned, she passed away before deciding, and now they kept the painting above her bed, adorning it with flowers each birthday. Otherwise, it remained hidden behind a small curtain. The girl in the image had a pleasant, sweet face, but the abundance of arms made her appear too much like a spider, in my opinion.

This girl maintained a scrapbook during her life, pasting in obituaries, accounts of accidents, and tales of suffering clipped from the Presbyterian Observer, accompanying them with original poetry. It was quite good verse. Here is what she composed regarding a boy named Stephen Dowling Bots who tumbled into a well and drowned:

ODE TO STEPHEN DOWLING BOTS, DEC’D

Did young Stephen grow ill,
 And did young Stephen die?
Did sorrowful hearts fill,
 And did the mourners cry?

No; such was not the destiny of
 Young Stephen Dowling Bots;
Though sorrowful hearts filled around him,
 It was not from sickness’ shots.

No whooping-cough racked his body,
 Nor dreary measles with spots;
Not these tarnished the sacred name
 Of Stephen Dowling Bots.

Rejected love brought no misery
 To that head of curly knots,
Nor did stomach ailments lay him low,
 Young Stephen Dowling Bots.

O no. Then listen with tearful eye,
 While I recount his fate.
His soul departed this cold world
 By falling down a well.

They retrieved him and drained him;
 Alas, it was too late;
His spirit had gone to frolic above
 In the realms of the good and great.

The following Sunday, we traveled about three miles to attend services, with everyone riding on horseback. The men carried their rifles, Buck included, holding them between their legs or leaning them conveniently against the walls. The Shepherdsons followed the same practice. The sermon was quite tedious, focusing on themes like brotherly love and similar tiresome topics; however, everyone praised it as an excellent message. They discussed it at length on the journey home, debating faith, good deeds, divine grace, and predestination with such intensity that it felt like one of the most exhausting Sundays I had ever experienced.

Roughly an hour after our midday meal, everyone was drowsing, either in their seats or their bedrooms, and the atmosphere grew quite stagnant. Buck and a dog lay sprawled on the lawn, fast asleep in the sunlight. I headed toward our room, intending to catch a nap myself. I discovered the lovely Miss Sophia standing in her doorway, which was adjacent to ours. She ushered me inside, closed the door quietly, and inquired if I liked her. I confirmed that I did. She then asked if I would perform a favor for her in secret, and I agreed. She explained that she had left her Testament in the church pew between two other volumes and requested that I sneak out, retrieve it, and keep the matter to myself. I promised to do so. I crept out and made my way up the road. The church was empty, save for a couple of hogs, as the building had no locks and the animals enjoyed the cool wooden floor during the summer heat. You might notice that most people only attend church when necessary, but hogs are different.

I pondered to myself that something was afoot; it seemed unnatural for a young woman to be so anxious over a Bible. I shook the book, and a small slip of paper fell out with the words “Half-past two” scribbled in pencil. I searched the rest of the volume but found nothing else. Unable to decipher the meaning, I tucked the paper back inside. Upon returning home and heading upstairs, I found Miss Sophia waiting at her door. She pulled me inside and locked it. She checked the Testament until she located the note, and upon reading it, her expression brightened. Before I could react, she hugged me tightly, called me the best boy in the world, and reminded me to tell no one. Her face flushed for a moment, and her eyes sparkled, making her look remarkably beautiful. I was quite surprised, but once I recovered, I asked what the note meant. She inquired if I had read it, and when I said no, she asked if I could read handwriting. I told her I could only read large print. She then claimed the paper was merely a bookmark to save her place, and told me I could go off and play.

They discussed it at length on the way home, talking so much about faith, good works, free grace, and predestination, and I don’t know what else, that it felt like one of the most exhausting Sundays I had ever experienced.

Jim maintained that it was ghosts, but I countered, "No, ghosts wouldn't exclaim, 'Dern the dern fog.'"

As soon as darkness fell, we pushed off. Once we reached the center of the river, we left the raft to drift with the current. We ignited our pipes, hung our feet over the edge, and chatted about various topics. We remained unclothed day and night, provided the mosquitoes allowed it; the new garments provided by Buck’s family were too fine to be cozy, and I never cared much for wearing clothes anyway.

At times, we had the entire river to ourselves for extended periods. Across the water lay the banks and islands, marked occasionally by a faint glimmer—a candle in a cabin window. Sometimes, we spotted similar lights on the water from a raft or flatboat, and perhaps we could hear the faint sound of a fiddle or a song drifting from those vessels. Life on a raft is wonderful. Above us, the sky was covered in stars, and we would lie on our backs, gazing upward and debating whether they were created or simply came into existence. Jim insisted they were made, but I argued they just happened; I figured it would have taken too long to create so many. Jim suggested the moon might have laid them, which seemed plausible enough that I didn't object, having seen a frog lay nearly as many, so it was clearly possible. We also watched for falling stars, observing them streak across the sky. Jim claimed they had gone bad and were tossed out of the nest.

Once or twice each night, we would watch a steamboat glide through the darkness. Occasionally, it would spew a multitude of sparks from its chimneys, which would rain down into the river, creating a beautiful sight. Then, it would round a bend, its lights would vanish, its noise would cease, and the river would return to silence. Eventually, its wake would reach us, long after the boat had passed, rocking the raft slightly. Afterward, there would be nothing to hear for an indefinite time, save perhaps for the sound of frogs.

After midnight, the people on the banks retired, leaving the shores in darkness for two or three hours without a single light in any cabin window. These lights served as our clock; the first one to reappear signaled that morning was approaching, prompting us to immediately seek a spot to hide and moor the raft.

One morning near dawn, I discovered a canoe and crossed a narrow channel to the main bank—a distance of only two hundred yards—and paddled about a mile up a creek through the cypress trees, hoping to gather some berries. Just as I passed a spot where a cowpath intersected the creek, two men came rushing down the path as fast as they could run. I feared I was finished, for whenever someone was chasing someone else, I assumed it was me—or perhaps Jim.

Jim said he thought it was ghosts, but I said, “No, ghosts wouldn’t say, ‘Darn the darn fog.’”

As soon as it was dark, we pushed off. Once we reached the middle of the river, we let the raft drift wherever the current took it. We lit our pipes, dangled our legs in the water, and talked about everything. We were always naked, day and night, whenever the mosquitoes allowed it; the new clothes Buck’s family made for me were too nice to be comfortable, and besides, I never really cared for clothes anyway.

Sometimes we had the entire river to ourselves for a long time. Over there were the banks and islands, and maybe a tiny spark—a candle in a cabin window. Sometimes on the water, you could see a spark or two from a raft or a flatboat, and you might hear a fiddle or a song drifting over from one of those boats. It’s lovely living on a raft. We had the sky above us, all speckled with stars, and we used to lie on our backs, look up at them, and argue about whether they were created or just happened. Jim believed they were created, but I figured they just happened; I thought it would have taken too long to make that many. Jim said the moon could have laid them, which sounded reasonable enough, so I didn’t argue, since I’ve seen a frog lay just about that many, so it could certainly be done. We also watched the shooting stars streak across the sky. Jim thought they had gone bad and were thrown out of the nest.

Once or twice a night, we would see a steamboat slipping through the dark, occasionally belching a cloud of sparks from its chimneys that would rain down into the river, looking beautiful. Then it would turn a corner, its lights would wink out, the noise would stop, and the river would be still again. Eventually, its wake would reach us, long after the boat was gone, rocking the raft a little. After that, you wouldn’t hear anything for a long time, except maybe for frogs or similar sounds.

After midnight, the people on shore went to bed, and for two or three hours, the banks were pitch black with no more sparks in the cabin windows. These sparks were our clock; the first one to reappear meant morning was coming, so we would immediately look for a place to hide and tie up.

That sounded reasonable, so I didn't argue, because I have seen a frog lay just as many, so it was clearly possible. We also watched the falling stars streak across the sky. Jim thought they had gone bad and were thrown out of the nest.

Once or twice a night, we would see a steamboat slipping through the dark. Occasionally, it would belch a cloud of sparks from its chimneys, which rained down into the river and looked beautiful. Then it would turn a corner, its lights would vanish, the noise would stop, and the river would grow still again. A long time later, its wake would reach us, rocking the raft slightly. After that, we would hear nothing for a long time, except perhaps for frogs.

After midnight, the people on shore went to bed, and for two or three hours, the banks were pitch black with no more lights in the cabin windows. These lights were our clock; the first one to reappear meant morning was coming, so we would immediately look for a place to hide and tie up.

One morning, around daybreak, I found a canoe and crossed a narrow channel to the main shore—only about two hundred yards—and paddled a mile up a creek through the cypress woods to see if I could find some berries. Just as I passed a spot where a cow path crossed the creek, two men came tearing down the path as fast as they could run. I thought I was finished, because whenever anyone was chasing someone, I assumed it was me—or perhaps Jim.

Jim was nearly overcome with laughter. He was always the most prone to laughter of any man I had ever known.

I retrieved the watch, and Jim settled down to sleep, snoring soundly. Eventually, the storm ceased entirely, and as soon as the first light appeared from a cabin, I woke him, and we moved the raft into a concealed spot to remain for the day.

Following breakfast, the king produced a worn-out pack of cards, and he and the duke played seven-up for a nickel a round. Growing bored, they decided to plan a scheme, as they termed it. The duke rummaged through his carpet-bag, pulled out several printed handbills, and read them aloud. One announced that the famous Dr. Armand de Montalban, from Paris, would lecture on phrenology at a specific location on a certain date for ten cents, offering character charts for an additional quarter. The duke claimed that was his identity. Other bills identified him as the world-famous Shakespearean actor, Garrick the Younger, from London’s Drury Lane. Additional notices listed various other aliases and impressive feats, such as locating water or gold using a divining rod and breaking witchcraft spells. Finally, he remarked:

“But the dramatic arts are the true passion. Have you ever performed on stage, your Majesty?”

“Never,” the king replied.

“You shall do so before three days pass, your fallen Highness,” the duke declared. “At the first suitable town, we will rent a hall and perform the sword fight from Richard III and the balcony scene from Romeo and Juliet. How does that sound to you?”

“I am fully committed to anything that brings in money, Bilgewater; however, I know nothing of acting and have seen very little of it. I was too young when my father hosted such performances at the palace. Do you think you can teach me?”

“It will be simple!”

“Very well. I am eager for something new regardless. Let us begin immediately.”

Consequently, the duke explained the roles of Romeo and Juliet, noting that he usually played Romeo, so the king would take the part of Juliet.

“But if Juliet is such a young girl, duke, my bald head and white beard might look rather strange for the role.”

“Do not concern yourself; these rural folks will never notice. Furthermore, you will be in costume, which changes everything. Juliet is on a balcony, taking in the moonlight before retiring, wearing her nightgown and a ruffled nightcap. Here are the outfits for the roles.”

He produced several suits made of curtain calico, which he described as medieval armor for Richard III and the other character, along with a long white cotton nightshirt and a matching ruffled cap. The king was content, so the duke retrieved his book and recited the lines with grand, dramatic flair, pacing and gesturing to demonstrate the proper technique. He then handed the book to the king, instructing him to memorize his lines.

Three miles down the river bend lay a small, insignificant town. After their meal, the duke mentioned he had devised a plan to travel during daylight hours without endangering Jim, so he intended to head into town to arrange the matter.

It nearly killed Jim with laughter. He was the easiest person to make laugh that I had ever met, anyway.

I took the watch, and Jim lay down and snored away. Eventually, the storm stopped for good, and as soon as the first cabin light appeared, I woke him up and we slid the raft into a hiding spot for the day.

The king pulled out an old, tattered deck of cards after breakfast, and he and the duke played seven-up for a while, at five cents a game. Then they grew tired of it and decided they would “plan a campaign,” as they called it. The duke reached into his carpetbag, pulled out a stack of small printed flyers, and read them aloud. One flyer stated that “the celebrated Dr. Armand de Montalban, of Paris,” would “lecture on the Science of Phrenology” at a certain place on a certain date, with a ten-cent admission fee, and would “provide character charts for twenty-five cents each.” The duke claimed that was him. In another flyer, he was the “world-renowned Shakespearean tragedian, Garrick the Younger, of Drury Lane, London.” In other flyers, he used various names and claimed to do other amazing things, like finding water and gold with a “divining rod,” “breaking witch spells,” and so on. Eventually, he said:

“But the acting profession is the best. Have you ever performed on stage, Your Majesty?”

“No,” the king replied.

“You will, then, before three days have passed, your fallen highness,” the duke said. “The first decent town we reach, we will rent a hall and perform the sword fight from Richard III and the balcony scene from Romeo and Juliet. How does that sound to you?”

“I am all in for anything that pays, Bilgewater; but, you see, I don’t know anything about acting, and I haven’t seen much of it. I was too young when my father used to have them at the palace. Do you think you can teach me?”

“Easily!”

“All right. I am just dying for something new, anyway. Let’s start right now.”

So the duke explained everything about who Romeo and Juliet were, and said he was used to playing Romeo, so the king could be Juliet.

“But if Juliet is such a young girl, duke, my bald head and white whiskers are going to look unusually strange on her, perhaps.”

“No, don’t you worry; these country bumpkins won’t ever think of that. Besides, you know, you will be in costume, and that makes all the difference in the world. Juliet is on a balcony, enjoying the moonlight before she goes to bed, and she is wearing her nightgown and a ruffled nightcap. Here are the costumes for the parts.”

He pulled out two or three suits made of curtain calico, which he said were medieval armor for Richard III and the other character, along with a long white cotton nightshirt and a matching ruffled nightcap. The king was satisfied, so the duke took out his book and read the parts in a most dramatic, grandiloquent style, prancing around and acting at the same time to demonstrate how it had to be done. Then he gave the book to the king and told him to memorize his lines.

There was a small, insignificant town about three miles down the river bend, and after dinner, the duke said he had figured out his plan for how to travel in daylight without it being dangerous for Jim; so he decided he would go down to the town and arrange it.

Jim laughed until he was nearly exhausted. He was always the easiest person to make laugh that I had ever known.

There was a small, quiet town about three miles down the river. After dinner, the duke said he had figured out a way to travel during the day without it being dangerous for Jim, so he decided to go to the town and arrange it.

The inhabitants had vacated those structures. The riverbank had eroded beneath the corner of several others, leaving that section suspended in mid-air. People still occupied some, though it was perilous, as occasionally a section of earth as broad as a house would collapse at once. Sometimes a strip of land a quarter-mile wide begins to crumble, sliding bit by bit into the river throughout a single summer. Such a settlement must constantly retreat further inland, as the current is perpetually consuming it.

As the day approached noon, the streets grew increasingly crowded with horses and wagons, with more arriving continuously. Families brought their midday meals from the countryside, consuming them within their carriages. There was a fair amount of alcohol consumption, and I witnessed three separate brawls. Eventually, someone shouted:

“Look, here is old Boggs! He has arrived from the country for his customary monthly spree; here he comes, fellows!”

All the idlers appeared pleased; I assumed they were accustomed to deriving amusement from Boggs. One remarked:

“I wonder whom he intends to devour this time. If he had actually consumed every man he has threatened to eat over the past two decades, he would possess quite a reputation by now.”

Another added, “I wish old Boggs would threaten me, because then I would know I was not destined to perish for a thousand years.”

Boggs arrived galloping on his horse, hollering and shrieking like a savage, calling out:

“Clear the way, there. I am on the warpath, and the cost of burial caskets is about to increase.”

He was intoxicated, swaying in his saddle; he was past fifty, with a deeply flushed face. Everyone cheered, mocked, and taunted him, and he retorted, claiming he would deal with them and dispatch them in their proper order, but he could not delay because he had come to town to slay old Colonel Sherburn, and his philosophy was, “Main course first, and soft food for dessert.”

He spotted me, rode closer, and asked:

“Where did you arrive from, lad? Are you ready to meet your maker?”

Then he rode onward. I was frightened, but a man remarked:

“He signifies nothing; he always behaves in this manner when he is in his cups. He is the most good-natured old simpleton in Arkansas—he has never harmed a soul, whether intoxicated or sober.”

Boggs pulled up before the largest shop in the village, lowered his head to peer beneath the awning’s edge, and bellowed:

“Step outside, Sherburn! Come out and confront the man you have cheated. You are the cur I am hunting, and I intend to catch you, too!”

He continued in this fashion, hurling every insult he could muster at Sherburn, while the entire street was jammed with onlookers who were listening, chuckling, and carrying on. Presently, a dignified-looking gentleman of about fifty-five—who was also by far the most elegantly dressed person in the town—emerged from the shop, and the throng parted on either side to allow him passage. He addressed Boggs, very composed and deliberate—he stated:

“I am weary of this, but I shall tolerate it until one o’clock.”

The residents had moved out of those buildings. The riverbank had eroded beneath the corner of several others, leaving them hanging over the edge. People still lived in some, but it was hazardous, as a strip of land as wide as a house could collapse at any moment. Occasionally, a section of land a quarter-mile deep would begin to crumble, eroding steadily until the entire piece fell into the river over the course of a single summer. A town like that had to keep moving further inland because the river was constantly eating away at it.

As noon approached that day, the streets became increasingly crowded with wagons and horses, with more arriving constantly. Families brought their lunches from the countryside and ate them in their wagons. There was a fair amount of drinking, and I witnessed three fights. Eventually, someone shouted:

“Here comes old Boggs! He’s in from the country for his monthly bender; here he comes, boys!”

All the idlers looked pleased; I figured they were accustomed to getting entertainment out of Boggs. One of them remarked:

“I wonder who he’s going to tear into this time. If he’d actually fought all the men he’s threatened to fight over the last twenty years, he’d have quite a reputation by now.”

Another added, “I wish old Boggs would threaten me, because then I’d know I wasn’t going to die for a thousand years.”

Boggs came charging along on his horse, whooping and yelling like a wild man, shouting:

“Clear the way! I’m on the warpath, and the price of coffins is about to go up.”

He was intoxicated and swaying in his saddle; he was over fifty years old and had a very red face. Everyone yelled at him, laughed at him, and taunted him, and he taunted them back, claiming he would deal with them in due time, but he couldn’t wait now because he had come to town to kill Colonel Sherburn, and his motto was, “Meat first, and dessert to finish.”

He saw me, rode up, and asked:

“Where did you come from, boy? Are you prepared to die?”

Then he rode on. I was frightened, but a man said:

“He doesn’t mean anything by it; he always carries on like that when he’s drunk. He’s the best-natured old fool in Arkansas—he’s never hurt anyone, drunk or sober.”

Boggs rode up to the largest store in town, bent his head to see under the awning, and yelled:

“Come out here, Sherburn! Come out and face the man you’ve swindled. You’re the dog I’m after, and I’m going to get you, too!”

He continued, calling Sherburn every insult he could think of, while the entire street was packed with people listening, laughing, and carrying on. Eventually, a proud-looking man of about fifty-five—who was also by far the best-dressed man in town—stepped out of the store, and the crowd stepped back on both sides to let him through. He spoke to Boggs, very calmly and slowly:

“I’m tired of this, but I’ll put up with it until one o’clock.”

The people had moved out of those houses. The riverbank had eroded under the corner of some others, leaving that corner hanging over the water. A town like that has to keep moving further back because the river is always gnawing away at it.

He kept going, calling Sherburn every name he could think of, while the whole street was packed with people listening, laughing, and carrying on. He said to Boggs, very calmly and slowly:

“I’m tired of this, but I’ll put up with it until one o’clock.”

The crowd started jeering and mocking him, which infuriated the man, causing him to lash out. This agitated the spectators, and many men climbed down from their seats, rushing toward the ring while shouting, “Knock him down! Throw him out!” A few women began to shriek. The ringmaster then delivered a brief address, expressing his hope that there would be no further disruption, and promised that if the man pledged to behave, he would be allowed to ride, provided he could maintain his seat. The audience laughed and agreed, so the man mounted. The moment he was on, the horse began to buck, leap, and prance, with two circus workers clutching the bridle to restrain it, while the intoxicated man clung to its neck, his heels kicking into the air with every bound. The entire crowd stood up, cheering and laughing until they were weeping. Eventually, despite the best efforts of the circus staff, the horse broke free and bolted, racing around the ring with the drunkard sprawled across its neck, his legs dangling near the ground on alternating sides, while the people went wild. I did not find it amusing, however; I was shaking with fear for his safety. Soon, he managed to pull himself upright, grabbed the reins, and swayed back and forth. A moment later, he leaped to his feet, released the bridle, and stood tall while the horse galloped at full speed. He stood there, circling the ring as composed and steady as if he had never been intoxicated in his life, and then began stripping off his garments, tossing them aside. He shed them so rapidly they seemed to fill the air, eventually discarding seventeen layers. Beneath them, he appeared slender and striking, wearing the most vibrant and beautiful costume imaginable. He struck the horse with his whip, making it race even faster, then leaped off, bowed, and danced toward the dressing room, leaving the audience roaring with delight and amazement.

The ringmaster realized he had been deceived, and I believe he was the most miserable man I have ever seen. It turned out to be one of his own performers! He had devised the entire prank himself without telling a soul. I felt foolish for being tricked, but I would not have traded places with that ringmaster for a thousand dollars. I cannot say for sure, but while there may be superior circuses, I have yet to encounter one. Regardless, it was perfectly satisfactory for me, and I will patronize it whenever I happen upon it.

I did not need to be told twice to go for a steamboat trip. I reached the bank a half-mile above the village, then moved quickly along the bluff in the calm water. Soon, we encountered a pleasant, guileless-looking young country fellow sitting on a log, wiping perspiration from his forehead, as the weather was extremely hot; he had two large carpet-bags beside him.

“Run her nose into the bank,” the king commanded. I obeyed. “Where are you headed, young man?”

“To the steamboat; I am going to Orleans.”

“Come aboard,” the king said. “Wait a moment, my servant will assist you with those bags. Jump out and help the gentleman, Adolphus”—referring to me, I realized.

I did so, and the three of us continued on. The young man was very grateful; he remarked that it was difficult labor carrying his luggage in such heat. He inquired where the king was traveling, and the king replied that he had come down the river and arrived at the other village that morning, and was now heading a few miles up to visit an old acquaintance on a farm. The young man remarked:

“When I first saw you, I thought to myself, ‘That is surely Mr. Wilks, and he nearly arrived in time.’ But then I thought again, ‘No, I suppose it is not him, or he would not be traveling up the river.’ You are not him, are you?”

“No, my name is Blodgett—Alexander Blodgett—Reverend Alexander Blodgett, I suppose I must say, as I am one of the Lord’s humble servants. But I am just as capable of feeling sorry for Mr. Wilks for not arriving in time, if he has lost anything by it—which I hope he has not.”

“Well, he does not lose any property by it, because he will receive that just fine; but he missed seeing his brother Peter pass away—which he might not mind, no one can say—but his brother would have given anything in this world to see him before he died; he spoke of nothing else for these three weeks; he had not seen him since they were boys together—and had never seen his brother William at all—that is the deaf and mute one—William is no more than thirty or thirty-five. Peter and George were the only ones who came out here; George was the married brother; he and his wife both passed away last year. Harvey and William are the only ones remaining now; and, as I was saying, they did not arrive in time.”

“Did anyone notify them?”

“Oh, yes; a month or two ago, when Peter first fell ill; because Peter said then that he felt as though he might not recover this time. You see, he was quite elderly, and George’s girls were too young to be much company for him, except Mary Jane, the red-headed one; and so he was rather lonely after George and his wife died, and did not seem to care much about living. He desperately wanted to see Harvey—and William, too, for that matter—because he was the type of person who cannot bear to write a will.”

Soon, we came to a nice, innocent-looking young country fellow sitting on a log, wiping sweat from his face, for the weather was extremely hot. He had two large carpet-bags beside him.

“To the steamboat; I am going to New Orleans.”

“Get aboard,” the king said. “Wait a minute, my servant will help you with those bags. Jump out and help the gentleman, Adolphus”—meaning me, I realized.

I did so, and then all three of us started off again. The young man was very grateful; he said it was hard work carrying his luggage in such weather. “No, my name is Blodgett—Alexander Blodgett—Reverend Alexander Blodgett, I suppose I should say, as I am one of the Lord’s humble servants. But I am still just as sorry for Mr. Wilks for not arriving in time, if he has missed anything by it—which I hope he has not.”

I went to bed fully dressed, yet sleep was impossible because I was so anxious to finish my task. Eventually, I heard the king and the duke arrive, so I slid off my mattress and rested my chin on the ladder’s edge, watching for any activity. Nothing occurred.

I waited until the night noises ceased and the morning sounds had not yet started, then descended the ladder.

I crept toward their rooms and listened; they were snoring. I moved quietly downstairs without incident. Everything was silent. Peering through a gap in the dining-room door, I saw the men guarding the body fast asleep in their chairs. The door to the parlor where the corpse lay was open, and candles burned in both rooms. I walked past, noting the parlor was empty except for Peter’s remains, but the front door was locked and the key was missing. Suddenly, I heard footsteps behind me on the stairs. I dashed into the parlor and scanned the room, realizing the coffin was the only place to hide the bag. The lid was pushed aside by about a foot, revealing the dead man’s face beneath a damp cloth and his burial shroud. I shoved the money-bag under the lid, just past his crossed hands—which made me shudder, they were so cold—and then I hurried across the room to hide behind the door.

Mary Jane was the one approaching. She walked to the coffin, knelt down gently, and looked inside. She raised her handkerchief and began to weep, though I could not hear her, as her back was turned toward me. I slipped away, checking the dining-room crack as I passed to ensure the guards had not noticed me; they remained undisturbed.

I went back to bed, feeling rather discouraged that things had turned out this way after all my effort and risk. I reasoned that if the money stayed put, it might be fine; once we were a hundred or two miles downriver, I could write to Mary Jane so she could retrieve it. However, that was unlikely; the money would surely be discovered when they fastened the lid. Then the king would reclaim it, and it would be a long time before anyone else had a chance to steal it from him. Naturally, I wanted to go back and retrieve it, but I dared not. It was growing later by the minute, and the watchers would soon stir, risking my capture with six thousand dollars in my possession that I had no authority to hold.

I went to bed with all my clothes on, but I couldn’t have slept even if I wanted to; I was too anxious to finish the job. I realized I had spoken too impulsively and revealed too much, landing myself in a difficult spot. Then I spoke:

“Miss Mary Jane, is there somewhere outside of town where you could stay for three or four days?”

“Yes, at Mr. Lothrop’s. Why?”

“Don’t worry about the reason yet. If I can explain how I know the enslaved people will reunite within two weeks—right here in this house—and provide proof, will you go to Mr. Lothrop’s and remain for four days?”

“Four days!” she exclaimed. “I would stay for a year!”

“Very well,” I replied. “I don’t need anything more from you than your promise; I would prefer it over any other man’s sworn oath.” She smiled and blushed charmingly, and I added, “If you don’t mind, I’m going to close the door and bolt it.”

I returned to my seat and continued:

“Don’t scream. Just sit quietly and take this like a soldier. I must be honest, and you need to steel yourself, Miss Mary, because it is unpleasant news and difficult to hear, but there is no way around it. Well, they conducted the auction in the public square, toward the end of the afternoon, and it dragged on and on, and the old man was present and looking his most venomous, standing there beside the auctioneer, and interjecting a bit of Scripture now and then, or some pious remark of some sort, and the duke was nearby acting pathetic for sympathy as best he knew how, and generally making a spectacle of himself.

But eventually the affair concluded, and everything was sold—everything except a small, insignificant plot in the graveyard. So they had to dispose of that—I never saw such a glutton as the king was for wanting to swallow everything. Well, while they were at it, a steamboat arrived, and in about two minutes a crowd came up whooping and yelling and laughing and carrying on, and shouting out:

“Here is your opposition line! Here are your two sets of heirs to old Peter Wilks—and you pay your money and you take your choice!”

“All right,” they said, and left to wait for their uncles, and give them the love and the kisses, and tell them the message.

Everything was fine now. The girls wouldn’t say anything because they wanted to go to England; and the king and the duke would rather Mary Jane was off working for the auction than around in reach of Doctor Robinson. I felt very good; I figured I had done it pretty neatly—I reckoned Tom Sawyer couldn’t have done it any neater himself. Of course he would have added more style to it, but I can’t do that very easily, not being raised to it.

Well, they held the auction in the public square, toward the end of the afternoon, and it dragged on and on, and the old man was there looking his absolute worst, up there beside the auctioneer, and adding a little Scripture now and then, or a little pious saying of some kind, and the duke was around acting pathetic for sympathy as much as he knew how, and just showing off generally.

But eventually the thing finished, and everything was sold—everything but a little old worthless lot in the graveyard. So they had to work that off—I never saw such a glutton as the king was for wanting to swallow everything. Well, while they were at it a steamboat landed, and in about two minutes up came a crowd whooping and yelling and laughing and carrying on, and shouting:

“Here’s your opposition line! Here’s your two sets of heirs to old Peter Wilks—you pay your money and you take your choice!”

They were bringing a very nice-looking old gentleman along, and a nice-looking younger one, with his right arm in a sling.

“Can’t you see that they would go and tell? There is no way except to not tell anybody at all.”

“Well, maybe you are right—yes, I think you are right.”

“But I suppose we ought to tell Uncle Harvey that she has gone out for a while, so he won’t be worried about her?”

“Yes, Miss Mary Jane wanted you to do that. She says, ‘Tell them to give Uncle Harvey and William my love and a kiss, and say I have run across the river to see Mr.’—Mr.—what is the name of that rich family your uncle Peter used to think so much of?—I mean the one that—”

“Why, you must mean the Apthorps, right?”

“Of course; bother those kinds of names, a person can never seem to remember them half the time. Yes, she said to say she has gone over to ask the Apthorps to be sure and come to the auction and buy this house, because she thought her uncle Peter would rather they had it than anyone else; and she is going to stay with them until they promise to come, and then, if she isn’t too tired, she is coming home; and if she is, she will be home in the morning anyway. She said not to say anything about the Proctors, but only about the Apthorps—which will be perfectly true, because she is going there to speak about them buying the house; I know it, because she told me so herself.”

“All right,” they said, and left to wait for their uncles, to give them the love and the kisses, and tell them the message.

Everything was fine now. The girls wouldn’t say anything because they wanted to go to England; and the king and the duke would rather Mary Jane was off working for the auction than around where Doctor Robinson could reach her. I felt very good; I thought I had handled it quite neatly—I figured Tom Sawyer couldn’t have done it any neater himself. Of course, he would have added more style to it, but I can’t do that very easily, not being raised to it.

Well, they held the auction in the public square toward the end of the afternoon. It dragged on and on, and the old man was there looking his most poisonous, standing up there beside the auctioneer, adding a little Scripture now and then, or some goody-goody saying, and the duke was around acting pathetic to get sympathy, just showing off in general.

But eventually the thing finished, and everything was sold—everything except for a small, worthless plot in the graveyard. So they had to work that off—I never saw such a greedy person as the king was for wanting to swallow everything. Well, while they were at it, a steamboat landed, and in about two minutes a crowd came up whooping and yelling and laughing and carrying on, shouting:

“Here is your opposition line! Here are your two sets of heirs to old Peter Wilks—you pay your money and you take your choice!”

They were bringing a very nice-looking old gentleman along, and a nice-looking younger one, with his right arm in a sling.

Upon reaching the town, I noticed the storm had cleared the streets, so I walked directly down the main thoroughfare instead of seeking side paths. As I neared our residence, I stared intently at it. The building was completely dark, which left me feeling unexpectedly sad and let down. However, just as I was passing, a light suddenly flickered in Mary Jane’s window. My heart surged with such intensity it felt as though it might burst, and in that same instant, the house vanished into the darkness behind me, never to be seen again. She was truly the finest and bravest girl I had ever known.

Once I had traveled far enough past the town to spot the tow-head, I began searching intently for a boat to take. When a flash of lightning revealed one that was not chained, I grabbed it and pushed off. It was merely a canoe tied with a simple rope. The tow-head was quite a distance away, situated in the middle of the river, but I hurried toward it. By the time I reached the raft, I was so exhausted that I would have collapsed to catch my breath if I had the time. Instead, I jumped aboard and shouted, “Get moving, Jim, and cast us off! Thank goodness, we are finally rid of them!”

Jim rushed toward me with his arms wide, overflowing with happiness. However, when I caught sight of him during a lightning flash, I was so startled by his appearance—dressed as King Lear and a drowned Arab—that I fell backward into the water. The sight nearly terrified me to death. Jim pulled me back onto the raft and tried to embrace me in his relief that I had returned and that we were free of the king and the duke, but I interrupted him: “Not now; save it for breakfast! Just cut the line and let us go!”

Within moments, we were drifting down the river, and it felt wonderful to be alone and liberated on the water once more. I felt so relieved that I began jumping around and clicking my heels together. Yet, after a few moments, I heard a familiar sound. I held my breath to listen, and when the next flash of lightning illuminated the river, I saw them approaching, rowing their skiff with great speed. It was the king and the duke. I collapsed onto the wooden planks, defeated, and struggled to hold back tears.

When they climbed onto the raft, the king grabbed me by the collar and demanded, “Trying to run away from us, were you, you little rascal? Tired of our company, are you?” I replied, “No, your majesty, we were not—please, your majesty, don’t!”

When I reached town, I saw no one out in the storm, so I didn't bother with back alleys but walked straight down the main street. As I neared our house, I stared at it. No light; the place was completely dark, which left me feeling strangely sad and disappointed. But just as I was passing by, a light flashed in Mary Jane’s window. My heart suddenly swelled, nearly bursting. A second later, the house was behind me in the dark, never to be seen by me again in this life. She was the finest girl I had ever met, and the bravest.

Once I was far enough past town to reach the sandbar, I started looking for a boat to borrow. As soon as the lightning revealed one that wasn't chained, I grabbed it and pushed off. It was a canoe, tied only with a rope. The sandbar was a long way off, out in the middle of the river, but I didn't waste any time. When I finally reached the raft, I was so exhausted I would have just collapsed to catch my breath if I could have afforded to. But I didn't. As I jumped aboard, I shouted, "Get out here, Jim, and cut her loose! Thank goodness, we’re finally rid of them!"

Jim rushed out, arms wide, so full of joy; but when I caught sight of him in the lightning, my heart jumped into my throat and I fell backward overboard. I had forgotten he was dressed as old King Lear and a drowned Arab, and it scared the life out of me. Jim pulled me out, ready to hug and bless me, so happy that I was back and that we were free of the king and the duke. But I said, "Not now; save it for breakfast! Cut the line and let her go!"

Within two seconds, we were sliding down the river. It felt so good to be free again, all by ourselves on the wide river with no one to bother us. I had to move around, jumping up and clicking my heels together—I couldn't help it. But around the third jump, I heard a sound I knew all too well. I held my breath, listening and waiting. Sure enough, when the next flash of lightning broke over the water, there they were, rowing hard and making their skiff fly. It was the king and the duke.

I collapsed onto the planks, defeated; it was all I could do to keep from crying.

CHAPTER XXX.

When they climbed aboard, the king grabbed me by the collar and said, "Trying to ditch us, were you, you pup? Tired of our company, eh?"

I replied, "No, your majesty, we weren't—please don't, your majesty!"

When I reached the town, I saw that nobody was out in the storm, so I did not look for any back streets but walked straight through the main one. As I approached our house, I stared at it. There was no light; the house was completely dark, which made me feel sad and disappointed, though I did not know why. But finally, just as I was passing by, a light flashed in Mary Jane’s window. My heart swelled up suddenly, as if it might burst. In that same second, the house and everything else were behind me in the dark, never to be in front of me again in this world. She was the best girl I had ever seen and had the most courage.

The minute I was far enough past the town to see that I could reach the sandbar, I began to look closely for a boat to borrow. The first time the lightning showed me one that was not chained, I grabbed it and pushed off. It was a canoe, and it was only tied with a rope. The sandbar was a long way off, way out there in the middle of the river, but I did not waste any time. When I finally reached the raft, I was so exhausted that I would have just laid down to rest and catch my breath if I could have afforded to. But I did not. As I jumped aboard, I shouted:

“Get out here, Jim, and set her loose! Thank goodness, we are rid of them!”

Jim came out and was running toward me with both arms spread, he was so full of joy. But when I saw him in the lightning, my heart jumped into my throat and I fell overboard backward. I had forgotten that he was dressed as old King Lear and a drowned Arab all in one, and it nearly scared the life out of me. But Jim pulled me out and was going to hug me and bless me, he was so glad I was back and that we were rid of the king and the duke, but I said:

“Not now; save it for breakfast, save it for breakfast! Cut the rope and let her go!”

So, in two seconds, we were sliding down the river. It felt so good to be free again and all by ourselves on the big river, with nobody to bother us. I had to move around a bit and jump up and click my heels together a few times—I could not help it. But around the third jump, I noticed a sound that I knew very well. I held my breath, listened, and waited. Sure enough, when the next flash of lightning broke over the water, there they came! They were rowing hard and making their small boat move fast. It was the king and the duke.

I collapsed right down onto the planks and gave up; it was all I could do to keep from crying.

CHAPTER XXX.

When they got aboard, the king came for me, shook me by the collar, and said:

“Trying to give us the slip, were you, you pup! Tired of our company, hey?”

I said:

“No, your majesty, we were not—please do not, your majesty!”

I once reasoned that it would be far better for Jim to remain a slave back home with his family, provided he had to be a slave at all, and I considered writing to Tom Sawyer to have him inform Miss Watson of Jim’s location. I quickly abandoned that idea for two reasons: she would be furious and repulsed by his perceived treachery and lack of gratitude for running away, leading her to sell him down the river immediately. Even if she didn't, people naturally look down on an ungrateful slave, and they would make Jim suffer for it, leaving him feeling degraded and ashamed. Furthermore, consider my own position! It would become common knowledge that Huck Finn assisted a slave in escaping, and if I ever encountered anyone from that town again, I would be so humiliated I would be ready to grovel at their feet. That is how it goes: a person commits a shameful act and then wishes to avoid the repercussions. They believe that as long as the deed remains secret, it carries no disgrace. That was precisely my situation. The more I pondered this, the more my conscience tormented me, and the more wicked and base I felt. Finally, when it struck me that this was clearly the hand of Providence striking me, signaling that my sins were being observed from heaven while I was stealing a poor woman’s slave who had never harmed me, and that there is a Higher Power watching who will not permit such wretched behavior to continue indefinitely, I was so terrified I nearly collapsed. I attempted to justify my actions by claiming I had been raised to be wicked and therefore was not entirely at fault, but an inner voice countered, “You had the opportunity to attend Sunday school; had you gone, you would have been taught that those who behave as you have regarding that slave are destined for eternal fire.”

It caused me to tremble. I nearly resolved to pray, hoping to cease being the boy I was and become a better person. I knelt down, yet the words failed to materialize. Why? It was futile to attempt to conceal the truth from Him, or from myself. I understood perfectly why the words would not come. My heart was not in the right place; I was not being honest; I was acting hypocritically. I pretended to renounce my sins, yet deep within, I clung to the greatest one of all. I tried to force my lips to declare that I would do the right thing and write to the slave’s owner to disclose his whereabouts, but in my heart, I knew it was a falsehood, and He knew it as well. You cannot offer a prayer that is a lie—I discovered that for myself.

I once told myself it would be a thousand times better for Jim to be a slave back home with his family, since he had to be a slave anyway, and that I should write to Tom Sawyer and have him tell Miss Watson where Jim was. But I quickly abandoned that idea for two reasons: she would be furious and disgusted at his dishonesty and ingratitude for running away, and she would immediately sell him down the river again. Even if she didn't, everyone naturally looks down on an ungrateful Black man, and they would make Jim feel it constantly, leaving him feeling degraded and ashamed. And then, think of me! It would get around that Huck Finn helped a Black man gain his freedom; if I ever saw anyone from that town again, I would be so ashamed I would want to crawl on the ground. That is how it goes: a person does something shameful, and then they do not want to face the consequences. They think as long as they can hide it, it is not a disgrace. That was exactly my situation. The more I thought about it, the more my conscience gnawed at me, and the more wicked and low I felt. Finally, when it suddenly struck me that this was the clear hand of Providence slapping me in the face, letting me know my wickedness was being watched from heaven while I was stealing a poor woman’s property—someone who had never harmed me—and that there is a God who is always watching and will not allow such miserable behavior to go beyond a certain point, I nearly collapsed from fear. I tried my best to justify it by saying I was raised to be wicked and was not entirely to blame, but something inside kept saying, “You could have gone to Sunday school; if you had, they would have taught you that people who act the way you have been acting regarding that man go to eternal fire.”

It made me shiver. I almost decided to pray and see if I could stop being the kind of boy I was and become better. So, I knelt down. But the words would not come. Why wouldn't they? It was no use trying to hide it from Him, or from myself. I knew very well why they would not come. It was because my heart was not right; it was because I was not being honest; it was because I was being two-faced. I was pretending to give up sin, but deep inside I was holding onto the biggest one of all. I was trying to make my mouth say I would do the right thing and go write to that man’s owner to tell her where he was, but deep down I knew it was a lie, and He knew it too. You cannot pray a lie—I found that out.

I once told myself it would be much better for Jim to be a slave back home with his family, since he had to be a slave anyway, and that I should write to Tom Sawyer to tell Miss Watson where Jim was. I soon gave up that idea for two reasons: she would be angry and disgusted by his ungratefulness for running away, so she would sell him down the river immediately. Even if she didn't, everyone naturally looks down on an ungrateful runaway, and they would make Jim feel it constantly, leaving him feeling disgraced. Then I thought of myself. It would get around that Huck Finn helped a slave gain his freedom, and if I ever saw anyone from that town again, I would be so ashamed I would want to crawl on the ground. That is how it goes: a person does something wrong and then refuses to face the consequences, thinking that as long as he can hide it, it is not shameful. That was exactly my situation. The more I thought about it, the more my conscience bothered me, and the more wicked and low I felt. Finally, it hit me that this was the hand of God punishing me, letting me know my wickedness was being watched from heaven. I was stealing a poor woman’s slave who had never harmed me, and God was showing me that He is always watching and will not allow such miserable behavior to continue. I was so scared I nearly collapsed. I tried to excuse myself by saying I was raised to be wicked and was not entirely to blame, but something inside me kept saying that I could have gone to Sunday school, where they would have taught me that people who act as I had been acting go to hell.

It made me shiver. I decided to pray and try to stop being the kind of boy I was and become better. I knelt down, but the words would not come. Why? It was no use trying to hide the truth from God or from myself. I knew exactly why they would not come. My heart was not right; I was not being honest; I was being two-faced. I was pretending to give up sin, but deep down I was holding onto the biggest sin of all. I tried to make my mouth say I would do the right thing and write to the slave’s owner to tell them where he was, but deep down I knew it was a lie, and God knew it too. You cannot pray a lie—I found that out.

However, their joy was nothing compared to mine; I felt as though I had been reborn, so relieved was I to discover my identity. They clung to me for two hours, and eventually, when my jaw was so exhausted it could barely move, I had recounted more tales about my family—the Sawyer family—than could have possibly occurred to any six such families combined. I described in detail how we had blown a cylinder-head near the mouth of the White River, requiring three days for repairs. This fabrication served me perfectly, as they had no reason to doubt that such a repair would take that long. I could have just as easily claimed it was a bolthead, and it would have been just as effective.

I felt quite at ease on one side, yet deeply uneasy on the other. Pretending to be Tom Sawyer was simple enough until I heard a steamboat chugging down the river. I worried that if Tom Sawyer arrived on that vessel and walked in, he might shout my name before I could signal him to remain silent. That scenario was impossible; I had to intercept him. I informed the family that I intended to head into town to retrieve my luggage. The old man offered to accompany me, but I declined, insisting I could manage the horse alone and preferred he not trouble himself.

I set off toward town in the wagon, and halfway there, I spotted another vehicle approaching. It was indeed Tom Sawyer, so I pulled over and waited. I called out for him to stop, and as he pulled alongside, his mouth dropped open in shock. He gulped several times, as if his throat were parched, and asked why I had returned to haunt him when he had never done me any wrong.

I assured him I had not returned because I had never left. Hearing my voice calmed him slightly, though he remained skeptical. He warned me not to trick him and asked, in all sincerity, if I was truly not a ghost. I gave him my word that I was not.

He admitted that should settle it, yet he still could not grasp the situation. He asked if I had ever been murdered at all. I explained that I had not, but had merely staged the event, inviting him to touch me to verify my existence. He did so, and upon being convinced, he was overjoyed to see me. He immediately wanted the full story, as the mystery and adventure appealed to his nature. I suggested we wait, instructed his driver to hold, and we moved a short distance away so I could explain my predicament and ask for his advice.

But if they were joyful, it was nothing compared to how I felt; it was like being born again, I was so relieved to know who I was. They stuck to me for two hours, and by the time my jaw was so tired it could barely move, I had told them more about my family—I mean the Sawyer family—than had ever happened to any six Sawyer families combined. I explained all about how we blew a cylinder-head at the mouth of the White River, and it took us three days to repair it. That worked out perfectly, because they had no idea whether it should take three days or not. If I had called it a bolthead, it would have worked just as well.

I was feeling pretty comfortable on one side, but quite uneasy on the other. Being Tom Sawyer was easy, but that feeling lasted only until I heard a steamboat chugging down the river. I thought to myself, what if Tom Sawyer is on that boat? What if he walks in here any minute and calls out my name before I can signal him to keep quiet? I couldn’t let that happen; it would be a disaster. I had to head up the road and intercept him. I told the family I thought I would go into town to get my luggage. The old gentleman offered to go with me, but I said no, I could drive the horse myself, and I would rather he didn’t go to any trouble for me.

I started for town in the wagon, and halfway there I saw another wagon approaching. Sure enough, it was Tom Sawyer, so I stopped and waited for him to reach me. I yelled, “Hold on!” and he pulled up alongside. His mouth dropped open like a trunk and stayed that way. He swallowed a few times like someone with a dry throat, then said, “I’ve never done you any harm. You know that. So, why have you come back to haunt me?”

I replied, “I haven’t come back—I haven’t been gone.”

When he heard my voice, he seemed to recover a bit, but he still wasn’t entirely convinced. He said, “Don’t play any tricks on me, because I wouldn’t do that to you. Honestly, now, you aren’t a ghost?”

“Honestly, I’m not,” I said.

“Well—I—well, that should settle it, of course; but I just can’t seem to understand it. Look here, weren’t you murdered at all?”

“No. I wasn’t murdered at all—I faked it. Come over here and touch me if you don’t believe me.”

He did, and it satisfied him. He was so happy to see me again he didn’t know what to do. He wanted to know the whole story immediately, because it was a grand, mysterious adventure that really appealed to him. But I told him to let it go for now, instructed his driver to wait, and we drove a short distance away so I could explain the predicament I was in and ask what he thought we should do.

But if they were joyful, it was nothing compared to how I felt; it was like being born again, I was so happy to know who I was. They stuck to me for two hours, and finally, when my chin was so tired it could hardly move, I had told them more about my family—I mean the Sawyer family—than had ever happened to any six Sawyer families combined. I explained how we blew out a cylinder-head at the mouth of the White River, and it took us three days to fix it. That worked perfectly, because they had no idea how long it should take to fix. If I had called it a bolthead, it would have worked just as well.

I was feeling comfortable on one side, but quite uneasy on the other. Being Tom Sawyer was easy, but that feeling lasted only until I heard a steamboat coughing down the river. I thought to myself, what if the real Tom Sawyer is on that boat? What if he walks in here at any moment and calls out my name before I can signal him to stay quiet? That would be a disaster. I had to go up the road and intercept him. I told the family I thought I would go into town to fetch my luggage. The old gentleman wanted to come with me, but I said no, I could drive the horse myself, and I would rather he did not go to any trouble for me.

So I started for town in the wagon. Halfway there, I saw another wagon coming, and sure enough, it was Tom Sawyer. I stopped and waited until he reached me. I called out, “Hold on!” and he stopped alongside. His mouth dropped open like a trunk and stayed that way. He swallowed a few times like someone with a dry throat, then said, “I have never done you any harm. You know that. So why have you come back to haunt me?”

I said, “I haven’t come back—I haven’t been gone.”

When he heard my voice, he calmed down a little, but he still was not convinced. He said, “Don’t play any tricks on me, because I wouldn’t on you. Honestly, now, you aren’t a ghost?”

“Honestly, I am not,” I said.

“Well—I—well, that should settle it, of course; but I cannot seem to understand it at all. Look here, were you never murdered at all?”

“No. I was never murdered—I faked it. Come here and feel me if you do not believe me.”

He did, and it satisfied him. He was so glad to see me again he did not know what to do. He wanted to know the whole story immediately, because it was a grand, mysterious adventure that really excited him. But I told him to leave it for later. I told his driver to wait, and we drove off a short distance so I could explain the situation I was in and ask what he thought we should do.

If I owned a yellow dog as ignorant as a person’s conscience, I would poison it. It occupies more space than everything else inside a person, yet it serves no purpose whatsoever. Tom Sawyer holds the same opinion.

CHAPTER XXXIV.

We ceased our conversation and fell into deep thought. Eventually, Tom remarked:

“Listen here, Huck, what idiots we are for not realizing this sooner! I am certain I know where Jim is kept.”

“No! Where?”

“Inside that shed near the ash-hopper. Just consider this. When we were eating, didn’t you observe a black man enter with some food?”

“I did.”

“What did you assume the food was for?”

“For a dog.”

“I thought so too. Well, it was not for a dog.”

“Why not?”

“Because part of it was watermelon.”

“That is true—I noticed it. It is truly remarkable that I never considered a dog would not eat watermelon. It demonstrates how a person can look at something and yet fail to see it.”

“Well, the man unlocked the padlock upon entering, and he secured it again when he departed. He handed the uncle a key just as we finished our meal—the same key, I am sure. Watermelon indicates a man, the lock indicates a prisoner; and it is unlikely there are two prisoners on such a small farm, especially where the people are so kind and generous. Jim is the prisoner. Very well—I am pleased we discovered it using detective methods; I would not value any other approach. Now, you apply your mind to devise a scheme to liberate Jim, and I will formulate one as well; then we shall select the one we prefer.”

What an intellect for a mere boy to possess! If I possessed Tom Sawyer’s brain, I would not trade it to become a duke, a steamboat mate, a circus clown, or anything else I can imagine. I began to contemplate a strategy, though only to keep busy; I knew perfectly well where the superior plan would originate. Shortly, Tom asked:

“Are you ready?”

“Yes,” I replied.

“Very well—present it.”

“My plan is this,” I said. “We can easily verify if Jim is inside. Then, I will retrieve my canoe tomorrow night and bring my raft over from the island. Then, on the first dark night, we steal the key from the old man’s trousers after he retires, and depart down the river on the raft with Jim, hiding during the day and traveling at night, just as Jim and I used to do. Would that plan not succeed?”

“Succeed? Why, certainly it would succeed, like rats fighting. But it is far too simple; there is nothing to it. What is the value of a plan that requires no more effort than that? It is as weak as goose-milk. Why, Huck, it would cause no more excitement than breaking into a soap factory.”

I remained silent, as I expected nothing else; yet I knew quite well that whenever he revealed his own plan, it would be free of such flaws.

And it was. He explained his idea, and I immediately recognized it was fifteen times better than mine in terms of style, and it would make Jim just as free as my plan would, while perhaps getting us all killed in the process.

If I owned a yellow dog that didn’t have any more sense than a person’s conscience, I would poison him. It takes up more space than all the rest of a person’s insides, and yet it isn’t any good at all. Tom Sawyer says the same thing.

CHAPTER XXXIV.

We stopped talking and started thinking. After a while, Tom says:

“Look here, Huck, what fools we are for not thinking of this sooner! I bet I know where Jim is.”

“No! Where?”

“In that hut down by the ash hopper. Look here. When we were at dinner, didn’t you see a man go in there with some food?”

“Yes.”

“What did you think the food was for?”

“For a dog.”

“Me too. Well, it wasn’t for a dog.”

“Why?”

“Because part of it was watermelon.”

“So it was—I noticed it. Well, it’s amazing that I never thought about a dog not eating watermelon. It shows how a person can look at something and not really see it at the same time.”

“Well, the man unlocked the padlock when he went in, and he locked it again when he came out. He brought Uncle a key right around the time we got up from the table—the same key, I bet. Watermelon proves it’s a man, the lock proves he’s a prisoner; and it isn’t likely there are two prisoners on such a small plantation, especially where the people are all so kind and good. Jim is the prisoner. All right—I’m glad we figured it out detective-style; I wouldn’t care for any other way. Now you use your brain and come up with a plan to steal Jim, and I’ll come up with one too; then we’ll pick the one we like best.”

What a mind for a boy to have! If I had Tom Sawyer’s brain, I wouldn’t trade it to be a duke, or a steamboat mate, or a circus clown, or anything else I can think of. I started thinking of a plan, but only just to keep busy; I knew very well where the real plan was going to come from. Pretty soon, Tom says:

“Ready?”

“Yes,” I say.

“All right—let’s hear it.”

“My plan is this,” I say. “We can easily find out if it’s Jim in there. Then I’ll get my canoe tomorrow night and bring my raft over from the island. Then, the first dark night that comes, we steal the key out of the old man’s pants after he goes to bed, and head down the river on the raft with Jim, hiding during the day and traveling at night, the way Jim and I used to do before. Wouldn’t that plan work?”

“Work? Why, certainly it would work, like rats fighting. But it’s too darn simple; there’s nothing to it. What’s the point of a plan that isn’t any more trouble than that? It’s as weak as water. Why, Huck, it wouldn’t cause any more excitement than breaking into a soap factory.”

I didn’t say anything, because I wasn’t expecting anything different; but I knew perfectly well that whenever he got his plan ready, it wouldn’t have any of those flaws.

And it didn’t. He told me what it was, and I saw in a minute it was worth fifteen of mine for style, and would make Jim just as free a man as mine would, and maybe get us all killed besides.

If I owned a yellow dog that didn’t know any more than a person’s conscience does, I would poison him. It takes up more room than all the rest of a person’s insides, and yet it is no good at all. Tom Sawyer says the same thing.

CHAPTER XXXIV.

We stopped talking and started thinking. After a while, Tom said:

“Look here, Huck, what fools we are for not thinking of this sooner! I bet I know where Jim is.”

“No! Where?”

“In that hut down by the ash-hopper. Look here. When we were at dinner, didn’t you see a Black man go in there with some food?”

“Yes.”

“What did you think the food was for?”

“For a dog.”

“I thought so too. Well, it wasn’t for a dog.”

“Why?”

“Because part of it was watermelon.”

“So it was—I noticed it. It is amazing that I never thought about a dog not eating watermelon. It shows how a person can see something and not really see it at the same time.”

“Well, the man unlocked the padlock when he went in, and he locked it again when he came out. He brought Uncle a key around the time we got up from the table—the same key, I bet. Watermelon proves it is a man, and the lock proves he is a prisoner. It is unlikely there are two prisoners on such a small plantation, especially where the people are all so kind and good. Jim is the prisoner. All right—I am glad we figured it out like detectives; I wouldn’t care for any other way. Now, you use your mind and think of a plan to steal Jim, and I will think of one too; then we will choose the one we like best.”

What a mind for a boy to have! If I had Tom Sawyer’s brain, I wouldn’t trade it to be a duke, a steamboat captain, a circus clown, or anything else I can think of. I started thinking of a plan, but only just to have something to do; I knew very well where the real plan was going to come from. Pretty soon, Tom said:

“Ready?”

“Yes,” I said.

“All right—let’s hear it.”

“That has absolutely nothing to do with the matter, Huck Finn. All he must do is scratch words onto the plate and toss it away. You are not required to be able to decipher it. Why, half the time, you cannot read what a prisoner scrawls on a tin plate or anywhere else.”

He stopped abruptly, as we heard the breakfast horn sounding. Consequently, we hurried toward the house.

During the morning, I took a sheet and a white shirt from the clothesline; I located an old sack, placed them inside, and we went to gather fox-fire, adding that to the bag as well. I referred to it as borrowing, since that was the term my father always used; however, Tom insisted it wasn’t borrowing, but stealing. He explained that we were acting as prisoners, and prisoners do not concern themselves with how they obtain items as long as they get them, and no one faults them for it. Tom claimed it is not a crime for a prisoner to steal what is necessary for an escape; it is his right. Therefore, as long as we were playing the part of prisoners, we had every right to steal anything on the property that we might find useful for our liberation. He noted that if we were not prisoners, the situation would be quite different, and only a low, despicable person would steal when not in captivity. Thus, we decided we would steal anything that was convenient. Yet, he made a great deal of trouble later when I stole a watermelon from the slave patch and ate it; he forced me to give the slaves a dime without explaining why. Tom argued that he meant we could only steal what we required. Well, I replied, I required the watermelon. But he insisted I did not need it to escape from prison; that was the distinction. He said if I had wanted it to conceal a knife and smuggle it to Jim to slay the jailer, it would have been acceptable. So I dropped the subject, although I could see no benefit in pretending to be a prisoner if I had to sit and debate such fine, gold-leaf distinctions every time I saw an opportunity to snatch a watermelon.

Once that passed, we were fine again; the sudden shock had simply rattled us for a moment. Uncle Silas remarked:

“It is truly strange, I cannot fathom it. I am certain I removed it, because—”

“Because you are only wearing one. Just hear the man! I am aware you took it off, and I have better proof than your wandering mind, since I saw it on the clothesline myself yesterday. Regardless, it has vanished, and you will simply have to wear a red flannel one until I find the time to sew another. That will be the third I have produced in two years. One would assume you might learn to be more careful with them at your age.”

“I realize that, Sally, and I truly try. But it should not be entirely blamed on me, as I never see them or handle them except when I am wearing them; I do not believe I have ever lost one while it was on my back.”

“Well, it is not your fault if you have not; you would have if you could, I suppose. And the shirt is not the only thing missing, either. A spoon is gone; and that is not the end of it. There were ten, and now there are only nine. The calf might have taken the shirt, I suppose, but the calf certainly did not take the spoon.”

“Why, what else is missing, Sally?”

“Six candles are gone—that is what. The rats could have taken the candles, and I suspect they did; I am surprised they do not carry off the entire house, given how you always promise to plug their holes but never do; if they were not foolish, they would nest in your hair, Silas—you would never notice; but you cannot blame the spoon on the rats, of that I am certain.”

“Well, Sally, I am to blame, and I admit it; I have been negligent; but I will not let tomorrow pass without sealing those holes.”

“Oh, do not rush; next year will be soon enough. Matilda Angelina Araminta Phelps!”

“But the damage is already finished if the snake strikes me while I am testing him. Mars Tom, I am prepared to handle most things that are not unreasonable, but if you and Huck bring a rattlesnake in here for me to domesticate, I am going to depart, that is certain.”

“Very well, then, forget it, forget it, if you are so stubborn regarding the matter. We can obtain some garter snakes, and you can fasten some buttons to their tails, and pretend they are rattlesnakes, and I suppose that will have to suffice.”

“I can tolerate those, Mars Tom, but I swear I could manage without them, I tell you that. I never realized before that it was such a nuisance and hardship to be a prisoner.”

“Well, it is always that way when it is performed correctly. Do you have any rats in this vicinity?”

“No, sir, I have not observed any.”

However, Jim and I were conferring and reflecting. After a moment of thought, I spoke.

“Tell me, Jim.”

He replied:

“Well, then, here is how it appears to me, Huck. If it were him being liberated, and one of the boys were to be shot, would he declare, ‘Proceed and rescue me, never mind a physician to save this one?’ Is that like Master Tom Sawyer? Would he utter that? You can bet he would not! Well, then, is Jim going to say it? No, sir—I am not moving an inch from this spot without a doctor; not even if it takes forty years!”

I understood he possessed a noble heart, and I figured he would speak exactly as he did—so everything was settled, and I informed Tom I was departing to fetch a physician. He made quite a fuss, but Jim and I remained firm and would not yield; he attempted to crawl out and release the raft himself, but we prevented him. He then scolded us, yet it accomplished nothing.

When he observed me preparing the canoe, he remarked:

“Well, then, if you are determined to go, I will instruct you on how to proceed once you reach the town. Close the door, bind the doctor’s eyes securely, and force him to swear to absolute silence. Place a purse of gold in his hand, lead him through the back alleys and everywhere in the darkness, then bring him here by canoe, taking a circuitous route through the islands. Search him and confiscate his chalk, and do not return it until you have brought him back to the village, or he will mark this raft so he can locate it again. That is the standard procedure.”

I agreed, then departed, with Jim instructed to conceal himself in the woods upon the doctor’s arrival until he had left.

CHAPTER XLI.

The physician was an elderly man; a very pleasant, kind-appearing gentleman once I roused him. I explained that my brother and I were hunting on Spanish Island yesterday afternoon and had camped on a section of a raft we discovered. Around midnight, he must have kicked his firearm while dreaming, causing it to discharge and wound his leg. We requested that he travel there to treat it without mentioning a word to anyone, as we intended to return home this evening to surprise our family.

“Who are your people?” he inquired.

“The Phelpses, down that way.”

“Oh,” he said. After a moment, he added:

“How did you say he was shot?”

“He had a dream,” I answered, “and it shot him.”

“A peculiar dream,” he remarked.

He ignited his lantern, retrieved his saddlebags, and we set off. Upon seeing the canoe, however, he appeared uneasy—stating it was large enough for one, but did not seem particularly safe for two. I replied:

“Oh, you need not be afraid, sir; it carried the three of us easily enough.”

“Man the oars—man the oars!”

But Jim and I were consulting—and thinking. After we had thought for a minute, I said:

“Say it, Jim.”

So he said:

“Well, then, this is how it looks to me, Huck. If it were him being set free, and one of the boys got shot, would he say, ‘Go on and save me, never mind about a doctor to save this one?’ Is that like Master Tom Sawyer? Would he say that? You bet he wouldn’t! Well, then, is Jim going to say it? No, sir—I won’t budge a step out of this place without a doctor; not if it takes forty years!”

I knew he was a good man at heart, and I figured he would say exactly what he did say—so it was all right now, and I told Tom I was going for a doctor. He made a considerable fuss about it, but Jim and I stuck to our guns and wouldn’t budge; he even tried to crawl out and set the raft loose himself, but we wouldn’t let him. Then he gave us a piece of his mind, but it didn’t do any good.

So when he saw me getting the canoe ready, he said:

“Well, then, if you are determined to go, I’ll tell you how to handle it when you get to the village. Shut the door, blindfold the doctor tight, and make him swear to be silent as the grave. Put a purse full of gold in his hand, lead him all around the back alleys in the dark, and then bring him here in the canoe by a roundabout way through the islands. Search him and take his chalk away from him, and don’t give it back until you get him back to the village, or else he will mark this raft so he can find it again. That’s how they all do it.”

I agreed, and left, with instructions for Jim to hide in the woods when he saw the doctor coming until he had left again.
