As she departed, she gazed into my eyes with a gaze both constant and kind, remarking: “The door shall remain unlocked, Tom, and the window and the pole are accessible; yet you will behave, won’t you? You will not leave? Do it for my sake.” Heaven knows I desired to depart to check on Tom and fully intended to do so, but following that request, I would not have left for all the kingdoms on earth. Still, she and Tom occupied my thoughts, causing me to sleep fitfully. Twice during the night, I descended the pole and crept toward the front, observing her seated by her candle in the window, her gaze fixed upon the road and tears in her eyes. I longed to assist her, yet could do nothing but vow never to cause her further sorrow. Upon waking at dawn for a third time, I slid down to find her still there; her candle had nearly burned out, and her aged gray head rested upon her hand as she slumbered. CHAPTER XLII. The old man returned from town before breakfast, having found no trace of Tom. Both sat at the table in silence, looking somber, their coffee cooling as they neglected their meal. Eventually, the old man inquired: “Did I hand you the letter?” “What letter?” “The one I retrieved from the post office yesterday.” “No, you did not give me any letter.” “I must have forgotten it.” He searched his pockets, then retrieved it from where he had set it down and presented it to her. She remarked: “Why, it is from St. Petersburg—it is from Sis.” I thought a walk might benefit me, but I remained motionless. Before she could open the envelope, she dropped it and ran, having spotted something. I saw it too. It was Tom Sawyer on a mattress, accompanied by the old doctor, and Jim, wearing her calico dress with his hands bound behind him, followed by a crowd. I concealed the letter behind the nearest object and hurried forward. She threw herself toward Tom, weeping, and cried: “Oh, he is dead, he is dead, I know he is dead!” Tom turned his head slightly and mumbled something, indicating he was not in his right mind. She raised her hands and exclaimed: “He is alive, thank God! That is enough!” She kissed him quickly and rushed toward the house to prepare the bed, issuing rapid commands to the servants and everyone else as she moved. I trailed the men to see how they would handle Jim, while the doctor and Uncle Silas followed Tom inside. The men were quite irritable, and some suggested hanging Jim as a warning to other enslaved people, hoping to prevent them from attempting to escape like Jim had, which caused such immense trouble and kept the family in a state of terror for days.

As she was leaving, she looked into my eyes, steady and gentle, and said:

“The door won’t be locked, Tom, and there’s the window and the lightning rod; but you’ll be good, won’t you? And you won’t leave? For my sake.”

Heaven knows I wanted to go badly enough to check on Tom, and I had fully intended to; but after she said that, I wouldn’t have left, not for all the kingdoms on earth.

But she was on my mind and Tom was on my mind, so I slept very restlessly. Twice during the night I climbed down the rod and slipped around to the front, seeing her sitting there by her candle in the window, her eyes fixed on the road with tears in them. I wished I could do something for her, but I couldn’t, except to swear that I would never do anything to grieve her again. The third time I woke up at dawn and slid down, she was still there, her candle nearly out, her old gray head resting on her hand as she slept.

Admittedly, the knife was incapable of slicing anything, yet it was a genuine Barlow, and that fact held unimaginable magnificence—though why the boys of the West ever believed such a tool could be imitated to its detriment is a profound puzzle that will likely stay that way. Tom managed to scratch the cupboard with it and was preparing to work on the bureau when he was summoned to get ready for Sunday school. Mary handed him a tin bowl of water and a bar of soap, and he stepped outside to place the basin on a small bench; he then dipped the soap into the water, set it aside, rolled up his sleeves, poured the water onto the ground with care, and walked into the kitchen to scrub his face vigorously with the towel hanging behind the door. However, Mary took the towel away and remarked, "Aren't you ashamed, Tom? You shouldn't be so naughty. Water won't cause you any harm." Tom felt slightly rattled. The basin was filled again, and this time he hovered over it for a moment, summoning his courage; he took a deep breath and started. When he walked into the kitchen a moment later, eyes squeezed shut and hands feeling for the towel, his face was dripping with the respectable evidence of soap and water. Yet, upon stepping out from the towel, he still was not quite clean, as the scrubbed area ended abruptly at his chin and jawline like a mask; beneath that border lay a dark region of unwashed skin that extended down his chest and around the back of his neck. Mary took charge, and once she finished, he was a fellow human being, uniform in complexion, with his damp hair carefully combed and the short ringlets styled into a delicate, balanced look. [He secretly flattened the curls with great effort, plastering his hair against his scalp, as he considered curls to be womanly, and his own were a constant source of misery.] Mary then produced a set of clothes that had served only for Sundays over the past two years—they were simply known as his "other clothes," which gives us an idea of the extent of his wardrobe. The girl tidied him up once he had finished dressing; she fastened his tidy jacket to his chin, folded his large shirt collar over his shoulders, brushed him off, and topped him with his speckled straw hat. He appeared significantly better but felt quite uneasy. He was just as uncomfortable as he appeared, for the restriction of tidy, whole clothing irritated him. He prayed that Mary would overlook his shoes, but his wish was crushed; she covered them completely with tallow, as was the tradition, and presented them. He grew angry and complained that he was constantly forced to do things he disliked. But Mary said, in a coaxing tone, "Please, Tom—be a good boy." So he put on the shoes while grumbling. Mary was soon prepared, and the three children departed for Sunday school—a location Tom despised with all his might, though Sid and Mary enjoyed it. Sunday school lasted from nine until ten-thirty, followed by the church service.

He hoped Mary would forget his shoes, but that hope was crushed; she greased them thoroughly with tallow, as was the custom, and brought them out. So, he put on the shoes while grumbling. Mary was soon ready, and the three children left for Sunday school—a place Tom hated with all his heart, though Sid and Mary enjoyed it. Sunday school lasted from nine until ten-thirty, followed by the church service.

It was true that the knife could not cut anything, but it was a genuine Barlow, and there was incredible status in that—though why the boys in the West ever thought such a knife could be faked or ruined is a mystery that will likely always remain. Tom managed to scratch the cupboard with it and was planning to start on the dresser when he was called away to get ready for Sunday school.

Mary gave him a tin basin of water and a bar of soap. He went outside, set the basin on a small bench, dipped the soap in the water, and set it down. He rolled up his sleeves, poured the water onto the ground, and went back into the kitchen to wipe his face on the towel hanging behind the door. However, Mary took the towel away and said, "Aren't you ashamed, Tom? You shouldn't be so naughty. Water won't hurt you."

Tom felt a bit embarrassed. The basin was refilled, and this time he stood over it for a moment to gather his courage. He took a deep breath and began. When he walked into the kitchen a moment later, eyes shut and hands feeling for the towel, his face was dripping with soap and water. But when he finished drying off, he still was not clean enough; the clean area stopped at his chin and jaw like a mask. Below that line was a dark area of unwashed skin that spread down his chest and around his neck. Mary took over, and when she finished, he was clean, his wet hair was neatly brushed, and his short curls were styled into a tidy, symmetrical look. He secretly smoothed out the curls with great effort, plastering his hair flat against his head, because he thought curls were girly, and his own hair made him miserable. Then, Mary brought out his Sunday suit—the only other clothes he owned. The girl helped him finish getting ready; she buttoned his jacket to his chin, folded his large shirt collar over his shoulders, brushed him off, and put his speckled straw hat on his head. He looked much better but felt very uncomfortable. He was as miserable as he looked, because the restriction of clean, nice clothes bothered him. He hoped Mary would forget his shoes, but his hope was crushed; she covered them in tallow, as was the custom, and brought them out. He lost his temper and complained that he was always forced to do things he did not want to do. But Mary said kindly, "Please, Tom—be a good boy."

So, he put on the shoes while grumbling. Mary was soon ready, and the three children left for Sunday school—a place Tom hated with all his heart, though Sid and Mary enjoyed it. Sunday school lasted from nine until ten-thirty, followed by the church service.

And so it continued. There is no need to record the remainder of the address. It followed a standard, unchanging format, making it well-known to everyone present.

The final portion of the talk was disrupted by the return of scuffles and other diversions among the mischievous boys, along with fidgeting and murmurs that spread throughout the room, eventually reaching even the most steadfast and well-behaved students like Sid and Mary. However, every noise vanished abruptly when Mr. Walters stopped speaking, and the end of his address was greeted with a wave of quiet relief.

Much of the murmuring had been triggered by a somewhat unusual occurrence: the arrival of guests. Lawyer Thatcher entered with a frail, elderly man, a distinguished, stout gentleman of middle age with iron-gray hair, and a stately woman who was clearly the gentleman's wife. The woman was guiding a young girl. Tom had been agitated and filled with complaints; he also felt guilty, unable to face Amy Lawrence or endure her affectionate look. Yet, upon seeing this new girl, his spirit was instantly ignited with joy. Immediately, he began to display his prowess with great intensity—striking other boys, tugging hair, and pulling faces—employing every tactic he thought might charm a girl and gain her admiration. His delight was only slightly dampened by the memory of his embarrassment in this girl's garden, but that recollection was rapidly fading beneath the tides of happiness now washing over him.

The guests were seated in the most prominent place of honor, and once Mr. Walters concluded his speech, he presented them to the students. The middle-aged man proved to be a remarkable figure—none other than the county judge—the most imposing person these children had ever beheld. They marveled at his nature, half-hoping to hear him speak with authority while simultaneously fearing he might. He hailed from Constantinople, twelve miles distant, meaning he had journeyed and experienced the world; he had even gazed upon the county courthouse, which was rumored to have a roof made of tin. The reverence these thoughts provoked was evident in the profound silence and the rows of wide-eyed children. This was the renowned Judge Thatcher, the brother of their local lawyer. Jeff Thatcher promptly stepped forward to associate with the great man and draw the envy of his classmates. It would have delighted him to overhear the hushed comments:

“Observe him, Jim! He is heading up there. Look—he is going to shake his hand—he is actually shaking hands with him! Goodness, don't you wish you were Jeff?”

Mr. Walters began to perform for the audience, engaging in various official displays and busywork, issuing commands, making rulings, and giving instructions wherever he could find an opportunity. The librarian also put on a show, rushing back and forth with his arms burdened by books, creating the sort of frantic commotion that petty authority enjoys. The young female teachers likewise performed, leaning gracefully over students they had recently disciplined, raising delicate fingers to warn naughty boys while affectionately patting the well-behaved ones.

And so on and so forth. There is no need to record the rest of the speech. It followed a standard pattern that never changes, and so it is familiar to us all.

The final third of the address was interrupted by the return of fighting and other distractions among some of the troublemakers, along with fidgeting and whispering that spread far and wide, reaching even the most composed and steady students like Sid and Mary. But now every sound stopped abruptly as Mr. Walters’ voice faded, and the end of the speech was met with a wave of silent relief.

Amen.

Fabric rustled as the standing crowd took their seats. The lad whose story this narrative follows did not find pleasure in the prayer; he merely tolerated it, if he managed even that. He remained fidgety throughout; he tracked the prayer’s components without thinking, as he was not truly attending, yet he was familiar with the minister’s customary path. Whenever a small bit of novel content was inserted, his ears caught it and his entire being bristled; he viewed such additions as dishonest and villainous. During the prayer, a fly landed on the pew ahead of him, tormenting his spirit by serenely rubbing its hands, clasping its head, and scrubbing it so intensely that it seemed nearly detached from its frame, revealing its thin neck. It scraped its wings with its hind legs, smoothing them against its sides like coat-tails, performing its entire grooming routine as calmly as if it understood it was secure. It was indeed safe; though Tom’s hands ached to snatch it, he dared not, fearing his soul would be immediately ruined if he committed such an act during the prayer. Yet, as the final sentence concluded, his hand began to arch and creep forward, and the moment the word “Amen” was spoken, the fly became a captive. His aunt noticed the deed and forced him to release it.

The preacher announced his text and droned on in a dull tone through a discourse so tedious that many heads eventually began to droop, even though the argument concerned endless fire and brimstone, narrowing the predestined chosen few to a group so tiny it hardly seemed worth the effort of salvation. Tom tallied the sermon’s pages; after the service, he could always state how many pages there were, though he rarely recalled anything else regarding the speech. Still, he was briefly engaged this time. The minister painted a grand, stirring image of the world’s multitudes gathering at the millennium, where the lion and the lamb would rest together and a small child would guide them. Yet the emotion, the instruction, and the moral of the magnificent scene were wasted on the boy; he only considered how prominent the main figure would be before the watching nations. His face brightened at the notion, and he mused that he would like to be that child, provided the lion was tame.

He returned to his misery as the dry lecture resumed. Soon, he remembered a prize he possessed and retrieved it. It was a large, dark beetle with intimidating mandibles—a “pinchbug,” as he termed it. It was kept inside a percussion-cap container. The beetle immediately bit his finger. A reflexive flick followed, the insect tumbled into the aisle and landed on its back, and the wounded finger went into the boy’s mouth. The beetle remained there, struggling with its helpless legs, unable to right itself.

Amen.

There was a rustling of dresses, and the standing congregation sat down. The boy whose story this book tells did not enjoy the prayer; he merely endured it—if he even managed that much. He was restless throughout; he kept track of the prayer’s details unconsciously, for he was not listening, but he knew the territory well and the minister’s predictable path through it. When a small bit of new material was inserted, his ear caught it and his entire nature resented it; he considered additions unfair and underhanded. He drifted off, feeling like a hero stripped of his glory. Before long, Tom encountered the village’s young outcast, Huckleberry Finn, the offspring of the local drunk. Mothers throughout the town deeply loathed and feared Huckleberry, viewing him as lazy, rebellious, crude, and wicked. They also resented that their own children idolized him, relished his forbidden company, and secretly wished they had the courage to emulate him. Like the other well-behaved boys, Tom envied Huckleberry’s flashy, marginalized lifestyle and was strictly forbidden from associating with him. Consequently, he spent time with him whenever the opportunity arose. Huckleberry was perpetually clad in discarded adult garments that were constantly tattered and flapping with rags. His hat was a massive wreck with a large chunk missing from the brim; his coat, when he bothered to wear one, reached nearly to his ankles with buttons positioned low on the back; a single suspender held up his trousers, which sagged empty in the seat, their frayed hems trailing in the dust unless he rolled them up.

Huckleberry lived entirely on his own terms. He spent warm nights sleeping on porches and rainy ones inside empty barrels; he was exempt from school, church, and the authority of any master; he could swim or fish whenever and wherever he liked, for as long as he pleased; no one stopped him from fighting; he could stay awake as late as he wanted; he was invariably the first boy to go barefoot each spring and the last to wear shoes in the autumn; he never had to bathe or change into fresh clothing; and he possessed a remarkable talent for swearing. In short, that boy enjoyed everything that made existence worthwhile. That was the consensus among every burdened, restricted, respectable boy in St. Petersburg.

Tom greeted the adventurous pariah:

“Hi, Huckleberry!”

“Hi to you, and see how you enjoy it.”

“What are you carrying?”

“A dead cat.”

“Let me look at him, Huck. Wow, he’s quite rigid. Where did you find him?”

“I purchased him from a boy.”

“What did you trade?”

“I gave him a blue ticket and a bladder I picked up at the slaughterhouse.”

“Where did you get that blue ticket?”

“I bought it from Ben Rogers a fortnight ago for a hoop-stick.”

“Tell me—what are dead cats useful for, Huck?”

“Useful for? They cure warts.”

“Really! Is that true? I know of something even better.”

“I doubt it. What is it?”

“Well, spunk-water.”

“Spunk-water! I wouldn’t give a damn for spunk-water.”

“You wouldn’t, really? Have you ever tested it?”

“No, I haven’t. But Bob Tanner did.”

“Who told you that?”

“Well, he told Jeff Thatcher, and Jeff told Johnny Baker, and Johnny told Jim Hollis, and Jim told Ben Rogers, and Ben told a Black man, and the Black man told me. There!”

“So what? They all lie. At least, all of them except the Black man. I don’t know him. But I’ve never met a Black man who wouldn’t lie. Pshaw! Now explain how Bob Tanner did it, Huck.”

“Well, he dipped his hand into a decaying tree stump filled with rainwater.”

“During the day?”

“Of course.”

“Facing the stump?”

“Yes. At least, I assume so.”

He wandered off, a defeated hero.

Now Tom started to scribble something on the slate, concealing the writing from the girl. But she was not hesitant this time. She pleaded to look. Tom remarked:

“Oh, it isn’t anything.”

“Yes it is.”

“No it isn’t. You don’t want to see.”

“Yes I do, truly I do. Please let me.”

“You’ll tell.”

“No I won’t—truly and truly and double truly I won’t.”

“You won’t tell anyone at all? Ever, as long as you live?”

“No, I won’t ever tell anyone. Now let me.”

“Oh, you don’t want to see!”

“Now that you act this way, I will see.” And she placed her small hand upon his and a brief struggle followed, Tom feigning to resist seriously but allowing his hand to slip bit by bit until these words were shown: “I love you.”

“Oh, you naughty thing!” And she struck his hand a sharp blow, yet flushed and appeared delighted, nonetheless.

Just at this moment the boy felt a slow, inevitable grip tightening on his ear, and a constant lifting force. In that manner he was carried across the room and placed in his own chair, amidst a barrage of laughter from the entire class. Then the teacher stood above him for a few dreadful moments, and eventually walked away to his desk without uttering a word. But although Tom’s ear throbbed, his spirit was triumphant.

As the classroom grew quiet Tom made a sincere attempt to focus, but the agitation inside him was too intense. In turn he joined the reading group and performed poorly; then in the geography group and transformed lakes into mountains, mountains into rivers, and rivers into continents, until total confusion reigned; then in the spelling group, and was defeated by a series of simple words, until he ended up at the bottom and surrendered the metal badge which he had displayed with pride for months.


CHAPTER VII


The more Tom attempted to fix his attention on his textbook, the more his thoughts drifted. So finally, with a sigh and a yawn, he abandoned the effort. It felt to him that the noon break would never arrive. The atmosphere was completely stagnant. There was not a breeze moving. It was the drowsiest of drowsy days. The sleepy drone of the twenty-five studying students calmed the spirit like the charm found in the humming of bees. Far away in the blazing sunlight, Cardiff Hill raised its gentle green slopes through a shimmering haze of heat, colored with the purple of distance; a few birds drifted on idle wings high in the sky; no other living creature was seen except for some cows, and they were slumbering. Tom’s heart longed to be liberated, or else to have something engaging to do to occupy the tedious time. His hand reached into his pocket and his expression brightened with a warmth of thankfulness that was a prayer, though he did not realize it.

“Oh, will you? When?”

“At noon. Do you go home for lunch?”

“I’ll stay if you do.”

“Great—it’s a deal. What’s your name?”

“Becky Thatcher. What’s yours? Oh, I know. It’s Thomas Sawyer.”

CHAPTER XVI After the midday meal, the entire group went to search for turtle eggs on the sandbar. They poked the sand with sticks, and upon finding a soft spot, they knelt and dug with their hands. Occasionally, they would pull fifty or sixty eggs from a single nest. These were perfectly round, white objects slightly smaller than a walnut. The night was nearly over. It was broad daylight by the time he reached the island sandbar. He rested again until the sun was high, shining brightly over the great river, and then he jumped into the water. A short time later, he stood dripping at the edge of the camp and heard Joe say: "No, Tom is loyal, Huck, and he will come back. He won't desert us. He knows that would be a disgrace to a pirate, and Tom is too proud for that. He is planning something. I wonder what it is?" "Well, the things are ours anyway, aren't they?" "Almost, but not yet, Huck. The note says they are ours if he isn't back here by breakfast." "Which he is!" Tom exclaimed with a dramatic flair, stepping grandly into the camp. A delicious breakfast of bacon and fish was soon ready, and as the boys ate, Tom told his story, adding a few extra details for effect. They felt like a group of proud, boastful heroes when the story ended. Then Tom hid in a shady spot to sleep until noon, while the other pirates prepared to fish and explore. After dinner, the whole group went out to hunt for turtle eggs on the sandbar. They poked sticks into the sand, and when they found a soft spot, they knelt down and dug with their hands. Sometimes they would pull fifty or sixty eggs from a single hole. They were perfectly round, white objects, a little smaller than an English walnut. They had a wonderful fried-egg feast that night and another on Friday morning. After breakfast, they ran and jumped around the sandbar, chasing each other and shedding their clothes until they were naked. They continued their game far up the shallow water of the bar against the strong current, which occasionally knocked their legs out from under them and made the fun even better. Now and then, they would gather in a group and splash water in each other's faces with their palms, moving closer while turning their heads to avoid the spray. Finally, they would grab and wrestle until the strongest boy dunked his friend, and they all went under in a tangle of arms and legs, coming up blowing, sputtering, laughing, and gasping for air all at once. When they were exhausted, they would run out and sprawl on the dry, hot sand, covering themselves with it before eventually running back to the water to repeat the whole performance. Eventually, they realized their bare skin looked like flesh-colored tights, so they drew a ring in the sand and held a circus, with all three acting as clowns since none would give up that role to the others. Next, they played marbles until they grew bored. Joe and Huck went for another swim, but Tom refused to join them because he realized that when he took off his trousers, he had lost the string of rattlesnake rattles from his ankle. He wondered how he had managed to avoid getting a cramp for so long without the protection of that mysterious charm.

A heavy, ominous stillness hung in the air, suggesting an impending event. The boys gathered close, seeking comfort near the campfire, even though the stagnant, sweltering heat felt suffocating. They remained motionless, focused and expectant. The grave silence persisted. Beyond the fire’s reach, everything was engulfed in absolute darkness. Soon, a flickering light appeared, briefly illuminating the leaves before disappearing. Eventually, another glow followed, slightly brighter, then another. A soft, mournful sound sighed through the forest canopy, and as the boys felt a brief breeze against their faces, they trembled, imagining the Spirit of the Night had passed. A silence followed. Then, a strange flash transformed the night into day, revealing every individual blade of grass near their feet, as well as three pale, frightened faces. A profound roll of thunder echoed across the sky, fading into distant, grumpy rumblings. A gust of cold air swept past, stirring the leaves and scattering ashes from the fire. Another intense glare lit the woods, followed immediately by a crash that seemed to split the treetops directly above them. They huddled together in fear during the ensuing darkness. A few heavy raindrops began to patter against the foliage.

“Hurry! boys, head for the tent!” Tom shouted.

They bolted, tripping over roots and vines in the dark, each running in a different direction. A violent gale roared through the trees, causing them to whistle. Blinding flashes occurred in rapid succession, accompanied by deafening claps of thunder. A torrential downpour began, and the intensifying hurricane drove the rain in sheets across the ground. The boys called out to one another, but the howling wind and booming thunder completely swallowed their voices. Eventually, they arrived one by one at the tent, shivering, terrified, and soaked; yet, finding comfort in their shared misery. They were unable to speak, as the old canvas flapped violently, even if the surrounding noise had permitted conversation. The storm intensified, and soon the sail ripped from its moorings, carried away by the wind. The boys grabbed each other’s hands and scrambled, falling and bruising themselves, toward the protection of a large oak tree on the riverbank. The storm reached its peak. Under the constant barrage of lightning flashing across the sky, everything below was visible with sharp, shadowless clarity: the swaying trees, the churning river white with foam, the flying spray, and the faint shapes of the high bluffs across the water, seen through the moving clouds and the slanted curtain of rain. Periodically, a massive tree would succumb to the gale, crashing through the smaller vegetation; the relentless thunder arrived in ear-splitting, sharp, and terrifying bursts. The tempest reached a climax in a singular, unmatched display that seemed capable of shattering the island, incinerating it, submerging it, blowing it away, and deafening every living thing upon it, all at once.

CHAPTER XX

There was a quality to Aunt Polly’s behavior when she kissed Tom that banished his gloom and restored his cheerful spirits. He headed toward school and happened to encounter Becky Thatcher near the start of Meadow Lane. His temperament always dictated his behavior. Without pausing, he hurried to her and said:

“I behaved quite poorly today, Becky, and I am truly sorry. I will never, ever act that way again as long as I live—please forgive me, won’t you?”

The girl halted and stared at him with disdain:

“I would appreciate it if you kept to yourself, Mr. Thomas Sawyer. I shall never speak to you again.”

She turned away and walked on. Tom was so shocked that he lacked the presence of mind to retort “Who cares, Miss Smarty?” until the opportunity had passed. Consequently, he remained silent, though he was filled with intense fury. He shuffled into the schoolyard, wishing she were a boy so he could imagine how he might thrash her. He soon crossed her path and delivered a biting comment as he walked by. She fired a sharp reply back, and the hostile rift was finalized. In her heated indignation, Becky felt she could barely wait for classes to begin, so eager was she to witness Tom being whipped for the damaged spelling book.

“So I just put the bark back in my pocket and stayed quiet.”

“What bark?”

“The bark I wrote on to tell you we had gone pirating. I really wish you had woken up when I kissed you—I do, honestly.”

The harsh lines in his aunt’s face softened, and a sudden tenderness appeared in her eyes.

“Did you kiss me, Tom?”

“Well, yes, I did.”

“Are you sure you did, Tom?”

“Yes, I did, Auntie—absolutely sure.”

“Why did you kiss me, Tom?”

“Because I loved you so much, and you were lying there moaning, and I felt so sorry for you.”

The words sounded like the truth. The old lady could not hide a tremor in her voice when she said:

“Kiss me again, Tom! Now get off to school and don’t bother me anymore.”

The moment he left, she ran to a closet and pulled out the ruined jacket Tom had worn while pirating. She paused with it in her hand and said to herself:

“No, I don’t dare. Poor boy, I suspect he lied about it—but it’s a blessed, blessed lie, and it brings me such comfort. I hope the Lord—I know the Lord will forgive him, because it was such a kind-hearted thing for him to say. But I don’t want to find out it’s a lie. I won’t look.”

She put the jacket away and stood there thinking for a minute. Twice she reached out to take the garment again, and twice she stopped herself. Once more she ventured, and this time she reassured herself with the thought: “It’s a good lie—it’s a good lie—I won’t let it upset me.” So she checked the jacket pocket. A moment later, she was reading Tom’s piece of bark through flowing tears, saying: “I could forgive the boy now, even if he had committed a million sins!”

CHAPTER XXXIII

In moments the report had traveled, and several small boats carrying men departed for McDougal’s cave, followed shortly by the ferryboat packed with people. Tom Sawyer traveled in the boat carrying Judge Thatcher.

Upon unlocking the cave entrance, a grim scene appeared in the faint light. Injun Joe lay prone on the floor, deceased, his face pressed near the door’s gap, as though his desperate gaze had remained fixed, until his final breath, upon the brightness and warmth of the world beyond. Tom felt moved, understanding from his own ordeal the agony this man had endured. Compassion stirred within him, yet he simultaneously experienced a profound sense of safety and liberation, realizing for the first time the immense burden of fear he had carried since the moment he testified against the violent fugitive.

Injun Joe’s bowie-knife rested nearby, the blade snapped in two. The heavy support beam of the door had been laboriously chipped and cut; it was futile work, as the natural rock created a threshold outside, and the knife had made no impression on that unyielding surface; the only result was the destruction of the blade. Even without the stone barrier, the effort would have been in vain, for had the beam been entirely removed, Injun Joe could not have fit his frame beneath the door, a fact he understood. He had merely hacked at the wood to occupy himself—to endure the agonizing passage of time—to keep his tormented mind active. Usually, one might discover several candle stubs tucked into the vestibule’s cracks, abandoned by visitors; none remained now. The captive had hunted them down and consumed them. He had also managed to trap a few bats, which he had likewise eaten, leaving only the claws. The wretched man had perished from starvation. Nearby, a stalagmite had been rising from the floor for centuries, formed by water dripping from a stalactite above. The prisoner had snapped off the stalagmite and set a stone upon the base, having carved a small depression into it to collect the vital drop that fell every three minutes with the monotonous rhythm of a clock—a single spoonful every twenty-four hours. That drop was falling when the Pyramids were young; when Troy collapsed; when Rome was founded; when Christ was crucified; when the Conqueror established the British empire; when Columbus voyaged; when the Lexington massacre was fresh news.

It continues to fall now; it will persist long after these events have faded into the late afternoon of history and the dusk of legend, eventually consumed by the deep darkness of total forgetfulness.

“Why?”

“Because I had its heavy door reinforced with boiler iron two weeks ago, and triple-locked it—and I have the keys.”

Tom turned as pale as a sheet.

“What’s wrong, boy! Someone, run! Get a glass of water!”

The water was brought and splashed onto Tom’s face.

“There, you’re fine now. What was wrong with you, Tom?”

“Oh, Judge, Injun Joe is in the cave!”

Injun Joe lay stretched out on the ground, dead, his face pressed against the crack in the door, as if his longing eyes had been fixed, until his final moment, on the light and warmth of the free world outside. Tom was moved, for he knew from his own experience how much this wretch had suffered. He felt pity, yet he also experienced an overwhelming sense of relief and security, which revealed to him more clearly than before just how heavy a burden of fear he had carried since the day he testified against this murderous outcast.

Injun Joe’s bowie knife lay nearby, its blade snapped in two. The heavy support beam of the door had been chipped and hacked through with exhausting effort; it was futile work, too, because the solid rock formed a sill outside it, and the knife had made no impression on that stubborn material; the only damage was to the knife itself. Even without the stone obstruction, the labor would have been pointless, for even if the beam had been cut away, Injun Joe could not have squeezed his body under the door, and he knew it. He had only hacked at that spot to keep busy—to pass the agonizing time—to occupy his tortured mind. Usually, one could find several candle stubs left by tourists in the crevices of this entrance, but there were none now. The prisoner had hunted them down and eaten them. He had also managed to catch a few bats, which he had also eaten, leaving only their claws. The poor man had starved to death. Nearby, a stalagmite had been slowly growing from the floor for ages, formed by water dripping from a stalactite above. The captive had broken off the stalagmite and placed a stone on the stump, having carved a shallow hollow into it to catch the precious drop that fell once every three minutes with the dreary regularity of a clock tick—a dessert spoonful every twenty-four hours. That drop was falling when the Pyramids were new; when Troy fell; when the foundations of Rome were laid; when Christ was crucified; when the Conqueror created the British Empire; when Columbus sailed; when the massacre at Lexington was “news.”

It is falling now; it will still be falling when all these events have faded into the afternoon of history and the twilight of tradition, and been swallowed up in the deep night of oblivion.

“Why?”

“Because I had its heavy door covered with boiler iron two weeks ago and triple-locked it—and I have the keys.”

Tom turned as pale as a sheet.

“What is wrong, boy! Someone, run! Get a glass of water!”

The water was brought and splashed onto Tom’s face.

“There, you are all right now. What was the matter with you, Tom?”

“Oh, Judge, Injun Joe is in the cave!”

The artist pondered for a brief interval. “He is fond of me,” he replied eventually; “I am certain of it. Naturally, I offer him excessive flattery. I derive a peculiar satisfaction from uttering remarks to him that I know I will later regret. Generally, he is delightful toward me, and we remain in the studio discussing countless topics. Occasionally, however, he is dreadfully inconsiderate and appears to find genuine joy in causing me distress. At those moments, Harry, I feel as though I have surrendered my entire spirit to someone who views it merely as a blossom for his lapel, a trivial adornment to flatter his ego, or a seasonal decoration.”

“Summer days have a tendency to drag on,” Lord Henry whispered. “Perhaps you will grow weary before he does. It is a melancholy thought, yet there is no question that talent outlives physical beauty. That explains why we exert such effort to over-educate ourselves. In the fierce competition for survival, we desire something permanent, so we clutter our intellects with useless trivia and data, foolishly hoping to maintain our standing. The perfectly informed individual is the modern ideal. Yet, the mind of such a person is a terrifying thing. It resembles a shop filled with curiosities, cluttered with dust and grotesque items, each marked with an inflated price. Regardless, I suspect you will be the first to tire. One day you will observe your friend and find him slightly distorted, or perhaps you will dislike his complexion or some other detail. You will harbor bitter resentment toward him and conclude that he has treated you poorly. When he visits next, you will be entirely detached and aloof. It would be a great shame, as it would change you. Your account is quite a romance—a romance of art, perhaps—and the misfortune of any romance is that it leaves one feeling so unromantic.”

One’s own soul and the passions of one’s companions—these were the truly enthralling elements of existence.

At half-past twelve the following day, Lord Henry Wotton walked from Curzon Street to the Albany to visit his uncle, Lord Fermor. The son, having served as his father’s assistant, had resigned alongside him, a move many considered unwise at the time. He owned two expansive town residences but chose to reside in apartments to avoid bother, taking most meals at his club. He occasionally oversaw his coal mines in the Midlands, justifying this minor exertion by claiming the sole benefit of coal was that it allowed a gentleman the luxury of burning timber in his own fireplace. Politically, he was a Tory, except when that party held power, at which point he loudly criticized them as a group of Radicals. He was a hero to his personal servant, who intimidated him, and a menace to his kin, whom he intimidated in return. Only England could have fostered such a man, and he frequently declared that the nation was headed toward ruin. His beliefs were archaic, yet there was significant merit to his biases.

“I trust you.”

CHAPTER VIII.

It was well past midday when he roused himself. His servant had tiptoed into the chamber repeatedly to check if he had stirred, puzzled by his master’s late slumber. At last, the bell rang, and Victor entered quietly, bearing a cup of tea and a stack of correspondence on a tray of antique Sevres porcelain. He pulled back the olive-satin drapes, which featured shimmering blue linings and covered the three grand windows.

“What time is it, Victor?” Dorian Gray inquired sleepily.

He opened the remaining items with indifference. They consisted of the typical assortment of calling cards, dinner invitations, gallery passes, and charity concert schedules that are routinely sent to stylish young gentlemen throughout the social season. There was also a substantial invoice for a decorative silver Louis-Quinze vanity set; he had not yet dared to forward it to his guardians, who were quite traditional and failed to grasp that we reside in an era where superfluous items are our sole requirements. Additionally, there were several polite notes from Jermyn Street creditors, proposing to provide loans of any amount instantly at highly competitive interest rates.

Yet it was watching him, with its beautiful, marred face and its cruel smile. Its bright hair shone in the early sunlight. Its blue eyes met his own. A sense of infinite pity, not for himself, but for the painted image of himself, washed over him. It had already altered, and would alter more. Its gold would wither into grey. Its red and white roses would die. For every sin he committed, a stain would mark and ruin its beauty. But he would not sin. The picture, changed or unchanged, would be to him the visible symbol of his conscience. He would resist temptation. He would not see Lord Henry anymore—or, at any rate, he would not listen to those subtle, poisonous theories that had first stirred within him the passion for impossible things in Basil Hallward’s garden. He repeated her name over and over. The birds singing in the dew-drenched garden seemed to be telling the flowers about her.

Eventually, Dorian Gray glanced upward. “You have clarified my own nature to me, Harry,” he whispered, exhaling with a hint of ease. “I sensed everything you described, yet I was somehow intimidated by it and unable to articulate it to myself. She remained a heroine throughout. During her final performance—the evening you witnessed her—she performed poorly because she had grasped the truth of love. Once she understood its falsity, she perished, much as Juliet might have. She returned to the realm of art. There is a touch of the martyr in her. Her passing possesses all the tragic futility of martyrdom, all its squandered grace. Yet, as I mentioned, you must not assume I have been without pain. No one can, unless they are sentimental. You are being terribly unfair, Basil. You arrive here to offer comfort. That is lovely of you. You find me already comforted, and you are enraged. How typical of a sympathetic soul! You bring to mind a tale Harry shared regarding a philanthropist who dedicated two decades to rectifying some wrong or changing an unfair statute—I cannot recall the specifics. Eventually, he triumphed, and his frustration was boundless. With nothing left to occupy him, he nearly perished from boredom and turned into a committed misanthrope. Furthermore, my dear old Basil, if you truly wish to console me, instruct me instead on how to forget these events or view them through a proper aesthetic lens. Was it not Gautier who wrote regarding the consolation of the arts? I recall finding a small vellum-bound volume in your studio once and stumbling upon that lovely expression. Well, I am not like that youth you described when we were at Marlow, the one who claimed yellow satin could offer solace for every life misery. I cherish beautiful objects one can touch and hold. Regret, denial, or forgetfulness could do that. But the future was unavoidable. There were passions within him that would find their terrible outlet, and dreams that would make the shadow of their evil real.

He picked up the large purple-and-gold cloth that covered the couch, and, holding it in his hands, stepped behind the screen. Was the face on the canvas viler than before? It seemed to him that it was unchanged, yet his loathing of it had grown stronger. Gold hair, blue eyes, and rose-red lips—they were all there. It was only the expression that had changed. That was horrible in its cruelty. Compared to the judgment he saw in it, how shallow Basil’s reproaches about Sibyl Vane had been!—how shallow, and of how little importance! His own soul was looking out at him from the canvas and calling him to account. A look of pain crossed his face, and he threw the rich cloth over the picture. As he did so, a knock came at the door. He walked out as his servant entered.

“The men are here, sir.”

He felt that he must get rid of the man at once. He could not let him know where the picture was being taken. There was something sly about him, and he had thoughtful, treacherous eyes. Sitting down at the writing-table, he scribbled a note to Lord Henry, asking him to send over something to read and reminding him that they were to meet at eight-fifteen that evening.

“Wait for an answer,” he said, handing it to him, “and show the men in here.”

In two or three minutes there was another knock, and Mr. Hubbard, the famous frame-maker from South Audley Street, came in with a somewhat rough-looking young assistant. Mr. Hubbard was a florid, red-whiskered little man whose love for art was significantly limited by the constant poverty of most of the artists who dealt with him. As a rule, he never left his shop. He waited for people to come to him. But he always made an exception for Dorian Gray. There was something about Dorian that charmed everyone. It was a pleasure even to see him.

“What can I do for you, Mr. Gray?” he said, rubbing his fat, freckled hands. “I thought I would do myself the honor of coming over in person. I have just found a beautiful frame, sir. I picked it up at an auction.”

What connection could Dorian Gray possibly have to the passing of Sibyl Vane? There was no reason for alarm. Dorian Gray had not caused her death.

His gaze landed upon the yellow-covered volume Lord Henry had provided. He pondered its nature. Moving toward the small, pearl-hued octagonal pedestal that always reminded him of the handiwork of exotic Egyptian bees crafting in silver, he picked up the book, collapsed into a chair, and started flipping through the pages. Within moments, he was captivated. It was the most peculiar work he had ever encountered. It appeared to him that the transgressions of humanity were parading before him in silent pantomime, dressed in magnificent attire and accompanied by the soft melodies of flutes. Concepts he had only vaguely imagined suddenly became tangible. Ideas he had never conceived were slowly unveiled.

It was a story devoid of a traditional plot, featuring only a single protagonist; essentially, it was a psychological examination of a young man from Paris who dedicated his existence to manifesting, within the nineteenth century, every emotion and philosophical outlook belonging to eras other than his own. He sought to embody the diverse temperaments the world-spirit had experienced, cherishing for their very artifice those acts of self-denial that people foolishly labeled virtue, just as much as those inherent rebellions that the wise still termed sin. The prose was crafted in that distinctive, ornate style—simultaneously vivid and cryptic—brimming with slang, archaic terms, specialized jargon, and intricate phrasing, which marks the output of the most skilled French Symbolist writers. The metaphors within were as grotesque as orchids and as nuanced in their hues. The experiences of the senses were depicted through the language of mystical philosophy. At times, it was difficult to discern if one was perusing the divine raptures of a medieval saint or the diseased admissions of a contemporary transgressor. It was a corrupting volume. A dense scent of incense appeared to linger upon the leaves, clouding the intellect. The very rhythm of the prose, with its delicate, hypnotic repetition and complex, recurring motifs, induced in the young man, as he progressed through the chapters, a state of trance—a sickness of fantasy—that rendered him oblivious to the fading daylight and the encroaching gloom.

A clear, copper-green sky, punctured by a single lonely star, shone through the glass. He continued reading by that pale illumination until he could see no longer. Eventually, after his servant had repeatedly alerted him to the lateness of the hour, he rose, walked into the adjacent chamber, set the book upon the small Florentine stand that sat by his bed, and commenced dressing for his evening meal.

It was nearly nine o'clock when he arrived at the club, where he discovered Lord Henry waiting by himself in the morning-room, appearing quite weary. 

"I apologize sincerely, Harry," he exclaimed, "but it is truly your doing. That volume you gave me held me so spellbound that I lost track of the time."

"Indeed, I suspected you would enjoy it," his host answered, standing up from his seat.

What did Dorian Gray have to do with Sibyl Vane’s death? There was nothing to fear. Dorian Gray had not killed her.

His gaze landed on the yellow book that Lord Henry had sent him. He wondered what it was. He walked toward the small, pearl-colored octagonal stand that had always reminded him of the work of strange Egyptian bees crafting in silver, and picking up the volume, he threw himself into an armchair and began to flip through the pages. After a few minutes, he was completely absorbed. It was the strangest book he had ever read. It felt as though the sins of the world were parading before him in silence, dressed in exquisite clothing and accompanied by the delicate sound of flutes. Things he had only vaguely imagined suddenly became real to him. Things he had never even dreamed of were gradually revealed.

It was a novel without a plot and with only one character; it was essentially a psychological study of a young Parisian who spent his life trying to experience in the nineteenth century all the passions and ways of thinking that belonged to every century but his own. He sought to embody, as it were, the various moods the world-spirit had ever experienced, valuing for their sheer artificiality those renunciations that people have foolishly called virtue, just as much as those natural rebellions that wise men still call sin. The writing style was that curious, jeweled prose—vivid and obscure at once, full of slang and archaic terms, technical jargon, and elaborate phrasing—that characterizes the work of some of the finest artists of the French Symbolist school. It contained metaphors as monstrous as orchids and as subtle in color. The life of the senses was described in the language of mystical philosophy. At times, one could hardly tell if one were reading the spiritual ecstasies of a medieval saint or the morbid confessions of a modern sinner. It was a poisonous book. The heavy scent of incense seemed to cling to its pages and cloud the mind. The very rhythm of the sentences, the subtle monotony of their music, filled as it was with complex refrains and elaborately repeated movements, induced in the young man, as he moved from chapter to chapter, a kind of reverie, a sickness of dreaming that made him unaware of the fading day and the creeping shadows.

Cloudless and pierced by a single solitary star, a copper-green sky shone through the windows. He read on by its pale light until he could read no more. Then, after his valet had reminded him several times how late it was, he got up, went into the next room, placed the book on the small Florentine table that always stood by his bedside, and began to dress for dinner.

It was nearly nine o’clock before he reached the club, where he found Lord Henry sitting alone in the morning room, looking very bored.

“I am so sorry, Harry,” he called out, “but it really is entirely your fault. That book you sent me fascinated me so much that I lost track of time.”

“Yes, I thought you would like it,” his host replied, rising from his chair.

Slowly, pale fingers of light slide through the drapes, appearing to quiver. Mute, dark shadows take on strange, jagged forms as they creep into the room’s corners to huddle. Beyond the walls, birds rustle in the foliage, laborers begin their daily tasks, and the wind sighs and sobs as it descends from the hills, circling the quiet house as if hesitant to disturb those sleeping, yet compelled to summon slumber from its violet depths. Layer after layer of thin, dark mist is drawn away, and gradually, objects regain their shapes and hues, allowing us to observe the dawn recreating the world in its ancient design. The pale mirrors recover their reflected existence. The extinguished candles remain where they were placed, accompanied by the partially read volume we were examining, the artificial flower worn at the dance, or the letter we dreaded opening or had perused too frequently. Everything appears unchanged. From the illusory shadows of night, the familiar reality of our lives returns. We are forced to take it up exactly where we paused, and a crushing awareness of the need to persist in the same monotonous cycle of habitual actions creeps over us, or perhaps a desperate wish that our eyes might wake to a world refashioned in the dark for our delight—a world where objects possess new forms and colors, hold different secrets, and where the past has no place or survives without the burden of duty or remorse, since even the memory of joy carries bitterness and the recollection of pleasure brings pain.

Dorian Gray believed that the creation of such worlds was the primary purpose, or among the primary purposes, of existence. In his pursuit of sensations that were simultaneously novel and pleasurable, while holding that quality of strangeness vital to romance, he frequently embraced ways of thinking he knew were foreign to his character. He would surrender to their delicate influence, and once he had absorbed their essence and sated his mental curiosity, he would abandon them with a detached indifference that is not at odds with a truly passionate nature, and which, according to some contemporary psychologists, is often a prerequisite for it.

It was once whispered that he intended to convert to Roman Catholicism, and the Roman liturgy certainly held a profound fascination for him. The daily sacrifice, more solemn than any from the ancient world, moved him as much by its magnificent denial of sensory evidence as by the elemental simplicity of its components and the enduring sorrow of the human drama it aimed to represent. He enjoyed kneeling upon the chilly marble floor to observe the priest, clad in a stiff, embroidered dalmatic, moving the tabernacle veil aside with slow, white hands, or elevating the ornate, lantern-like monstrance containing the pale wafer that one might wish to believe is truly the “panis cælestis,” the bread of angels, or, dressed in the vestments of Christ’s Passion, breaking the Host into the chalice while striking his breast in penance for his sins.

Slowly, white fingers of light creep through the curtains, appearing to tremble. Dark, strange shapes of shadows crawl into the corners of the room and crouch there. Outside, there is the rustling of birds in the leaves, the sounds of men heading to work, or the sighing and sobbing of the wind coming down from the hills, wandering around the silent house as if it feared to wake the sleepers yet felt compelled to call sleep from her purple cave. Veil after veil of thin, dusky gauze is lifted, and gradually the shapes and colors of objects are restored, and we watch the dawn recreate the world in its ancient pattern. The pale mirrors regain their reflected life. The unlit candles stand where we left them, and beside them lies the half-read book we had been studying, the wired flower we had worn to the ball, or the letter we had been afraid to read, or had read too often. Nothing seems changed to us. Out of the unreal shadows of the night returns the real life we have known. We must resume it where we left off, and a terrible sense of the necessity to continue our energy in the same tiresome cycle of routine habits steals over us, or perhaps a wild longing that our eyes might open one morning to a world refashioned in the darkness for our pleasure—a world where things have fresh shapes and colors, are changed, or hold different secrets; a world where the past has little or no place, or at least does not survive in any conscious form of obligation or regret, since even the memory of joy has its bitterness and the memory of pleasure its pain.

It was the creation of such worlds that seemed to Dorian Gray to be the true purpose, or among the true purposes, of life. In his search for sensations that were both new and delightful, possessing that element of strangeness so essential to romance, he would often adopt certain modes of thought he knew were truly alien to his nature, surrender himself to their subtle influences, and then, having captured their essence and satisfied his intellectual curiosity, abandon them with that curious indifference which is not incompatible with a real intensity of temperament, and which, according to some modern psychologists, is often a condition of it.

It was marvelous even to peruse accounts of the opulence belonging to those long deceased. He then shifted his focus to needlework and the tapestries that served as wall paintings within the cold chambers of northern European lands. As he delved into this topic—possessing a remarkable talent for becoming entirely consumed by whatever task he undertook—he felt a sense of melancholy regarding the decay that time inflicts upon exquisite and miraculous objects. He, however, had avoided such a fate. Seasons turned, yellow daffodils blossomed and withered repeatedly, and nights of terror recounted their tale of disgrace, yet he remained unaltered. No winter season scarred his features or dulled his youthful, petal-like complexion. How contrasting this was to physical items! Where had they vanished? What became of the magnificent saffron-hued garment, depicting the gods battling giants, crafted by dark-skinned maidens to delight Athena? Where was the massive velarium Nero had spanned over the Roman Colosseum, that colossal purple sail portraying the starry heavens and Apollo steering a chariot pulled by white horses with golden reins? He yearned to behold the peculiar napkins fashioned for the Priest of the Sun, which displayed every delicacy and dish required for a banquet; the burial shroud of King Chilperic, adorned with three hundred golden bees; the eccentric garments that provoked the Bishop of Pontus, featuring “lions, panthers, bears, dogs, forests, rocks, hunters—everything, in truth, that an artist might replicate from the natural world”; and the tunic once donned by Charles of Orleans, upon whose sleeves were stitched the lyrics of a song starting “Madame, je suis tout joyeux,” with the musical notation rendered in gold thread, each square-shaped note of that era composed of four pearls. He studied the chamber prepared at the Rheims palace for Queen Joan of Burgundy, embellished with “one thousand three hundred and twenty-one parrots, created in needlework and emblazoned with the royal crest, alongside five hundred and sixty-one butterflies, their wings similarly decorated with the queen’s arms, all executed in gold.” Catherine de Medicis possessed a mourning bed of black velvet dusted with crescents and suns. Its hangings were of damask, featuring foliage wreaths and garlands set against a gold and silver backdrop, bordered with pearl embroidery, situated in a room draped with the queen’s emblems in cut black velvet upon silver cloth. Louis XIV displayed gold-stitched caryatids fifteen feet in height within his quarters. The royal bed of Sobieski, King of Poland, consisted of Smyrna gold brocade stitched with turquoises and verses from the Koran. Its frame was of chased silver gilt, lavishly inlaid with enameled and jeweled medallions. It had been seized from the Turkish encampment outside Vienna, and the banner of Mohammed had once rested beneath its shimmering gilt canopy.

Consequently, throughout an entire year, he endeavored to gather the most refined examples of textile and needlework he could discover, acquiring delicate Delhi muslins, intricately decorated with gold-thread palmettes and studded with iridescent beetle wings; Dacca gauzes, known in the Orient for their transparency as “woven air,” “running water,” and “evening dew”; exotic patterned fabrics from Java; complex yellow Chinese wall hangings; volumes bound in tan satins or pale blue silks and adorned with fleurs-de-lis, birds, and figures; veils of lacis crafted in Hungarian point; Sicilian brocades and rigid Spanish velvets; Georgian pieces featuring gold coins; and Japanese Foukousas, with their greenish golds and brilliantly feathered birds.

Even reading about the luxuries of the dead was fascinating. Then, he turned his focus to embroideries and the tapestries that served as frescoes in the cold rooms of northern Europe. They all possessed a ghastly allure. He envisioned them during the night, and they haunted his thoughts throughout the day. The Renaissance era understood peculiar methods of assassination—lethal contact via a helmet or a flaming torch, an ornate glove or a decorated fan, a gilded scent-box or a necklace of amber. Dorian Gray had been corrupted by a volume. There were times when he viewed wickedness merely as a medium through which he might achieve his vision of beauty.

CHAPTER XII.

It occurred on the ninth of November, the night preceding his thirty-eighth birthday, as he frequently recalled in later years.

He was returning home around eleven o’clock after dining at Lord Henry’s, bundled in thick furs against the chilly, mist-filled night. Near the intersection of Grosvenor Square and South Audley Street, a figure hurried past him through the fog, wearing a grey overcoat with the collar raised. The man carried a satchel. Dorian identified him. It was Basil Hallward. An inexplicable feeling of dread washed over him. He offered no greeting and continued rapidly toward his residence.

However, Hallward had spotted him. Dorian heard him pause on the sidewalk before hastening to catch up. Moments later, a hand gripped his arm.

“Dorian! What a remarkable stroke of fortune! I have been lingering in your library since nine o’clock. Eventually, I took pity on your exhausted servant and instructed him to retire as he showed me out.”

His internal disposition had rebelled against the overwhelming misery that threatened to damage his tranquil state. It appeared to me rather theatrical. London abounds in such odd spectacles. A rainy Sunday, a crude believer in a raincoat, a circle of pale, sickly faces beneath a canopy of leaking umbrellas, and a striking remark tossed into the atmosphere by piercing, frantic voices—it was truly effective in its own fashion, a genuine inspiration. I considered informing the preacher that while art possesses a soul, humanity does not. I suspect, however, he would have failed to grasp my meaning.

“Do not, Harry. The soul is a frightening reality. It can be purchased, traded, and exchanged. It can be corrupted or perfected. Every one of us possesses a soul. I am certain of it.”

“Are you entirely convinced of that, Dorian?”

“Entirely convinced.”

“Ah! Then it must be a delusion. The matters one feels completely positive about are never factual. That is the curse of belief and the moral of romance. How solemn you are! Do not be so earnest. What concern have you or I with the myths of our time? No: we have abandoned our faith in the soul. Perform something for me. Play a nocturne, Dorian, and while you play, whisper to me how you have preserved your youth. You must possess some mystery. I am merely ten years your senior, yet I am lined, exhausted, and sallow. You are truly remarkable, Dorian. You have never appeared more delightful than you do this evening. You bring to mind the day we first met. You were somewhat impudent, quite timid, and entirely exceptional. You have altered, certainly, but not in your looks. I wish you would share your secret. To regain my youth, I would attempt anything, provided it did not involve physical exertion, rising early, or living respectably. Youth! There is nothing comparable. It is ridiculous to speak of the naivety of youth. The only individuals whose views I currently value are those much younger than myself. They appear ahead of me. Life has unveiled its newest marvels to them. As for the elderly, I habitually disagree with them. I do so as a matter of principle. If you inquire about their perspective on a recent event, they gravely offer the sentiments prevalent in 1820, when men wore high cravats, believed in everything, and understood absolutely nothing. How beautiful that piece you are performing is! I wonder, did Chopin compose it in Majorca, with the ocean sobbing around the villa and the salty mist striking the windows? It is wonderfully romantic. What a gift it is that one art remains to us that is not merely imitative! Do not cease. I desire music tonight. It strikes me that you are the youthful Apollo and I am Marsyas listening to you. I harbor my own griefs, Dorian, of which even you are unaware. The tragedy of aging is not that one becomes old, but that one remains young inside. I am occasionally astonished by my own candor. Ah, Dorian, how fortunate you are! What a magnificent life you have led! You have tasted everything fully. You have pressed the grapes against your tongue. Nothing has been concealed from you.

The agonizing decay of his own spirit was what disturbed him. Basil had created the painting that had ruined his existence, and he could not pardon him for it. The portrait was the source of all his troubles. Basil had uttered intolerable remarks to him, which he had nevertheless endured with restraint. The killing had been merely a fleeting fit of insanity. Regarding Alan Campbell, his self-destruction was his own decision; he had elected to take that path. It meant nothing to him.

A fresh start! That was his desire, the thing he awaited. Surely, he had already commenced it. He had protected one blameless creature, at the very least. He would never again lead innocence astray. He would lead a virtuous life.

Reflecting on Hetty Merton, he started to question if the painting in the secured chamber had altered. Surely, it could not remain as ghastly as it once was? Perhaps, if his existence turned righteous, he might purge every trace of wicked desire from the visage. Perhaps the marks of depravity had already vanished. He would go and inspect it.

He lifted the lamp from the stand and stole upstairs. As he unfastened the door, a gleam of happiness crossed his remarkably youthful countenance and rested briefly upon his mouth. Indeed, he would be virtuous, and the grotesque object he had concealed would no longer haunt him. He felt as though the burden had already been removed.

He entered silently, securing the door behind him as he usually did, and pulled the violet curtain away from the canvas. A shout of agony and resentment escaped him. He observed no transformation, except for a glint of craftiness in the eyes and the arched crease of a deceiver about the lips. The object remained repulsive—even more so, if that were possible—and the crimson moisture staining the hand appeared more vivid, resembling freshly shed blood. Then he shook. Was it merely conceit that had prompted his single virtuous act? Or was it the craving for a novel experience, as Lord Henry had suggested with his derisive chuckle? Or perhaps that urge to perform a role that occasionally drives us to actions nobler than our true selves? Or maybe a combination of these? And why had the crimson mark expanded? It seemed to have spread like a vile contagion across the shriveled digits. There was blood upon the painted feet, as if the figure had leaked—blood even upon the hand that had not wielded the blade. Confess? Did that imply he must admit his guilt? To surrender himself and face execution? He chuckled. He considered the notion absurd. Furthermore, even if he were to confess, who would credit him? No evidence of the victim remained. Everything associated with the man had been obliterated. He had personally incinerated the items from the lower floor. The public would simply deem him insane. They would confine him if he insisted on his tale.... Yet, he felt an obligation to confess, to endure public disgrace, and to provide public restitution. There existed a God who summoned men to reveal their transgressions to the world as well as to heaven.

It was the living death of his own soul that haunted him. Basil had painted the portrait that had ruined his life, and he could not forgive him for it. The portrait was the root of everything. Basil had said things to him that were intolerable, yet he had endured them with patience. The murder had been nothing more than a momentary fit of madness. As for Alan Campbell, his suicide was his own choice; he had decided to do it, and it meant nothing to Dorian.

A new life! That was what he craved and what he awaited. Surely he had already begun it. He had spared one innocent person, at least. He would never again lead innocence into temptation. He would be good.

As he thought of Hetty Merton, he wondered if the portrait in the locked room had changed. Surely it was not still as horrific as it had been? Perhaps if his life became pure, he could erase every sign of evil passion from the face. Perhaps the marks of wickedness had already faded. He would go and see.

He took the lamp from the table and crept upstairs. As he unbolted the door, a joyful smile flickered across his strangely youthful face and lingered on his lips. Yes, he would be good, and the hideous thing he had hidden away would no longer terrify him. He felt as if a weight had already been lifted from his shoulders.

CHAPTER II

Ten minutes afterward, his complexion drained of color and his gaze frantic with sorrow, Lord Arthur Savile bolted from Bentinck House, pushing past the cluster of liveried servants gathered beneath the expansive striped canopy, seemingly blind and deaf to his surroundings. The evening air was biting, and the streetlights circling the square danced and wavered in the sharp breeze; yet his palms felt feverish, and his brow scorched. He pressed forward, moving with the unsteady rhythm of a man intoxicated. A constable observed him with suspicion, and a vagrant, emerging from a passage to beg, recoiled in alarm at the sight of suffering more profound than his own. At one point, he halted beneath a streetlamp to examine his hands. He imagined he could perceive the mark of blood upon them, and a soft moan escaped his quivering mouth.

Murder! That was the vision the palmist had discovered there. Murder! The night itself appeared conscious of the fact, and the lonely wind seemed to shriek it into his hearing. The shadows of the lanes were saturated with it. It leered at him from the gables of the buildings.

Initially, he arrived at the Park, whose dark groves appeared to draw him in. He rested heavily against the iron fence, pressing his forehead against the damp metal to cool it, while he listened to the shivering quiet of the woods. ‘Murder! Murder!’ he murmured repeatedly, as if the repetition might soften the terror of the term. The sound of his own speech caused him to tremble, though he half-wished that an echo might catch his words and rouse the sleeping metropolis from its slumber. He felt a frantic urge to halt a stranger and confess the entire truth.

He then drifted past Oxford Street into dismal, disreputable lanes. A pair of women with made-up faces jeered at him as he passed. From a gloomy courtyard drifted the noise of curses and violence, followed by piercing cries, and, curled upon a sodden threshold, he observed the twisted figures of destitution and old age. A peculiar compassion stirred within him. Were these souls born of vice and woe fated to their conclusion, just as he was to his? Were they, like him, merely marionettes in a grotesque performance?

Yet it was not the enigma, but the farce of agony that impacted him; its total futility, its absurd lack of purpose. How disjointed everything appeared! How devoid of any unity! He was astonished by the clash between the superficial cheerfulness of the era and the actual realities of life. He remained quite youthful.

Eventually, he arrived before Marylebone Church. The quiet street resembled a lengthy ribbon of gleaming silver, marked here and there by the dark, intricate patterns of swaying shadows. The row of wavering gaslights curved far into the distance, and parked outside a small enclosed dwelling sat a lone hansom cab, its coachman slumbering within.

Mr. Podgers looked around nervously and pulled the heavy curtain across the door.

‘It will take a little time, Lord Arthur; you had better sit down.’

‘Be quick, sir,’ Lord Arthur shouted again, stamping his foot angrily on the polished floor.

Mr. Podgers smiled, took a small magnifying glass from his breast pocket, and wiped it carefully with his handkerchief.

‘I am quite ready,’ he said.

CHAPTER II

Ten minutes later, his face pale with terror and his eyes wild with grief, Lord Arthur Savile rushed out of Bentinck House, pushing through the crowd of fur-coated footmen standing around the large striped awning, seemingly unable to see or hear anything. The night was bitterly cold, and the gas lamps around the square flared and flickered in the sharp wind; yet his hands were hot with fever, and his forehead burned like fire. He kept moving, walking almost like a drunkard. A police officer watched him curiously as he passed, and a beggar, who slouched out from an archway to ask for money, became frightened, seeing misery greater than his own. Once, he stopped under a lamp and looked at his hands. He thought he could already detect the stain of blood on them, and a faint cry escaped his trembling lips.

Murder! That was what the palm reader had seen there. Murder! The very night seemed to know it, and the desolate wind seemed to howl it into his ear. The dark corners of the streets were full of it. It grinned at him from the rooftops. 

First, he reached the Park, whose somber woodland seemed to fascinate him. He leaned wearily against the railings, cooling his brow against the wet metal and listening to the tremulous silence of the trees. ‘Murder! Murder!’ he kept repeating, as if repetition could dull the horror of the word. The sound of his own voice made him shudder, yet he almost hoped that an echo might hear him and wake the slumbering city from its dreams. He felt a mad desire to stop a passerby and tell them everything.

Then he wandered across Oxford Street into narrow, shameful alleys. Two women with painted faces mocked him as he went by. From a dark courtyard came the sound of swearing and blows, followed by shrill screams, and, huddled on a damp doorstep, he saw the hunched forms of poverty and old age. A strange pity washed over him. Were these children of sin and misery predestined to their end, just as he was to his? Were they, like him, merely puppets in a monstrous show?

And yet it was not the mystery, but the comedy of suffering that struck him; its absolute uselessness, its grotesque lack of meaning. How incoherent everything seemed! How lacking in all harmony! He was amazed at the discord between the shallow optimism of the day and the real facts of existence. He was still very young.

THE DEANERY, CHICHESTER, 27th May.

My Dearest Aunt,

Many thanks for the flannel for the Dorcas Society, as well as the gingham. Maria was quite frightened, but it appeared so absurd that James and I burst into laughter, and even father was entertained. Do you believe Arthur would appreciate one as a wedding gift? I assume they are quite the trend in London. Father claims they should accomplish much good, as they illustrate that Liberty cannot endure, but must inevitably collapse. Father says Liberty was conceived during the French Revolution. How dreadful it seems!

I must now depart for the Dorcas, where I shall read them your most enlightening letter. How accurate, dear aunt, is your notion that in their station of life they ought to wear what is unflattering.

At seven-thirty he attired himself and proceeded to the club. Surbiton was present with a group of young men, and he was compelled to eat with them. As he was leaving the club, the porter delivered a letter to him. It was from Herr Winckelkopf, requesting that he visit the following evening to examine an explosive umbrella that detonated the moment it was unfurled. He then drifted toward the Thames Embankment and remained by the river for hours. Then the railway lamps extinguished, leaving one solitary light glowing like a massive ruby on a colossal mast, and the city’s clamor grew more distant.

At two o’clock he rose and wandered toward Blackfriars. How illusory everything appeared! How much like a bizarre vision! The structures on the opposite bank seemed constructed from gloom. One might have claimed that silver and shadow had recreated the world.
