THE CANTERVILLE GHOST
A HYLO-IDEALISTIC ROMANCE

CHAPTER I

WHEN Mr. Hiram B. Otis, the American Minister, acquired Canterville Chase, everyone warned him he was acting quite foolishly, as there was no question that the estate was haunted. In fact, Lord Canterville himself, a man of the most scrupulous integrity, felt obligated to inform Mr. Otis of this circumstance while they negotiated the agreement.

'We have not cared to reside there ourselves,' Lord Canterville remarked, 'ever since my grandaunt, the Dowager Duchess of Bolton, was frightened into a seizure—from which she never truly regained her health—by a pair of skeletal hands resting upon her shoulders while she prepared for dinner. I feel compelled to inform you, Mr. Otis, that the apparition has been witnessed by various living relatives of mine, as well as by the parish rector, the Reverend Augustus Dampier, who is a Fellow of King’s College, Cambridge. Following the Duchess's unfortunate incident, none of our younger domestic staff would remain with us, and Lady Canterville frequently suffered from a lack of sleep due to the inexplicable sounds emanating from the hallway and the library.'

'My Lord,' the Minister replied, 'I shall accept the furnishings and the phantom at an appraised price. I hail from a modern nation where we possess everything wealth can procure; and with our energetic young men painting the Old World red and whisking away your finest actresses and prima donnas, I suspect that if a ghost truly existed in Europe, we would have it back home quite soon, either in one of our public museums or touring as a spectacle.'

'I am afraid the ghost is real,' Lord Canterville said with a smile, 'even if it has resisted the proposals of your ambitious promoters. It has been documented for three hundred years, since 1584 in fact, and invariably manifests prior to the passing of any member of our family.'

Besides, they act terribly if you are actually kind to them. Do you recall that awful Mr. Podgers? He was a complete fraud. Naturally, I didn’t mind that at all, and even when he asked to borrow money I forgave him, but I couldn’t tolerate him making passes at me. He has truly made me loathe palmistry. I’m into telepathy now. It is much more entertaining.’

‘You mustn’t speak against palmistry here, Lady Windermere; it is the only topic Arthur dislikes people joking about. I assure you he is quite serious regarding it.’

‘You don’t mean to say he believes in it, Sybil?’

‘Ask him, Lady Windermere, here he comes’; and Lord Arthur approached through the garden with a large bouquet of yellow roses in his hand, and his two children dancing around him.

‘Lord Arthur?’

‘Yes, Lady Windermere.’

‘You don’t mean to say that you believe in palmistry?’

‘Of course I do,’ the young man said, smiling.

‘But why?’

‘Because I owe all the happiness of my life to it,’ he murmured, sinking into a wicker chair.

‘My dear Lord Arthur, what do you owe to it?’

‘Sybil,’ he replied, handing his wife the roses and gazing into her violet eyes.

‘What nonsense!’ Lady Windermere exclaimed. ‘I have never heard such nonsense in my entire life.’

THE tempest blew with great intensity throughout the night, yet nothing of significance transpired. The following morning, however, upon descending for breakfast, they discovered the ghastly blood-stain had returned to the floor. ‘I cannot believe the Paragon Detergent is to blame,’ Washington remarked, ‘as I have tested it on everything. Her step was so quiet and his sorrow so profound that he remained unaware of her until she addressed him.

THE MODEL MILLIONAIRE
A NOTE OF ADMIRATION

UNLESS one possesses wealth, there is little benefit in being a delightful individual. Romance is the prerogative of the affluent, not the vocation of the idle. The impoverished ought to remain grounded and dull. It is superior to possess a steady revenue than to be captivating. These represent the fundamental realities of contemporary existence that Hughie Erskine never grasped. Poor Hughie! Mentally, we must concede, he lacked significance. He never uttered a clever or even a malicious remark throughout his existence. Yet he was remarkably handsome, with his wavy chestnut hair, his sharp features, and his slate-colored eyes. He was as well-liked by gentlemen as by ladies, and he possessed every talent except that of accumulating wealth. His father had left him his military saber and a History of the Peninsular War spanning fifteen books. Hughie suspended the former above his mirror, placed the latter upon a ledge between Ruff’s Guide and Bailey’s Magazine, and subsisted on two hundred pounds annually provided by an elderly aunt. He had attempted various pursuits. He had worked at the Stock Exchange for half a year; yet what could a social butterfly achieve among aggressive financiers? He had been a tea-trader for a slightly longer duration, but soon grew weary of pekoe and souchong. Then he attempted vending dry sherry. That failed; the wine was somewhat too parched. Eventually, he became nothing, a charming, unproductive youth with an ideal visage and no career.

To exacerbate the situation, he was smitten.

I knocked on the door, and a respectable-looking woman opened it. I asked if she had any rooms available. “Well, sir,” she replied, “the drawing-rooms are technically rented, but I haven’t seen the lady in three months, and since she owes rent, you can have them.” “Is this the lady?” I asked, showing her the photograph. “That’s her, all right,” she exclaimed, “and when is she coming back, sir?” “The lady is dead,” I replied. “Oh, sir, I hope not!” the woman said. “She was my best tenant. She paid me three guineas a week just to sit in my drawing-rooms occasionally.” “Did she meet someone here?” I asked, but the woman insisted that wasn’t the case, saying she always came alone and saw no one. “What on earth did she do here?” I cried. “She simply sat in the drawing-room, sir, reading books, and sometimes had tea,” the woman answered. I didn’t know what to say, so I gave her a sovereign and left. Now, what do you think it all meant? You don’t believe the woman was telling the truth?”

“I do.”

“Then why did Lady Alroy go there?”

“My dear Gerald,” I answered, “Lady Alroy was simply a woman with a mania for mystery. She rented those rooms for the thrill of going there with her veil down and imagining she was a heroine. She had a passion for secrecy, but she was merely a Sphinx without a secret.”

“Do you really think so?”

“I am sure of it,” I replied.

He took out the leather case, opened it, and looked at the photograph. “I wonder?” he said at last.

It must be admitted that he was initially drawn to Hughie entirely because of his personal charm. He used to say that the only people a painter should know are those who are dull and beautiful—people who are a pleasure to look at and restful to talk to. He believed that stylish men and charming women should rule the world. Once he knew Hughie better, however, he liked him just as much for his bright, cheerful spirit and his generous, reckless nature, and he gave him permanent access to his studio.

When Hughie arrived, he found Trevor putting the finishing touches on a wonderful life-size painting of a beggar. The beggar was standing on a raised platform in the corner of the studio. He was a withered old man with a face like wrinkled parchment and a very pitiful expression. A coarse brown cloak, full of tears and tatters, was draped over his shoulders. His thick boots were patched, and he leaned on a rough stick with one hand while holding out a battered hat for money with the other.

“What an amazing model!” Hughie whispered as he shook hands with his friend.

“An amazing model?” Trevor shouted at the top of his voice. “I should think so! You don’t find beggars like him every day. He is a rare find, my dear fellow; a living Velasquez! Good heavens, what an etching Rembrandt could have made of him!”

“Poor old man,” Hughie said. “He looks so miserable! But I suppose, to you painters, his face is his fortune?”

“Certainly,” Trevor replied. “You wouldn’t want a beggar to look happy, would you?”

“How much does a model get paid for sitting?” Hughie asked as he found a comfortable seat on a sofa.

“A shilling an hour.”

“And how much do you get for your painting, Alan?”

“Oh, for this one I get two thousand!”

“Pounds?”

“Guineas. Painters, poets, and doctors are always paid in guineas.”

“Well, I think the model should get a percentage,” Hughie laughed. “They work just as hard as you do.”

“Nonsense! Think of the trouble of applying the paint alone, and standing at an easel all day! It is all very well for you to talk, Hughie, but I assure you that there are moments when art almost reaches the level of manual labor. But you must not chatter; I am very busy. Smoke a cigarette and keep quiet.”

After some time, the servant entered and told Trevor that the frame-maker wanted to speak with him.

“Don’t run away, Hughie,” he said as he left. “I will be back in a moment.”

The old beggar took advantage of Trevor’s absence to rest for a moment on a wooden bench behind him. He looked so forlorn and wretched that Hughie could not help but pity him, and he felt in his pockets to see what money he had. All he could find was a sovereign and some copper coins. “Poor old fellow,” he thought to himself, “he needs it more than I do, even if it means no carriage rides for two weeks.” He walked across the studio and slipped the sovereign into the beggar’s hand.

Poetry and the theater were his two primary sources of joy. During his time at Eton, he constantly engaged in costumed performances of Shakespeare, and upon arriving at Trinity, he joined the A.D.C. during his initial term. I recall feeling quite envious of his theatrical talents. I was foolishly attached to him, likely because we were such opposites. I was a clumsy, frail boy with oversized feet and a face covered in freckles. Freckles are as common in Scottish lineages as gout is among the English. Cyril often remarked that he would choose the gout over the freckles; he placed an exaggerated importance on physical beauty, even presenting a lecture to our debate club arguing that being attractive was superior to being virtuous. He was undeniably striking. Those who disliked him—Philistines, university instructors, and divinity students—often dismissed him as merely pretty, but his features held far more depth than that. I consider him the most magnificent person I have ever encountered, possessing unmatched grace in his gestures and a captivating demeanor. He enchanted everyone worth enchanting, along with many who were not. He was frequently headstrong and irritable, and I often perceived him as deeply dishonest, though I believe this stemmed mostly from his excessive need to be liked. Poor Cyril! I once remarked that he settled for trivial victories, but he simply chuckled. He was dreadfully pampered. I suspect all delightful individuals are pampered; it is the core of their allure.

‘Regardless, I must recount Cyril’s acting. You are aware that women were not permitted to perform with the A.D.C., at least not during my era. I am uncertain of the current rules. Naturally, Cyril was always assigned the female roles, and when they staged As You Like It, he portrayed Rosalind. It was a stunning portrayal. In truth, Cyril Graham remains the only flawless Rosalind I have witnessed. It is impossible to convey the elegance, subtlety, and sophistication of his work. It caused quite a stir, and the cramped little theater was packed every evening. Even now, reading the play brings Cyril to mind. It seemed as if it were composed specifically for him. The following term, he graduated and moved to London to study for a diplomatic career. However, he never applied himself. He passed his days studying Shakespeare’s Sonnets and his nights at the theater. Naturally, he was desperate to pursue acting. It took all the effort Lord Crediton and I could muster to stop him. Perhaps he would still be with us had he followed that path. Offering advice is always foolish, but offering sound advice is entirely disastrous. I trust you will never commit that mistake. If you do, you will surely regret it.

‘Now, to reach the heart of the matter, I received a note from Cyril one day, requesting that I visit his chambers that night.

The two things that truly brought him joy were poetry and acting. At Eton, he was constantly dressing up and reciting Shakespeare, and when we started at Trinity, he joined the A.D.C. during his first term. I recall being quite jealous of his acting. I was foolishly devoted to him, likely because we were so different in certain ways. I was a rather awkward, frail boy with enormous feet and a face covered in freckles. Freckles are common in Scottish families just as gout is in English ones. Cyril used to say he preferred the gout of the two, but he always placed an absurdly high value on physical appearance, even reading a paper to our debating society to argue that it was better to be attractive than to be virtuous. He was certainly remarkably handsome. People who disliked him—Philistines, college tutors, and young men studying for the clergy—would claim he was merely pretty, but there was much more to his face than simple prettiness. I believe he was the most magnificent person I ever encountered, and nothing could surpass the grace of his movements or the charm of his manner. He fascinated everyone worth fascinating, along with many who were not. He was often willful and petulant, and I used to think him terribly insincere. I believe this was mainly due to his excessive desire to please. Poor Cyril! I once told him he was satisfied with very cheap triumphs, but he only laughed. He was dreadfully spoiled. I suspect all charming people are spoiled; it is the secret of their appeal.

However, I must tell you about Cyril’s acting. You know that no actresses were permitted to perform at the A.D.C., at least not during my time. I am not sure how it is now. Naturally, Cyril was always cast in the female roles, and when *As You Like It* was produced, he played Rosalind. It was a marvelous performance. In fact, Cyril Graham was the only perfect Rosalind I have ever seen. It would be impossible to describe the beauty, delicacy, and refinement of his portrayal. It caused a massive sensation, and the cramped little theater, as it was then, was packed every night. Even now, when I read the play, I cannot help but think of Cyril. If you do, you will regret it.

To him, just as to me, they represented verses of grave and sorrowful significance, extracted from the anguish of Shakespeare’s spirit and sweetened by the nectar of his speech. He was even less inclined to concede that they functioned as a mere philosophical metaphor, or that within them Shakespeare was speaking to his Ideal Self, his concept of Ideal Manhood, the Spirit of Beauty, Reason, the Divine Logos, or the Catholic Church. He sensed, as I believe we all must, that the Sonnets are directed toward a specific person—a particular youth whose character for some reason appeared to have flooded Shakespeare’s soul with both harrowing joy and equally harrowing despair.

Having cleared the path in this fashion, Cyril requested that I set aside any prior notions I might have held regarding the matter and grant his own hypothesis an impartial and open-minded hearing. The dilemma he highlighted was this: Who was that young man of Shakespeare’s era who, despite lacking noble lineage or even a noble disposition, was spoken to by him with such fervent devotion that we can only marvel at this peculiar adoration, feeling almost hesitant to turn the key that reveals the secret of the poet’s heart? Who was he whose bodily grace was such that it served as the foundation of Shakespeare’s craft, the primary fountain of his inspiration, and the very embodiment of his visions? To view him merely as the subject of certain love verses is to overlook the entire significance of the poems, for the art Shakespeare discusses in the Sonnets is not the art of the Sonnets themselves—which were to him but minor and private trifles—but rather the art of the playwright to which he is constantly referring. And he to whom Shakespeare declared, 

Thou art all my art, and dost advance
As high as learning my rude ignorance,

he to whom he pledged immortality, 

Where breath most breathes, even in the mouths of men,—

was surely none other than the boy-actor for whom he fashioned Viola and Imogen, Juliet and Rosalind, Portia and Desdemona, and Cleopatra herself. This was Cyril Graham’s hypothesis, developed, as you can see, entirely from the Sonnets themselves, and relying for its validity not so much on verifiable proof or structured evidence, but on a sort of spiritual and aesthetic intuition, through which alone he maintained the true significance of the poems could be perceived. I recall him reciting that exquisite sonnet—

How can my Muse want subject to invent,
While thou dost breathe, that pour’st into my verse
Thine own sweet argument, too excellent
For every vulgar paper to rehearse?
O, give thyself the thanks, if aught in me
Worthy perusal stand against thy sight;
For who’s so dumb that cannot write to thee,
When thou thyself dost give invention light?
Be thou the tenth Muse, ten times more in worth
Than those old nine which rhymers invocate;
And he that calls on thee, let him bring forth
Eternal numbers to outlive long date—

and demonstrating how thoroughly it supported his claim; indeed, he examined every one of the Sonnets meticulously, showing, or believing he showed, that under his new interpretation, passages that had appeared murky, immoral, or hyperbolic became lucid, logical, and of profound artistic value, demonstrating Shakespeare’s understanding of the genuine connection between the actor’s craft and the dramatist’s art.

To him, as to me, they were poems of serious and tragic significance, wrung from the bitterness of Shakespeare’s heart and sweetened by the honey of his lips. He was even less willing to admit that they were merely a philosophical allegory, or that in them Shakespeare was addressing his Ideal Self, Ideal Manhood, the Spirit of Beauty, Reason, the Divine Logos, or the Catholic Church. He felt, as I believe we all must, that the Sonnets are addressed to an individual—a specific young man whose personality for some reason seems to have filled Shakespeare’s soul with both terrible joy and terrible despair.

The problem, he pointed out, was this: Who was that young man of Shakespeare’s day who, without being of noble birth or even noble character, was addressed in terms of such passionate adoration that we can only wonder at the strange worship and feel almost afraid to unlock the mystery of the poet’s heart? Who was he whose physical beauty became the very cornerstone of Shakespeare’s art, the source of his inspiration, and the incarnation of his dreams? ‘What was I supposed to respond? It never crossed my mind that Cyril Graham was deceiving me or attempting to validate his hypothesis through a counterfeit.’

‘But is it actually a forgery?’ I inquired.

‘Undoubtedly,’ Erskine replied. ‘It is an excellent fake, but a fake nonetheless. I recall Cyril seemed remarkably composed about the situation at the time, though he frequently insisted he personally needed no such evidence, believing his theory stood complete without it. I mocked him, insisting the theory would collapse otherwise, and I offered him my enthusiastic congratulations on his incredible find. We subsequently planned to have the image etched or reproduced as the frontispiece for Cyril’s edition of the Sonnets. For three months, we did nothing but review every line of the poems until we had resolved every textual or interpretive ambiguity. One unfortunate day, while visiting a print shop in Holborn, I noticed some exquisite silver-point sketches on the counter. I was so captivated that I purchased them, and the owner, a man named Rawlings, explained they were the work of a young artist named Edward Merton—a talented but destitute man. I visited Merton a few days later, having obtained his address from the dealer, and encountered a pale, intriguing young man accompanied by a rather plain wife, who I later learned served as his model. I expressed my admiration for his work, which clearly pleased him, and I requested to see more of his portfolio. While examining a collection of truly beautiful pieces—for Merton possessed a remarkably delicate and charming style—I suddenly spotted a sketch of the Mr. W. H. portrait. There was no mistaking it. It was nearly an exact replica, differing only in that the masks of Tragedy and Comedy were not hanging from the marble table as they appear in the painting, but were instead resting on the floor near the young man’s feet.’

CHAPTER II

It was after midday when I roused, and the sunlight poured through my chamber curtains in elongated, dusty golden rays. I instructed my attendant to deny all visitors; and after consuming a cup of chocolate and a roll, I retrieved my volume of Shakespeare’s Sonnets from the shelf and commenced a meticulous examination. Each poem appeared to support Cyril Graham’s hypothesis. I felt as though I held Shakespeare’s heart in my grasp, measuring every individual beat and rhythm of his desire. I pondered the remarkable young actor, perceiving his features within every verse.

I recall being particularly impressed by two sonnets: the 53rd and the 67th. In the former, Shakespeare, while praising Willie Hughes for the adaptability of his performance and his extensive repertoire—a range spanning from Rosalind to Juliet, and from Beatrice to Ophelia—inquires of him—

What is your substance, whereof are you made,
That millions of strange shadows on you tend?
Since every one hath, every one, one shade,
And you, but one, can every shadow lend—

These verses would be incomprehensible if not directed toward a performer, as the term 'shadow' possessed a specific theatrical connotation during Shakespeare’s era. 'The best in this kind are but shadows,' Theseus remarks regarding the players in A Midsummer Night’s Dream, and numerous comparable references exist in the writings of that period. These sonnets clearly pertained to the collection wherein Shakespeare examines the essence of the actor’s craft and the peculiar, uncommon temperament required for the ideal performer. 'How is it,' Shakespeare asks Willie Hughes, 'that you possess such a multitude of identities?' He then proceeds to observe that his attractiveness is such that it seems to manifest every variety and aspect of fantasy, to personify each vision of the inventive mind—a concept developed further in the subsequent sonnet, which commences with the elegant reflection,

O, how much more doth beauty beauteous seem
By that sweet ornament which truth doth give!

“Besides, everyone knows the Sonnets were written to Lord Pembroke. The issue is settled once and for all.”

“The issue is not settled!” I exclaimed. “I will pick up the theory where Cyril Graham left off, and I will prove to the world that he was right.”

“Foolish boy,” Erskine said. “Go home; it is past two, and stop thinking about Willie Hughes. I regret telling you anything about it, and I am truly sorry that I convinced you of something I don’t even believe in myself.”

“You have handed me the key to the greatest mystery in modern literature,” I replied, “and I will not rest until I have made you acknowledge—until I have made everyone acknowledge—that Cyril Graham was the most insightful Shakespearean critic of our time.”

CHAPTER III

Following the passage of three weeks, I resolved to make a vigorous plea to Erskine to honor the legacy of Cyril Graham and to share with the public his extraordinary reading of the Sonnets—the sole interpretation that fully resolved the mystery. I lament that I possess no duplicate of my correspondence, nor have I managed to locate the original; yet I recall that I traversed the entire subject, filling numerous pages with fervent restatements of the evidence and logic my research had provided. It appeared to me that I was not simply reinstating Cyril Graham to his rightful position in the annals of literature, but also salvaging the reputation of Shakespeare himself from the tiresome recollection of a mundane affair. I infused the letter with all my fervor. I infused the letter with all my conviction.

In truth, the moment I dispatched it, a strange shift occurred within me. It felt as though I had surrendered my ability to trust in the Willie Hughes hypothesis regarding the Sonnets, as if something had departed from me, and I felt entirely detached from the entire matter. What had transpired? It is hard to define. Perhaps, by articulating a passion perfectly, I had depleted the passion itself. Emotional energies, much like the energies of physical existence, possess their definite boundaries. Perhaps the mere endeavor to persuade another of a theory necessitates a form of abandoning one’s own capacity for belief. Perhaps I was simply weary of the entire affair, and, my zeal having extinguished, my intellect was left to its own dispassionate assessment. However it transpired, and I cannot claim to account for it, there was no question that Willie Hughes abruptly transformed for me into a mere legend, a hollow reverie, the youthful whim of a man who, like many passionate souls, was more eager to persuade his peers than to be persuaded himself.

There could certainly be no more appropriate place for the man to whom Shakespeare said, ‘you are all my art,’ than this small vineyard outside the city walls. After all, did Tragedy not spring from the sorrows of Dionysos? Was the light laughter of Comedy, with its carefree joy and quick wit, not first heard from the lips of Sicilian grape-pickers? Indeed, did the purple and red stains of wine foam on faces and limbs not provide the first hint of the charm and fascination of disguise—the desire for self-concealment and the sense of the value of objectivity, thus appearing in the crude beginnings of the art? Regardless of where he lay—whether in that small vineyard at the gate of the Gothic town, or in some dim London churchyard amidst the roar and bustle of our great city—no grand monument marked his resting place. His true tomb, as Shakespeare understood, was the poet’s verse, and his true monument was the permanence of the drama. This had been the case for others whose beauty had provided a new creative impulse to their era. The ivory body of the Bithynian slave rots in the green mud of the Nile, and the dust of the young Athenian is scattered on the yellow hills of the Cerameicus; yet Antinous lives on in sculpture, and Charmides in philosophy.

CHAPTER III

After three weeks had passed, I decided to make a strong appeal to Erskine to do justice to the memory of Cyril Graham and to share with the world his marvelous interpretation of the Sonnets—the only interpretation that fully explained the problem. I regret to say that I do not have a copy of my letter, nor have I been able to find the original; but I remember that I covered the entire subject, filling sheets of paper with passionate restatements of the arguments and proofs my study had suggested to me. It felt as though I were not merely restoring Cyril Graham to his rightful place in literary history, but rescuing the honor of Shakespeare himself from the tedious memory of a mundane affair. I poured all my enthusiasm into that letter. I poured all my faith into it.

In fact, no sooner had I sent it off than a strange reaction came over me. It felt as if I had given away my capacity to believe in the Willie Hughes theory of the Sonnets, that something had left me, and that I was completely indifferent to the entire subject. What had happened? It is difficult to say. Perhaps by finding perfect expression for a passion, I had exhausted the passion itself. Emotional forces, like the forces of physical life, have their definite limits. Perhaps the mere effort to convert someone to a theory involves some form of renouncing the power of belief. Perhaps I was simply tired of the whole thing, and with my enthusiasm burnt out, my reason was left to its own dispassionate judgment. However it happened, and I cannot pretend to explain it, there was no doubt that Willie Hughes suddenly became to me a mere myth, an idle dream, the boyish fancy of a young man who, like most ardent spirits, was more anxious to convince others than to be convinced himself.

Perishing for one’s religious convictions is the most regrettable way to spend a life, yet to perish for a theory of literature! It appeared beyond belief.

I checked the date. The correspondence was seven days old. An unlucky series of events had kept me away from the club for a few days, or I might have received it soon enough to rescue him. Perhaps there was still time. I hurried to my quarters, gathered my belongings, and departed on the evening train from Charing Cross. The travel was agonizing. I felt as though I would never reach my destination. Upon arrival, I took a carriage to the Hôtel l’Angleterre. I was informed that Erskine had been interred two days prior in the English burial ground. The entire catastrophe possessed a ghastly, absurd quality. I uttered various frantic remarks, drawing curious stares from the individuals in the lobby.

Abruptly, Lady Erskine, dressed in heavy mourning attire, crossed the foyer. Upon noticing me, she approached, whispered a few words regarding her unfortunate son, and began to weep. I guided her into her private parlor, where an older man was waiting. It was the English physician.

We spoke at length about Erskine, yet I remained silent regarding his reasons for taking his own life. It was clear he had shared nothing with his mother about the cause that had pushed him toward such a lethal, irrational deed. Eventually, Lady Erskine stood and remarked, “George left you a keepsake. It was an object he valued deeply. I shall retrieve it for you.”

Once she had exited, I turned to the doctor and remarked, “What a terrible blow this must be for Lady Erskine! I am surprised she handles it with such composure.”

“Oh, she was aware for months that this was inevitable,” he replied.

“Aware for months!” I exclaimed. “But why did she not intervene? Why did she not have him supervised? He must have been insane.”

The doctor gazed at me in confusion. “I do not follow your meaning,” he stated.

“Well,” I insisted, “if a mother is aware that her son intends to kill himself—”

“Suicide!” he countered. “Poor Erskine did not take his own life. He succumbed to tuberculosis. He traveled here to pass away. From the moment I examined him, I realized there was no chance of recovery. One lung was nearly destroyed, and the other was severely compromised. Three days before his passing, he inquired if there was any hope. I informed him honestly that there was none, and that he had only a few days remaining. He composed several letters and remained entirely composed, keeping his faculties until the end.”

Just then, Lady Erskine returned with the fateful portrait of Willie Hughes. “As George was dying, he requested that I present this to you,” she said. As I accepted it, her tears dropped onto my hand.

The portrait is currently displayed in my library, where it receives much praise from my acquaintances in the arts. They have concluded it is an Oudry rather than a Clouet. I have never felt the need to share its actual history. Yet, at times, when I gaze upon it, I reflect that there is truly much to support the Willie Hughes hypothesis regarding the Sonnets of Shakespeare.

Dying for one’s religious beliefs is the worst way a person can spend their life, but dying for a literary theory! It seemed impossible.

I checked the date. The letter was a week old. Some unfortunate stroke of bad luck had kept me from the club for several days, or I might have received it in time to save him. Perhaps it wasn’t too late. I drove to my apartment, packed my bags, and caught the night train from Charing Cross. The journey was unbearable. I thought I would never arrive. As soon as I did, I drove to the Hôtel l’Angleterre. They told me Erskine had been buried two days earlier in the English cemetery. There was something horribly absurd about the whole tragedy. I said all sorts of frantic things, and the people in the lobby stared at me with curiosity.

Suddenly, Lady Erskine, dressed in deep mourning, walked across the lobby. When she saw me, she approached, murmured something about her poor son, and burst into tears. I led her into her sitting room. An elderly gentleman was waiting for her there. It was the English doctor.

We talked a great deal about Erskine, but I said nothing about his motive for committing suicide. It was clear that he hadn’t told his mother anything about the reason that had driven him to such a fatal, insane act. Finally, Lady Erskine stood up and said, “George left you something as a keepsake. It was something he valued very much. I will go get it for you.”

As soon as she left the room, I turned to the doctor and said, “What a terrible shock this must have been for Lady Erskine! I’m surprised she is holding up as well as she is.”

“Oh, she knew for months that it was coming,” he replied.

“Knew for months!” I exclaimed. “But why didn’t she stop him? Why didn’t she have him watched? He must have been out of his mind.”

The doctor stared at me. “I don’t know what you mean,” he said.

“Well,” I cried, “if a mother knows that her son is going to commit suicide—”

“Suicide!” he answered. “Poor Erskine didn’t commit suicide. He died of tuberculosis. He came here to die. The moment I saw him, I knew there was no hope. One lung was almost gone, and the other was severely affected. Three days before he died, he asked me if there was any hope. I told him honestly that there wasn’t, and that he had only a few days to live. He wrote some letters and was quite resigned, remaining clear-headed until the end.”

At that moment, Lady Erskine entered the room with the fateful picture of Willie Hughes in her hand. “When George was dying, he begged me to give you this,” she said. As I took it from her, her tears fell onto my hand.

The picture hangs now in my library, where it is highly admired by my artistic friends. They have decided it is not a Clouet, but an Oudry. I have never cared to tell them its true history. But sometimes, when I look at it, I think there is actually a great deal to be said for the Willie Hughes theory of Shakespeare’s Sonnets.
